docs.text: • Multi-feature DATASET lookup (agent/consumer_agent.py, agent/graph.py) — When the user asks "is there a dataset containing feature1 and feature2?", the agent now returns three layers of information: which datasets contain feature1, which contain feature2, and which datasets contain both (intersection). The classifier recognises comma-separated feature names in object_name via two new few-shot examples. skill_dataset delegates to a single-feature path for one name and a new multi-feature path otherwise; the dataset creation flow (Step 1–3) is only triggered for single-feature no-match results.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• MCP server integration for the consumer agent (agent/consumer_agent.py, agent/graph.py, agent/chatbot.py) — The consumer agent can now delegate questions to an external MCP (Model Context Protocol) server when no feature store skill applies.
  • Configure the MCP endpoint via TDFS4DS_MCP_SERVER_URL in .env or as an OS env var (e.g. TDFS4DS_MCP_SERVER_URL=http://localhost:8000/sse); the variable is auto-loaded into tdfs4ds.MCP_SERVER_URL on import.
  • Toggle at runtime via the new mcp_enabled parameter on consumer_agent() and on the Gradio chatbot's "Enable MCP Tools" checkbox (hidden when no URL is configured).
  • Safety guardrails enforced at both the tool and LLM-prompt level:
    • Forbidden objects: SELECT statements against feature store process views or feature storage tables are refused outright; _get_forbidden_objects() fetches the live lists from the process and feature catalogs at call time.
    • Allowed data access: the sub-agent's system prompt directs it to only query datasets registered in the dataset catalog when feature data is needed.
    • Row cap: all SELECT queries are rewritten to SELECT TOP 20 by _rewrite_select_top20(); a ⚠ notice is appended to the answer when the 20-row limit is reached.
  • Uses langchain-mcp-adapters (MultiServerMCPClient + SSE transport) as a lazy import inside skill_mcp_tools(), so the dependency is optional.
  • Jupyter-compatible async execution: probes asyncio.get_running_loop() and falls back to a ThreadPoolExecutor when a loop is already running.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• fs-upload skill (tdfs4ds/skills/fs-upload/SKILL.md) — added mandatory LOCKING ROW FOR ACCESS to the Pattern A SQL template, the partitioning= comment in the upload_features call, and two new rules: access-lock mandate and hyper-segmented ML partition column guidance.
  • fs-dataset skill (tdfs4ds/skills/fs-dataset/SKILL.md) — added rule explaining that a partition column must be registered as a feature via upload_features before it can appear in a dataset (hyper-segmented ML pattern).

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • generate_partitioning_clause crashes on unparenthesised PARTITION BY (utils/info.py) — extract_partition_content uses a regex that requires outer parentheses (PARTITION BY (...)). When the user passes a bare string such as 'PARTITION BY RANGE_N(region BETWEEN 1 AND 10 EACH 1, NO RANGE OR UNKNOWN)', the regex returns None and the subsequent len(None) raised TypeError. Fixed by detecting the None result and falling back to extracting everything after PARTITION BY as the content, so FEATURE_ID partitioning is correctly prepended.

docs.title: Added
docs.text: • drop_dataset module-level function (dataset/dataset_catalog.py) — expose DatasetCatalog.drop_dataset as a standalone importable function so callers can write from tdfs4ds.dataset.dataset_catalog import drop_dataset without instantiating the class directly. Accepts dataset_id and an optional schema_name (defaults to tdfs4ds.SCHEMA).

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • No feature columns documented in dataset documentation (genai/documentation.py) — _extract_feature_process_map_from_ddl captured the full qualified column alias produced by build_dataset (e.g. provider_velocity_7d__demo_user___vw_feat_claims_) as the feature-name key. _propagate_docs_from_processes then tried to match that full alias against the short names returned by retrieve_documentation (e.g. provider_velocity_7d), which always failed, so every feature ended up in missing_features and the dataset documentation showed "No feature columns documented." Fixed by stripping the __{database}___{view}_ qualifier in _extract_feature_process_map_from_ddl (split on first __) so the map keys are always short business names. Duplicate short names across multiple processes are deduplicated by keeping the first encountered process (any documented process provides the same description).

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • Entity columns silently dropped for intermediate views (genai/documentation.py) — _document_intermediate_view hardcoded entity_columns=[] when calling document_sql_query_columns, so intermediate views (e.g. vw_temp_aggregate) were always documented with 0 entity columns regardless of the actual entity definition. Fixed by adding an entity_columns parameter to _document_intermediate_view and threading it through from both call sites: document_process_incremental passes process_info.get('ENTITY_COLUMNS', []) and document_dataset_incremental passes its local entity_columns. Matching is case-insensitive (uppercased set lookup) while preserving the column casing from the view's own column list.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • Missing skills/*/prompts/*.md in installed package (setup.py) — the package_data glob only included skills/*/SKILL.md (top-level Claude Code skill descriptions). Nested LLM prompt files under skills/*/prompts/*.md were never packaged, so a non-editable pip install . produced a site-packages/tdfs4ds/skills/ tree with no prompts/ subdirectory. Every skill_prompt("qo-summary"), skill_prompt("qo-explain"), etc. raised FileNotFoundError: No bundled skill named 'qo-summary'. Fixed by adding 'skills/*/prompts/*.md' to package_data.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • _generate_dataset_description AIMessage bug (genai/documentation.py) — the vLLM and fallback chains piped the LLM output directly into _robust_json_parser, which expects a plain str. LangChain passes an AIMessage through the | operator, causing a ValueError: Expected string response, got AIMessage. Fixed by wrapping with RunnableLambda(_parse) that extracts .content first — the same pattern already used in build_sql_documentation_chain and build_explain_documentation_chain.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• BUILD_DATASET entity clarification fallback (graph.py, consumer_agent.py) — when the feature catalog returns no features for the active domain, the agent no longer dead-ends immediately. Instead it enters a new awaiting_entity_clarification pending state and asks the user to name the entity or subject area (e.g. "Customer", "Transaction"). The user's answer is used to retry _get_feature_catalog_with_docs(entity_filter=…) and, if features are found, the flow continues normally to feature proposal. Only when the retry also returns nothing does the agent report no_features_found with a precise "no features matching X" message. The hard-stop message now includes the entity hint for better diagnostics.
  • _DATASET_PENDING_ACTIONS (graph.py) gains "awaiting_entity_clarification" so the LLM classifier short-circuit correctly routes the clarification reply back to node_skill_build_dataset.
  • ca-build-dataset/SKILL.md updated to document the new awaiting_entity_clarification pending state and the two-attempt strategy.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• BUILD_DATASET consumer-agent intent — a new 9th intent that lets a business user ask the agent to autonomously create a feature store dataset from a plain-language use-case description (e.g. "build me a dataset for churn analysis"). No feature name is required to start the flow.
  • tdfs4ds/skills/ca-build-dataset/SKILL.md — skill file for the new intent, auto-discovered by consumer_agent_skill_catalog() (uses the ca- prefix convention). Registers BUILD_DATASET into the dynamic intent classifier and graph without any hardwired lists.
  • resolve_features_for_dataset(entity_name, feature_list) (consumer_agent.py) — resolves a list of {name, description} feature dicts to the selected_features dict format required by build_dataset(). Calls get_feature_versions() and splits results into a clean {feature_name: uuid} dict (single-version features, ready for build_dataset()) and a conflicts list ([{feature_name, versions: [{process_id, process_view_name}]}]) for features with multiple registered process IDs.
  • skill_build_dataset(entity_name, selected_features, dataset_name, ...) (consumer_agent.py) — end-to-end dataset creation skill: calls build_dataset() then, when an LLM provider is configured, auto-calls document_dataset_incremental(). Returns status=dataset_created with a doc_status field (success / failed / skipped), or status=creation_failed on error. Accepts return_query=True for dry-run / unit-test use without a live connection.
  • node_skill_build_dataset(state) (graph.py) — LangGraph node implementing a 4-step autonomous creation flow:
  • Step 0 (no pending): resolves entity from the catalog (uses the feature-richest entity as a heuristic when no feature name is given), calls skill_propose_features() immediately to propose the top-20 relevant features for the use case.
  • Step 1 (awaiting_feature_confirmation): user replies "ok" or "all"; calls resolve_features_for_dataset() to detect version conflicts.
  • Step 2 (awaiting_version_selection): if conflicts exist, presents options (numbered) and waits for the user to pick a version per feature; parses replies by number or view-name substring.
  • Step 3 (awaiting_dataset_name): validates the proposed name with skill_check_dataset_name(); on success immediately calls skill_build_dataset() and creates the Teradata view + docs.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• IntentResult Literal extended to include "BUILD_DATASET" (hardwired fallback model, consumer_agent.py).
  • Intent classifier examples gain three BUILD_DATASET examples (English and French) to disambiguate from the existing DATASET intent.
  • _DATASET_PENDING_ACTIONS (graph.py) gains "awaiting_version_selection" so the LLM classifier short-circuit also covers the version-conflict resolution turn.
  • _node_classify_impl short-circuit (graph.py) now preserves the active intent instead of forcing "DATASET" — BUILD_DATASET flows remain correctly routed to their own node across all pending turns.
  • synthesize_answer() (consumer_agent.py) gains a full BUILD_DATASET branch covering all status codes: features_proposed, features_confirmed, version_conflict, name_available, name_taken, dataset_created, creation_failed, no_features_found.
  • _INTENT_NODE_MAP and _SKILL_LABEL (graph.py) both include "BUILD_DATASET".

docs.title: Added
docs.text: • 11 qo-* prompt files reorganized into nested prompts/ subfolders under their owning qo-skill-* directories (e.g. qo-skill-explain/prompts/qo-explain.md instead of standalone qo-explain/SKILL.md). Reduces skill namespace clutter while keeping related prompts together.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• skill_path() in cli.py now searches nested prompt paths: When a flat SKILL.md is not found, it searches <skill>/prompts/<name>.md, allowing skill_prompt('qo-explain') to resolve transparently from the new nested location.
  • install_skills() in cli.py copies prompts/ subfolders: When installing skills with tdfs4ds-install-skills(), the prompts/ subfolder (if present) is copied alongside SKILL.md so relative links in skill bodies resolve correctly.
  • qo-skill-*/SKILL.md bodies rewritten with ## Execution sections: Each now documents its full execution pipeline, references prompt files with relative [prompt-name](prompts/prompt-name.md) links, and explains inputs/outputs, matching the discoverable-skill pattern from ca-* and fs-* skills.
  • Argument-hint bracket format fixed on qo-skill-* SKILL.md ([...] instead of <...> for consistency with other skills).
  • All fs-* SKILL.md files now have name: frontmatter field with human-readable Feature Store: <Action> naming (e.g. name: Feature Store: Upload), consistent with Consumer Agent: * and Query Optimizer: * conventions.
  • Query optimizer report naming changed to timestamp-first (tdfs4ds_optim_{timestamp}_{view}_{process_id}.md instead of tdfs4ds_optim_{view}_{process_id}_{timestamp}.md). Reports now sort naturally by date in file listings.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• 8 consumer-agent SKILL.md files (tdfs4ds/skills/ca-*/SKILL.md): Each of the 8 consumer-agent intents (SEARCH, DEFINITION, USAGE, FRESHNESS, SUMMARY, LINEAGE, EXPLAIN, DATASET) is now a first-class Claude Code skill in tdfs4ds/skills/ with compliant frontmatter (name, intent, description, argument-hint, allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob). Skills are identified by the ca- name prefix convention. They serve dual purpose: Claude Code users can invoke them as reference skills, and build_graph() reads them at runtime to configure the consumer agent dynamically. Bundled via package_data.
  • consumer_agent_skill_catalog() (cli.py / top-level API): Returns metadata for all ca-* skills (filtered by name prefix), keyed by skill name. Used by build_graph() to discover available intents at runtime without any hardwired lists.
  • reset_consumer_agent() (tdfs4ds/agent/graph.py): Clears the compiled-graph singleton cache so the next consumer_agent() call picks up any newly added or removed ca-* SKILL.md files.
  • _build_intent_model(intents) (tdfs4ds/agent/consumer_agent.py): Helper that builds a dynamic Pydantic model for structured intent classification output using Union[Literal["A"], Literal["B"], ...] (equivalent to Literal["A", "B", ...] per PEP 586) and pydantic.create_model.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• consumer_agent() gains skills= parameter (tdfs4ds/agent/graph.py): Pass a list of intent names (e.g. ['SEARCH', 'SUMMARY']) to restrict the agent to a subset of skills. Different subsets use separate compiled-graph instances (and separate MemorySaver state), so thread_id namespacing is preserved per skill set.
  • build_graph() is now data-driven (tdfs4ds/agent/graph.py): Graph construction, routing, and the intent-classifier system prompt are all built dynamically from consumer_agent_skill_catalog() instead of being hardwired. Adding or removing a ca-* SKILL.md file (and calling reset_consumer_agent()) changes the available intents without any Python edits.
  • intent_classifier() accepts dynamic model and descriptions (tdfs4ds/agent/consumer_agent.py): New optional parameters intent_model and intent_descriptions allow the graph to inject a dynamically constructed Pydantic model and per-intent descriptions. Falls back to the hardwired IntentResult and static descriptions when called without arguments (backward compatibility).
  • install_skills() installs all 19 skills (cli.py): ca-* skills are valid Claude Code skills and are now installed alongside fs-* skills. The total bundled skill count increases from 11 to 19.
  • fs-agent/SKILL.md updated (tdfs4ds/skills/ and .claude/skills/): The hardwired intents table is replaced with a reference to the ca-* SKILL.md files and documentation for the new skills= parameter and consumer_agent_skill_catalog().

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• build_dataset grouped query strategy (grouped=True) (dataset/builder.py): A new grouped parameter (default False) activates a MAX(CASE WHEN FEATURE_ID=… THEN FEATURE_VALUE END) … GROUP BY pivot strategy instead of the default one-sub-query- per-feature multi-join approach. Features sharing the same (table, process_id) pair are collapsed into a single sub-query, reducing the number of JOINs from N (one per feature) to one per unique source group. Single-feature groups emit a plain FEATURE_ID = N scan (no GROUP BY overhead). INNER JOIN semantics are preserved via a HAVING clause that rejects entities missing any feature in the group. Works with both snapshot (feature_store_time=False) and time-travel (feature_store_time=True) modes — the appropriate temporal qualifier (SEQUENCED VALIDTIME or VALIDTIME AS OF TIMESTAMP '…') is injected inside each sub-query.
  • Demo notebooks 20 and 21 (demos/notebooks/):
  • 20 - Build a Dataset with Grouped Queries (snapshot).ipynb — mirrors notebook 03 with grouped=True, SQL inspection section, sub-query count comparison, and row-count equivalence validation.
  • 21 - Build a Dataset in the past with Grouped Queries (snapshot).ipynb — combines grouped=True with feature_store_time=True, confirming both VALIDTIME AS OF TIMESTAMP and MAX(CASE WHEN) appear in the generated DDL.
  • fs-dataset skill updated (tdfs4ds/skills/fs-dataset/SKILL.md): The skill now covers three parameters that were previously missing: grouped (the MAX(CASE WHEN) pivot strategy added in this release), join_type ('INNER' vs 'FULL OUTER'), and return_query (inspect the generated DDL before executing). The code template shows all three as commented-out options; the Rules section explains when to prefer grouped=True and when join_type='FULL OUTER' is appropriate.
  • td-explain skill synced to .claude/skills/ — the skill existed in tdfs4ds/skills/td-explain/ but was missing from the project-level .claude/skills/ directory.
  • Claude Code Skills documentation (README.md, docs_src/getting-started/installation.md, docs_src/skills/overview.md): All three surfaces now document the Python API and CLI entry point for skill installation, explain the difference between user-level (~/.claude/skills/) and project-level (.claude/skills/) targets, and describe the two teammate sharing patterns (git-committed .claude/skills/ vs per-venv tdfs4ds-install-skills). The README gains a new ## Claude Code Skills section with the full skill catalogue table.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text: • export_skills default overwrite changed from True to False (cli.py): export_skills is the "add missing skills" function; its default should skip files that already exist at the destination. Pass overwrite=True explicitly for unconditional replacement. install_skills retains overwrite=True as its default (upgrade semantics).

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • build_dataset return_query=True was executing side effects (dataset/builder.py): The early-return for return_query=True was placed after tdml.execute_sql(query) and the dataset catalog registration calls. Every "inspect SQL" invocation silently re-created the view and re-registered catalog entries. Fixed by moving the early-return to immediately after query generation, before any DDL execution.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• build_dataset time-travel (feature_store_time=True) now works correctly (dataset/builder.py): Thorough MCP-validated investigation found two separate Teradata constraints:
  • VALIDTIME AS OF TIMESTAMP '…' cannot be placed before AS in REPLACE VIEW DDL (Teradata requires AS immediately after the view name — Error 3707).
  • An outer temporal qualifier placed after AS LOCK ROW FOR ACCESS fails with Error 9330 ("ValidTime qualifier requires at least one table with ValidTime") because the qualifier cannot reach temporal tables that are wrapped inside derived-table sub-queries. Fix: mirror the existing SEQUENCED VALIDTIME pattern exactly — place VALIDTIME AS OF TIMESTAMP '…' inside each sub-query directly against the temporal feature table, and emit no outer temporal qualifier (the outer view body is a plain SELECT). Verified against Teradata 20.0.0 via EXPLAIN before committing.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • build_dataset time-travel fix (superseded by 0.2.9.6) — incorrectly assumed outer qualifier propagates into derived-table sub-queries; replaced immediately.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • build_dataset time-travel fix (superseded by 0.2.9.5) — incorrect approach, replaced immediately.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• upload_features fails with AttributeError: 'NoneType' has no attribute 'fetchall' (feature_store/feature_store_management.py, utils/query_management.py): A refactor of execute_query added a fetch_results parameter (default False) and stopped returning the cursor on the modern teradataml path when fetch_results=False. GetTheLargestFeatureID (called during register_features inside upload_features) still chained .fetchall() directly on the return value, which is now None. Fixed by passing fetch_results=True to that call site.
  • execute_query error handler references undefined tdfs4ds.DEBUG — the attribute is DEBUG_MODE. Would raise AttributeError whenever a SQL error triggered the handler. Fixed to use tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• Infinite recursion / Jupyter DeadKernelError when accessing tdfs4ds.genai (tdfs4ds/__init__.py): The __getattr__ lazy-loader used from . import genai, which triggers importlib._handle_fromlist. That function calls hasattr(tdfs4ds, 'genai') to check whether the attribute already exists; since it does not yet, Python re-enters __getattr__('genai'), which issues another from . import genai, producing unbounded recursion. With Python's default 1000-frame limit the resulting RecursionError can crash the Jupyter kernel process before the surrounding try/except in setup() can catch it, surfacing as DeadKernelError. Fixed by writing a None placeholder into tdfs4ds.__dict__['genai'] before the actual import starts. _handle_fromlist finds the attribute already set and skips the recursive lookup. After importlib.import_module('tdfs4ds.genai') returns, the placeholder is replaced with the real module; on failure it is removed and the exception re-raises normally.
  • The bug was masked in ≤ 0.2.9.0 by a typo: setup() called genai.documentations_tables_creation() (extra s), which raised AttributeError before the recursion loop could start. Once the typo was corrected in 0.2.9.1 the loop became reachable, causing the kernel crash.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• Silent VARCHAR truncation in documentation uploads (genai/documentation.py): tdml.copy_to_sql calls for staging volatile tables in upload_documentation, upload_business_dictionary_objects, upload_business_dictionary_columns, and upload_business_dictionary_sections did not pass explicit column types. teradataml inferred VARCHAR sizes from the dataframe and could default to a width far smaller than the destination's VARCHAR(32000), silently truncating long values (notably ENTITY_COLUMNS_JSON, BUSINESS_LOGIC_DESCRIPTION, ENTITY_DESCRIPTION, FEATURE_DESCRIPTION, BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION, SECTION_CONTENT) before the MERGE into the destination tables. The truncated value was then unparseable as JSON when read back, surfacing as "Failed to parse JSON from LLM response" in retrieve_documentation and a malformed ENTITY_COLUMNS_JSON warning.
  • All five staging-table copy_to_sql calls now pass explicit types={...} with tdml.VARCHAR(length=32000, charset='LATIN') for description/JSON columns and matching widths for identifier columns.
  • Backward compatibility: no destination DDL change required — destination tables were always created with VARCHAR(32000). The bug was strictly at the staging layer (volatile, recreated on every call), so no schema migration is needed. Rows previously written with truncated values must be repaired by re-running the upload, e.g. document_process_incremental(process_id=..., force_update=True).

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• EXPLAIN execution metrics tracked as first-class signals (documentation.py): every EXPLAIN analysis now extracts two deterministic counters from the raw Teradata EXPLAIN text and surfaces them alongside the 1–5 scores.
  • n_steps — number of numbered execution steps in the plan (e.g. 1) First, ...).
  • n_spool_objects — count of distinct Spool N objects referenced in the plan (intermediate materialisations).
  • Parsed by _parse_explain_metrics(); both values returned by document_sql_query_explain() and run_explain_documentation().
  • Stored in FS_PROCESS_DOCUMENTATION_EXPLAIN via two new columns N_STEPS and N_SPOOL_OBJECTS (auto-migrated on first upgrade by documentation_tables_creation() / migrate_documentation_explain_schema() — older rows show NULL until re-documented).
  • Returned by retrieve_explain_documentation() and propagated through get_process_info() as EXPLAIN_N_STEPS / EXPLAIN_N_SPOOL_OBJECTS.
  • Rendered in the HTML documentation panel (_print_documentation) as two new badges next to the User/Overall scores.
  • Score Summary table in optimizer reports now reports four metrics (query_optimizer.py): User score, Global score, EXPLAIN steps, Spool objects — shown as Before → After (or Input → Simplified → Optimized when simplification applied). Negative deltas on steps/spools are highlighted as improvements.
  • Score-vs-cost trade-off note in reports: when a rewrite improves the user score but adds steps or spool objects, the report now appends an explanatory note so reviewers understand the optimizer traded a small amount of intermediate materialisation (typically single-row CTEs) for a genuine structural SQL fix.
  • Per-round metric tracking in skill_generate_candidates: every refinement round records n_steps and n_spool_objects in addition to user_score and remaining_you_items — visible in the per-candidate breakdown of the report.
  • Ground-truth EXPLAIN signal parser _parse_explain_signals() (documentation.py): extracts deterministic signals from the raw EXPLAIN (presence of real product joins, per-table scan counts, data redistribution) used to cross-validate LLM-generated [You] items before scoring.
  • Semantic [You] item classification _classify_you_items() (documentation.py): categorises each [You] item (e.g. real product join vs scalar-subquery false positive) in a single LLM call; items in known false-positive categories are filtered from the deterministic score computation. Regex fallback retained for when the LLM is not configured.
  • classify parameter added to document_sql_query_explain(), run_explain_documentation(), and _compute_user_score_from_you_items(): intermediate scoring in the optimizer uses classify=False to keep the raw [You] signal; the final public-facing score (via document_process_incremental) uses classify=True to filter noise.
  • Deterministic tiebreaker in plan comparison (query_optimizer.py): when two candidates have equal user scores, the optimizer now deterministically picks the one with fewer EXPLAIN steps + Spool objects. Logged as "Tiebreaker: …" with full metric context in the report's reasoning section.
  • Refinement acceptance rule: a refinement round is rejected when the user score is unchanged but steps/spools regressed — ensures the structurally lightest SQL at each score tier is retained.
  • Plan comparison prompt criteria extended: the selection prompt now tells the LLM to prefer candidates with fewer EXPLAIN steps and Spool objects as a tiebreaker when scores are equal (before falling back to readability).
  • Simplification baseline preserved (skill_simplify_query): the full baseline EXPLAIN analysis is now stored in the result dict under _baseline_analysis, enabling the optimizer report's 3-stage table to show pre-simplification steps/spools alongside the user score.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• get_process_info fails when documentation tables return no row (process_store/process_store_catalog_management.py): direct key access (documentation['DOCUMENTED_SQL'], etc.) raised KeyError when retrieve_documentation / retrieve_explain_documentation returned an empty dict. Replaced with .get(...) with safe defaults; the success log line is now gated on a non-empty result so empty-lookup cases no longer claim success.
  • retrieve_explain_documentation schema compatibility: the query now probes the live DDL via SHOW TABLE and only selects N_STEPS / N_SPOOL_OBJECTS when those columns are present — older installs that haven't run the schema migration continue to work.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• migrate_documentation_explain_schema() now multi-column (documentation.py): migrates USER_SCORE, N_STEPS, and N_SPOOL_OBJECTS in one DDL-probe pass. Idempotent — only runs ALTER TABLE ADD <col> for columns actually missing from SHOW TABLE output.
  • upload_documentation_explain NULL-safe: all integer metric columns use a shared _int_or_null helper so new EXPLAIN rows write NULL cleanly when a score or counter could not be computed.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• SQL simplification step (Step 0.5) in skill_optimize_query (query_optimizer.py): before any EXPLAIN analysis the optimizer now runs a structural compaction pass that merges unnecessary nesting layers (multiple derived-table wrappers → CTE + single outer SELECT) and rewrites verbose subquery structures into more readable forms — giving the LLM a cleaner baseline to work with and reducing candidate tokens.
  • skill_simplify_query(sql_query, process_id) — new standalone public function. Returns {"simplified_sql", "original_sql", "simplified", "original_score", "simplified_score"}. Accepts raw SQL or a process_id; runs deterministic sanitisation, baseline EXPLAIN, LLM compaction, then accepts the result only when the simplified score ≥ original score.
  • simplify_query(sql_query, process_id) — synchronous public wrapper (no LangGraph) exported from tdfs4ds and tdfs4ds.agent.
  • _simplify_sql_sync() — LLM helper; adapted from the retired _compact_sql_sync with a new CRITICAL SEMANTIC RULE: scalar subqueries must preserve their exact scope (uncorrelated global aggregates must not gain a correlated WHERE clause).
  • Step 0.5 is recorded in steps as {"step": "simplification", "simplified": bool, ...}.
  • When simplification changes the SQL, the stored EXPLAIN analysis is considered stale and a fresh EXPLAIN is run on the simplified query as the optimizer's new baseline.
  • Report ## Simplification section: present in every report showing whether compaction was applied, the original vs simplified score, and a collapsible block with the simplified SQL when it differs.
  • 3-stage query comparison in reports: Input → After Simplification → After Optimization. The "Input Query" section now always shows the original (pre-simplification) SQL; "After Simplification" and "After Optimization" sections follow.
  • ALWAYS CHECK FOR REDUNDANT TABLE SCANS instruction in the EXPLAIN prompt (documentation.py): mandatory whole-query structural check. If the same source table is scanned more than once with the same GROUP BY key — as separate CTEs, subqueries, or JOIN branches — the LLM must flag it as a [You] warning regardless of per-step efficiency. Example detected: two CTEs both scanning transactions GROUP BY CustomerID that can be merged into a single conditional-aggregation pass.
  • singlepass strategy CTE structure rule (query_optimizer.py): the singlepass technique constraint now explicitly instructs the LLM to place the aggregation in a CTE and compute derived columns (ratios, date arithmetic, alias-referencing expressions) in the outer SELECT — preventing expression duplication when SQL aliases cannot be referenced within the same SELECT level.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• "Query (Unchanged)" label after simplification (query_optimizer.py): when a simplification pass had already improved the SQL but no further optimization candidate was accepted, the report incorrectly showed "Query (Unchanged)". The report now shows "Input Query / After Simplification" with the correct per-stage scores.
  • Missing "Remaining Issues" section at selected_idx = -1 (query_optimizer.py): when no optimization candidate was selected (all candidates regressed or only one strategy was tried and failed), the Remaining Issues section was absent. It is now populated from original_analysis when selected_idx = -1.
  • Non-actionable [You] items inflating scores (documentation.py): EXPLAIN sometimes generates [You] items that confirm the query is already correct ("no action needed", "already efficient", "approach is correct", etc.) — these items inflate the issue count and lower the deterministic user score unfairly. A _non_actionable regex filter now removes them before score computation. A matching instruction was added to the EXPLAIN prompt: [You] items must always describe something to fix, never confirm a pattern is already fine.
  • Duplicate uncorrelated scalar subquery flagged as false positive (documentation.py): the EXPLAIN scorer was generating [You] items for scalar subqueries appearing more than once in the same SELECT list (e.g. high_value_transaction_count and high_value_ratio both containing the same SELECT AVG(...) FROM t expression). In Teradata the optimizer evaluates identical uncorrelated scalar subqueries once and reuses the result — this is a SQL-text artifact, not a performance problem. Added to "Do NOT lower the score for" in the EXPLAIN prompt and to the _non_actionable filter.
  • Teradata reserved word DAY causing Error 3706 (query_optimizer.py): LLM-generated rewrites using DATEADD(DAY, n, date) or (date1 - date2) DAY caused "expected something between '(' and the string 'DAY'" because DAY is a Teradata reserved word and putting (DAY together is always illegal. Added explicit entries to _teradata_sql_guide(): a "DATE minus DATE" section (Teradata subtraction already returns integer days — no DAY qualifier needed) and explicit ✗ DATEADD(DAY, ...) / ✗ DATEDIFF(DAY, ...) examples noting these are SQL Server functions that do not exist in Teradata.
  • Error 5480 / Error 3504 alternating fix loop (query_optimizer.py): when a window function was nested inside another (Error 5480), the LLM fix removed the nesting but placed an aggregate and a window function in the same SELECT + GROUP BY (Error 3504). Added both error patterns with correct separation examples to _teradata_sql_guide().
  • INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL=None rejected for OpenAI provider (documentation.py): the URL presence check if not INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL was triggered even when the provider was openai (which uses a fixed endpoint and does not require a URL). Fixed by checking _url_required = INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER not in ('openai', 'bedrock') first.

docs.title: Removed
docs.text: • _compact_sql_sync() and _try_compaction() (query_optimizer.py): retired. The compaction logic is now the pre-optimization simplification step (Step 0.5), which runs on every query rather than only after a perfect 5/5 EXPLAIN. Compaction call sites in skill_generate_candidates have been removed accordingly.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Query Optimizer Agent (tdfs4ds/agent/query_optimizer.py): LLM-powered SQL rewrite pipeline for Teradata feature-engineering queries. Process-aware — when a process_id is supplied, SQL and stored EXPLAIN documentation are pulled directly from the process catalog, avoiding redundant LLM calls.
  • 8-step pipeline inside skill_optimize_query:
    1. Process context — get_process_info fetch (skipped when no process_id)
    2. Structured EXPLAIN analysis — document_sql_query_explain scores the plan 1–5 with [You]-prefixed author-actionable warnings and recommendations
    3. Lineage graph — Primary Index + partition columns collected per underlying object via build_teradata_dependency_graph
    4. DDL fetch — SHOW TABLE / SHOW VIEW for every referenced object
    5. Candidate generation loop — LLM proposes ≤ N rewrites focused on [You]-actionable items; near-duplicate guard (SequenceMatcher ≥ 0.97) discards cosmetic reformats; deterministic _try_flatten_identity_wrapper applied to every candidate before EXPLAIN
    6. Candidate EXPLAIN — document_sql_query_explain run per surviving candidate
    7. Plan comparison — LLM selects the best plan; deterministic safeguard overrides a regressing LLM choice and promotes the highest-scoring valid candidate
    8. FilterManager check — partitioned-but-unfiltered objects flagged; auto-generates a ready-to-run FilterManager loop snippet
  • query_optimizer() — synchronous LangGraph entry point; multi-turn via MemorySaver singleton (_get_checkpointer()); retrieves SQL from history on follow-up questions that do not supply new SQL
  • aquery_optimizer() — async wrapper for Jupyter notebooks (avoids background-thread overhead from asyncio.run)
  • skill_generate_candidates() — exported candidate generation loop; two independent counters (valid_count, failure_count); early-stop only at perfect 5/5 score; QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_FAILURES caps syntax/EXPLAIN failures separately from the candidate budget
  • display_optimization_result() — Jupyter display helper: renders a before/after score comparison widget (colour-coded Δ Change cells, strategy and business-logic-preservation footer) followed by the full Markdown report
  • score_delta result key: optimized, best_strategy, original_user_score, original_global_score, best_user_score, best_global_score, user_score_delta, global_score_delta, business_logic_preserved
  • Native Teradata tools (no MCP server required): seven read-only metadata functions over the active teradataml connection — _td_list_databases, _td_list_tables, _td_list_views, _td_get_object_type, _td_get_table_ddl, _td_get_view_ddl, _td_get_query_explain
  • _teradata_sql_guide(): single source of Teradata syntax rules injected into LLM prompts (date arithmetic, percentile/ordered-set aggregates, INTERVAL literals, NULL/conditional, common Error 3706/3707 patterns)
  • _fix_answer_fences(): post-processes LLM output to close fenced code blocks that the model forgot to close before a Markdown section heading
  • Three new globals (tdfs4ds/__init__.py):
  • QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES (default 5) — max valid rewrites to evaluate
  • QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_FAILURES (default 5) — max EXPLAIN/syntax failures before the candidate loop stops; failures do not count toward MAX_CANDIDATES
  • QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATE_TOKENS (default None) — hard cap on completion tokens per candidate call; set to e.g. 4096 for small-context models
  • Notebook 11 - Query Optimizer Agent with tdfs4ds.ipynb (notebook dev/genai/): end-to-end walkthrough — process-aware entry, multi-turn follow-ups, score comparison widget, and FilterManager recommendation.
  • README: added Query Optimizer Agent section with pipeline table, quick start, all result keys, score_delta structure, display_optimization_result usage, and FilterManager integration snippet; added consumer agent notebook references (09, 10); added EXPLAIN→DEFINITION drill-down paragraph and resolved_column_sources state field to the consumer agent section.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• EXPLAIN→DEFINITION drill-down (consumer_agent.py, graph.py): after an EXPLAIN turn the agent now remembers every source column mentioned in the feature formula.
  • SourceColumnRef Pydantic model captures column_name and source_table from the LLM response.
  • ExplainOutput gained a source_columns field; the LLM is asked to resolve all distinct column references (resolving through subquery aliases to the real base table/view wherever possible).
  • synthesize_answer appends a "Variables involved" list after the explanation (- \col` (from `table`)`) so users can immediately ask "what is X?" about any input variable.
  • node_skill_explain writes the column→source-table mapping into the new resolved_column_sources field in AgentState (persisted via MemorySaver).
  • BD_COLUMNS secondary definition path (consumer_agent.py): skill_definition gained a new lookup path that queries FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS for columns that are not registered features (dimension keys, derived columns, etc.).
  • New optional parameter hint_table_name: when set (from a prior EXPLAIN turn via resolved_column_sources), the search is narrowed to the correct table immediately.
  • node_skill_definition reads resolved_column_sources and passes the matched table as hint_table_name.
  • Fuzzy follow-up resolution for DEFINITION (graph.py): new _fuzzy_resolve_from_column_sources(question, col_sources) helper. When the intent classifier extracts no explicit object_name for a DEFINITION question, the helper tokenises the user's question (with a bilingual EN/FR stop-word list) and checks whether any token is a substring of a known column name or source table. A match is used as the resolved object name — no need for the user to quote the exact identifier.
  • resolved_column_sources in AgentState (graph.py): new optional dict field (keys: uppercase column names; values: bare source table/view names). Displayed in the Gradio trace panel under "Feature Context (remembered)".

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• connect() round-trip reduction (lifecycle.py): replaced two db_list_tables() and two DataFrame(in_schema(...)).columns / get_ddl() calls with a single targeted DBC.TablesV query and a combined DBC.ColumnsV query. Falls back to the original approach automatically when DBC access is restricted.
  • DatasetCatalog._exists() accepts pre-computed object set (dataset_catalog.py): new optional existing_objects parameter. When supplied by connect(), the db_list_tables() round-trip inside the constructor is skipped entirely.
  • Lazy LangChain / sqlparse imports (genai/documentation.py): langchain_openai, langchain_core, and sqlparse are now imported inside the functions that need them. This removes the 30–40 s startup cost incurred on every plain import tdfs4ds when LangChain is installed.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• Multi-turn memory loss (graph.py): consumer_agent() was calling build_graph() on every invocation, creating a fresh MemorySaver each time. All cross-turn state (resolved_object_name, resolved_feature_triplet, resolved_data_domain, etc.) was wiped after every question, so follow-up questions like "when has this feature been updated?" could not resolve back to the feature discussed in the previous turn. Fixed by introducing a module-level _graph_instance singleton via _get_graph() — the compiled graph and its MemorySaver are now built once and reused for the lifetime of the process.
  • Ambiguous-feature prompt on contextual follow-ups (consumer_agent.py): when a feature name exists across multiple entity types and the user asked a follow-up question without repeating the entity, skill_freshness and skill_definition returned ambiguous_feature and asked for clarification even though the entity was already established from a previous turn. Fixed by passing hint_entity_name from resolved_entity_name state: if the hint uniquely identifies one row in the catalog, disambiguation is silent and the skill proceeds normally.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Entity and feature-group context in AgentState (graph.py): two new persisted fields carried by MemorySaver across turns:
  • resolved_entity_name — entity currently in focus (e.g. "CustomerID"); updated whenever a feature triplet is resolved.
  • resolved_feature_list — list of feature names currently in focus; holds a single name after a single-feature resolution, or the full entity feature list after a SUMMARY turn covering one entity.
  • skill_freshness / skill_definition accept hint_entity_name (consumer_agent.py): optional parameter used for silent disambiguation (see fix above).
  • skill_summary returns feature group context (consumer_agent.py): the result dict now includes feature_names (flat list) and feature_names_by_entity (entity → list of feature names). node_skill_summary uses these to update resolved_feature_list and, when exactly one entity is covered, resolved_entity_name.
  • Trace panel shows feature-group size (graph.py): when resolved_feature_list holds more than one feature, the Feature Context section of the agent trace displays the count and names of all features in focus.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• Intent classifier — verbatim extraction of feature name and domain (consumer_agent.py): the intent_classifier system prompt now explicitly instructs the LLM to copy both the feature/dataset name and the domain name exactly as typed (no spelling correction, no pluralisation change, no case normalisation). Previously the LLM would silently "fix" names it considered misspelled (e.g. nb_days_since_last_transactions → nb_days_since_last_transaction), causing catalog lookups to fail on perfectly valid feature names. The same risk applied to domain abbreviations (e.g. ATR expanded to a full word), which would cause skill_summary to summarise under the wrong label.
  • process_view_not_found error response (synthesize_answer): the synthesizer is now explicitly told not to suggest alternative spellings or guess at correct names — it can only see what the skill returned, so any suggestions it invented were hallucinations.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Fuzzy feature-name matching (consumer_agent.py): new helper _fuzzy_match_feature(object_name) uses difflib.get_close_matches (stdlib, no new dependency) with a similarity cutoff of 0.75 against the full feature catalog across all data domains. Called automatically when an exact lookup fails in skill_explain, skill_lineage, and skill_freshness.
  • Single close match → the skill auto-corrects to the catalog name, runs normally, and includes a correction_note in the result dict.
  • Multiple close matches → returns a new feature_not_found_did_you_mean error with the candidate list (no answer is attempted).
  • feature_not_found_did_you_mean error handler (synthesize_answer): new error path that tells the user the exact name was not found and lists the similar catalog names found — without guessing what those features do.
  • Auto-correction notice in answers: when a single-candidate auto-correction is applied, synthesize_answer prepends an italic note to the direct answer, e.g. 'nb_days_since_last_transactons' was not found — answered for the closest match 'nb_days_since_last_transactions'.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Consumer agent module (tdfs4ds/agent/): conversational chatbot for business consumers of the feature store. Non-technical users can ask natural-language questions about features, datasets, definitions, data freshness, usage guidance, data lineage, and calculation logic — in English or French.
  • Intent classifier (consumer_agent.py): uses Pydantic structured output via llm.with_structured_output() to route questions to one of 7 skills: SEARCH, DEFINITION, USAGE, FRESHNESS, SUMMARY, LINEAGE, EXPLAIN.
  • 7 skills (consumer_agent.py): skill_search (semantic vector search), skill_definition (process view → column docs), skill_usage (audience, granularity, regulatory guidance), skill_freshness (latest execution timestamps), skill_summary (full feature list with descriptions), skill_lineage (upstream dependency graph with LLM summary), skill_explain (SHOW VIEW DDL → plain- language business explanation).
  • LangGraph orchestration (graph.py): StateGraph with conditional routing (classify → detect_domain → skill → synthesize → END) and MemorySaver checkpointer for multi-turn conversations.
  • DATA_DOMAIN auto-detection (graph.py): node_detect_domain node scans all DATA_DOMAINs via feature_catalog(all_data_domains=True) to find which domain owns the requested object. Stores resolved_data_domain in LangGraph state; persisted across turns via MemorySaver so follow-up questions reuse the last resolved domain. All skill nodes set tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN via a context manager. New public functions: get_data_domain(), detect_data_domain_for_object().
  • Feature description resolution chain (consumer_agent.py): _get_feature_catalog_with_docs() resolves feature descriptions via get_list_entity → get_list_features → get_feature_versions → VIEW_NAME → FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS. Used by DEFINITION and SUMMARY.
  • Embedding factory (embedding.py): get_embeddings() returns a LangChain Embeddings instance; supports openai/vllm/azure/bedrock providers. Falls back from EMBEDDING_MODEL_* to INSTRUCT_MODEL_* if not set. list_embedding_models(sub_paths=...) queries available models on the embedding endpoint.
  • Chroma vector index (vector_index.py): build_vector_index(force_rebuild=False) builds three collections (objects, sections, columns) from the business dictionary. Incremental by default. Documents are embedded in batches of 8 to respect TEI server limits. search_vector_index(query, sources, top_k) searches with optional section_name filtering and deduplicates on OBJECT_NAME.
  • Gradio chatbot (chatbot.py): launch_chatbot() starts a Gradio Blocks UI with JupyterHub proxy support. launch_chatbot_with_index(port, force_rebuild, ...) builds/refreshes the Chroma vector index then launches the Gradio UI — single command for end-to-end startup.
  • Agent trace panel (chatbot.py): collapsible gr.Accordion showing Intent Classification, DATA_DOMAIN Detection (available / active / source), Skill Executed, and Skill Result. Errors include per-step diagnostic messages. consumer_agent(return_trace=True) returns (answer, trace_md).
  • Model listing: list_instruct_models() and list_embedding_models() query the configured API endpoints for available models.
  • Demo notebooks: 09 - Consumer Agent Chatbot with tdfs4ds.ipynb (architecture, 7-intent test suite, 4-turn multi-turn demo) and 10 - Launch Consumer Agent Chatbot.ipynb (minimal 4-cell one-command launch).
  • Unit tests: tests/test_consumer_agent.py — 17 tests covering intent classification, skills, embeddings, vector index, graph routing, and multi-turn.
  • document_feature_store_incremental() (tdfs4ds/genai/documentation.py): single-pass, topo-sorted documentation of every registered process and dataset. Builds a combined lineage graph seeded from all dataset DDLs (expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=True) and any orphan processes; topologically sorts it (leaves first, roots last); then walks the order calling document_process_incremental and document_dataset_incremental with a shared pair of visited-sets so each process view is documented at most once regardless of how many datasets reference it. Returns a summary dict with processes_documented, datasets_documented, processes_skipped, datasets_skipped. Supports force_update, all_data_domains, and fallback documentation for objects absent from the graph.
  • Business dictionary mirroring (upload_documentation): after persisting process documentation to the process-documentation tables, the function now also writes:
  • The entity description to FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS (type 'V')
  • Per-feature descriptions to FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS (keyed by view name + feature name) Both writes are best-effort — failures are logged as warnings without aborting.
  • Shared visited-sets in document_dataset_incremental: new optional private parameters _visited_processes and _visited_views allow an outer orchestration call (e.g. document_feature_store_incremental) to pass a shared recursion guard, preventing duplicate documentation when multiple datasets share upstream process views.
  • Embedding model configuration variables (tdfs4ds/__init__.py, config.py): EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER, EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL, EMBEDDING_MODEL_API_KEY, EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL, EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM — mirror the INSTRUCT_MODEL_* pattern. All loadable from .env / tdfs4ds.json / TDFS4DS_* env vars.
  • Chroma configuration variables: CHROMA_MODE ('local'/'server'), CHROMA_PATH, CHROMA_HOST, CHROMA_PORT.
  • Claude Code skill: fs-agent — generates boilerplate for the consumer agent chatbot workflow.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• skill_definition: primary path now resolves via the process documentation chain (process view → column docs) instead of querying the feature name directly in the objects table. Falls back to dataset-level lookup, then vector search.
  • skill_summary: replaced bare feature_catalog() call (no descriptions) with _get_feature_catalog_with_docs() so each feature line includes its business description from process documentation.
  • synthesize_answer: DEFINITION, USAGE, FRESHNESS, SUMMARY, LINEAGE, EXPLAIN now short-circuit to their skill's final answer key (no second LLM pass). Only SEARCH still uses the generic synthesis path.
  • skill_freshness diagnostics: all three failure points (feature_catalog miss, process_catalog miss, empty followup) now return a diagnostic key with per-step detail instead of collapsing to the same opaque no_execution_history error.
  • _get_process_view: primary lookup is now feature_catalog → PROCESS_ID → process_catalog → VIEW_NAME. The FEATURE_NAMES comma-list search is kept as a fallback only.
  • setup.py: added langchain_chroma, langchain_teradata, langgraph, chromadb, pydantic, gradio to install_requires.
  • fs-setup skill: added commented embedding model and Chroma configuration blocks.
  • fs-document skill: added rule to rebuild vector index after documenting.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Dataset documentation (tdfs4ds/genai/documentation.py): document_dataset_incremental(dataset_id) generates documentation for a dataset by walking its full lineage bottom-up.
  • Source tables: uses business dictionary if available, warns if missing.
  • Intermediate views: auto-documented via LLM if undocumented.
  • Process views: actively calls document_process_incremental if undocumented.
  • Feature/entity column descriptions are propagated from process documentation (no redundant LLM call for 1:1 feature mappings).
  • A single JSON-constrained LLM call generates five structured sections for the dataset itself: OVERVIEW, ENTITY, FEATURE_THEMES, BUSINESS_QUESTIONS, INTENDED_AUDIENCE.

  • Notebook 08 - Dataset documentation with tdfs4ds.ipynb (notebook dev/genai/).

  • Business dictionary sections table (FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS): new temporal table keyed on (DATABASE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME, SECTION_NAME). Each row stores one section of an object's documentation as an independent RAG document. Together with the existing objects and columns tables, this forms a 3-level hierarchy designed for chunking-free hierarchical retrieval:

  • Level 0: FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS — object summary (OVERVIEW)
  • Level 1: FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS — one row per section
  • Level 2: FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS — one row per column
  • New upload function: upload_business_dictionary_sections(df).
  • New helper: _fetch_sections_for_node(db, obj).
  • Table created automatically on next setup() or connect(create_if_missing=True).

docs.title: Changed
docs.text: • _format_upstream_context: type label for 'F' changed from 'Feature Store Dataset' to 'Feature Store Process' (datasets use type 'D').

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Dataset lineage via process catalog (tdfs4ds/lineage/network.py): new expand_datasets_via_process_catalog parameter on build_teradata_dependency_graph (default True). When a dataset node is detected the graph parses the dataset view DDL to extract FEATURE_VERSION UUIDs (each is a PROCESS_ID), looks them up in FS_V_PROCESS_CATALOG, and adds edges directly to the registered feature-engineering views — bypassing the internal FS_T_... storage tables. Set expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=False to restore the previous leaf behaviour (dataset node connected directly to its raw storage tables). Expected lineage chain: dataset → process view(s) → source table(s).
  • graph_to_migration_manifest(graph, *, filter_database=None) (tdfs4ds/lineage/network.py, exported from tdfs4ds.lineage): converts a lineage graph into a JSON-serialisable migration manifest with three keys:
  • "views" — {database, name, type} entries for every view/dataset node
  • "tables" — {database, name, type, primary_index, partition_columns} entries
  • "edges" — {from, to} dependency pairs Pass filter_database to scope output to a single feature store schema; cross-boundary edges are excluded automatically.
  • Notebook 07 - Dataset Lineage via Process Catalog.ipynb (notebook dev/lineage/): demonstrates both behaviours side-by-side and the migration manifest export.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• Orphaned FS_T_... storage-table nodes are pruned from the graph result after their edges are replaced by process-view edges.
  • Pre-added edges from a dataset root to its raw storage tables are purged only when expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=True; when False those edges are preserved and the old output is unchanged.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Business dictionary (tdfs4ds/genai/documentation.py): two new temporal tables and corresponding upload functions for storing business-oriented descriptions independently of the process documentation workflow. No LLM required — descriptions are plain text supplied by the user in a pandas DataFrame.
  • FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS — object-level descriptions (DATABASE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME, OBJECT_TYPE 'T'/'V', BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION). Temporal VALIDTIME table.
  • FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS — column-level descriptions (DATABASE_NAME, TABLE_NAME, COLUMN_NAME, BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION). Temporal VALIDTIME table.
  • upload_business_dictionary_objects(df) — inserts or updates object descriptions via CURRENT VALIDTIME MERGE; raises ValueError on missing columns.
  • upload_business_dictionary_columns(df) — inserts or updates column descriptions via CURRENT VALIDTIME MERGE; raises ValueError on missing columns.
  • Both tables are provisioned automatically by documentation_tables_creation(), called from setup() and connect(create_if_missing=True).
  • connect() accepts two new optional keyword arguments — documentation_business_dictionary_objects and documentation_business_dictionary_columns — to override the default table names, consistent with all other catalog parameters.
  • External configuration support (tdfs4ds/config.py): global variables can now be set from three external sources, eliminating the boilerplate setup cell that every notebook previously required.
  • JSON config file (tdfs4ds.json / ~/.tdfs4ds/config.json) — project defaults for non-sensitive settings. Keys are case-insensitive.
  • .env file (./.env / ~/.tdfs4ds/.env) — local overrides and credentials. Only TDFS4DS_* variables are read; the file is parsed without modifying os.environ. Add .env to .gitignore to keep secrets out of source control. Supports KEY=VALUE, quoted values, and export KEY=VALUE.
  • OS environment variables — prefix TDFS4DS_, e.g. TDFS4DS_SCHEMA, TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN. Intended for CI/CD pipelines.
  • INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY is rejected from JSON config files (credential safety); use .env or an OS env var instead.
  • tdfs4ds.load_config(path=None, dotenv_path=None) — public function to (re-)load config at any point, or to load from non-default file paths.
  • Priority chain (highest first): programmatic → OS env var → .env file → JSON config → teradataml auto-detection (SCHEMA) → built-in defaults.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text: • SCHEMA auto-detection from the teradataml context now only runs when SCHEMA is still None after config/env loading, so env-var or file settings are not silently overwritten on import.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • tqdm rendering in VSCode Jupyter: switched all import tqdm / tqdm.tqdm(...) usages in entity_management.py, feature_store_management.py, and utils/lineage.py to from tqdm.auto import tqdm. tqdm.auto selects the widget renderer in JupyterLab/VSCode Jupyter and falls back to the standard terminal bar elsewhere.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • Teradata Error 6706 when uploading LLM-generated documentation to LATIN-charset columns. Added _sanitize_for_teradata() in genai/documentation.py that replaces common Unicode characters produced by LLMs (smart quotes, em/en dashes, ellipsis, bullet points, non-breaking spaces) with ASCII equivalents, then encodes the remainder as ISO-8859-1. Applied in both upload_documentation and upload_documentation_explain.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• Reshape: refactored tdfs4ds/__init__.py into dedicated submodules for better maintainability (catalog.py, data_domain.py, execution.py, lifecycle.py, dataset/builder.py). Public API surface is unchanged.
  • Added Claude Code skill definitions (.claude/skills/, skills/) for feature-store workflows: fs-setup, fs-upload, fs-inspect, fs-filter, fs-rollout, fs-dataset, fs-document, fs-lineage, fs-analyze.
  • Extended demo notebooks and added pre-executed notebook exports under demos/notebooks_executed/.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Lineage module: SQL query parsing, Teradata DDL analysis, dependency graph construction, and Sankey diagram visualisation.
  • Primary-index and partition-column inspection for source tables.
  • AWS Bedrock support in the GenAI documentation module.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Retrieve and update stored documentation from documentation tables.
  • Pretty-print display of process documentation (display_process_info).
  • Manual documentation entry via chatbot output (feed_process_info_with_prompt_result).

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • Bug fixes in documentation table creation and volatile-table drop logic.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• GenAI module (genai/documentation.py): LLM-powered auto-documentation of SQL processes using LangChain with OpenAI or AWS Bedrock.
  • document_process(): generates business-logic description, entity description, entity-column annotations, and feature-column descriptions from a process view.
  • get_the_explain() + document_sql_query_explain(): EXPLAIN-plan retrieval and LLM-powered optimisation analysis with warnings and recommendations.
  • upload_documentation() / upload_documentation_explain(): persist generated documentation into temporal documentation tables.
  • documentation_tables_creation(): provisions the documentation catalog tables.
  • Support for json_constraint mode (structured JSON output) and prompt_only mode (returns prompt without calling the LLM).

docs.title: Business dictionary
docs.text:
Three temporal tables store business-oriented descriptions for any database object, its columns, and its sections. They form a 3-level hierarchy designed for chunking-free hierarchical RAG.

Level Table Key Extra columns 0 FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS (DATABASE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME) OBJECT_TYPE CHAR(1) ('T'/'V'/'D'), BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION 1 FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS (DATABASE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME, SECTION_NAME) SECTION_CONTENT VARCHAR(32000) 2 FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS (DATABASE_NAME, TABLE_NAME, COLUMN_NAME) BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION

All three are VALIDTIME temporal — every write uses CURRENT VALIDTIME MERGE, so the full history of descriptions is preserved.

docs.title: Provisioning
docs.text:
The tables are created automatically by tdfs4ds.setup() and tdfs4ds.connect(create_if_missing=True). Override the default names via keyword arguments to connect:

tdfs4ds.connect(
    database = 'my_db',
    documentation_business_dictionary_objects = 'MY_BIZ_OBJECTS',
    documentation_business_dictionary_columns = 'MY_BIZ_COLUMNS',
)

docs.title: Populating without an LLM
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
from tdfs4ds.genai import (
    upload_business_dictionary_objects,
    upload_business_dictionary_columns,
    upload_business_dictionary_sections,
)

# Level 0 — objects
upload_business_dictionary_objects(pd.DataFrame([
    {
        'DATABASE_NAME'       : 'MY_DB',
        'OBJECT_NAME'         : 'CUSTOMER',
        'OBJECT_TYPE'         : 'T',
        'BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION': 'Core customer table — one row per enrolled customer.',
    },
    {
        'DATABASE_NAME'       : 'MY_DB',
        'OBJECT_NAME'         : 'V_CUSTOMER_ORDERS',
        'OBJECT_TYPE'         : 'V',
        'BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION': 'Business view joining customers with their order history for reporting.',
    },
]))

# Level 1 — sections
upload_business_dictionary_sections(pd.DataFrame([
    {
        'DATABASE_NAME'  : 'MY_DB',
        'OBJECT_NAME'    : 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND',
        'SECTION_NAME'   : 'OVERVIEW',
        'SECTION_CONTENT': 'Customer-level analytical dataset combining spend metrics …',
    },
]))

# Level 2 — columns
upload_business_dictionary_columns(pd.DataFrame([
    {
        'DATABASE_NAME'       : 'MY_DB',
        'TABLE_NAME'          : 'CUSTOMER',
        'COLUMN_NAME'         : 'CUSTOMER_ID',
        'BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION': 'Unique customer identifier assigned at enrolment.',
    },
    {
        'DATABASE_NAME'       : 'MY_DB',
        'TABLE_NAME'          : 'CUSTOMER',
        'COLUMN_NAME'         : 'BIRTH_DATE',
        'BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION': 'Customer date of birth, used for age-band segmentation.',
    },
]))

All three functions:

  • Validate that the required columns are present and raise ValueError with the list of missing columns if not.
  • Use CURRENT VALIDTIME MERGE — re-running them updates existing descriptions and preserves history.
  • Require no LLM — provide whatever text you want.

docs.title: How LLM documentation populates the dictionary
docs.text: Call What it writes to the dictionary document_process Mirrors process descriptions into FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS (OBJECT_TYPE='V') and column-level descriptions into FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS. document_dataset_incremental Writes OBJECT_TYPE='D' into FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS, the five generated sections into FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS, and propagated column docs into FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS. document_feature_store_incremental Calls both of the above for every catalogued process and dataset.

docs.title: Why a sections table
docs.text: A common RAG mistake is to store one giant text blob per object and rely on embedding-time chunking. With a (OBJECT_NAME, SECTION_NAME) key, every section is independently embeddable and retrievable. The consumer agent uses this granularity: it returns the right section for the asked intent (OVERVIEW for "summary", BUSINESS_QUESTIONS for "what can I ask about …", etc.) without ever splitting a section in the middle.

docs.title: Consumer agent
docs.text: A conversational chatbot built on LangGraph + Gradio with a pluggable vector store (ChromaDB by default, Teradata Enterprise Vector Store as an opt-in). Business users ask natural-language questions; the agent answers from the business dictionary.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The agent executes a multi-stage LangGraph StateGraph with full cross-turn context persistence via a process-level MemorySaver singleton:

START
  ↓
classify
  (LLM: intent classification + object name extraction via Pydantic structured output)
  ↓
detect_domain
  (Scan all DATA_DOMAINs; resolve owning domain; persisted across turns)
  ↓
recognize_entity
  (Type-tag the object: feature, dataset, or process view)
  ↓
[conditional routing by intent]
  ├─ SEARCH         → skill_search        → synthesize → END
  ├─ DEFINITION     → skill_definition    → synthesize → END
  ├─ USAGE          → skill_usage         → synthesize → END
  ├─ FRESHNESS      → skill_freshness     → synthesize → END
  ├─ SUMMARY        → skill_summary       → synthesize → END
  ├─ LINEAGE        → skill_lineage       → synthesize → END
  ├─ EXPLAIN        → skill_explain       → synthesize → END
  ├─ ALGORITHM      → skill_algorithm     → synthesize → END (MCP only; dual-intent runs EXPLAIN first)
  ├─ DATASET        → skill_dataset       → synthesize → END
  ├─ BUILD_DATASET  → skill_build_dataset → synthesize → END
  ├─ RECAP          → skill_recap         → synthesize → END (memory only)
  ├─ OUT_OF_SCOPE   → (polite redirect)   → synthesize → END
  └─ DATA_QUERY     → skill_mcp_tools     → synthesize → END (MCP only)

RECAP and OUT_OF_SCOPE are built-in intents — they have no ca-* SKILL.md file. build_graph() injects their descriptions into the dynamic Pydantic intent model and the classifier prompt, and _build_classifier_examples() seeds explicit few-shot examples so they fire reliably. RECAP reads only the accumulated conversation memory (no Teradata query) to summarise the features, entities, and datasets discussed so far; OUT_OF_SCOPE politely redirects off-topic questions (weather, general coding, etc.).

docs.title: Graph Singleton & State Persistence
docs.text:
The graph instance is cached in a module-level dict _graph_instances, keyed by (frozenset(skills), plugin_key, mcp_enabled). This singleton pattern ensures:

  • One MemorySaver checkpoint per (skills, MCP) combo — cross-turn state (resolved_data_domain, resolved_feature_triplet, etc.) persists in LangGraph's built-in checkpointer without an external database.
  • Automatic graph recompilation when skills change or MCP is toggled.
  • Clean reset via reset_consumer_agent() which clears the entire cache.

docs.title: Context as a Named-Entity Knowledge Graph
docs.text:
The AgentState TypedDict is a stateful knowledge graph whose fields are persisted across conversation turns. Users can refer back without repeating context:

State field What it represents resolved_data_domain Active DATA_DOMAIN (project namespace) resolved_object_name Last explicitly named feature or dataset (remembered) resolved_feature_triplet {feature_name, entity_name, process_id, view_name} — the "current node" in the feature graph resolved_entity_name Entity type currently in scope (e.g. "CustomerID") resolved_feature_list One or many features currently discussed resolved_column_sources {COLUMN_UPPER: source_table} — populated by EXPLAIN, enables drill-down DEFINITION resolved_dataset_id UUID from the last SUMMARY turn on a dataset resolved_dataset_features Features loaded from that dataset resolved_entity_name Entity picked during an entity-clarification turn; drives skill_definition's entity hint pending_action / pending_action_context State machine for multi-step flows (dataset creation, entity clarification, ambiguous-query clarification)

The effective current object is resolved by _effective_object(state) with this priority: explicit this-turn → resolved_feature_triplet.feature_name → resolved_object_name.

docs.title: Accumulated-context feature keying
docs.text: accumulated_context_graph.features is keyed by FEATURE_NAME::ENTITY_NAME (both uppercased) — not by the bare feature name — so the same feature name registered under two entities never collides in the cross-turn cache. The features, datasets, entities, and source_views sections all use uppercase keys for stable lookup (processes and freshness_timeline stay keyed by their process-id UUIDs). Skills resolve entries through _feat_cache_lookup(), which tries the exact composite key, then a FEATURE:: prefix scan, then the legacy plain-FEATURE key (so older snapshots still load).

docs.title: MCP / DATA_QUERY integration
docs.text:
When tdfs4ds.MCP_SERVER_URL is configured, users can enable the "Enable MCP Tools" checkbox in the chatbot to route questions to remote data sources via the MCP (Model Context Protocol).

How it works:

  1. The graph registers an additional DATA_QUERY intent and corresponding skill_mcp_tools node.
  2. When MCP is enabled and no feature-store skill matches the question, the classifier may assign DATA_QUERY intent.
  3. _skill_mcp_tools_async() uses langchain_mcp_adapters.MultiServerMCPClient to wrap the remote tools (e.g. SQL query execution, file upload, database operations).
  4. A ReAct agent (langgraph.prebuilt.create_react_agent) chains tool calls until the query is resolved.

Data governance & safety:

  • _make_guarded_tools() wraps all MCP tools with rejection logic: queries referencing internal feature-store objects (FS_T_*, FS_V_*, process view names) are blocked.
  • SELECT queries automatically get TOP 20 injected; if results hit the limit, a warning is appended so the user knows data was truncated.
  • Event loop compatibility: uses ThreadPoolExecutor inside a running Jupyter loop, asyncio.run() in other contexts.

docs.title: Intents
docs.text:
Intent Trigger example What happens SEARCH "What features analyse customer spending?" Semantic search over objects / sections / columns collections (Chroma or Teradata Vector Store) DEFINITION "What does total_amount measure?" Resolves feature → process view → column doc in FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS USAGE "How do I use avg_amount in Tableau?" Audience, granularity, regulatory guidance FRESHNESS "Is total_amount up to date?" / "When was DS_CLAIMS last refreshed?" Latest run from follow_up_report(). Works for individual features (resolves single process) and datasets (aggregates across all processes feeding the dataset; returns last_successful_run, days_since_update, has_recent_failure). Returns "not found" only when the named object is neither a registered feature nor a dataset. SUMMARY "List all features for customers" / "What features belong to the provider_id entity?" Full feature list with entity + description; scoped to one entity when entity_filter resolves LINEAGE "Where does total_amount come from?" build_teradata_dependency_graph upstream EXPLAIN "How is total_amount calculated?" SHOW VIEW DDL → LLM plain-language explanation + per-column source map (also accumulates into the context graph) ALGORITHM "What algorithm is behind ml_risk_score?" (MCP only) Walks the tdfs4ds→tdstone2 STO metadata chain to explain the ML model: algorithm, hyperparameters, training configuration, Python packages, partition count. Returns answer directly (no second LLM pass). Disabled when MCP is off — algorithm-shaped questions get a "please enable MCP" message instead. RECAP "What are the features we are talking about?" Reads accumulated conversation memory only — no Teradata query OUT_OF_SCOPE "What's the weather today?" Polite redirect; the question is unrelated to the feature store

DATASET, BUILD_DATASET, and DATA_QUERY (MCP only) are additional routable intents; see the routing diagram above.

docs.title: Compound EXPLAIN + ALGORITHM questions
docs.text:
A compound question "How is ml_risk_score computed? What algorithm is behind it?" carries both EXPLAIN signals ("how is … computed") and ALGORITHM signals ("algorithm"). With MCP on:

  1. Routing — _node_classify_impl re-routes EXPLAIN → ALGORITHM when both signal sets match.
  2. Dual-intent execution — node_skill_algorithm runs skill_explain first (SQL formula), prepends its answer with a --- divider above the ALGORITHM answer (ML model details), and persists the EXPLAIN column sources so a follow-up "what is <column>?" still resolves.

With MCP off, the algorithm half is unavailable and the question routes to EXPLAIN only (the _looks_like_algorithm_question guard fires the "enable MCP" message in that case).

docs.title: Entity-scoped SUMMARY
docs.text: When the named object resolves to a registered entity (not a feature or dataset), or when entity_filter is supplied, skill_summary lists only the features that belong to that entity — answering "what features belong to entity X?". _recognize_object_type now type-tags entity and data_domain objects in addition to feature / dataset / process.

docs.title: State remembered across turns
docs.text:
State field What is remembered resolved_data_domain Domain owning the current feature resolved_object_name Last explicitly named feature / dataset resolved_feature_triplet (feature_name, entity_name, process_id, view_name) resolved_entity_name Entity in focus (e.g. CustomerID) resolved_feature_list One or many feature names currently discussed resolved_column_sources Column → source-table map from the last EXPLAIN turn resolved_entity_name Entity picked during an entity-clarification turn

Follow-up questions that omit the feature name reuse the previous one automatically. When the same feature name exists across multiple entities, the remembered entity silently disambiguates.

docs.title: Ambiguous-feature entity clarification
docs.text:
When a feature name exists under several entities (different granularities), the agent first tries to resolve the entity without asking:

  1. Classifier extraction — the intent models carry an entity_name field; "What is total_amount for pharmacy_id?" extracts pharmacy_id directly and it becomes the entity hint for the skill (top priority in the hint chain).
  2. Question scan — _entity_from_question() is a deterministic safety net at every ambiguous_feature site (DEFINITION, FRESHNESS, LINEAGE, EXPLAIN): the raw question is scanned for the candidate entity names (case-insensitive, _/space-normalised, multi-key entities match on any key component) and auto-resolves when exactly one matches.

Only when neither layer resolves it does skill_definition return error="ambiguous_feature" with the valid entity list. Rather than re-classifying the user's clarifying reply from scratch — which used to spiral into nonsense clarification loops — the agent resolves it deterministically:

  1. node_skill_definition raises a tracked awaiting_feature_entity_clarification pending action (via _ambiguous_feature_pending) carrying the entity list, the original feature, and the origin intent. synthesize_answer renders a numbered menu — a deterministic EN/FR template, not an LLM call (an LLM-rendered clarification was observed embellishing the question with a guessed generic definition of the feature).
  2. On the next turn, _node_classify_impl short-circuits the LLM classifier and resolves the reply through _resolve_clarification_choice (digit / ordinal / fuzzy label).
  3. Role separation + intent inheritance — the entity comes from the reply, while the feature (object_name) and intent (DEFINITION) are inherited from context. So both a bare "1" and a mixed-concept reply like "the total_amount feature of the provider_id entity" resolve correctly. The chosen entity is stored in resolved_entity_name and used as skill_definition's entity hint — the answer comes back scoped, with no second menu.

docs.title: Typo recovery
docs.text: The classifier pins intent to question shape, not name recognition: "what is ml_rsk_scre?" stays DEFINITION even though the token matches nothing (it may be a typo), instead of falling into SEARCH's speculative synthesis. When the documentation lookup then misses, _fuzzy_match_feature (difflib, cutoff 0.75) kicks in: a single close candidate is answered directly with a visible correction note ("'ml_rsk_scre' was not found — answered for the closest match 'ml_risk_score'"); multiple candidates produce a did-you-mean list. DEFINITION and FRESHNESS auto-correct this way, matching the existing LINEAGE / EXPLAIN behaviour.

docs.title: Lineage grounding
docs.text: Lineage answers only ever mention objects that exist in a dependency graph. The cross-turn cache is consulted only when a previous LINEAGE turn actually traced upstream objects — the bare feature → process_view association written by a DEFINITION turn is treated as a cache miss and triggers a real SHOW VIEW/SHOW TABLE walk. The summarisation prompt forbids inventing object names; when no upstream is found the agent says so explicitly instead of calling the LLM.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import tdfs4ds
from tdfs4ds.agent import launch_chatbot_with_index

tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'vllm'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL      = 'https://api.example.com/v1'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY  = 'my-key'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = 'mistral-7b-instruct'
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL     = 'https://api.example.com/v1/e5'
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL   = 'bge-m3'

# One call: build the vector index (incremental) then launch the chatbot
demo = launch_chatbot_with_index(port=7860, force_rebuild=False)

docs.title: Programmatic API
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.agent import consumer_agent

answer = consumer_agent("What features are available?", thread_id="session-1")
answer = consumer_agent("How is nb_days_since_last_transactions calculated?", thread_id="session-1")
answer = consumer_agent("When was it last updated?", thread_id="session-1")
answer = consumer_agent("And for CustomerID?", thread_id="session-1")   # entity remembered, new feature group

consumer_agent returns just the answer string by default. Two opt-in flags change the return shape:

answer, trace_md = consumer_agent(q, thread_id="s1", return_trace=True)
answer, trace_md, acc_ctx = consumer_agent(q, thread_id="s1", return_context=True)
  • return_trace=True → (answer, trace_md) — the markdown agent trace.
