  • return_context=True → (answer, trace_md, acc_ctx) — a 3-tuple that also returns the accumulated_context_graph dict (the cross-turn knowledge graph), so callers always get the trace alongside the context.

docs.title: EXPLAIN → DEFINITION drill-down
docs.text:
After an EXPLAIN turn, the agent lists every variable involved in the formula with its source table. Asking "what is <column>?" resolves the column through FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS — even when the column is not a registered feature.

USER: How is total_amount calculated?
AGENT: total_amount sums AMOUNT_NET grouped by CUSTOMER_ID.
       Variables involved:
         AMOUNT_NET  ←  SOURCE_DB.TRANSACTIONS
         CUSTOMER_ID ←  SOURCE_DB.TRANSACTIONS

USER: what is AMOUNT_NET?
AGENT: AMOUNT_NET is the net transaction amount in EUR, post-discount …

docs.title: Agent trace panel
docs.text:
The chatbot includes a collapsible Agent trace accordion showing full execution:

  • Intent Classification — detected intent, object name (this turn vs. remembered)
  • Feature Context — triplet (feature, entity, process ID, view), feature list, column sources
  • Dataset Context — dataset ID and feature count (if applicable)
  • Pending Action — multi-step flow state (e.g. awaiting dataset name)
  • DATA_DOMAIN — available domains, active domain, resolution source
  • Skill Executed — name + input object
  • Skill Result — structured output (for single features) or context graph (for multi-object)
  • Errors include per-step diagnostic messages

docs.title: Context knowledge graph panel
docs.text:
A second collapsible accordion displays an interactive Plotly network graph visualising the agent's accumulated context:

  • Nodes (colour-coded by type):
  • Domain (blue diamond)
  • Entity (green square)
  • Features (orange circles)
  • Process views (grey squares)
  • Datasets (purple triangles)
  • Column sources (red nodes, when EXPLAIN was used)
  • Edges show relationships: domain → entity → features → views → datasets

The graph is rebuilt after each turn to reflect new resolutions, multi-object DEFINITION expansions, and EXPLAIN column mappings.

docs.title: Admin parameters panel (launch_chatbot_app)
docs.text:
launch_chatbot_app() is an additive wrapper around the chatbot: same chat UI, plus a collapsible ⚙️ Parameters panel for changing configuration live — without restarting the process. (launch_chatbot and launch_chatbot_with_index are unchanged; use them when you don't need the panel.)

from tdfs4ds.agent import launch_chatbot_app

# Same prerequisites as launch_chatbot (a connected context + built index).
demo = launch_chatbot_app(port=7860)

The panel edits the same globals as the agent

The admin panel does not keep its own configuration. Every field maps directly to the same tdfs4ds.* global that the consumer agent already reads (tdfs4ds.SCHEMA, INSTRUCT_MODEL_*, EMBEDDING_MODEL_*, VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND, CHROMA_PATH, …). Applying a change in the panel is exactly equivalent to setting that global in Python, so it also affects any consumer_agent(...) / query_optimizer_agent(...) calls made from the same process afterwards. Values are pre-filled from the current globals when the app starts — including whatever was loaded from env vars / .env / JSON config (see Configuration).

The panel has four groups; each has an Apply button and a status line:

Group Fields What Apply does Feature store database database name tdfs4ds.connect(database=…, create_if_missing=False). Validated: a database that does not contain a tdfs4ds feature store is rejected and the active store is not changed. On success: sets tdfs4ds.SCHEMA, rebuilds the index, resets the agent, clears the chat. Vector store backend (chroma/teradata), Chroma path; Refresh / rebuild button Sets VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND / CHROMA_PATH and rebuilds. Refresh alone re-runs build_vector_index(force_rebuild=True) — use it after the business dictionary changes, since the local Chroma index does not auto-update. Instruct model provider, model, URL, API key, proxy Sets INSTRUCT_MODEL_* (incl. INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROXY) and resets the agent. The next LLM call builds a fresh client from these globals. Embedding model provider, model, URL, API key, proxy, dimension Sets EMBEDDING_MODEL_*, then rebuilds the index (embeddings changed) and resets the agent.

Any change that affects the data or the model rebuilds the index when needed, resets the agent (reset_consumer_agent()), rotates the conversation thread, and clears the chat so the session starts clean against the new configuration.

When run via the API server (python -m tdfs4ds.serve), this admin app is served automatically on a second port — see Deployment → OpenAI-compatible API server.

Database choice is process-wide — isolate per user with separate processes

Because the panel mutates process-wide globals, switching the feature-store database changes it for everyone served by that process. To give each user their own database, run a separate process / container per user (one container per user, each with its own TDFS4DS_SCHEMA) rather than sharing one — see Per-user database choice. There is no per-session database isolation within a single process.

docs.title: Business dictionary & retrieval architecture
docs.text: The agent answers from a 3-level documentation hierarchy designed for chunk-free hierarchical RAG, using both structured SQL and vector search.

docs.title: Documentation tables (all VALIDTIME temporal)
docs.text: Level Table What it stores Populated by 0 FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS Object overview (BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION) document_process, document_dataset_incremental 1 FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS Named sections per object (OVERVIEW, ENTITY, FEATURE_THEMES, BUSINESS_QUESTIONS, INTENDED_AUDIENCE) document_dataset_incremental 2 FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS Per-column descriptions (feature name → view name → description) document_process, document_dataset_incremental

docs.title: What gets vectorised
docs.text:
The vector store maintains 3 collections, populated by build_vector_index():

  • {prefix}_objects ← BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION from OBJECTS
  • {prefix}_sections ← SECTION_CONTENT from SECTIONS
  • {prefix}_columns ← BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION from COLUMNS

The prefix is tdfs4ds for Chroma (cosine-distance HNSW collections) and tdfs4ds.TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX for the Teradata backend.

Documents are embedded in batches of 8 to respect rate limits (OpenAI, TEI, etc.).

docs.title: Backend selection
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND = 'chroma'    # default — local or remote ChromaDB
# or
tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND = 'teradata'  # Teradata Enterprise Vector Store

build_vector_index() and search_vector_index() dispatch on this setting; all skill code remains unchanged. The Teradata backend persists the three collections as named TeradataVectorStores directly in Vantage and accepts either a LangChain Embeddings object (default — keeps your vLLM / TEI / OpenAI-compatible setup) or a teradatagenai.TeradataAI object (set TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE to opt in). See LLM & embedding models for the full configuration.

docs.title: Hybrid retrieval strategy
docs.text:
Each skill uses a mix of structured SQL (for precision) and vector search (for recall when names don't match exactly):

Skill Structured lookups Vector search SEARCH BD_SECTIONS (FEATURE_THEMES, BUSINESS_QUESTIONS), BD_OBJECTS, catalog Yes — semantic priority, results prepended DEFINITION Feature catalog → process view → BD_COLUMNS; BD_OBJECTS + BD_SECTIONS for datasets Fallback only (when SQL returns nothing) USAGE BD_SECTIONS (INTENDED_AUDIENCE, OVERVIEW) Fallback only FRESHNESS Feature catalog → process catalog → follow_up_report (single feature); dataset catalog → all process_ids for the dataset's features → aggregated follow_up_report (dataset) None SUMMARY Feature catalog + BD_SECTIONS (FEATURE_THEMES, OVERVIEW) None LINEAGE build_teradata_dependency_graph (DDL walk) None EXPLAIN SHOW VIEW DDL None

For multi-object DEFINITION queries, the skill also builds a context_graph dict (features, shared views, shared datasets) stored in skill_result and visualised in the chatbot's context panel. EXPLAIN does the same — _skill_explain_multi returns a context_graph (carrying each feature's process_id + view_name) and a resolved_triplet focus pointer, which node_skill_explain merges into accumulated_context_graph, so a follow-up turn after an EXPLAIN can resolve from cache.

docs.title: Vector index management
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.agent import build_vector_index, search_vector_index

build_vector_index(force_rebuild=False)    # incremental: indexes only new rows (Chroma)
build_vector_index(force_rebuild=True)     # full rebuild: wipe collections and reindex
search_vector_index('total_amount', k=5)   # raw vector hits (not used directly by skills)

docs.title: Listing available models
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.agent import list_instruct_models, list_embedding_models

list_instruct_models()
list_embedding_models(sub_paths=['e5', 'code'])

docs.title: Notebooks
docs.text:
• notebook dev/genai/09 - Consumer Agent Chatbot with tdfs4ds.ipynb — architecture walkthrough, 7-intent test suite, 4-turn demo.
  • notebook dev/genai/10 - Launch Consumer Agent Chatbot.ipynb — minimal 4-cell launch.

docs.title: Dataset documentation
docs.text: document_dataset_incremental walks a dataset's lineage bottom-up and produces structured documentation without re-running anything that is already documented.

docs.title: What it does
docs.text:
1. Builds the dependency graph with expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=True.
  2. For every undocumented intermediate view or process view found in the graph, calls document_process_incremental so upstream context is available before the dataset itself is described.
  3. Propagates feature and entity column descriptions from the process docs into the dataset's column docs — no extra LLM call.
  4. Issues a single JSON-constrained LLM call that produces five sections:
  5. OVERVIEW
  6. ENTITY
  7. FEATURE_THEMES
  8. BUSINESS_QUESTIONS
  9. INTENDED_AUDIENCE
  10. Stores each section as one row in FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS so the consumer agent can retrieve them without chunking.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.genai import document_dataset_incremental

result = document_dataset_incremental(
    dataset_id   = '<UUID>',   # from dataset_catalog()
    force_update = False,
    upload       = True,
)

result['DATASET_SECTIONS']     # dict of {section_name: content}
result['COLUMN_DESCRIPTIONS']  # propagated column docs

Pass force_update=True to ignore the unchanged-hash short-circuit when the underlying SQL has not changed but you want a fresh LLM pass.

docs.title: Finding the dataset ID
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.dataset_catalog()
# Pick a row; its DATASET_ID column is what document_dataset_incremental wants.

You can also retrieve it directly:

qry = f"""
CURRENT VALIDTIME
SELECT DATASET_ID
FROM {tdfs4ds.SCHEMA}.V_{tdfs4ds.DATASET_CATALOG_NAME}_CATALOG
WHERE DATASET_NAME = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND'
"""
dataset_id = tdml.execute_sql(qry).fetchall()[0][0]

docs.title: Why bottom-up
docs.text:
If the dataset depends on an undocumented process view, the LLM has no context for the columns it sees. The bottom-up walk guarantees that:

  • Source tables are documented (or skipped if a description already exists).
  • Every process view between source and dataset is documented.
  • The dataset description sits on top of a fully resolved column-level context — no hallucinations about what total_amount means.

A shared visited-set across the recursive calls ensures every process view is documented at most once, even when several datasets share it.

docs.title: What the agent reads
docs.text: The consumer agent's LINEAGE, DEFINITION, and EXPLAIN skills query the business dictionary populated by this call. Skipping documentation for a dataset means the agent's SEARCH and DEFINITION answers will fall back to the underlying process docs — usually fine, but you lose the dataset-level overview.

docs.title: EXPLAIN scoring
docs.text:
Every EXPLAIN analysis in tdfs4ds returns four signals side by side. The first two are LLM-assessed scores; the last two are deterministic counters parsed from the raw EXPLAIN text.

Key Type Source Meaning user_score 1–5 int LLM, after [You]-item validation SQL quality — what the author controls global_score 1–5 int LLM Overall plan quality including infrastructure factors n_steps int Regex on raw EXPLAIN Numbered execution steps (plan depth) n_spool_objects int Regex on raw EXPLAIN Distinct Spool N objects referenced (intermediate materialisation count)

All four are stored in FS_PROCESS_DOCUMENTATION_EXPLAIN and returned via retrieve_explain_documentation() / get_process_info() as USER_SCORE, OPTIMIZATION_SCORE, EXPLAIN_N_STEPS, EXPLAIN_N_SPOOL_OBJECTS.

docs.title: Why two scores
docs.text:
Score What it answers user_score "How good is the SQL itself? Is the data scientist writing efficient queries?" global_score "How good is the execution plan? Is the database tuned for this workload?"

A user_score = 5 with global_score = 2 means the SQL is well written but the underlying tables need a DBA action (Primary Index re-design, stats collection, partitioning). A user_score = 2 is the data scientist's problem — try the query optimizer.

docs.title: Why two counters
docs.text:
The counters complement the scores by giving you deterministic evidence:

  • n_steps — fewer steps usually means less work. Halving the number of steps is a strong signal that a rewrite paid off.
  • n_spool_objects — fewer spools means less intermediate materialisation (a proxy for memory + IO).

Both come from a regex on the raw EXPLAIN text — they are not opinions, so they are useful as a sanity check on the LLM scores.

docs.title: Helper functions
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.genai import (
    document_sql_query_explain,
    _parse_explain_metrics,
    _parse_explain_signals,
)

scored = document_sql_query_explain(sql_query="SELECT ...", classify=True)
# {
#     'explanation':    '...',
#     'user_score':     4,
#     'global_score':   3,
#     'n_steps':        9,
#     'n_spool_objects':4,
#     'warnings':       ['...'],
#     'recommendations':['[You] ...', '[DBA] ...'],
# }

# Pure deterministic counters (no LLM)
metrics = _parse_explain_metrics(scored['raw_plan'])
# {'n_steps': 9, 'n_spool_objects': 4}

# Ground-truth booleans + scan counts
signals = _parse_explain_signals(scored['raw_plan'])
# {
#   'has_product_join':     False,
#   'table_scan_counts':    {'TRANSACTIONS': 1},
#   'has_duplicate_scans':  False,
#   'has_redistribution':   True,
# }

document_sql_query_explain(classify=False) keeps raw [You] items as flagged by the LLM. Use classify=True to filter out false positives like uncorrelated scalar subqueries that Teradata evaluates once.

docs.title: Schema migration on upgrade
docs.text: The columns USER_SCORE, N_STEPS, and N_SPOOL_OBJECTS were added in v0.2.9. On upgrade, documentation_tables_creation() and migrate_documentation_explain_schema() add them idempotently — a SHOW TABLE probe runs first. Older rows read back as NULL until re-documented; retrieve_explain_documentation() probes the live DDL and only selects the new columns when present, so the call works on un-migrated installs.

docs.title: Visual flow diagram
docs.text: For interactive HTML flow diagrams of any raw EXPLAIN output — including ones not coming from a registered tdfs4ds process — use the td-explain skill.

docs.title: AI capabilities — overview
docs.text:
tdfs4ds ships with four LLM-powered features layered on top of the feature store. They are independent — you can turn any one of them on without enabling the others.

Process documentation

LLM business-logic description + per-feature column docs + EXPLAIN score 1–5.

Dataset documentation

Lineage-aware dataset docs: bottom-up walk through every process and source.

Business dictionary

Three temporal tables, no LLM required — feed the agent with structured business descriptions.

Consumer agent

Conversational chatbot over the dictionary: SEARCH, DEFINITION, USAGE, FRESHNESS, SUMMARY, LINEAGE, EXPLAIN, ALGORITHM (MCP).

Query optimizer

9-step pipeline: simplify → EXPLAIN → candidates → compare → FilterManager proposal.

EXPLAIN scoring

1–5 USER / GLOBAL scores plus deterministic counters parsed from the raw plan.

docs.title: Common configuration
docs.text:
All four features share the same LLM config:

tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'openai'       # 'openai' | 'azure' | 'vllm' | 'bedrock'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = 'gpt-4o'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY  = os.getenv('OPENAI_API_KEY')

See Configuration / LLM & embedding models for full provider details.

docs.title: Lazy loading
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.genai and tdfs4ds.agent import LangChain, LangGraph, Chroma, and Gradio — together a few seconds at first access. The package defers both behind a module __getattr__, so plain feature-store use stays fast.

import tdfs4ds                           # ~ 400 ms
tdfs4ds.genai.document_process(...)     # langchain* loaded here
tdfs4ds.agent.launch_chatbot(...)       # langgraph + gradio loaded here

docs.title: Serve the agents over HTTP
docs.text: The consumer agent and the query optimizer can be exposed as an OpenAI-compatible API (two models, one per agent) and shipped in Docker — see Deployment / OpenAI-compatible API server.

docs.title: Process documentation
docs.text:
document_process calls the configured LLM to produce three artefacts for any registered process and persist them in the feature store:

Artefact Stored in What it contains Business logic FS_PROCESS_DOCUMENTATION_BUSINESS_LOGIC Markdown description of what the SQL does, written in business terms Feature descriptions FS_PROCESS_DOCUMENTATION_FEATURES One row per feature column with a plain-language definition EXPLAIN analysis FS_PROCESS_DOCUMENTATION_EXPLAIN Scored EXPLAIN with USER_SCORE, OPTIMIZATION_SCORE, N_STEPS, N_SPOOL_OBJECTS, raw plan

All three tables are VALIDTIME temporal — re-running the function overwrites the current row and preserves the history.

docs.title: Synchronous call
docs.text:
import tdfs4ds
from tdfs4ds.genai import document_process

tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'openai'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = 'gpt-4o'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY  = os.getenv('OPENAI_API_KEY')

doc = document_process(
    process_id        = '<UUID>',
    show_sql_query    = True,
    show_explain_plan = True,
)

doc['DOCUMENTED_SQL']           # business logic + per-feature descriptions
doc['EXPLAIN_ANALYSIS']         # plain-text plan analysis
doc['USER_SCORE']               # 1–5, SQL author quality
doc['OPTIMIZATION_SCORE']       # 1–5, overall execution quality
doc['EXPLAIN_WARNINGS']         # list[str]
doc['EXPLAIN_RECOMMENDATIONS']  # list[str] split [You] / [DBA]

docs.title: Resolve by view name
docs.text:
When you only have the qualified view name, use get_process_id:

from tdfs4ds.process_store.process_query_administration import get_process_id
process_id = get_process_id('"my_db"."FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER"')

get_process_id strips quotes, accepts db.view or plain view names, and falls back to tdfs4ds.SCHEMA when no database prefix is given.

docs.title: Incremental variant
docs.text:
document_process_incremental is identical to document_process but skips processes that already have a stored documentation and an unchanged SQL hash. It is the right call from a notebook that documents many processes in a loop:

from tdfs4ds.genai import document_process_incremental

for pid in tdfs4ds.process_catalog()[['PROCESS_ID']].to_pandas().PROCESS_ID:
    document_process_incremental(process_id=pid)

docs.title: Full-store one-liner
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.genai import document_feature_store_incremental

summary = document_feature_store_incremental(
    language     = 'English',
    force_update = False,
    upload       = True,
)
# summary keys: processes_documented, datasets_documented,
#               processes_skipped,    datasets_skipped

Objects are processed in dependency order (leaves first, roots last) so upstream context is always available when documenting downstream views.

docs.title: Retrieve without re-running
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.process_store.process_store_catalog_management import get_process_info

info = get_process_info(process_id)
print(info.get('DOCUMENTED_SQL'))
print(info.get('EXPLAIN_ANALYSIS'))

docs.title: EXPLAIN score interpretation
docs.text:
Score Meaning 5/5 Optimal use of PI and partitioning 3–4/5 Partial optimisation; some full-AMP / full-partition scans remain 1–2/5 Full-table scans, product joins, or spool-heavy operations

See EXPLAIN scoring for the full metric definitions and how the deterministic counters complement the LLM scores.

docs.title: Programmatic SQL scoring
docs.text:
If you want to score raw SQL without registering a process:

from tdfs4ds.genai import document_sql_query_explain

scored = document_sql_query_explain(
    sql_query = "SELECT ... FROM ...",
    classify  = True,        # filter LLM false positives via deterministic signals
)

classify=False is the right choice when the score is consumed by another automated step (the query optimizer uses it that way internally).

docs.title: Query optimizer
docs.text: tdfs4ds.query_optimizer analyses and rewrites Teradata SQL feature-engineering queries to improve EXPLAIN scores and reduce spool usage. It is process-aware: when you pass a process_id, it pulls the registered SQL and any stored EXPLAIN documentation directly from the catalog, avoiding redundant LLM calls.

docs.title: 9-step pipeline
docs.text: Step Action 0 Process context — SQL + stored EXPLAIN analysis fetched from the catalog (skipped when no process_id). 0.5 SQL simplification — structural compaction merges unnecessary nesting into CTE + single outer SELECT. The simplified form is accepted only when its EXPLAIN score ≥ original. 1 Structured EXPLAIN analysis — scores the simplified query 1–5 with [You]-prefixed author-actionable warnings. 2 Lineage graph — Primary Index + partition columns collected for every underlying object. 3 DDL fetch — SHOW TABLE / SHOW VIEW for every referenced object. 4 Candidate generation — LLM proposes up to QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES rewrites, each tagged with a strategy. 5 Candidate EXPLAIN — document_sql_query_explain per candidate. 6 Plan comparison — LLM picks the best plan by score delta and resolved warnings. 7 FilterManager check — partitioned-but-unfiltered objects are flagged for incremental processing. 8 Final report — Markdown with Score Summary, Simplification, 3-stage comparison (Input → Simplified → Optimized), Candidates, Selected Optimisation, FilterManager.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import tdfs4ds

tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'vllm'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = 'mistral-7b-instruct'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY  = os.getenv('FS_API_KEY')

tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES       = 5
tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_FAILURES         = 5
tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATE_TOKENS = None

# Process-aware
result = tdfs4ds.query_optimizer(process_id='<UUID>', thread_id='session-1')

# Or pass raw SQL directly
result = tdfs4ds.query_optimizer(
    sql_query="SELECT ... FROM db.tbl",
    thread_id='session-2',
)

In a Jupyter notebook prefer the async entry point — it avoids the background-thread overhead from LangGraph's sync wrapper:

from tdfs4ds.agent import aquery_optimizer
result = await aquery_optimizer(process_id='<UUID>', thread_id='session-async')

docs.title: Multi-turn follow-ups
docs.text:
Reuse the same thread_id to keep the SQL and report in context:

result = tdfs4ds.query_optimizer(
    "Would adding a Secondary Index on the join column help?",
    thread_id='session-1',
)

docs.title: Result dict
docs.text: Key Content answer Structured Markdown report (Summary, Score Summary, Lineage, ...) best_sql Recommended SQL — equal to the original when no rewrite improves the score score_delta Before/after comparison of scores + execution metrics original_analysis explanation, user_score, global_score, n_steps, n_spool_objects, warnings, recommendations candidates List of {sql, strategy, rationale, analysis} comparison best_index, reasoning, business_logic_preserved filtermanager_applicable True when a FilterManager rewrite is recommended process_info Full process catalog record (when process_id is supplied) steps Every pipeline step with inputs and outputs

docs.title: Score delta
docs.text:
sd = result['score_delta']
# {
#   'optimized':                True,
#   'best_strategy':            'partition_pruning',
#   'original_user_score':      2,   'best_user_score':      4,  'user_score_delta':   2.0,
#   'original_global_score':    3,   'best_global_score':    4,  'global_score_delta': 1.0,
#   'original_n_steps':         14,  'best_n_steps':         9,  'steps_delta':       -5,
#   'original_n_spools':        6,   'best_n_spools':        4,  'spools_delta':      -2,
#   'business_logic_preserved': True,
# }

Negative steps_delta and spools_delta mean the rewrite is lighter than the baseline. When the score improves but adds steps or spools (typically single-row CTEs backing a precomputed threshold), the report includes an explanatory note about the trade-off.

docs.title: Standalone simplification
docs.text:
The simplification pass is callable on its own:

result = tdfs4ds.simplify_query(sql_query="SELECT ...")
# or
result = tdfs4ds.simplify_query(process_id='<UUID>')

# result keys: simplified_sql, original_sql, simplified (bool),
#              original_score, simplified_score
if result['simplified']:
    print(result['simplified_sql'])

docs.title: Notebook display
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.agent import display_optimization_result
display_optimization_result(result)

Renders a before/after table with colour-coded Δ Change cells (green = improvement, red = regression) followed by the full Markdown report.

docs.title: FilterManager hand-off
docs.text:
When filtermanager_applicable is True, the report includes a ready-to-use snippet:

fm = tdfs4ds.FilterManager(
    schema_name = tdfs4ds.SCHEMA,
    view_name   = 'TRANSACTIONS',
    col_names   = ['transaction_date'],
)

for filter_id in range(fm.nb_filters):
    fm.update(filter_id)
    tdfs4ds.run(process_id)

See User guide / FilterManager for the full pattern.

docs.title: Report files
docs.text:
Each run writes a Markdown report under:

{QUERY_OPTIMIZER_REPORT_DIR}/{DATA_DOMAIN}/tdfs4ds_optim_{view}_{process_id}.md

QUERY_OPTIMIZER_REPORT_DIR defaults to ./tdfs4ds_optim_reports/. The directory is created automatically.

docs.title: Using the optimizer via the API server
docs.text: When tdfs4ds is deployed with python -m tdfs4ds.serve, the query optimizer is exposed through two HTTP endpoints. Choose the one that fits your workflow:

docs.title: POST /v1/optimize — structured JSON (programmatic)
docs.text:
Accepts raw SQL, returns a structured JSON object. No OpenAI framing needed.

curl

curl -s http://localhost:8000/v1/optimize \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
        "sql": "SELECT c.customer_id, SUM(s.amount) AS total FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id GROUP BY 1"
      }'

Response:

{
  "optimized_sql": "...",
  "optimization_strategy": "Replaced implicit PI join with explicit PI alignment ...",
  "token_consumption": {
    "prompt_tokens": 1842,
    "completion_tokens": 312,
    "total_tokens": 2154
  }
}

Python (requests)

import requests

result = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:8000/v1/optimize",
    json={"sql": "SELECT * FROM my_db.my_view WHERE ..."},
).json()

print(result["optimized_sql"])
print(result["optimization_strategy"])
print("tokens:", result["token_consumption"]["total_tokens"])

docs.title: POST /v1/chat/completions — Markdown report (conversational)
docs.text:
Use the tdfs4ds-query-optimizer model for multi-turn interaction and full Markdown reports with scores, strategy details, and download links.

curl

curl -s http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
        "model": "tdfs4ds-query-optimizer",
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Optimize: SELECT * FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id"}]
      }'

openai Python SDK

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="not-needed")

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="tdfs4ds-query-optimizer",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Optimize: SELECT * FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id"}],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

# Score metadata is available in the usage block
print(resp.usage.model_extra.get("optimized"))     # True / False
print(resp.usage.model_extra.get("score_delta"))   # {original_user_score, best_user_score, ...}

LangChain (ChatOpenAI)

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage

llm = ChatOpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="not-needed",
    model="tdfs4ds-query-optimizer",
)

sql = "SELECT * FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id WHERE c.region = 'EU'"
response = llm.invoke([HumanMessage(content=f"Optimize: {sql}")])
print(response.content)

See Deployment / API server for the full endpoint reference, including path-prefix, cloud deployments, and the comparison table between the two optimizer endpoints.

docs.title: feature_catalog
docs.text:
def feature_catalog()

Retrieve a list of all features available in the feature store.

This function queries the feature store to obtain a comprehensive list of features that have been defined and stored. It leverages the feature store's query retrieval functionality to compile and return this list. This can be useful for understanding what features are available for analysis, machine learning models, or other purposes within the data ecosystem.

Returns: - list: A list of features available in the feature store as a teradata dataframe at the tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME valid time.

docs.title: process_catalog
docs.text:
def process_catalog()

Retrieve a list of all processes registered in the process store.

This function performs a query against the process store to gather a list of all processes that have been registered and are administrable. It utilizes the process store's process query administration capabilities to fetch and return this list. This is useful for users looking to get an overview of the data processing workflows, transformations, and other processes that have been established within the data infrastructure.

Returns: - list: A list of processes registered in the process store as a teradata dataframe at the tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME valid time.

docs.title: build_dataset
docs.text:
def build_dataset(entity_id,
                  selected_features,
                  view_name,
                  schema_name=None,
                  comment=None,
                  return_query=False,
                  feature_store_time=False,
                  join_type='INNER')

Generate a SQL query and create a Teradata view to retrieve records for the specified entity IDs based on selected features.

docs.title: Parameters:
docs.text:
entity_id : list, dict, or str The entity IDs for which records are needed. Can be a list, dictionary, or string. - If list: contains multiple entity IDs. - If dict: keys are the entity IDs. - If str: a single entity ID.

selected_features : dict A dictionary where the keys are feature table names, and the values are lists of tuples (feature_id, feature_version, feature_name) specifying the features to retrieve. NOTE: feature_version may be either: - a single UUID string, or - a list of dicts like:

view_name : str The name of the view to be created in the database.

schema_name : str, optional The name of the database schema where the view will be created. Defaults to the global tdfs4ds.SCHEMA if not provided.

comment : str, optional A comment to attach to the created view for documentation purposes. Defaults to None.

return_query : bool, optional If True, the function will return the SQL query string instead of executing it. Defaults to False.

feature_store_time : bool, optional If True, the query will use a specific VALIDTIME based on tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME. If False, the query will use CURRENT VALIDTIME. Defaults to False.

join_type : str, optional Specifies the type of join to use between features: 'INNER' (default) or 'FULL OUTER'.

docs.title: Returns:
docs.text: str or tdml.DataFrame - If return_query is True, returns the generated SQL query as a string. - Otherwise, returns a Teradata DataFrame containing the results.

docs.title: Raises:
docs.text: ValueError If an invalid join_type is provided.

docs.title: augment_source_with_features
docs.text:
def augment_source_with_features(source_schema,
                                 source_name,
                                 entity_id,
                                 feature_selection,
                                 output_view_schema,
                                 output_view_name,
                                 join_type='LEFT JOIN')

Augment a source table with selected features by creating a view that joins the source data with feature tables based on specified entity identifiers and feature selections. The join type can be specified as either 'LEFT JOIN' or 'INNER JOIN':

  • 'LEFT JOIN': Retains all rows from the source table, with missing values for unmatched features.
  • 'INNER JOIN': Returns only rows where all specified features have matching, non-missing values.

Parameters: source_schema (str): The schema of the source table to augment. source_name (str): The name of the source table to augment. entity_id (str or list of str): The entity ID(s) for joining the source data with feature tables. Can be a single string or a list of strings. feature_selection (list): A list of features to select and join with the source data. Each feature is represented as a tuple with (feature_id, feature_version). output_view_schema (str): The schema in which to create the output view. output_view_name (str): The name of the output view to create with augmented features. join_type (str, optional): The type of SQL join to use, either 'LEFT JOIN' for a full dataset with possible missing values or 'INNER JOIN' for a dataset with only complete feature matches. Defaults to 'LEFT JOIN'.

Returns: tdml.DataFrame: A DataFrame object that references the newly created view with augmented features.

docs.title: upload_tdstone2_scores
docs.text:
def upload_tdstone2_scores(model)

Uploads features from a model's predictions to the Teradata feature store. This function handles the entire workflow from extracting feature names and types, registering them in the feature catalog, preparing features for ingestion, storing them in the feature store, and finally creating a dataset view in the feature store.

Parameters: - model: The model object whose predictions contain features to be uploaded. This model should have methods to extract predictions and feature information.

Returns: - DataFrame: A DataFrame representing the dataset view created in the feature store, which includes features from the model's predictions.

Note: - The function assumes that the model provides a method get_model_predictions which returns a Teradata DataFrame. - Entity ID for the model is extracted and registered in the data domain. - The function cleans up by dropping the volatile table created during the process. - The feature names and their types are extracted from the model's predictions and are registered in the feature catalog.

docs.title: roll_out
docs.text:
def roll_out(process_list,
             time_manager,
             time_id_start=1,
             time_id_end=None,
             force_compute=False,
             force_display_logs=False)

Executes a series of processes for each date in a given list, managing time, computation settings, and logging.

This function iterates over a range of time steps, updating a TimeManager object with each step, and then executes a list of processes for that time step. It also manages synchronization of time for the feature store and optionally controls forced computation and log display behavior.

Parameters: - process_list (list): A list of process IDs that need to be executed for each time step. - time_manager (TimeManager): An object that manages time-related operations, like updating or retrieving time. - time_id_start (int, optional): The starting time step ID. Default is 1. - time_id_end (int, optional): The ending time step ID. If None, it will run until the last time step in the time manager. - force_compute (bool, optional): If True, forces each process to recompute even if previous results exist. Default is False. - force_display_logs (bool, optional): If True, forces log display during the rollout even if global log display is disabled. Default is False.

Side Effects: - Temporarily modifies global variables DISPLAY_LOGS, PROCESS_TYPE, RUN_ID, and FEATURE_STORE_TIME. - Restores DISPLAY_LOGS setting after execution. - Catches and prints exceptions along with the time step on which they occurred.

Steps performed: 1. Disables display logs by default unless force_display_logs is True. 2. Sets process type to 'ROLL_OUT' and initializes a unique run ID. 3. Iterates over the specified range of time steps. 4. Updates the time manager with the current time step. 5. Synchronizes the feature store time with the current time step. 6. Executes each process in the process list with optional forced computation. 7. Restores original display log settings after completion.

Example:

process_list = ['process_1', 'process_2'] time_manager = TimeManager(...) roll_out(process_list, time_manager, time_id_start=1, time_id_end=10, force_compute=True, force_display_logs=True)

docs.title: outstanding_amounts_dataset
docs.text:
def outstanding_amounts_dataset()

Load and return a dataset of outstanding amounts from a CSV file.

This function reads a CSV file named by the variable outstanding_amounts_dataset_filename located in the "data" subdirectory of the directory specified by package_dir. It parses the 'OUTSTANDING_DATE' column in the dataset as dates.

Returns: pandas.DataFrame: A DataFrame containing the dataset of outstanding amounts. The 'OUTSTANDING_DATE' column is parsed as datetime objects.

Note: - This function requires the pandas library to be imported as pd. - The variables package_dir and outstanding_amounts_dataset_filename must be defined in the scope where this function is called. - The CSV file must have a column named 'OUTSTANDING_DATE' for the date parsing to work correctly.

docs.title: upload_outstanding_amounts_dataset
docs.text:
def upload_outstanding_amounts_dataset(table_name='outstanding_amount_dataset',
                                       **kwargs)

Uploads the outstanding amounts dataset to a database table and returns the table as a DataFrame.

This function uploads the dataset returned by outstanding_amounts_dataset() to a specified table in a database. If a 'schema_name' is provided via keyword arguments, it uploads the data to that schema; otherwise, it uses the default schema. It prints to the console where the dataset is uploaded. After uploading, it retrieves the newly uploaded table into a DataFrame and returns it.

Parameters: table_name (str, optional): The name of the database table where the dataset will be uploaded. Defaults to 'outstanding_amount_dataset'. **kwargs: Arbitrary keyword arguments. Can be used to specify 'schema_name' and other parameters that tdml.copy_to_sql() might accept, such as connection details.

Returns: tdml.DataFrame: A DataFrame representing the database table into which the dataset was uploaded. This DataFrame is created either in the specified schema (if 'schema_name' is provided) or the default schema.

Note: - This function requires the tdml library to be available and correctly configured to interact with the target database. - The outstanding_amounts_dataset() function is called within this function to obtain the dataset to upload. - If 'schema_name' is provided in kwargs, it is used for both uploading the dataset and retrieving it into a DataFrame. If not provided, a notice is printed, and the default schema is used.

docs.title: register_entity
docs.text:
def register_entity(entity_id,
                    feature_types,
                    primary_index=None,
                    partitioning='')

Registers an entity within the feature store by facilitating the creation of feature store tables across multiple data types.

This function leverages the feature_store_table_creation function to create feature store tables tailored for different data types: 'FLOAT', 'BIGINT', 'VARCHAR_UNICODE', and 'VARCHAR_LATIN'. By doing so, it ensures a comprehensive setup for the specified entity, allowing for a diverse range of feature data types to be stored efficiently.

Parameters: - entity_id (str): Unique identifier for the entity. This ID is utilized to construct table names and associate them with the corresponding entity within the feature store. - primary_index (list, optional): Specifies the columns to be used as the primary index for the tables. A well-chosen primary index can significantly enhance query performance. - partitioning (str, optional): Defines the partitioning strategy for the tables, which can help manage and optimize the storage and retrieval of large datasets.

Returns: tuple: Contains the names of the created feature store tables. This tuple includes names for tables designed to store 'FLOAT', 'BIGINT', 'VARCHAR_UNICODE', and 'VARCHAR_LATIN' feature types, in the order they were created.

Example Usage:

register_entity("customer123") ('customer123_float_table', 'customer123_bigint_table', 'customer123_varchar_unicode_table', 'customer123_varchar_latin_table')

Note: - This function is integral to initializing the feature storage for a new entity, ensuring that data of various types can be accommodated. - The function assumes that the feature_store_table_creation function handles the intricacies of table creation, including dealing with existing tables based on the 'if_exists' parameter and applying specified partitioning and indexing strategies.

docs.title: tdstone2_entity_id
docs.text:
def tdstone2_entity_id(existing_model)

Generate a dictionary mapping entity IDs to their respective data types in a given tdstone2 model.

This function analyzes the provided tdstone2 model object to determine the type of the model ('model scoring' or 'feature engineering'). Depending on the model type, it retrieves the list of entity IDs from the appropriate mapper attribute ('mapper_scoring' or 'mapper'). It then constructs a dictionary where each key is an entity ID, and its corresponding value is the data type of that entity ID, as specified in the model's mapper attributes ('types').

Args: existing_model (object): A model object created using the tdstone2 package. This object should contain the necessary mapper attributes ('mapper_scoring' or 'mapper') with 'id_row', 'id_partition', and 'types'.

Returns: dict: A dictionary mapping entity IDs to their data types. Keys are entity IDs, and values are data types (e.g., 'BIGINT').

Raises: TypeError: If the relevant ID attribute ('id_row' or 'id_partition') in the model is neither a list nor a single value.

Note: - The function dynamically determines the type of the model (scoring or feature engineering) based on the presence of specific attributes. - 'id_row' or 'id_partition' is converted to a list if it is not already one. - This function assumes the model is correctly instantiated and the necessary attributes are properly defined.

Example: model = entity_id_types = tdstone2_entity_id(model) # entity_id_types might look like {'ID': 'BIGINT'}

docs.title: remove_entity
docs.text:
def remove_entity(entity, data_domain=None)

Removes all database entities (tables and views) associated with a given entity name within a specific data domain, and then removes the entity's metadata from the feature catalog.

This function first constructs a SQL query to select distinct table and view names associated with the given entity name and data domain from the feature catalog. It then iterates over the list of tables and views, attempting to drop each one. Progress and status of these operations are tracked using a progress bar. If an error occurs during the drop operation, the error message is displayed in the progress bar description. Finally, the function constructs and executes another SQL query to delete the entity's metadata from the feature catalog.

Parameters: - entity (str): The name of the entity to be removed. - data_domain (str, optional): The data domain where the entity is located. If not provided, the default data domain from tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN is used.

The function utilizes the tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE flag to control the printing of SQL queries for debugging purposes. If DEBUG_MODE is set to True, the queries for selecting and deleting entities are printed to the console.

Dependencies: - Requires the tdfs4ds module for configuration constants like DATA_DOMAIN, SCHEMA, and FEATURE_CATALOG_NAME, as well as the DEBUG_MODE flag. - Requires the tdml module or object with an execute_sql method for database operations. - Uses the tqdm module to display a progress bar for the operations.

Note: - This function assumes that the necessary permissions are in place for dropping tables and views, as well as deleting metadata entries from the feature catalog. - Exception handling within the dropping operations is limited to displaying the first line of any encountered error messages in the progress bar description. Further error handling or cleanup may be necessary depending on the specific requirements and database configuration.

Returns: - None. The primary effect of this function is to alter the state of the database by removing entities and their metadata, not to return data to the caller.

docs.title: generate_collect_stats
docs.text:
def generate_collect_stats(entity_id, primary_index='', partitioning='')

Generate a COLLECT STATISTICS SQL query based on specified entity identifiers, primary index, and partitioning information. The query collects statistics for columns relevant to the data structure, including partitioning and entity identifiers, for optimized query performance.

Parameters: entity_id (list or dict): A list of entity IDs or a dictionary where keys represent entity IDs. Used to identify the columns requiring statistical collection. primary_index (str or list, optional): The primary index column(s) for the table, provided as a single string or list of strings. Defaults to an empty string. partitioning (str, optional): A string defining partitioning information, used to identify additional columns for statistical collection. Defaults to an empty string.

Returns: tuple: A tuple containing two strings: - The base COLLECT STATISTICS SQL query. - The extended COLLECT STATISTICS SQL query with sampling and thresholds applied.

docs.title: prepare_feature_ingestion
docs.text:
def prepare_feature_ingestion(df,
                              entity_id,
                              feature_names,
                              feature_versions=None,
                              primary_index=None,
                              partitioning='',
                              entity_null_substitute={},
                              **kwargs)

Transforms and prepares a DataFrame for feature ingestion into a feature store by unpivoting it.

This function accepts a DataFrame along with the specifications for entity IDs, feature names, and optionally, feature versions. It generates a new DataFrame with the data unpivoted to align with the requirements of a feature store ingestion process. Additionally, it creates a volatile table in the database to facilitate the transformation.

Parameters: - df (tdml.DataFrame): The input DataFrame containing the feature data. - entity_id (list/dict/other): Specifies the entity IDs. If a dictionary, the keys are used as entity IDs. If a list or another type, it is used directly as the entity ID. - feature_names (list): Names of features to be unpivoted. - feature_versions (dict, optional): Maps feature names to their versions. If not provided, a default version is applied. - primary_index (list/str, optional): Primary index(es) for the volatile table. If not specified, entity IDs are used. - **kwargs: Additional keyword arguments for customization.

Returns: tuple: A tuple containing: 1. A tdml.DataFrame with the transformed data ready for ingestion. 2. The name of the volatile table created in the database.

Raises: Exception: If the function encounters an issue during the database operations or data transformations.

Note: - The function handles different data types for 'entity_id' to ensure correct SQL query formation. - It assumes a specific structure for 'df' to properly execute SQL commands for data transformation.

Example:

df = tdml.DataFrame(...) entity_id = {'customer_id': 'INTEGER'} feature_names = ['feature1', 'feature2'] transformed_df, volatile_table_name = prepare_feature_ingestion(df, entity_id, feature_names)

docs.title: apply_collect_stats
docs.text:
def apply_collect_stats(entity_id: Mapping[str, Any] | Iterable[str],
                        primary_index: Optional[str],
                        partitioning: Optional[str],
                        feature_infos: pd.DataFrame) -> Dict[str, Any]

Run COLLECT STATS on all target feature tables, with fallbacks and timing.

Steps: 1) Determine a stable ordering of entity IDs (for deterministic query gen). 2) Group feature_infos by FEATURE_DATABASE + FEATURE_TABLE to get unique targets. 3) Generate COLLECT STATS statements via generate_collect_stats(...) for fallback use. 4) For each target table: - Try a simple COLLECT STATS ON <db>.<table>. - On failure, retry with generated statements (and optional extension). 5) Log a compact summary (counts + total duration) and return it as a dict.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: entity_id : Mapping[str, Any] | Iterable[str] Entity identifiers used to parameterize collect-stat statements. If a mapping (e.g., dict), its keys are used and sorted. If an iterable (e.g., list/tuple), itΓÇÖs sorted directly. primary_index : Optional[str] Primary index used by generate_collect_stats (may be None). partitioning : Optional[str] Partitioning clause used by generate_collect_stats (may be None). feature_infos : pd.DataFrame Must contain columns: 'FEATURE_TABLE', 'FEATURE_DATABASE', 'FEATURE_ID'.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Dict[str, Any] Summary with keys: - total_tables: int - ok: int - retried: int - failed: int - duration_seconds: float - duration_hms: str - details: list[dict] # per-table status entries

docs.title: prepare_feature_ingestion_tdstone2
docs.text:
def prepare_feature_ingestion_tdstone2(df, entity_id)

Prepares a DataFrame for feature ingestion into a tdstone2 feature store by transforming the data structure. This function unpivots the DataFrame and adds necessary columns for entity IDs, feature names, feature values, and a predefined feature version. It creates a volatile table in the database to facilitate this transformation.

Parameters: - df (tdml.DataFrame): The input DataFrame containing the feature data. The DataFrame should be structured in a way that is compatible with tdstone2 feature store requirements. - entity_id (list/dict/other): A representation of the entity ID. If a dictionary, the keys are used as entity IDs. If a list or another data type, it is used directly as the entity ID.

Returns: - tdml.DataFrame: A transformed DataFrame suitable for feature store ingestion. - str: The name of the volatile table created in the database for storing the transformed data.

Note: - The function requires that the input DataFrame 'df' has a valid table name and is compatible with tdml operations. - It automatically handles the creation and management of a volatile table in the database. - 'ID_PROCESS' is used as the default feature version identifier. - The transformed DataFrame includes columns for each entity ID, 'FEATURE_NAME', 'FEATURE_VALUE', and 'FEATURE_VERSION'.

Example Usage:

input_df = tdml.DataFrame(...) entity_id_dict = {'customer_id': 'INTEGER'} transformed_df, table_name = prepare_feature_ingestion_tdstone2(input_df, entity_id_dict)

def list_features()

Retrieves and returns a list of features from a feature store as a DataFrame.

This function constructs a SQL query to fetch all features from a specific catalog view within a schema. The query uses the schema and catalog name configured in the tdfs4ds module. The function executes the query and returns the result as a DataFrame.

Returns: tdml.DataFrame: A DataFrame containing the list of features from the feature store.

Notes: - The function relies on the tdfs4ds module for configuration, including the SCHEMA and FEATURE_CATALOG_NAME_VIEW settings. - The tdml.DataFrame is used to execute the query and retrieve the data. - This function does not include temporal logic or filtering. It assumes the default behavior of querying the current valid state of the feature catalog.

docs.title: get_feature_store_table_name
docs.text:
def get_feature_store_table_name(entity_id,
                                 feature_type,
                                 data_domain=None,
                                 primary_index=None,
                                 partitioning='')

Constructs names for a database table and a corresponding view within a feature store, using the provided entity attributes, feature type, optional data domain, primary index, and partitioning information.

This function generates names that are intended for organizing and accessing features in a structured manner within a feature store. The naming convention integrates various elements, including a prefix (indicating tables or views), the specified data domain (or a default one), and aspects of the entity ID, ensuring uniqueness and clarity in the schema.

Parameters: - entity_id (dict, list, str, int): The identifier(s) for the entity. This can be: - a dictionary with keys representing entity attributes, - a list of attributes, or - a single attribute (string or integer). The function constructs the base part of the table and view names differently based on the type of entity_id. - feature_type (str): Specifies the type of feature, contributing to the naming to indicate the kind of data stored. - data_domain (str, optional): The categorization under which the table and view will be organized. If not provided, a default value from tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN is used. - primary_index (list, optional): Specifies the primary index attributes. If not provided, it defaults to the attributes derived from entity_id. This influences the order and inclusion of entity attributes in the name. - partitioning (str, optional): A string containing attributes used for partitioning, separated by spaces or commas. This affects how the name indicates the data partitioning strategy.

Returns: - tuple: Contains two strings; the first is the table name, and the second is the view name. These are constructed based on the input parameters to fit within a feature store's schema.

Example: - For entity_id={'customer_id': 123}, feature_type='purchase_history', data_domain='retail', primary_index=['customer_id'], and partitioning='year, month', this function would generate names like 'FS_T_retail_CUSTOMER_ID__YEAR_MONTH__PURCHASE_HISTORY' and 'FS_V_retail_CUSTOMER_ID__YEAR_MONTH__PURCHASE_HISTORY'.

Note: - Assumes the presence of a tdfs4ds module with a DATA_DOMAIN constant for default data domain usage. - Designed for flexibility in entity ID specification, supporting simple IDs, attribute lists, or attribute key dictionaries.

docs.title: get_available_features
docs.text:
def get_available_features(entity_id, display_details=False)

Retrieves the names of available features for a given entity ID from a feature store.

This function constructs and executes a SQL query to select feature names from a specific catalog within a schema for a given entity ID, optionally using a specific valid time if set in tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME. The function supports querying features as of a certain timestamp or the current valid time if no timestamp is specified. It also provides an option to display detailed information about the features.

Parameters: entity_id (str, list, or dict): The ID of the entity for which to retrieve available features. This can be a single ID (str), a list of IDs, or a dictionary of IDs where keys are entity names and values are their corresponding IDs. display_details (bool, optional): If True, prints detailed information about all features in the catalog. Defaults to False.

Returns: list: A list of feature names available for the specified entity ID.

Note: - tdfs4ds is assumed to be a configuration module or object containing settings for the feature store, including FEATURE_STORE_TIME, SCHEMA, FEATURE_CATALOG_NAME, DATA_DOMAIN, and DEBUG_MODE. - The entity ID(s) are used to filter the features specific to the entity. If entity_id is a dictionary, the keys are considered as entity names, and their values are IDs. If it's a list, it's assumed to be a list of entity names or IDs. For a single string, it is treated as a single entity name or ID. - FEATURE_STORE_TIME is used to determine the temporal context for querying the feature data. If it is None, the query defaults to the current valid time. - If DEBUG_MODE is enabled in tdfs4ds, the constructed query is printed to the console. - The function uses tdml.DataFrame.from_query to execute the SQL query and to_pandas to convert the result into a pandas DataFrame for further processing.

docs.title: get_feature_versions
docs.text:
def get_feature_versions(entity_name, features, domain=None)

Retrieve version identifiers for one or more features belonging to a given entity.

The function queries the underlying metadata tables to find the process (i.e., featureΓÇæversion) records that match the supplied entity and feature names. It returns a mapping from each requested feature name to either:

  • None ΓÇô if no matching rows were found.
  • A single UUID string ΓÇô if exactly one matching row exists for the feature.
  • A list of dictionaries ΓÇô if more than one matching row is found; each dictionary contains: process_id ΓÇô the UUID of the process that produced the version, process_view_name ΓÇô the humanΓÇæreadable view name associated with that process.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
entity_name : str | list[str] The name (or names) of the entity whose features we are querying. If a single string is supplied it is treated as a singleton list.

features : str | list[str] One or more feature names to look up. Accepts a single string or an iterable of strings; if a single string is provided it is wrapped in a list internally.

domain : str, optional The dataΓÇædomain partition to filter on. If omitted the default tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN constant is used.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, str | None | list[dict]] A dictionary keyed by feature name. Each value is either: * None ΓÇô no records were found for that feature. * str ΓÇô a single UUID string when exactly one row matched. * list[dict] ΓÇô multiple matches; each dict has keys process_id and process_view_name.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The query joins the feature catalog view with the process catalog (specifically the ΓÇ£feature splitΓÇ¥ view) on data domain, entity ID, and feature name.
  • SQL string literals are escaped by doubling single quotes; this is a lightweight escape that suffices for the current useΓÇæcase.
  • The function preserves insertion order of features in the returned dictionary (Python 3.7+ guarantees dict order).
  • When tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE is true, the generated SQL statement is printed to stdout ΓÇô useful for troubleshooting.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: get_feature_versions('user', ['age', 'income'])

docs.title: get_entity_tables
docs.text:
def get_entity_tables(entity_id, data_domain=None)

Retrieves a list of table names associated with a given entity ID or IDs from a feature catalog within a specific data domain.

This function constructs and executes an SQL query to select distinct table names from a feature catalog that match the given entity ID(s) and data domain. If the data domain is not specified, it defaults to the system's default data domain. The entity ID can be a single string or a list of strings. If DEBUG_MODE is enabled in the system, the function prints the SQL query before execution.

Parameters: - entity_id (str or list of str): The ID or IDs of the entities for which to retrieve table names. - data_domain (str, optional): The data domain within which to search for the entity IDs. Defaults to the system's default data domain if None.

Returns: - list of str: A list of unique table names (in the format 'FEATURE_DATABASE.FEATURE_TABLE') associated with the given entity ID(s) within the specified data domain.

Exceptions: - If the SQL query execution fails, the function attempts to retrieve the table names by converting the query results into a pandas DataFrame and then extracting the table names from it.

Note: - This function depends on the external modules tdfs4ds and tdml for database schema details, default configurations, and SQL execution.

docs.title: get_feature_store_content
docs.text:
def get_feature_store_content(entity_id,
                              data_domain=None,
                              convert_to_date=False,
                              time_manager=None,
                              condition='<=')

Retrieves content from a feature store for a given entity ID or IDs within a specified data domain, with options to convert datetime columns to dates, filter data based on a time management table, and apply a specific condition for the filter.

This function first retrieves a list of table names associated with the given entity ID(s) in the specified data domain using the get_entity_tables function. It then constructs SQL queries to select distinct records from these tables, focusing on the 'ValidStart' column and the entity ID(s). If convert_to_date is True, it converts 'ValidStart' datetime values to date values in the queries. Additionally, if a time_manager object is provided, the function filters records in each table to include only those where 'ValidStart' meets the specified condition (e.g., before, on, or after) relative to the business date specified in the time_manager's table. The condition for comparison is specified by the condition parameter.

Parameters: - entity_id (str or list of str): The ID or IDs of the entities for which to retrieve content. - data_domain (str, optional): The data domain within which to search for the entity IDs. Defaults to the system's default data domain if None. - convert_to_date (bool, optional): Flag indicating whether to convert 'ValidStart' datetime values to date values. Defaults to False. - time_manager (object, optional): An object that specifies the schema and table name of a time management table to filter records based on business date. The object should have schema_name and table_name attributes, as well as a data_type indicating the type of data (e.g., date, datetime). - condition (str, optional): A string specifying the condition to apply to the 'ValidStart' column when filtering records based on the business date from the time management table. Defaults to '<='.

Returns: - DataFrame: A DataFrame containing the retrieved content, with the 'ValidStart' column and the entity ID(s) from each table in the feature store that matches the criteria, optionally filtered by business date and specific condition.

Note: - If DEBUG_MODE is enabled in the system, the function prints the constructed SQL query before execution. - This function leverages the tdml module for executing the SQL query and returning the results as a DataFrame. - The function adapts its behavior based on the presence and specifications of the time_manager object and the condition parameter to provide flexible data retrieval options.

docs.title: write_where_clause_filter
docs.text:
def write_where_clause_filter(filtermanager,
                              filter_conditions,
                              list_entity_id,
                              filter_alias='FILTER',
                              table_alias='A')

Constructs a SQL WHERE clause based on filter criteria.

This function generates a SQL WHERE clause for filtering records in a database. The filter_conditions parameter is a dictionary containing key-value pairs, where each key corresponds to a column name in filtermanager.col_names, and each value is an entity ID or filter value. The clause is constructed using aliases for the table and filter provided through filter_alias and table_alias.

Parameters: filtermanager (object): An object that contains a col_names attribute, which is a list of valid column names used for validation against the keys in the filter dictionary. filter_conditions (dict): A dictionary where keys represent column names that must match filtermanager.col_names, and values represent entity IDs or specific filter values. list_entity_id (list): A list of entity IDs used to validate or restrict values in the filter dictionary. filter_alias (str, optional): The alias used for the filter table in the SQL query. Defaults to 'FILTER'. table_alias (str, optional): The alias used for the main table in the SQL query. Defaults to 'A'.

Returns: str: A SQL WHERE clause string that joins each column and entity ID in filter using the format <filter_alias>.<column> = <table_alias>.<value> with 'AND' operators.

Raises: ValueError: If any key in filter is not found in filtermanager.col_names or if any value in filter is not in list_entity_id.

Example: >>> filtermanager.col_names = ['column1', 'column2'] >>> filter_conditions = {'column1': 'entity_id1', 'column2': 'entity_id2'} >>> list_entity_id = ['entity_id1', 'entity_id2'] >>> write_where_clause_filter(filtermanager, filter_conditions, list_entity_id) 'FILTER.column1=A.entity_id1 AND FILTER.column2=A.entity_id2'

docs.title: feature_store_catalog_creation
docs.text:
def feature_store_catalog_creation(if_exists='replace',
                                   comment='this table is a feature catalog')

Creates or replaces a feature store catalog table in a Teradata database. The catalog table stores metadata about features such as their names, associated tables and databases, validity periods, and more.

Parameters: - if_exists (str, optional): Specifies the action to be taken if the catalog table already exists. Options are 'replace' (default) and 'fail'. 'replace' drops the existing table and creates a new one, while 'fail' will raise an exception if the table already exists. - comment (str, optional): A comment to be associated with the catalog table, describing its purpose or other information. The default comment is 'this table is a feature catalog'.

Returns: str: The name of the created or replaced catalog table.

Note: - The function uses default values for the schema and table name from the tdfs4ds module. - It creates the catalog table with a primary index on 'FEATURE_ID' and a secondary index on 'FEATURE_NAME'. - The function also handles the creation of the table's comment. - In case of an existing table and 'if_exists' set to 'replace', it will drop the existing table before creating the new one. - The function assumes access to the database and appropriate permissions to create or modify tables.

Example Usage:

catalog_table_name = feature_store_catalog_creation() print(f"Feature catalog table {catalog_table_name} created successfully.")
