blank = none) TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN TDFS4DS_DISPLAY_LOGS tdfs4ds.DISPLAY_LOGS TDFS4DS_DEBUG_MODE tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE TDFS4DS_STORE_FEATURE tdfs4ds.STORE_FEATURE TDFS4DS_VARCHAR_SIZE tdfs4ds.VARCHAR_SIZE TDFS4DS_FEATURE_STORE_TIME tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_API_KEY tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_API_KEY TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM TDFS4DS_CHROMA_MODE tdfs4ds.CHROMA_MODE TDFS4DS_CHROMA_PATH tdfs4ds.CHROMA_PATH TDFS4DS_CHROMA_HOST tdfs4ds.CHROMA_HOST TDFS4DS_CHROMA_PORT tdfs4ds.CHROMA_PORT TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND ('chroma'|'teradata') TDFS4DS_TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX tdfs4ds.TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX TDFS4DS_TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE TDFS4DS_TERADATA_AI_MODEL_NAME tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_MODEL_NAME TDFS4DS_TERADATA_AI_REGION tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_REGION TDFS4DS_TERADATA_AI_API_BASE tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_BASE TDFS4DS_TERADATA_AI_ACCESS_KEY tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_ACCESS_KEY (credential — .env / env var only) TDFS4DS_QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES TDFS4DS_QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_FAILURES tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_FAILURES TDFS4DS_QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_REFINE_ROUNDS tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_REFINE_ROUNDS TDFS4DS_QUERY_OPTIMIZER_REPORT_DIR tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_REPORT_DIR

docs.title: Type coercion
docs.text:
Values are parsed at import time:

  • true / false / 1 / 0 → bool for DISPLAY_LOGS, DEBUG_MODE, etc.
  • Numeric strings → int for VARCHAR_SIZE, EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM, CHROMA_PORT, …
  • Everything else → str

None is encoded as an empty value.

docs.title: CI/CD recipe
docs.text:
# GitHub Actions
env:
  TDFS4DS_SCHEMA: ${{ vars.TDFS4DS_SCHEMA }}
  TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN: ${{ vars.TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN }}
  TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER: openai
  TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}

docs.title: JSON config file
docs.text: tdfs4ds.json is the preferred place to keep non-secret project defaults so every notebook in the same repo starts from the same configuration.

docs.title: Search order
docs.text:
1. ./tdfs4ds.json (current working directory)
  2. ~/.tdfs4ds/config.json (user-wide)

Override with load_config(path='/path/to/feature_store.json').

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
{
    "schema": "MY_DATABASE",
    "data_domain": "MY_PROJECT",
    "display_logs": true,
    "store_feature": "MERGE",
    "varchar_size": 1024,
    "instruct_model_provider": "openai",
    "instruct_model_model": "gpt-4o",
    "instruct_model_url": null,
    "embedding_model_provider": "vllm",
    "embedding_model_model": "text-embedding-3-small",
    "embedding_model_dim": 1536,
    "chroma_mode": "local",
    "chroma_path": "./tdfs4ds_chroma"
}

Keys are case-insensitive. Both snake_case and SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE work.

docs.title: What you cannot put here
docs.text: instruct_model_api_key, embedding_model_api_key, and any other secret keys are rejected from JSON config to prevent accidental commits. Use a .env file or an OS environment variable instead.

docs.title: Reloading
docs.text: tdfs4ds.load_config(path='./tdfs4ds.json')

docs.title: LLM & embedding models
docs.text:
tdfs4ds decouples three model concerns:

Setting block Used by INSTRUCT_MODEL_* Process documentation, dataset documentation, intent classification, answer synthesis, query optimizer EMBEDDING_MODEL_* Consumer agent vector index — client-side embeddings (Chroma backend; default for the Teradata backend) CHROMA_* Where the Chroma vector store persists VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND 'chroma' (default) or 'teradata' — selects where the vector index lives TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_* Naming for the Teradata Enterprise Vector Store collections TERADATA_AI_* Opt-in in-database embeddings via teradatagenai.TeradataAI (Teradata backend only)

If EMBEDDING_MODEL_* is not set, it falls back to INSTRUCT_MODEL_*.

docs.title: Supported providers
docs.text:
Provider INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER Notes OpenAI 'openai' INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL optional Azure OpenAI 'azure' INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL required (deployment endpoint) vLLM / TGI 'vllm' OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoint AWS Bedrock 'bedrock' No URL / API key — uses boto3 default credentials

The same provider keys apply to embeddings via EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER.

docs.title: OpenAI-compatible example
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'openai'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = 'gpt-4o'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY  = os.getenv('OPENAI_API_KEY')

tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'openai'
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL    = 'text-embedding-3-small'
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM      = 1536

docs.title: vLLM behind LiteLLM example
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'vllm'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL      = 'https://api.example.com/v1'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY  = os.getenv('FS_API_KEY')
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = 'mistral-7b-instruct'

tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'vllm'
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL      = 'https://api.example.com/v1/e5'
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_API_KEY  = os.getenv('FS_API_KEY')
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL    = 'bge-m3'
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM      = 1024

docs.title: AWS Bedrock example
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'bedrock'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = 'anthropic.claude-3-sonnet-20240229-v1:0'
# Authentication: boto3 default chain (env vars, ~/.aws/credentials, IAM role)

docs.title: Listing available models
docs.text:
When the endpoint exposes /v1/models (OpenAI-compatible), discover models without leaving Python:

from tdfs4ds.agent import list_instruct_models, list_embedding_models

list_instruct_models()
list_embedding_models(sub_paths=['e5', 'code'])

docs.title: Vector store backend
docs.text:
The consumer agent's vector index is backend-agnostic. Pick where it lives via VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND:

tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND = 'chroma'     # default — local or remote ChromaDB
# or
tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND = 'teradata'   # Teradata Enterprise Vector Store

build_vector_index() and search_vector_index() dispatch on this setting; all callers (chatbot, search/definition skills) are unchanged.

docs.title: Chroma backend
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_MODE = 'local'                # persist to disk
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_PATH = './tdfs4ds_chroma'

# OR

tdfs4ds.CHROMA_MODE = 'server'               # connect to a Chroma server
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_HOST = 'localhost'
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_PORT = 8000

The Chroma backend holds three collections (objects, sections, columns) populated by build_vector_index() from the business dictionary. Embeddings come from the EMBEDDING_MODEL_* (or INSTRUCT_MODEL_*) configuration.

docs.title: Teradata backend
docs.text:
The Teradata Enterprise Vector Store persists the same three collections directly in Vantage as named vector stores:

tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND         = 'teradata'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX = 'tdfs4ds'   # → tdfs4ds_objects / _sections / _columns

By default the Teradata backend reuses the LangChain EMBEDDING_MODEL_* setup — your existing vLLM / TEI / LiteLLM / OpenAI configuration keeps working unchanged because TeradataVectorStore accepts a LangChain Embeddings object at its embedding= argument.

To opt into in-database embeddings via teradatagenai.TeradataAI, set TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE:

# Example: AWS Bedrock embeddings produced inside Vantage
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE   = 'aws'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_MODEL_NAME = 'amazon.titan-embed-text-v2:0'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_REGION     = 'us-west-2'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_ACCESS_KEY = os.getenv('AWS_ACCESS_KEY')  # or via .env

# Example: NIM / OpenAI-compatible in-database client
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE   = 'nim'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_MODEL_NAME = 'nvidia/nv-embedqa-e5-v5'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_BASE   = 'https://nim.example.com/v1'

Supported TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE values: aws, azure, gcp, nim, hugging_face, onnx. azure / gcp / hugging_face rely on the standard provider env vars (e.g. AZURE_OPENAI_*, GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS).

Incremental updates

The Enterprise Vector Store API does not yet guarantee per-id upsert, so a non-force_rebuild build will try add_documents and fall back to a destroy + rebuild if it is not available.

docs.title: Performance knobs
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_TIMEOUT     = 60       # seconds per LLM call
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MAX_RETRIES = 3        # transient-error retries

For very small-context models (≤ 16 k tokens), cap the candidate generation length so the query optimizer does not exhaust its output budget on verbose reasoning text:

tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATE_TOKENS = 4096

docs.title: Configuration overview
docs.text:
Every tdfs4ds setting can be supplied through four channels, evaluated in this priority order:

1. Programmatic           (tdfs4ds.X = value)
2. OS environment variable (TDFS4DS_X=...)
3. .env file              (./.env  or  ~/.tdfs4ds/.env)
4. JSON config file       (./tdfs4ds.json  or  ~/.tdfs4ds/config.json)
5. teradataml auto-detect (SCHEMA only)
6. built-in defaults

A lower-priority source never overrides a higher-priority one. Reload at any time with:

tdfs4ds.load_config()
tdfs4ds.load_config(path='/configs/feature_store.json', dotenv_path='/etc/.env.prod')

docs.title: Channels in depth
docs.text:
• Programmatic globals
  • .env file (local secrets)
  • JSON config file
  • Environment variables
  • LLM & embedding models

docs.title: Sensitive values
docs.text: API keys are accepted only through .env files or OS environment variables — never from the JSON file. tdfs4ds.config.load_config rejects the instruct_model_api_key key in JSON to prevent accidental commits.

docs.title: Programmatic globals
docs.text: The most direct way to configure tdfs4ds is to assign module globals after import. Programmatic assignment always wins over .env, JSON, and OS env vars.

docs.title: Core settings
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.SCHEMA                = 'my_database'        # target database for TABLES (auto-set from context)
tdfs4ds.SCHEMA_VIEW           = None                 # optional database for VIEWS (None = same as SCHEMA); resolve via tdfs4ds.view_schema()
tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME    = None                 # None = current; '2024-01-01 00:00:00' = time travel
tdfs4ds.DISPLAY_LOGS          = True                 # verbose logging gate
tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE            = False
tdfs4ds.STORE_FEATURE         = 'MERGE'              # 'MERGE' or 'UPDATE_INSERT'
tdfs4ds.VARCHAR_SIZE          = 1024                 # default VARCHAR width for new columns
tdfs4ds.USE_VOLATILE_TABLE    = True                 # use volatile staging tables during ingestion
tdfs4ds.BUILD_DATASET_AT_UPLOAD = False              # auto-build dataset view inside upload_features

Use tdfs4ds.create_data_domain('NAME') (or select_data_domain) instead of setting tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN directly — the helpers validate the domain exists and emit a log.

docs.title: GenAI / LLM
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'openai'           # 'openai' | 'azure' | 'vllm' | 'bedrock'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = 'gpt-4o'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL      = None                # required for 'vllm' / 'azure'
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY  = 'sk-...'           # prefer .env / env var
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_TIMEOUT     = 60
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MAX_RETRIES = 3

docs.title: Embeddings + vector store
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'vllm'             # falls back to INSTRUCT_MODEL_* when None
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL      = 'https://api.example.com/v1/e5'
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_API_KEY  = None
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL    = 'text-embedding-3-small'
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM      = 1536

# Vector store backend
tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND         = 'chroma'        # 'chroma' (default) | 'teradata'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX = 'tdfs4ds'       # base name for the 3 Teradata stores

# Chroma (when VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND='chroma')
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_MODE = 'local'                          # 'local' or 'server'
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_PATH = './tdfs4ds_chroma'               # persist directory (local)
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_HOST = 'localhost'                      # server mode
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_PORT = 8000                             # server mode

# TeradataAI in-database embedding — opt-in when VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND='teradata'.
# Leave TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE = None to keep using the LangChain EMBEDDING_MODEL_*
# settings above (vLLM / TEI / OpenAI-compatible work unchanged).
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE   = None                  # 'aws'|'azure'|'gcp'|'nim'|'hugging_face'|'onnx'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_MODEL_NAME = None
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_REGION     = None                  # aws
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_BASE   = None                  # nim
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_ACCESS_KEY = None                  # credential (prefer .env / env var)

See LLM & embedding models for full examples of both backends.

docs.title: Query optimizer
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES       = 10
tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_FAILURES         = 10
tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_REFINE_ROUNDS    = 3
tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATE_TOKENS = None    # cap output tokens for small-context models
tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_REPORT_DIR           = 'tdfs4ds_optim_reports'

Reports are stored under {REPORT_DIR}/{DATA_DOMAIN}/tdfs4ds_optim_<view>_<process_id>.md.

docs.title: Catalog names
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.FEATURE_CATALOG_NAME      = 'FS_FEATURE_CATALOG'
tdfs4ds.PROCESS_CATALOG_NAME      = 'FS_PROCESS_CATALOG'
tdfs4ds.DATASET_CATALOG_NAME      = 'FS_DATASET'
tdfs4ds.DATA_DISTRIBUTION_NAME    = 'FS_DATA_DISTRIBUTION'
tdfs4ds.FOLLOW_UP_NAME            = 'FS_FOLLOW_UP'
tdfs4ds.FILTER_MANAGER_NAME       = 'FS_FILTER_MANAGER'

Set these before the first setup() call. Renaming after deployment is not supported.

docs.title: Inspecting current values
docs.text: [v for v in dir(tdfs4ds) if v.isupper()]

docs.title: OpenAI-compatible API server
docs.text:
tdfs4ds can expose its two conversational agents over HTTP as an OpenAI-compatible API, so any OpenAI client (the openai Python SDK, Open WebUI, LibreChat, …) can talk to them. A LangServe playground is mounted alongside for interactive debugging.

The server publishes the two agents as two OpenAI models:

Model id Agent Purpose tdfs4ds-consumer consumer_agent Business-user Q&A over the feature store / business dictionary. tdfs4ds-query-optimizer query_optimizer_agent EXPLAIN / simplify / optimize a Teradata SQL query.

The OpenAI model field of each request selects the agent.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Choose how you want to run the server:

Docker (recommended)Run locally (Python)

Only Docker is required on the host. No Python, no pip install.

The image bundles everything — the package, all dependencies, and the server entry point. All you need is the repo checkout (for the Dockerfile and .env.example) and a working Docker installation.

# Verify Docker is available
docker --version

Install the [serve] extra, which adds FastAPI, uvicorn, and LangServe on top of the core package:

pip install "tdfs4ds[serve]"

This also installs pyvis (lineage graph), gradio (chatbot UI), and langserve (playground routes). These are kept as an optional extra so the base pip install tdfs4ds stays lean for notebook/script use.

docs.title: Configure
docs.text:
All configuration is supplied through TDFS4DS_* environment variables (or a .env file). Copy .env.example and fill it in:

# Teradata connection (read by the server bootstrap)
TDFS4DS_TD_HOST=your-vantage-host
TDFS4DS_TD_USERNAME=your_user
TDFS4DS_TD_PASSWORD=your_password
# TDFS4DS_TD_LOGMECH=LDAP        # optional
# TDFS4DS_TD_DATABASE=your_db    # optional
# TDFS4DS_TD_ENCRYPT=true        # optional wire encryption ('true' or 'false')

# Feature store scope
TDFS4DS_SCHEMA=your_feature_store_db
# TDFS4DS_SCHEMA_VIEW=your_views_db   # optional: separate database for views (blank = same as SCHEMA)
TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN=your_project

# Instruct (LLM) model
TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER=openai
TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL=gpt-4o
TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY=sk-...

# Embedding model (needed for the consumer agent's search)
TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER=openai
TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL=text-embedding-3-small
TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_API_KEY=sk-...
TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM=1536

# Vector store + server behaviour
TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=chroma
TDFS4DS_CHROMA_PATH=/data/chroma
TDFS4DS_SERVE_PORT=8000
TDFS4DS_SERVE_BUILD_INDEX=true     # set false for a query-optimizer-only deployment

# Vector Store authentication (only needed for VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=teradata)
# TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_BASE_URL=https://your-vantage-vs-endpoint
# TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_PAT_TOKEN=...      # PAT (tried first)
# TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_PEM_FILE=/path/to/key.pem
# TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_USERNAME=your_user  # optional for PAT, required for Basic
# TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_PASSWORD=...        # Basic (fallback)
# TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_CLIENT_ID=...       # OAuth (fallback)

Teradata connection vs. feature-store config

The TDFS4DS_TD_* group (HOST, USERNAME, PASSWORD, and optionally LOGMECH, DATABASE, ENCRYPT) and the TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_* group are both read directly by the server's startup bootstrap — the former to open the teradataml context, the latter to authenticate against the Vantage Vector Store REST API (only relevant when VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=teradata; see Vector Store authentication). Everything else is loaded by tdfs4ds itself — see Environment variables and .env file for the full list and precedence rules.

On startup the server opens the Teradata context, applies the TDFS4DS_* config, and (unless TDFS4DS_SERVE_BUILD_INDEX=false) builds the consumer agent's vector index so the first search does not pay a cold build.

docs.title: Run locally
docs.text:
python -m tdfs4ds.serve

The server listens on 0.0.0.0:8000 (override with TDFS4DS_SERVE_PORT). The admin web UI and the embedded chatbot are served on the same port as the API; a standalone chatbot and the docs server (both default enabled) start on their own ports.

Port Service URL 8000 Admin web UI (Teradata-styled shell) http://localhost:8000/ 8000 Chatbot (embedded Gradio iframe) http://localhost:8000/chatbot 8000 API (OpenAI /v1 + LangServe + /reports) http://localhost:8000/v1 8000 Lineage graph http://localhost:8000/lineage 7860 Standalone chatbot (Gradio, backward-compat) http://localhost:7860 8080 Documentation (static HTML) http://localhost:8080

The admin UI at / reproduces the Teradata "Tera" console (left-nav rail with Chatbot, Dashboard, Lineage, Query optimization, Evaluation, Parameters and Skills panels); the Chatbot entry embeds the Gradio chat at /chatbot. Toggle the standalone chatbot with TDFS4DS_SERVE_CHATBOT (default true, TDFS4DS_SERVE_CHATBOT_PORT default 7860) and the docs server with TDFS4DS_SERVE_DOCS (default true).

docs.title: Behind a reverse-proxy path prefix
docs.text:
To serve the whole UI + API under a sub-path (e.g. https://api-dmproject.myddns.me/tdfs4ds2), set TDFS4DS_SERVE_ROOT_PATH to the prefix:

TDFS4DS_SERVE_ROOT_PATH=/tdfs4ds2 python -m tdfs4ds.serve

create_app installs a lightweight ASGI middleware that strips the prefix from inbound paths and stashes the external base on each request, so the prefix-less shell / /ui/* / /v1/* routes match whether or not the reverse proxy already stripped it. The shell injects <base href="{prefix}/"> so all assets, fetch() calls and the /chatbot iframe resolve under the prefix. Gradio is mounted at the full prefixed path ({prefix}/chatbot) so its internal routing stays consistent. Configure your reverse proxy to forward the prefix unchanged (e.g. nginx location /tdfs4ds2/ { proxy_pass http://app:8000/tdfs4ds2/; }). Leave TDFS4DS_SERVE_ROOT_PATH blank to serve at the root.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text:
# 1. Clone the repo (skip if you already have it)
git clone https://github.com/denismolin/tdfs4ds.git
cd tdfs4ds

# 2. Create your .env from the template and fill in your values
cp .env.example .env

# 3. Build the image and start all services
docker compose up --build

That's it. docker compose up --build copies the working tree into the image, installs tdfs4ds[serve] and all dependencies inside it, and starts the app on port 8000 (admin UI + chatbot + API), the standalone chatbot on 7860, and the docs server on 8080. No Python or pip install needed on the host.

docs.title: Manual docker run (without Compose)
docs.text:
docker build -t tdfs4ds-serve .
docker run --env-file .env \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  -p 7860:7860 \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -v ./docs:/app/docs:ro \
  -v tdfs4ds_chroma:/data \
  tdfs4ds-serve

The /data volume persists the Chroma vector index across restarts. The ./docs bind mount allows mkdocs build on your host to update the documentation server without restarting the container.

Toggle the standalone chatbot and docs server with the TDFS4DS_SERVE_CHATBOT and TDFS4DS_SERVE_DOCS environment variables (both default true).

docs.title: Build and push to Docker Hub
docs.text:
The repo ships two Dockerfiles:

File Source Use Dockerfile the working tree (COPY . /app) local development / unreleased changes Dockerfile.pypi the published wheel (pip install) building a release image to publish

Dockerfile.pypi installs the released package straight from PyPI, so no repo checkout is needed and the build is reproducible from the version tag alone:

FROM python:3.11-slim
RUN apt-get update \
    && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends build-essential \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir "tdfs4ds[serve]"   # or pin: "tdfs4ds[serve]==0.3.1.108"
ENV TDFS4DS_CHROMA_PATH=/data/chroma
ENV TDFS4DS_SERVE_PORT=8000
EXPOSE 8000 7860 8080
VOLUME ["/data"]
CMD ["python", "-m", "tdfs4ds.serve"]

Build, tag, and push it to your Docker Hub account (replace you with your Docker Hub username and use the tdfs4ds version as the tag):

# 1. Build from the PyPI-based Dockerfile, tagging with the package version.
docker build -f Dockerfile.pypi -t you/tdfs4ds-serve:0.3.1.81 .
docker tag you/tdfs4ds-serve:0.3.1.81 you/tdfs4ds-serve:latest

# 2. Authenticate (once per machine; use a Docker Hub access token, not your password).
docker login -u you

# 3. Push both tags.
docker push you/tdfs4ds-serve:0.3.1.81
docker push you/tdfs4ds-serve:latest

Build for the server's architecture

If your dev machine is ARM (Apple Silicon) and your server is x86-64, build a multi-arch image with buildx so the server can pull a matching layer — this also pushes in one step:

docker buildx build -f Dockerfile.pypi \
  --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
  -t you/tdfs4ds-serve:0.3.1.81 -t you/tdfs4ds-serve:latest \
  --push .

On the target host, pull and run it with that host's .env:

docker pull you/tdfs4ds-serve:0.3.1.108
docker run --env-file .env -p 8000:8000 \
  -v tdfs4ds_chroma:/data you/tdfs4ds-serve:0.3.1.108

The image carries no secrets — every credential and connection setting is supplied at run time through --env-file / TDFS4DS_* variables — so the same image is safe to publish as a public or private Docker Hub repository.

docs.title: Deploy to the cloud
docs.text:
The image carries no secrets — credentials are always passed at run time via environment variables — so the same image works on any container platform. The steps below use the Docker Hub image (you/tdfs4ds-serve:latest) built and pushed in the previous section. Replace you with your registry path.

Chroma is for development only — use the Teradata vector store in production

The default TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=chroma stores the vector index as a local directory inside the container (/data/chroma). This is convenient for local development and demos but not suitable for production cloud deployments:

  • The index is lost every time the container restarts unless you mount a persistent volume.
  • Local Chroma cannot be shared across container replicas.

For production, switch to the Teradata backend:

TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=teradata

This stores the vector index inside Teradata Vantage — the same system you already have, fully persistent, accessible from any replica, and requiring no extra cloud storage. See Configuration › LLM & embeddings for the TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX and TERADATA_AI_* variables.

Secrets management

Avoid putting .env files on cloud hosts. Each platform has a native secrets store — the commands below show how to wire them in.

AWS — ECS FargateGCP — Cloud RunAzure — Container Apps

Services used: ECR (image registry), ECS Fargate (serverless container runtime), Secrets Manager (credentials), ALB (HTTPS termination).

Services used: Artifact Registry (image registry), Cloud Run (serverless container runtime), Secret Manager (credentials).

Services used: Azure Container Registry (ACR), Azure Container Apps (ACA), Key Vault (secrets).

docs.title: 1 — Push the image to ECR
docs.text:
# Create a private ECR repository (once)
aws ecr create-repository --repository-name tdfs4ds-serve --region eu-west-1

# Log Docker into ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region eu-west-1 \
  | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin \
      123456789012.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com

# Tag and push
docker tag you/tdfs4ds-serve:latest \
  123456789012.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/tdfs4ds-serve:latest
docker push \
  123456789012.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/tdfs4ds-serve:latest

docs.title: 2 — Store secrets in Secrets Manager
docs.text:
aws secretsmanager create-secret \
  --name tdfs4ds/env \
  --secret-string '{
    "TDFS4DS_TD_HOST":       "your-vantage-host",
    "TDFS4DS_TD_USERNAME":   "your_user",
    "TDFS4DS_TD_PASSWORD":   "your_password",
    "TDFS4DS_SCHEMA":        "your_feature_store_db",
    "TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN":   "your_project",
    "TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER": "bedrock",
    "TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL":    "anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0",
    "TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_REGION":   "eu-west-1",
    "TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER":"bedrock",
    "TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL":   "amazon.titan-embed-text-v2:0",
    "TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_REGION":  "eu-west-1",
    "TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND":    "teradata"
  }'

Set TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=teradata (recommended for production) — vectors are stored inside Vantage, no EFS mount needed.

docs.title: 3 — Create an ECS task definition
docs.text:
Save the JSON below as task-def.json, substituting your account ID, region, and secret ARN:

{
  "family": "tdfs4ds-serve",
  "networkMode": "awsvpc",
  "requiresCompatibilities": ["FARGATE"],
  "cpu": "1024",
  "memory": "2048",
  "executionRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/ecsTaskExecutionRole",
  "containerDefinitions": [{
    "name": "tdfs4ds-serve",
    "image": "123456789012.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/tdfs4ds-serve:latest",
    "portMappings": [{"containerPort": 8000, "protocol": "tcp"}],
    "secrets": [{
      "name": "TDFS4DS_TD_HOST",
      "valueFrom": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:eu-west-1:123456789012:secret:tdfs4ds/env:TDFS4DS_TD_HOST::"
    }],
    "logConfiguration": {
      "logDriver": "awslogs",
      "options": {
        "awslogs-group": "/ecs/tdfs4ds-serve",
        "awslogs-region": "eu-west-1",
        "awslogs-stream-prefix": "ecs"
      }
    }
  }]
}

Tip

ECS Fargate can also inject an entire Secrets Manager JSON blob as individual environment variables using the valueFrom: "arn:...:secret:tdfs4ds/env:::" wildcard form — one entry per key. This avoids repeating the ARN for every variable.

aws ecs register-task-definition --cli-input-json file://task-def.json

docs.title: 4 — Create the service
docs.text:
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster your-cluster \
  --service-name tdfs4ds-serve \
  --task-definition tdfs4ds-serve \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --desired-count 1 \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={
      subnets=[subnet-aaa,subnet-bbb],
      securityGroups=[sg-xxx],
      assignPublicIp=ENABLED}" \
  --load-balancers "targetGroupArn=arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:...,
      containerName=tdfs4ds-serve,containerPort=8000"

The ALB target group handles HTTPS termination; the container only speaks HTTP on port 8000.

docs.title: Connecting to Teradata
docs.text:
The container opens a JDBC/ODBC connection to TDFS4DS_TD_HOST on port 1025 (Teradata default). Fargate tasks run inside a VPC subnet, so Vantage must be reachable from that subnet.

Vantage location Connectivity approach AWS-hosted (e.g. ClearScape on EC2, Vantage on AWS) Place the ECS task and the Vantage host in the same VPC, or use VPC peering between the two VPCs. Add a security-group rule: inbound TCP 1025 from the ECS task's security group. On-premises Connect the VPC to your data-center via AWS Direct Connect or Site-to-Site VPN. The Fargate task then reaches Vantage over the private link. ClearScape Analytics Experience (SaaS trial) The host is internet-reachable. Set TDFS4DS_TD_ENCRYPT=true and allow outbound TCP 1025 from the task's security group to the ClearScape hostname.

Tip

Set TDFS4DS_TD_ENCRYPT=true whenever the connection crosses a network boundary you do not fully control (VPN, Direct Connect, public internet).

docs.title: Bedrock credentials
docs.text: With INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER=bedrock the SDK auto-discovers credentials from the task's IAM execution role — no API key needed. Attach the AmazonBedrockFullAccess managed policy (or a scoped equivalent) to the ECS task role and remove INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY from the secret.

docs.title: Required AWS permissions
docs.text:
Two principals need permissions: the deployer (the human or CI role running the commands above) and the ECS task (the runtime identity of the container).

Deployer IAM policy — attach to the human/CI role that runs steps 1–4:

{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "ECR",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ecr:CreateRepository",
        "ecr:GetAuthorizationToken",
        "ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability",
        "ecr:InitiateLayerUpload",
        "ecr:UploadLayerPart",
        "ecr:CompleteLayerUpload",
        "ecr:PutImage"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "SecretsManager",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "secretsmanager:CreateSecret",
        "secretsmanager:PutSecretValue",
        "secretsmanager:DescribeSecret"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:*:*:secret:tdfs4ds/*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "ECS",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition",
        "ecs:CreateService",
        "ecs:UpdateService",
        "ecs:DescribeServices",
        "ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition",
        "ecs:ListTaskDefinitions"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "IAMPassRole",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "iam:PassRole",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:iam::*:role/ecsTaskExecutionRole"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "CloudWatchLogs",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "logs:CreateLogGroup",
        "logs:DescribeLogGroups"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}

ECS task execution role (ecsTaskExecutionRole) — the standard AWS managed policy AmazonECSTaskExecutionRolePolicy covers ECR pull and CloudWatch Logs. Add one inline policy to let the task read secrets:

{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Sid": "ReadSecrets",
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": [
      "secretsmanager:GetSecretValue",
      "ssm:GetParameters"
    ],
    "Resource": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:*:*:secret:tdfs4ds/*"
  }]
}

ECS task role (the identity inside the container at runtime) — needed only when using Bedrock without an API key:

{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Sid": "Bedrock",
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": [
      "bedrock:InvokeModel",
      "bedrock:InvokeModelWithResponseStream"
    ],
    "Resource": "*"
  }]
}

Least privilege

Scope Resource to the specific model ARNs you use, e.g. arn:aws:bedrock:eu-west-1::foundation-model/anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0.

docs.title: 1 — Push the image to Artifact Registry
docs.text:
# Create a repository (once)
gcloud artifacts repositories create tdfs4ds \
  --repository-format docker \
  --location europe-west1

# Authenticate Docker
gcloud auth configure-docker europe-west1-docker.pkg.dev

# Tag and push
docker tag you/tdfs4ds-serve:latest \
  europe-west1-docker.pkg.dev/your-project/tdfs4ds/tdfs4ds-serve:latest
docker push \
  europe-west1-docker.pkg.dev/your-project/tdfs4ds/tdfs4ds-serve:latest

docs.title: 2 — Store secrets in Secret Manager
docs.text:
# Create each secret (repeat for every variable)
echo -n "your-vantage-host" \
  | gcloud secrets create TDFS4DS_TD_HOST --data-file=-

echo -n "your_password" \
  | gcloud secrets create TDFS4DS_TD_PASSWORD --data-file=-

# ... repeat for TD_USERNAME, SCHEMA, DATA_DOMAIN,
#     INSTRUCT_MODEL_*, EMBEDDING_MODEL_*, etc.

Set TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=teradata (recommended for production) — vectors are stored inside Vantage, no Filestore / Persistent Disk mount needed.

docs.title: 3 — Deploy to Cloud Run
docs.text:
gcloud run deploy tdfs4ds-serve \
  --image europe-west1-docker.pkg.dev/your-project/tdfs4ds/tdfs4ds-serve:latest \
  --region europe-west1 \
  --platform managed \
  --port 8000 \
  --memory 2Gi \
  --cpu 1 \
  --min-instances 1 \
  --no-allow-unauthenticated \
  --set-secrets \
      TDFS4DS_TD_HOST=TDFS4DS_TD_HOST:latest,\
      TDFS4DS_TD_USERNAME=TDFS4DS_TD_USERNAME:latest,\
      TDFS4DS_TD_PASSWORD=TDFS4DS_TD_PASSWORD:latest,\
      TDFS4DS_SCHEMA=TDFS4DS_SCHEMA:latest,\
      TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN=TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN:latest,\
      TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER=TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER:latest,\
      TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL=TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL:latest,\
      TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND:latest

--no-allow-unauthenticated restricts access to callers with a valid Google identity token — add --allow-unauthenticated only if the service is internet-facing and you control access another way (API gateway, VPC, etc.).

docs.title: Connecting to Teradata
docs.text:
Cloud Run tasks run on Google-managed infrastructure. Port 1025 must be reachable from that infrastructure to your Vantage system.

Vantage location Connectivity approach GCP-hosted (e.g. Vantage on GCP, Compute Engine) Use Direct VPC egress (--vpc-connector or --network / --subnet flags) to route Cloud Run traffic into your VPC. Add a firewall rule: allow TCP 1025 from the Cloud Run service account or subnet. On-premises Connect your GCP VPC to the data-center via Cloud Interconnect or Cloud VPN, then use Direct VPC egress. ClearScape Analytics Experience (SaaS trial) The host is internet-reachable. Cloud Run has outbound internet access by default. Set TDFS4DS_TD_ENCRYPT=true. 
# Example: enable Direct VPC egress for an existing Cloud Run service
gcloud run services update tdfs4ds-serve \
  --region europe-west1 \
  --network your-vpc-network \
  --subnet your-subnet \
  --vpc-egress all-traffic

docs.title: Vertex AI credentials
docs.text:
With INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER=gcp the SDK uses Application Default Credentials from the Cloud Run service account — no API key needed. Grant the service account the Vertex AI User IAM role and set:

TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_GCP_PROJECT=your-project
TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_GCP_LOCATION=europe-west1
TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL=gemini-2.0-flash-001

docs.title: Accessing the service
docs.text:
Cloud Run issues a https://<hash>-<region>.a.run.app URL. Callers must include a bearer token:

TOKEN=$(gcloud auth print-identity-token)
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  https://tdfs4ds-serve-<hash>-ew.a.run.app/v1/models

For service-to-service calls within GCP, use Workload Identity.

docs.title: Required GCP permissions
docs.text:
Two principals need roles: the deployer (human or CI service account running steps 1–3) and the Cloud Run service account (the runtime identity of the container).

Deployer — roles to grant at project level:

Role Purpose roles/artifactregistry.admin Create repository, push images roles/secretmanager.admin Create and version secrets roles/run.admin Deploy and update Cloud Run services roles/iam.serviceAccountUser Allow the deployer to act as the Cloud Run service account (--service-account) roles/compute.networkViewer Required when enabling VPC egress (read VPC/subnet details) 
# Grant all deployer roles to a CI service account
SA="deployer@your-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
for ROLE in \
    roles/artifactregistry.admin \
    roles/secretmanager.admin \
    roles/run.admin \
    roles/iam.serviceAccountUser \
    roles/compute.networkViewer; do
  gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding your-project \
    --member="serviceAccount:$SA" --role="$ROLE"
done

Cloud Run service account — the identity the container runs as at runtime. Create a dedicated one (recommended over using the default compute service account):

gcloud iam service-accounts create tdfs4ds-runtime \
  --display-name "tdfs4ds Cloud Run runtime"

Grant it the minimum roles it needs at runtime:

Role Purpose roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor Read the secrets injected as env vars roles/aiplatform.user Call Vertex AI (Gemini / embeddings) — only if using INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER=gcp roles/artifactregistry.reader Pull the container image from Artifact Registry 
RT="tdfs4ds-runtime@your-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
for ROLE in \
    roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor \
    roles/aiplatform.user \
    roles/artifactregistry.reader; do
  gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding your-project \
    --member="serviceAccount:$RT" --role="$ROLE"
done

Then pass --service-account=$RT to gcloud run deploy.

Least privilege

If you are not using Vertex AI (e.g. using an OpenAI-compatible endpoint instead), omit roles/aiplatform.user. If secrets are already set from a previous deploy, the deployer only needs roles/run.admin for updates.

docs.title: 1 — Push the image to ACR
docs.text:
# Create ACR (once)
az acr create --name youracr --resource-group your-rg \
  --sku Basic --admin-enabled true

# Log Docker in
az acr login --name youracr

# Tag and push
docker tag you/tdfs4ds-serve:latest \
  youracr.azurecr.io/tdfs4ds-serve:latest
docker push youracr.azurecr.io/tdfs4ds-serve:latest

docs.title: 2 — Store secrets in Key Vault
docs.text:
az keyvault create --name your-kv --resource-group your-rg \
  --location westeurope

az keyvault secret set --vault-name your-kv \
  --name TDFS4DS-TD-HOST     --value "your-vantage-host"
az keyvault secret set --vault-name your-kv \
  --name TDFS4DS-TD-PASSWORD --value "your_password"
# ... repeat for USERNAME, SCHEMA, DATA_DOMAIN, model config, etc.

docs.title: 3 — Create a Container Apps environment
docs.text:
az containerapp env create \
  --name tdfs4ds-env \
  --resource-group your-rg \
  --location westeurope

docs.title: 4 — Deploy the container app
docs.text:
Container Apps injects Key Vault secrets as environment variables via a managed identity. First create the app, then bind each secret:

az containerapp create \
  --name tdfs4ds-serve \
  --resource-group your-rg \
  --environment tdfs4ds-env \
  --image youracr.azurecr.io/tdfs4ds-serve:latest \
  --registry-server youracr.azurecr.io \
  --target-port 8000 \
  --ingress external \
  --min-replicas 1 \
  --max-replicas 3 \
  --cpu 1.0 --memory 2.0Gi \
  --secrets \
      td-host=keyvaultref:https://your-kv.vault.azure.net/secrets/TDFS4DS-TD-HOST,identityref:/subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/your-rg/providers/Microsoft.ManagedIdentity/userAssignedIdentities/your-mi \
      td-password=keyvaultref:https://your-kv.vault.azure.net/secrets/TDFS4DS-TD-PASSWORD,identityref:/subscriptions/... \
  --env-vars \
      TDFS4DS_TD_HOST=secretref:td-host \
      TDFS4DS_TD_PASSWORD=secretref:td-password \
      TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=teradata \
      TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER=azure \
      TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL=gpt-4o \
      TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL=https://your-instance.openai.azure.com/ \
      TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_AZURE_DEPLOYMENT=gpt-4o

docs.title: Connecting to Teradata
docs.text:
Container Apps run inside an ACA environment. Port 1025 must be reachable from that environment to your Vantage system.

Vantage location Connectivity approach Azure-hosted (e.g. Vantage on Azure, Azure VM) Deploy the Container Apps environment in a custom VNet (--infrastructure-subnet-resource-id). Add a network security group (NSG) rule: allow outbound TCP 1025 to the Vantage host or subnet. On-premises Connect the Azure VNet to your data-center via ExpressRoute or Azure VPN Gateway, then use the same custom-VNet Container Apps environment. ClearScape Analytics Experience (SaaS trial) The host is internet-reachable. Container Apps have outbound internet access by default. Set TDFS4DS_TD_ENCRYPT=true. 
# Create a VNet-integrated Container Apps environment
az containerapp env create \
  --name tdfs4ds-env \
  --resource-group your-rg \
  --location westeurope \
  --infrastructure-subnet-resource-id \
      /subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/your-rg/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/your-vnet/subnets/aca-subnet

docs.title: Azure OpenAI credentials
docs.text: With INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER=azure set TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY from Key Vault the same way as the Teradata password above. Alternatively, grant the container app's managed identity the Cognitive Services OpenAI User role on the Azure OpenAI resource — the SDK then uses managed identity and no key is needed.

docs.title: Required Azure permissions
docs.text:
Two principals need roles: the deployer (human or CI principal running steps 1–4) and the managed identity assigned to the container app at runtime.

Deployer — built-in roles to assign at resource-group scope:

Role Purpose Contributor Create and manage ACR, Container Apps environment, Container App, Key Vault AcrPush Push images to ACR (can be scoped to the ACR resource instead of the RG) Key Vault Secrets Officer Create Key Vault and write secrets User Access Administrator Assign roles to the managed identity (required for the Key Vault → Container App binding) 
DEPLOYER="<your-user-or-sp-object-id>"
RG="/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/your-rg"

az role assignment create --assignee $DEPLOYER \
  --role "Contributor"              --scope $RG
az role assignment create --assignee $DEPLOYER \
  --role "Key Vault Secrets Officer" --scope $RG
az role assignment create --assignee $DEPLOYER \
  --role "User Access Administrator" --scope $RG

Least privilege alternative

If your organisation forbids broad Contributor, break it down: AcrPush on the ACR, Container Apps Contributor + Container Apps Environment Contributor on the ACA namespace, and Key Vault Secrets Officer on the Key Vault. User Access Administrator can be scoped to just the Key Vault resource.

Managed identity — the runtime identity of the container. Create a user-assigned managed identity (recommended) and grant it:

Role Scope Purpose AcrPull ACR resource Pull the container image Key Vault Secrets User Key Vault resource Read the secrets at startup Cognitive Services OpenAI User Azure OpenAI resource Call Azure OpenAI without an API key (optional) 
MI_ID=$(az identity create --name tdfs4ds-runtime \
  --resource-group your-rg --query id -o tsv)
MI_PRINCIPAL=$(az identity show --name tdfs4ds-runtime \
  --resource-group your-rg --query principalId -o tsv)

# Pull images from ACR
ACR_ID=$(az acr show --name youracr --query id -o tsv)
az role assignment create --assignee-object-id $MI_PRINCIPAL \
  --role "AcrPull" --scope $ACR_ID

# Read Key Vault secrets
KV_ID=$(az keyvault show --name your-kv --query id -o tsv)
az role assignment create --assignee-object-id $MI_PRINCIPAL \
  --role "Key Vault Secrets User" --scope $KV_ID

# (Optional) Call Azure OpenAI without an API key
AOAI_ID=$(az cognitiveservices account show \
  --name your-aoai --resource-group your-rg --query id -o tsv)
az role assignment create --assignee-object-id $MI_PRINCIPAL \
  --role "Cognitive Services OpenAI User" --scope $AOAI_ID

Pass the managed identity to the container app:

az containerapp identity assign \
  --name tdfs4ds-serve --resource-group your-rg \
  --user-assigned $MI_ID

docs.title: Accessing the service
docs.text:
ACA issues a public FQDN when --ingress external is set. Restrict access with built-in IP allow-lists or by setting --ingress internal and placing an API Management gateway in front.

az containerapp show \
  --name tdfs4ds-serve --resource-group your-rg \
  --query properties.configuration.ingress.fqdn -o tsv
# → tdfs4ds-serve.<hash>.westeurope.azurecontainerapps.io

docs.title: Common notes for all cloud deployments
docs.text: Topic Recommendation Teradata connectivity The container connects to Vantage on TCP port 1025. On all cloud platforms this requires private networking (VPC / VNet integration, Direct Connect / Interconnect / ExpressRoute, or VPN) unless your Vantage host is internet-reachable (ClearScape SaaS). Always enable TDFS4DS_TD_ENCRYPT=true when the connection crosses a network boundary. Vector store Chroma is for dev/demo only — it is a local directory that is lost on container restart and cannot be shared across replicas. Use TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND=teradata in production: the index is stored inside Vantage, persistent and replica-safe with no extra cloud storage required. Port 8080 (docs) The admin UI and chatbot share the single API port (8000), so one ingress port serves them. The docs server uses a second port (8080) — disable it (TDFS4DS_SERVE_DOCS=false) on single-ingress managed services. Min replicas = 1 The container holds in-memory LangGraph state (conversation threads). Set --min-instances/--min-replicas to 1 to avoid cold starts and keep the Teradata connection warm. Health check All platforms can use GET /health (returns {"status": "ok"}) as the liveness probe. HTTPS Always terminate TLS at the platform's load balancer / ingress controller. The container speaks plain HTTP on port 8000.

docs.title: List models
docs.text: curl http://localhost:8000/v1/models

docs.title: Consumer agent
docs.text:
curlopenai Python SDKLangChain (ChatOpenAI)LangChain (invoke chain) 
curl -s http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
        "model": "tdfs4ds-consumer",
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What features are available for pharmacies?"}]
      }'

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="not-needed")

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="tdfs4ds-consumer",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What features are available for pharmacies?"}],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage

llm = ChatOpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="not-needed",
    model="tdfs4ds-consumer",
)

response = llm.invoke([HumanMessage(content="What features are available for pharmacies?")])
print(response.content)

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

llm = ChatOpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="not-needed",
    model="tdfs4ds-consumer",
)

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a data analyst. Answer using the feature store."),
    ("human", "{question}"),
])
chain = prompt | llm

response = chain.invoke({"question": "Which pharmacy features are freshest?"})
print(response.content)

docs.title: Query optimizer — /v1/optimize (programmatic, structured JSON)
docs.text:
POST /v1/optimize is the dedicated programmatic API for SQL optimisation. It accepts raw SQL and returns a structured JSON response — no chat framing needed, no report download required.

Request body

Field Type Required Description sql string ✓ The Teradata SQL query to optimise user_request string — Optional context hint (default: "Optimize this Teradata SQL query for performance.")

Response

Field Type Description optimized_sql string Recommended rewrite (equals the original when no improvement found) optimization_strategy string Plain-language explanation of the chosen strategy token_consumption object {prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, total_tokens} curlPython (requests)PowerShell (Invoke-RestMethod) 
curl -s http://localhost:8000/v1/optimize \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
        "sql": "SELECT * FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id WHERE c.region = '\''EU'\''"
      }'

Example response:

{
  "optimized_sql": "SELECT s.*, c.region\nFROM sales s\nINNER JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id\nWHERE c.region = 'EU'",
  "optimization_strategy": "Replaced SELECT * with explicit column list to reduce spool usage. Changed to INNER JOIN to allow the optimizer to apply join-order heuristics. Added partition pruning on region.",
  "token_consumption": {
    "prompt_tokens": 1842,
    "completion_tokens": 312,
    "total_tokens": 2154
  }
}

import requests

resp = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:8000/v1/optimize",
    json={
        "sql": "SELECT * FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id WHERE c.region = 'EU'",
        "user_request": "Focus on reducing spool usage.",
    },
)
data = resp.json()

print("Optimized SQL:\n", data["optimized_sql"])
print("\nStrategy:", data["optimization_strategy"])
print("\nTokens used:", data["token_consumption"]["total_tokens"])

$body = @{
    sql          = "SELECT * FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id WHERE c.region = 'EU'"
    user_request = "Focus on reducing spool usage."
} | ConvertTo-Json

$resp = Invoke-RestMethod `
    -Uri  "http://localhost:8000/v1/optimize" `
    -Method POST `
    -ContentType "application/json" `
    -Body $body

$resp.optimized_sql
$resp.optimization_strategy
$resp.token_consumption

docs.title: Query optimizer — /v1/chat/completions (conversational, Markdown report)
docs.text:
For interactive use or when you need the full Markdown report with scores, candidates, and FilterManager guidance, use the chat-completions surface and pass the SQL inline in the user message:

The full report is a download link, not the answer

For an OPTIMIZE request the answer is a compact summary — the score delta (before → after /5), whether an improvement was found, the optimized SQL, and FilterManager applicability — followed by a download link to the full Markdown report:

**Full report:** http://localhost:8000/reports/<DATA_DOMAIN>/tdfs4ds_optim_<ts>_<view>_<id>.md

The report is written under QUERY_OPTIMIZER_REPORT_DIR (tdfs4ds_optim_reports/<DATA_DOMAIN>/ by default) and served by the container. Browse all reports at GET /reports, or download one at GET /reports/{path}. Mount that directory (or the /data volume) to keep reports across restarts.

curlopenai Python SDKLangChain (ChatOpenAI) 
curl -s http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
        "model": "tdfs4ds-query-optimizer",
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Optimize this query: SELECT * FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id WHERE c.region = '\''EU'\''"}]
      }'

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="not-needed")

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="tdfs4ds-query-optimizer",
    messages=[{
        "role": "user",
        "content": "Optimize this query: SELECT * FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id WHERE c.region = 'EU'",
    }],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

# The usage block also carries optimiser-specific metadata
usage = resp.usage
# usage.prompt_tokens, usage.completion_tokens, usage.total_tokens
# usage.model_extra["optimized"]    → True/False
# usage.model_extra["score_delta"]  → {original_user_score, best_user_score, ...}

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage

llm = ChatOpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="not-needed",
    model="tdfs4ds-query-optimizer",
)

sql = "SELECT * FROM sales s JOIN customers c ON s.cust_id = c.id WHERE c.region = 'EU'"
response = llm.invoke([HumanMessage(content=f"Optimize this query: {sql}")])
print(response.content)

docs.title: Choosing between the two optimizer endpoints
docs.text: /v1/optimize /v1/chat/completions Output format Structured JSON Markdown answer (chat) Multi-turn No (stateless) Yes (via user field / thread_id) Full report No (strategy inline) Download link in answer Score delta No In usage.model_extra Best for Programmatic pipelines, CI checks, bulk rewrites Interactive exploration, full audit trail

docs.title: With the openai Python SDK (multi-model)
docs.text:
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="not-needed")

# Consumer agent
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="tdfs4ds-consumer",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise the customer features."}],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

# Query optimizer — full Markdown report via chat
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="tdfs4ds-query-optimizer",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain the plan for: SELECT ..."}],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

docs.title: Conversation memory
docs.text:
The agents own their multi-turn memory (LangGraph MemorySaver, keyed by a thread_id). To keep a conversation continuous across requests, set the OpenAI user field to a stable id — the server maps it to the agent's thread_id:

client.chat.completions.create(
    model="tdfs4ds-consumer",
    user="analyst-42",                       # stable id → continuous thread
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "And which of those are fresh?"}],
)

Without a user field each request is independent (a fresh thread_id).

docs.title: Streaming
docs.text: stream: true is supported and emits OpenAI chat.completion.chunk events. Because the agents return a complete answer (not a token stream), the current implementation sends the answer as a single content chunk followed by [DONE] — enough for clients that require streaming, without true token-level deltas.

docs.title: Admin web UI
docs.text:
The container serves a Teradata-styled admin web UI on the same port as the API (default 8000):

http://localhost:8000/

It is a server-rendered HTML/JS shell (left-nav rail: Chatbot, Dashboard, Lineage, Query optimization, Evaluation, Parameters, Skills) that reproduces the Teradata "Tera" console. The Chatbot entry embeds the Gradio consumer-agent chat (mounted at /chatbot); the other panels are rendered by the shell and fetch their data from /ui/* endpoints. Everything runs in the same process as the API, sharing the Teradata connection and the vector index — no second connection or index build.

v0.3.1.108 — single port, Gradio only for the chatbot

Earlier versions ran a full Gradio tabbed admin app on a second port (7860, via TDFS4DS_SERVE_CHATBOT*). That is gone: Gradio now renders only the chat, embedded in the custom shell, all on TDFS4DS_SERVE_PORT.

The ⚙️ Parameters panel lets you change, live:

  • Feature store database — switch the active feature store. The target is validated with tdfs4ds.connect(database=…, create_if_missing=False); a database that does not contain a tdfs4ds feature store is rejected and the switch does not happen.
  • Vector store — backend (chroma / teradata) and Chroma path, plus a Refresh / rebuild index button (useful because the local Chroma index goes stale as the business dictionary changes).
  • Instruct model and Embedding model — provider, model, URL, API key, and proxy (plus the embedding dimension).

Applying any change that affects the data or the model rebuilds the index when needed, resets the agent, rotates the conversation thread, and clears the chat so the session starts clean.

The panel shares the agent's configuration

The panel does not hold its own settings — each field is the same tdfs4ds.* global the agents read (SCHEMA, INSTRUCT_MODEL_*, EMBEDDING_MODEL_*, VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND, CHROMA_PATH, …). Fields are pre-filled from the values the container loaded from your .env / env vars at startup, and applying a change updates that global for the whole process. So the TDFS4DS_* variables you set in the .env are the panel's initial state, and a panel edit overrides them until the next restart.

The standalone chat-only Gradio UI is still available in notebooks:

from tdfs4ds.agent import launch_chatbot       # or launch_chatbot_with_index
launch_chatbot(port=7860)

launch_chatbot / launch_chatbot_with_index are unchanged. The full admin experience (the panels above) is the web shell served by python -m tdfs4ds.serve.

docs.title: Documentation server (port 8080)
docs.text:
The container serves the MkDocs-built documentation on a third port (default 8080):

http://localhost:8080

Toggle it with TDFS4DS_SERVE_DOCS (default true; set false to disable) and set the port with TDFS4DS_SERVE_DOCS_PORT.

docs.title: Live documentation updates
docs.text:
The docs/ directory is bind-mounted from your host:

# docker-compose.yml
volumes:
  - ./docs:/app/docs:ro   # host ./docs → /app/docs in container

So you can run mkdocs build locally and the served documentation updates without restarting the container — just refresh your browser.

If you prefer the docs baked into the image (for offline deployment or immutability), remove the bind mount from the compose file. The image already carries the documentation from the working tree — just omit the mount and the image's /app/docs directory will be served unchanged across container restarts.

docs.title: Dependency graph / lineage (port 8000 /lineage)
docs.text:
The API exposes an interactive lineage graph at:

http://localhost:8000/lineage

This renders the persisted FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH temporal table (built by tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph()) as an interactive pyvis network diagram, showing all object dependencies and relationships.

docs.title: Scoped subgraph
docs.text:
Filter the graph to a single object and its ancestors/descendants with the seed query parameter:

http://localhost:8000/lineage?seed=MY_DATABASE.MY_VIEW

The seed is parsed as DATABASE.OBJECT or OBJECT (auto-detected). Omit it for the full graph.

docs.title: Typical workflow
docs.text:
1. Populate the lineage once by running in Python: 

    import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph()

  2. Open the graph in your browser at http://localhost:8000/lineage.

  3. Explore interactively — pan, zoom, and click nodes to inspect them.

docs.title: Per-user database choice (multi-tenant)
docs.text:
The configuration is process-wide: tdfs4ds.SCHEMA and the catalog-name globals are module-level and shared by every request in the process. Switching the feature-store database — whether via the panel or TDFS4DS_SCHEMA — therefore changes it for all users of that container and for the OpenAI /v1 API in the same process.

To give each user their own feature-store database (or their own Teradata system), run one container per user rather than sharing one. Each container is an isolated process with its own globals, Teradata context, and Chroma index, so there is no cross-talk and nothing to change in code.

Recommended: one Compose service, one env file per user. A single service definition is instantiated once per user — the Compose project name (-p) namespaces both the container and the named volume, so each user automatically gets an isolated Chroma index with no extra wiring:

# docker-compose.yml — instantiated once per user via -p + --env-file
services:
  tdfs4ds-serve:
    image: you/tdfs4ds-serve:latest        # built once, reused for every user
    env_file:
      - ${TDFS4DS_ENV_FILE:-.env}
    ports:
      - "${TDFS4DS_API_PORT:-8000}:8000"        # host port varies; container port fixed (UI + chatbot + API)
    volumes:
      - chroma:/data                            # per-project volume → isolated index
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  chroma:

# User alice → feature store FS_TEAM_A on vantage-prod
docker compose -p alice --env-file alice.env up -d    # container alice-…, volume alice_chroma
# User bob → feature store FS_TEAM_B on a different system
docker compose -p bob   --env-file bob.env   up -d    # container bob-…,   volume bob_chroma

The container port stays fixed (8000, baked into the image); only the host side is a variable (TDFS4DS_API_PORT) so several users can co-exist on one host without clashing. If each user runs on their own host/VM you can keep the fixed 8000:8000 mapping and only swap the env file.

What each per-user .env must change vs. share:

Scope Variables Per user TDFS4DS_TD_HOST/USERNAME/PASSWORD (+ _LOGMECH/_DATABASE/_ENCRYPT), TDFS4DS_SCHEMA, TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN, and the host ports (if co-located) Shared INSTRUCT_MODEL_*, EMBEDDING_MODEL_*, VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND, SERVE_*

Keep TDFS4DS_CHROMA_PATH=/data/chroma identical for everyone — isolation comes from the per-project named volume (alice_chroma vs bob_chroma), not from the path. (If you bind-mount host directories instead, those must differ per user.)

Equivalent with raw docker run if you prefer not to use Compose:

docker run --env-file alice.env -p 8001:8000 -v alice_chroma:/data you/tdfs4ds-serve
docker run --env-file bob.env   -p 8002:8000 -v bob_chroma:/data   you/tdfs4ds-serve

Within a single container, the panel's database switch is safe — it just isn't a per-session boundary, so don't share one container across users who need different databases. Route users to their container with your normal reverse proxy / orchestration layer.

docs.title: Outbound proxy (HTTP or SOCKS5)
docs.text:
LLM and embedding calls can be routed through an outbound proxy — useful in locked-down environments. Set a single proxy URL whose scheme picks the protocol:

TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROXY=socks5://proxy-host:1080   # or http://proxy-host:3128
# TDFS4DS_EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROXY=...    # optional; blank reuses the instruct proxy

Notes:

  • Only the OpenAI-family clients (openai / vllm / openai-compatible / mistralai / azure) honour this; it does not proxy the Teradata connection. Bedrock / GCP use their own SDK proxy mechanisms.
  • socks5:// requires the httpx[socks] backend, which ships with the package.
  • Leave both unset for a direct connection (default).

docs.title: Connect and configure
docs.text: Every session starts the same way: open a Teradata context with teradataml, then import and connect tdfs4ds.

docs.title: Minimal setup
docs.text:
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import teradataml as tdml
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()
tdml.create_context(
    host              = os.getenv('TDML_HOST'),
    user              = os.getenv('TDML_USERNAME'),
    password          = os.getenv('TDML_PASSWORD'),
    database          = os.getenv('TDML_DATABASE'),
    temp_database_name= os.getenv('TDML_TEMP_DATABASE'),
)

# Import AFTER create_context — tdfs4ds auto-detects SCHEMA from the session
import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.connect(database=os.getenv('TDML_DATABASE'), create_if_missing=True)
tdfs4ds.create_data_domain('MY_PROJECT')   # idempotent; warns if it already exists

# Optional in-session overrides
# tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = '2024-01-01 00:00:00'   # time-travel point
# tdfs4ds.DISPLAY_LOGS       = True

Why import order matters

import tdfs4ds reads the active teradataml context to auto-detect SCHEMA. If you import the package before create_context, SCHEMA is None and you must set it manually.

docs.title: connect() vs setup()
docs.text:
Function When to call What it does tdfs4ds.setup() First time per database Provisions every catalog table and view (feature catalog, process catalog, data distribution, follow-up, business dictionary). Idempotent. tdfs4ds.connect(create_if_missing=True) Every session Hydrates Python globals from existing catalogs; creates any missing tables when create_if_missing=True.

If you are unsure, call connect(create_if_missing=True) — it covers both cases.

docs.title: Separate database for views (database_view)
docs.text:
Some environments keep views in one database and tables in another (governance / access-control policy). tdfs4ds supports this with an opt-in database_view argument on both setup() and connect():

# Tables (FS_T_*, catalogs, business dictionary, lineage) go in DATA_DB;
# every view (process views, dataset views, all FS_V_* framework views) goes in VIEW_DB.
tdfs4ds.setup(database='DATA_DB', database_view='VIEW_DB')
# or, every session:
tdfs4ds.connect(database='DATA_DB', database_view='VIEW_DB', create_if_missing=True)
  • database_view is optional. When omitted (None), views are co-located with the tables in database — identical to the historical behaviour, so existing code is unaffected.
  • You can also set it via the TDFS4DS_SCHEMA_VIEW environment variable / .env entry, or the schema_view JSON config key (same tier as SCHEMA).
  • Once set, registering a process or dataset with an unqualified name (e.g. target_view_name='my_source_view') places the view in the view database automatically — no need to prefix it every time. An explicit db.view name is still honoured as-is.

Grant required

A view in the view database that reads a table in the table database needs SELECT on the table database (and WITH GRANT OPTION to expose the view to downstream consumers). Grant this before using database_view.

Migrating an existing single-database store

Set database_view and call connect(create_if_missing=True) — the framework views are (re)created in the new view database. The now-stale views left in the old database are not dropped automatically.

docs.title: Read from other feature stores (databases_readonly)
docs.text:
You can connect to your primary store read-write as usual and extend the session to one or more other feature stores in read-only mode. Your uploads still write only to the primary store, but catalog / documentation / lineage reads — and build_dataset — span every attached store.

# Primary store DATA_DB (read-write) + two other stores read-only:
tdfs4ds.connect(database='DATA_DB', databases_readonly=['SHARED_FS', 'TEAM_B_FS'],
                create_if_missing=True)

# ...or attach/detach at any time after connecting:
tdfs4ds.extend_read_stores('SHARED_FS')          # a bare name (schema == view schema)
tdfs4ds.extend_read_stores({'schema': 'T_DB',    # or a descriptor for a table/view split
                            'schema_view': 'V_DB'})
tdfs4ds.list_read_stores()                       # inspect what's attached
tdfs4ds.detach_read_stores('SHARED_FS')          # remove one, or detach_read_stores() for all
  • databases_readonly is optional. When omitted, nothing changes — reads are single-store, byte-identical to before.
  • Also settable via the TDFS4DS_SCHEMA_READONLY environment variable / .env (comma-separated), or the schema_readonly JSON config key.
  • Once attached, feature_catalog(), dataset_catalog(), the consumer agent, and lineage all show rows from every store, tagged with a FEATURE_STORE column naming the source. build_dataset can select a read-only store's features into a dataset built in your primary store.
  • Writes never change. upload_features, documentation, and lineage writes always target your primary SCHEMA; read-only stores are never modified.

Grants required

The connecting user needs SELECT on each read-only store's FS_V_* catalog / business-dictionary / lineage views, its FS_T_* feature tables (for build_dataset), and — with VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND='teradata' — its vector collections. A store that isn't readable (or is a different tdfs4ds version) is logged and skipped; the others still attach.

Same-name features across stores

Resolution is exact when the stores hold different data domains / entities / feature names (the usual case). If two stores independently register the same (data domain, entity, feature name), the agent resolves it to the primary store. Semantic vector search spans read-only stores only with VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND='teradata' (each store keeps its own index); the local Chroma backend searches the primary only.

docs.title: Vector Store authentication
docs.text:
If you use tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND = 'teradata' (the Enterprise Vector Store, instead of the default local Chroma index), the consumer agent's vector index needs its own session auth against the Vantage Vector Store REST API — a separate channel from the tdml.create_context() connection above, via teradataml.set_auth_token(...).

Both setup() and connect() (and the tdfs4ds.serve server on startup) attempt this automatically, non-fatally, from environment variables. Set one of the following groups — the first fully-configured one wins:

Mechanism Required env vars PAT (tried first) TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_BASE_URL, TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_PAT_TOKEN, TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_PEM_FILE (+ optional TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_USERNAME) Basic TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_BASE_URL, TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_USERNAME, TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_PASSWORD OAuth TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_BASE_URL, TDFS4DS_VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_CLIENT_ID

The mechanism used (or "not configured") is logged at connect()/setup() time. If none of these are set, VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND='teradata' will not work until you configure one — the resulting error message repeats this list.

docs.title: Data domains
docs.text:
Features are isolated by DATA_DOMAIN. Use the dedicated helpers — never set the global directly:

tdfs4ds.get_data_domains()                # list every domain (* marks active)
tdfs4ds.create_data_domain('PROJECT_A')   # create + activate (idempotent)
tdfs4ds.select_data_domain('PROJECT_B')   # activate an existing domain

select_data_domain raises ValueError if the domain is unknown — guarding against silent typos that would otherwise cross-pollute features.

docs.title: Cleaning up
docs.text:
tdml.remove_context()

Always call this at the bottom of every script or notebook. Leaving open contexts behind blocks ports and slowly leaks connections.

docs.title: Server deployment
docs.text: When you're ready to expose the package to other users, tdfs4ds ships an OpenAI-compatible API server with an optional admin chatbot UI, documentation server, and interactive lineage graph. See Deployment for setup instructions (locally with python -m tdfs4ds.serve or in Docker with docker compose up).

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: Register your first feature in First feature.

docs.title: Running the Consumer Agent with AWS Bedrock (no Docker)
docs.text: This guide covers launching the tdfs4ds consumer agent chatbot on a remote machine accessed via VS Code SSH, using AWS Bedrock for both the instruct and embedding models. No Docker or separate Chroma server is required.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python environment with network access to your Teradata Vantage instance
  • AWS Bedrock access with a temporary Bearer token (generated from the AWS console)
  • VS Code with the Remote – SSH extension and port forwarding available

docs.title: Step 1 — Install the package
docs.text:
pip install tdfs4ds
pip install langchain-aws boto3

langchain-aws and boto3 are required for Bedrock support and are not always pulled in automatically on all platforms.

docs.title: Step 2 — One-time prerequisite setup
docs.text: These steps only need to be run once when setting up a feature store that already has registered processes and datasets but has not yet been initialised for the agent. Skip any step that has already been completed.

docs.title: 3a — Connect with create_if_missing=True
docs.text:
This creates the documentation and lineage tables if they do not yet exist (idempotent):

import teradataml as tdml
import tdfs4ds

tdml.create_context(
    host="<teradata-host>",
    username="<user>",
    password="<password>",
)

tdfs4ds.SCHEMA      = "<your_schema>"
tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN = "<your_data_domain>"

tdfs4ds.connect(create_if_missing=True)

docs.title: 3b — Upload the business dictionary (optional)
docs.text:
If you have object-level or column-level descriptions to seed the agent with, upload them now. All three functions accept a pandas DataFrame and merge into the temporal business dictionary tables.

import pandas as pd

# Object-level descriptions (tables, views, datasets)
objects_df = pd.DataFrame([
    {
        "DATABASE_NAME":        "<your_schema>",
        "OBJECT_NAME":          "MY_FEATURE_VIEW",
        "OBJECT_TYPE":          "V",          # 'V' = view, 'T' = table, 'D' = dataset
        "BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION": "Describes ...",
    },
    # add more rows as needed
])
tdfs4ds.upload_business_dictionary_objects(objects_df)

# Column-level descriptions
columns_df = pd.DataFrame([
    {
        "DATABASE_NAME":        "<your_schema>",
        "TABLE_NAME":           "MY_FEATURE_VIEW",
        "COLUMN_NAME":          "MY_FEATURE",
        "BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION": "Describes ...",
    },
])
tdfs4ds.upload_business_dictionary_columns(columns_df)

docs.title: 3c — Rebuild the lineage graph
docs.text:
Derives all dependency edges from every registered process view and dataset and persists them into FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH. This is required before generating documentation:

tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(all_data_domains=True)

docs.title: 3d — Rebuild documentation
docs.text:
Walks the persisted lineage graph bottom-up and generates LLM documentation for every process view and dataset that has not yet been documented:

import os
os.environ["AWS_DEFAULT_REGION"] = "us-east-1"   # replace with your Bedrock region

tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = "bedrock"
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_REGION   = "us-east-1"
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY  = "<your-temporary-bearer-token>"
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = "anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0"

tdfs4ds.rebuild_documentation(force_update=False, all_data_domains=True)

force_update=False skips objects that already have stored documentation. Set it to True to regenerate everything from scratch.

docs.title: Step 4 — Write a launch script
docs.text:
Create a file launch_agent.py (or use a notebook cell):

import os
import teradataml as tdml
import tdfs4ds

os.environ["AWS_DEFAULT_REGION"] = "us-east-1"   # replace with your Bedrock region

# ── 1. Connect to Teradata ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
tdml.create_context(
    host="<teradata-host>",
    username="<user>",
    password="<password>",
)

# ── 2. Target schema and data domain ────────────────────────────────────────
tdfs4ds.SCHEMA      = "<your_schema>"
tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN = "<your_data_domain>"

# ── 3. Instruct model (Bedrock) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = "bedrock"
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_REGION   = "us-east-1"   # must match AWS_DEFAULT_REGION
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY  = "<your-temporary-bearer-token>"  # from AWS console
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = "anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0"

# ── 4. Embedding model (Bedrock Titan) ──────────────────────────────────────
# EMBEDDING_MODEL_API_KEY falls back to INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY automatically.
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER = "bedrock"
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_REGION   = "us-east-1"
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL    = "amazon.titan-embed-text-v2:0"
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM      = 1024   # Titan v2 native dimension

# ── 5. Local Chroma vector index ────────────────────────────────────────────
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_PATH = "./tdfs4ds_chroma"   # persisted locally on the remote machine

# ── 6. Launch ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
tdfs4ds.launch_chatbot_with_index(
    port=7860,
    inline=False,   # set to True only inside a Jupyter notebook
    share=False,    # VS Code port forwarding handles external access
)

docs.title: Step 5 — Forward the port in VS Code
docs.text:
1. Open the Ports panel in VS Code (bottom bar → Ports tab, or View → Ports).
  2. Click Forward a Port and enter 7860.
  3. Open http://localhost:7860 in your local browser.

The chatbot is now accessible from your local machine via the SSH tunnel — no public URL or share=True needed.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Temporary credentials expire. Bearer tokens from the AWS console are short-lived. When they expire, Bedrock calls will fail with an authentication error. Restart the script with a fresh token — there is no automatic refresh.

Titan v2 embedding dimensions. amazon.titan-embed-text-v2:0 supports dimensions 256, 512, and 1024 (default). If you previously built a Chroma index with a different model or dimension (e.g. 1536 from OpenAI text-embedding-3-small), delete the ./tdfs4ds_chroma folder before the first run to avoid a dimension mismatch error.

Feature store already initialised? If the feature store tables already exist in SCHEMA you can skip tdfs4ds.setup(). If they do not exist yet, call it once before launching:

tdfs4ds.setup()

inline=True only works inside a Jupyter notebook. For a plain .py script use inline=False.

docs.title: Build your first dataset
docs.text: A dataset in tdfs4ds is a denormalised view that joins selected feature versions together for a given entity. It is always a view — never a table — so it stays consistent with the underlying temporal features.

docs.title: 1 · Discover what is available
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.feature_store.feature_query_retrieval import (
    get_list_entity,
    get_available_features,
    get_feature_versions,
)

get_list_entity()                          # list every entity in the active DATA_DOMAIN
get_available_features(entity_id='CUSTOMER_ID')

docs.title: 2 · Resolve feature versions
docs.text:
build_dataset needs {feature_name: process_uuid} — one entry per feature. Always go through get_feature_versions; never construct the dict by hand:

selected_features = get_feature_versions(
    entity_name = 'CUSTOMER_ID',
    features    = ['nb_transactions', 'total_amount', 'avg_amount'],
)
# selected_features == {'nb_transactions': '550e...', 'total_amount': '551a...', ...}

If two processes register the same feature name, get_feature_versions picks the most recent. To freeze a specific version, set tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME first.

docs.title: 3 · Build the view
docs.text:
dataset = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
    view_name         = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND',
    comment           = 'Customer spending features, current state',
)

dataset.shape

build_dataset creates DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND as a Teradata view, registers it in the dataset catalog, and returns a teradataml DataFrame you can query immediately.

docs.title: 4 · Reuse without rebuilding
docs.text:
The view is now permanent — reattach to it in any future session:

dataset = tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema(tdfs4ds.SCHEMA, 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND'))

docs.title: Inspect before you build
docs.text:
Use return_query=True to see the generated DDL without creating the view or touching the dataset catalog:

sql = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
    view_name         = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND',
    return_query      = True,
)
print(sql)

docs.title: Grouped query strategy
docs.text:
