Pass grouped=True to collapse features from the same source table into a single MAX(CASE WHEN) … GROUP BY sub-query instead of one sub-query per feature. Useful for wide feature sets (20+ features from the same table):

dataset = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
    view_name         = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND',
    grouped           = True,
)

docs.title: Point-in-time snapshots
docs.text:
Set FEATURE_STORE_TIME before calling build_dataset to materialise a historical view. Both grouped=False (default) and grouped=True work with time travel:

tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = '2024-01-01 00:00:00'

dataset_january = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
    view_name         = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND_2024_01_01',
)

tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = None    # back to current state

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
• Document the dataset with an LLM — AI / Dataset documentation.
  • Wire it into the consumer agent so business users can query it conversationally — AI / Consumer agent.
  • Inspect lineage to see exactly which source tables feed it — Lineage / Dependency graph.

docs.title: Register your first feature
docs.text: This walkthrough takes you from raw data to a registered, persisted, time-travel ready feature in three steps.

docs.title: 1 · Write the feature engineering view
docs.text:
Prefer raw SQL — it gives you the full Teradata expressivity (PERCENTILE_CONT, window functions, partition predicates). Persist the view directly with REPLACE VIEW:

feature_sql = """
SELECT
    CUSTOMER_ID,
    COUNT(*)        AS nb_transactions,
    SUM(AMOUNT)     AS total_amount,
    AVG(AMOUNT)     AS avg_amount
FROM SOURCE_DB.TRANSACTIONS
GROUP BY CUSTOMER_ID
"""

tdml.execute_sql(f"REPLACE VIEW {tdfs4ds.SCHEMA}.FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER AS {feature_sql}")
df_features = tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema(tdfs4ds.SCHEMA, 'FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER'))

If you must use the teradataml DataFrame API and it produces a temporary view, crystallize it first:

from tdfs4ds.utils.lineage import crystallize_view

df_features = crystallize_view(
    source_data.groupby('CUSTOMER_ID').agg({'AMOUNT': ['sum', 'mean']}),
    view_name   = 'FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER',
    schema_name = tdfs4ds.SCHEMA,
)

docs.title: 2 · Register and ingest with upload_features
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df_features,
    entity_id     = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},        # dict, not list
    feature_names = ['nb_transactions', 'total_amount', 'avg_amount'],
    metadata      = {'project': 'customer_analytics'},
    dataset_view_name = 'ds_customer_spend',          # optional — also builds a dataset
)

The single call performs eight operations:

  1. Registers the entity (CUSTOMER_ID) if not already present.
  2. Registers each feature in the feature catalog.
  3. Registers the process (the view + entity + feature list) in the process catalog.
  4. Records the source distribution.
  5. (Optional) simplify_query — if tdfs4ds.simplify_query is configured.
  6. (Optional) document_process_incremental — if an instruct model is configured and document=True (default).
  7. run(process_id) — ingests the feature values into the temporal storage tables.
  8. (Optional) Builds and documents ds_customer_spend.

entity_id must be a dict

Always pass entity_id as {'COLUMN': 'SQL_TYPE'}. A plain string works for single-column entities (auto-typed) but lists are rejected.

docs.title: 3 · Confirm registration
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.process_catalog()       # PROCESS_ID, VIEW_NAME, ENTITY_NAME, ...
tdfs4ds.feature_catalog()       # FEATURE_NAME, ENTITY_NAME, FEATURE_TABLE, PROCESS_ID, ...
tdfs4ds.dataset_catalog()       # VIEW_NAME, ENTITY_NAME, COMMENT, ...

To re-run the same logic later (e.g. nightly refresh), look up the process ID and call run:

from tdfs4ds.process_store.process_query_administration import get_process_id

process_id = get_process_id('"my_db"."FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER"')
tdfs4ds.run(process_id=process_id)

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
• Build a dataset combining several feature versions — First dataset.
  • Scale to large sources with FilterManager — User guide / FilterManager.
  • Backfill historical periods with TimeManager and roll_out — User guide / TimeManager.
  • Document the process with an LLM — AI / Process documentation.

docs.title: From PyPI
docs.text: pip install tdfs4ds

docs.title: From source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/denismolin/tdfs4ds.git
cd tdfs4ds
pip install -e .

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
tdfs4ds declares the following runtime dependencies in setup.py:

Library Purpose teradataml >= 17.20 Teradata client used for every query pandas, numpy DataFrame primitives plotly, tqdm, networkx Lineage Sankey + progress bars + graph algebra sqlparse Lightweight SQL parsing for the lineage walker langchain_openai, langchain_aws, langchain_core, langchain_chroma, langchain_teradata LLM and embedding adapters langgraph StateGraph orchestrating the consumer agent and query optimizer chromadb Persistent vector store for the consumer agent pydantic Structured output for intent classification gradio Chatbot UI nbformat Notebook utilities

LangChain / LangGraph / Gradio dependencies are lazy-loaded — they are imported only when the genai or agent sub-modules are first accessed, so import tdfs4ds stays fast.

docs.title: Verifying the install
docs.text:
import tdfs4ds
print(tdfs4ds.__version__)        # 0.2.9.9 or newer
print(tdfs4ds.list_skills())      # 22 bundled agent skills

docs.title: Installing the agent skills
docs.text:
The package ships with 22 discoverable SKILL.md files organized in four families that teach Claude Code (and compatible agents) how to drive the feature store end-to-end:

  • fs-* (10 skills) — Feature store operations (upload, document, analyze, etc.)
  • qo-skill-* (3 skills) — Query optimization with nested prompts
  • td-* (1 skill) — Teradata utilities (EXPLAIN visualization)
  • ca-* (9 skills) — Consumer agent intents (discovered dynamically when using the agent)

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
import tdfs4ds

# Copy all skills to ~/.claude/skills/ (Claude Code user-level, all projects)
tdfs4ds.install_skills()

# Or into the current project only
tdfs4ds.install_skills(target_dir='.claude/skills')

# Only add skills that are not present yet (safe for shared project dirs)
tdfs4ds.export_skills('.claude/skills')        # overwrite=False by default

# Install a specific subset
tdfs4ds.install_skills(skills=['fs-setup', 'fs-upload'])

docs.title: CLI (with the venv active)
docs.text:
# User-level install — picked up in every project
tdfs4ds-install-skills

# Project-level install
tdfs4ds-install-skills --target .claude/skills

# Skip skills already present
tdfs4ds-install-skills --target .claude/skills --no-overwrite

# List bundled skill names
tdfs4ds-install-skills --list

docs.title: Sharing with teammates
docs.text:
If the project repository already contains .claude/skills/, teammates get the skills automatically on git pull — no install step needed.

If each person installs tdfs4ds into their own virtual environment, they run tdfs4ds.install_skills() (or the CLI equivalent) once after activation.

See Agent skills for the full catalogue, workflow chains, and authoring guide.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: Open a Teradata context and provision the feature store catalogs in Connect and configure.

docs.title: Dependency graph
docs.text: build_teradata_dependency_graph walks a SQL query (or a dataset DDL) and returns a structured graph of every table and view involved. For each node it captures the primary index and the partition columns — exactly what you need to decide whether a FilterManager rewrite is worthwhile.

docs.title: Build the graph
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import build_teradata_dependency_graph

graph = build_teradata_dependency_graph(sql_query=sql)

# Returns:
# {
#   'nodes': {
#       '<db>.<name>': {
#           'object_type': 'table' | 'view' | 'dataset',
#           'table_info': {
#               'primary_index_columns':  ['CUSTOMER_ID', ...],
#               'partition_columns':      ['TX_DATE'],
#               'partitioning_levels':    [{'level': 1, 'kind': 'RANGE_N', 'columns': ['TX_DATE']}],
#           },
#       },
#       ...
#   },
#   'edges': [('<dst>', '<src>'), ...],
#   'roots': ['<top-level>', ...],
# }

The walk uses SHOW VIEW / SHOW TABLE DDL — every table reachable through views is resolved transitively. sqlparse is used at parse time; no EXPLAIN is run.

docs.title: Two source paths
docs.text:
# 1. From a registered process
from tdfs4ds.process_store.process_store_catalog_management import get_process_info
sql = get_process_info(process_id)['PROCESS_SQL']

# 2. From a dataset view DDL
sql = tdml.execute_sql("SHOW VIEW DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND").fetchall()[0][0].replace('\r', '\n')

Both are valid inputs to build_teradata_dependency_graph(sql_query=...).

docs.title: Dataset expansion
docs.text:
When the graph crosses a dataset view, two behaviours are available:

expand_datasets_via_process_catalog Behaviour True (default) Edges drawn to the process views that produce the dataset's columns. FEATURE_VERSION UUIDs in the dataset DDL are matched to PROCESS_ID entries in FS_V_PROCESS_CATALOG. False Edges drawn directly to the raw storage tables (FS_T_…).

The default surfaces the logical lineage — what computes the dataset — which is usually what users want. Set it to False for migration audits where physical dependencies matter.

docs.title: Inspect node by node
docs.text:
for name, node in graph['nodes'].items():
    ti = node.get('table_info')
    if ti is None:
        continue
    print(f"{name}")
    print(f"  Primary Index : {ti['primary_index_columns']}")
    print(f"  Partition cols: {ti['partition_columns']}")
    for lvl in ti.get('partitioning_levels', []):
        print(f"    Level {lvl['level']}: {lvl['kind']} on {lvl['columns']}")

docs.title: Decision aid for FilterManager
docs.text:
Field Why it matters primary_index_columns Used for AMP routing. Filtering on the PI in WHERE does not help — it only helps in joins. partition_columns Used for partition elimination. Filtering on these in WHERE skips irrelevant partitions.

When a source table has partition_columns that the current process SQL does not reference, a FilterManager rewrite usually pays off. See fs-analyze for an end-to-end recommendation workflow.

docs.title: When a node has no table_info
docs.text:
table_info is None when the DDL could not be fetched — typically:

  • The object is a temporary or volatile table.
  • The object is in a database the current user cannot read DDL from.
  • The object name in the SQL is mistyped (treat this as a warning).

The graph still records the node — the missing info is informative, not a failure.

docs.title: Persisted lineage graph
docs.text:
build_teradata_dependency_graph re-derives lineage on the fly every call. For repeated queries — and for the reverse / impact direction ("what depends on this?") — tdfs4ds also maintains a persisted, temporal lineage graph in the FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH table. It is created when missing at setup() and connect(create_if_missing=True), and refreshed at every upload_features call by record_process_lineage.

  • One row per directed edge parent → child (parent depends on child).
  • Nodes reuse the business-dictionary object identity (DATABASE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME) plus OBJECT_TYPE ('T'/'V'/'D'). No PROCESS_ID is stored on an edge — an edge connects two objects, and most objects are not processes. Use resolve_process_id_for_object(db, obj) to map a node to its process (returns None for a plain view).
  • Edges are the output of build_teradata_dependency_graph, so the feature store's internal technical tables are skipped and datasets appear as nodes.
from tdfs4ds.lineage import (
    get_descendants, get_ancestors, get_persisted_graph, plot_lineage_pyvis,
)

# Impact direction: every object that depends on a source table/view/process.
get_descendants("MY_DB", "V_FEAT_ENG")

# Upstream sources a view depends on.
get_ancestors("MY_DB", "DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND")

# Reconstruct the {'nodes','edges','roots'} shape from persisted data
# (drop-in for plot_lineage_sankey / graph_to_migration_manifest).
graph = get_persisted_graph(seed="MY_DB.DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND")

# Interactive HTML network.
plot_lineage_pyvis(graph, output_path="lineage.html")

All traversal loads the table once into a networkx.DiGraph (load_lineage_digraph) and walks it in memory — no recursive SQL.

docs.title: Full refresh / init
docs.text:
The write hook keeps lineage current as you upload features, but a store that already has registered processes/datasets — or one whose edges have drifted — can rebuild the whole graph in one pass:

import tdfs4ds

# Wipe FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH and re-derive every edge from every registered
# process view + dataset, across all data domains.
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph()              # all_data_domains=True by default
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(all_data_domains=False)   # current DATA_DOMAIN only

It clears the current edges (CURRENT VALIDTIME DELETE, so VALIDTIME history is retained), then seeds record_process_lineage from each process VIEW_NAME and each dataset view. Per-seed failures are non-fatal — one unreadable view does not abort the rebuild. Returns the total edge count written.

docs.title: Rebuild documentation from the persisted graph
docs.text:
Once the lineage graph is persisted, the whole feature store can be documented without re-deriving lineage — the graph is read once from the table instead of walking SHOW VIEW again:

import tdfs4ds

tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph()       # 1. persist the graph
tdfs4ds.rebuild_documentation()       # 2. document every process/dataset once
tdfs4ds.rebuild_documentation(force_update=True)   # regenerate everything

rebuild_documentation topologically sorts the persisted graph (leaves first), then walks it with shared visited-sets so each process and dataset is documented exactly once and shared upstream views are never re-documented. Seeds are the graph's top consumers (datasets + process views); base tables and intermediate views are documented only as a side effect of their owning process's walk. With the default force_update=False, already-documented objects are reused as context and only the gaps get an LLM call.

docs.title: Refreshing just the objects you changed
docs.text:
When you edit a non-process view (an intermediate view that feeds processes but isn't itself registered), refresh only the affected slice instead of a full rebuild:

import tdfs4ds

# 1. Refresh just this view's lineage edges (no global wipe), and check impact.
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=['vw_aggregate_features'], check=True)

# 2. Re-document the impact set: the view + everything downstream of it.
tdfs4ds.genai.rebuild_documentation(objects=['vw_aggregate_features'])

# Standalone: which dependent processes still compile after the change?
res = tdfs4ds.check_downstream_processes(['vw_aggregate_features'])
print(res['broken'])   # [{view, process_id, error}, ...] — empty if all OK

Name matching is safe. 'vw_aggregate_features', 'DEMO_USER.VW_AGGREGATE_FEATURES' and '"demo_user"."vw_aggregate_features"' all resolve to the same object — names are normalised (quotes stripped, upper-cased, db.name split) and verified against the persisted graph (and DBC.TablesV for a brand-new view). An unrecognised name raises a ValueError listing it, so a typo never silently does nothing.

Result-column impact. A changed view may rename or drop output columns, which can silently break the process views that read it. check_downstream_processes runs a cheap EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM <view> on every registered process downstream of the change — EXPLAIN only verifies the query still compiles, it does not run it — and reports any that fail so you know exactly which processes to fix. It runs automatically inside rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=..., check=True).

docs.title: Cascade re-documentation
docs.text: Because the graph knows what depends on a process, a changed process description can be propagated downstream. upload_features(..., cascade_redocumentation=True) re-documents every dependent process and dataset — but only when the process's own documentation actually changed — so a corrected parent meaning flows into the descendants' business descriptions and per-feature column docs. It is opt-in because it costs one LLM pass per affected descendant.

docs.title: Migration manifest
docs.text: graph_to_migration_manifest converts any lineage graph into a flat, JSON-serialisable manifest — useful for planning a feature-store migration between databases or environments.

docs.title: Build a manifest
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import build_teradata_dependency_graph, graph_to_migration_manifest
import json

graph = build_teradata_dependency_graph(sql_query=sql)

manifest = graph_to_migration_manifest(graph)
print(json.dumps(manifest, indent=2))

docs.title: Schema
docs.text:
{
  "views":  [{"database": "demo_user", "name": "DS_CUSTOMER", "type": "dataset"}],
  "tables": [{"database": "src_db",    "name": "TRANSACTIONS",
              "primary_index": ["TX_ID"], "partition_columns": ["TX_DATE"]}],
  "edges":  [{"from": "demo_user.DS_CUSTOMER", "to": "demo_user.FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER"}]
}

primary_index and partition_columns come from table_info collected by the graph walker — same data you see when iterating graph['nodes'].

docs.title: Scope to a single schema
docs.text:
manifest = graph_to_migration_manifest(graph, filter_database=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)

When filter_database is set, any edge whose source or target falls outside the filter is dropped. The result is a self-contained manifest for migrating one logical feature store boundary at a time.

docs.title: Typical migration steps
docs.text:
1. Build the manifest for the source environment.
  2. In the target environment, create_data_domain and setup().
  3. Re-create the source views in dependency order (manifest['edges'] gives you a topological hint).
  4. Re-run every registered process — run(process_id) once per process in the manifest's views[*] with type == 'view'.
  5. Re-build any datasets — build_dataset(...) for every type == 'dataset'.

docs.title: Comparing two environments
docs.text:
import json

with open('manifest_dev.json')  as f: dev  = json.load(f)
with open('manifest_prod.json') as f: prod = json.load(f)

dev_views  = {(v['database'], v['name']) for v in dev['views']}
prod_views = {(v['database'], v['name']) for v in prod['views']}

print('Missing in prod:', dev_views - prod_views)
print('Missing in dev :', prod_views - dev_views)

docs.title: Persisted lineage graph
docs.text:
The persisted lineage graph (FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH) is a temporal table that records every dependency edge in your feature store — which views and tables feed into which processes and datasets. Unlike the on-the-fly dependency graph, which re-derives lineage on demand, the persisted graph is:

  • Queryable in both directions: upstream sources (ancestors) and downstream consumers (descendants / impact).
  • Fast: one table read, then in-memory traversal with networkx.
  • Reusable: documentation, impact analysis, and visualization all share one persisted copy.
  • Temporal: full VALIDTIME history; you can audit what the graph was at any point in the past.

docs.title: Full rebuild (first time or recovery)
docs.text:
The first time you set up a feature store, or when you have a pre-existing store with registered processes and datasets, build the full graph in one call:

import tdfs4ds

n_edges = tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph()
print(f"Persisted {n_edges} edges")

This wipes the FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH table and re-derives every edge by walking the SHOW VIEW DDL of every registered process view and dataset (across all DATA_DOMAINs). Per-view failures are non-fatal — one unreadable DDL does not abort the rebuild.

To rebuild only the current data domain:

tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(all_data_domains=False)

docs.title: Automatic updates
docs.text:
Every time you call upload_features() with a new process, the write hook record_process_lineage() adds that process's edges to the graph. So most feature stores keep their graph current automatically.

To disable auto-update for a single upload_features call (edge case):

tdfs4ds.upload_features(..., record_lineage=False)

docs.title: Query the graph
docs.text: Once persisted, traverse the graph in-memory with deterministic functions:

docs.title: Impact direction (what breaks if I change this?)
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import get_descendants

# Every process/dataset that depends on this view
downstream = get_descendants(database='MY_DB', object_name='V_CUSTOMER_FEATURES')
for obj in downstream:
    print(obj)  # e.g. ("MY_DB", "DS_CHURN_RISK", "D")

The result is a list of (DATABASE, OBJECT_NAME, OBJECT_TYPE) tuples, ordered so a consumer always appears after its sources (safe for serial processing).

docs.title: Upstream sources (where does this come from?)
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import get_ancestors

# Every source table/view feeding into this dataset
upstream = get_ancestors(database='MY_DB', object_name='DS_CHURN_RISK')
for obj in upstream:
    print(obj)

docs.title: Full graph reconstruction
docs.text:
To get the same {'nodes', 'edges', 'roots'} shape as build_teradata_dependency_graph() but from persisted data (no SHOW VIEW re-scan):

from tdfs4ds.lineage import get_persisted_graph

# Whole graph
graph = get_persisted_graph()

# Subgraph: this object + all ancestors + all descendants
graph = get_persisted_graph(seed='MY_DB.DS_CHURN_RISK')

docs.title: Rebuild documentation from the graph
docs.text:
Once the lineage graph is persisted, you can document your entire feature store in a single pass without re-deriving lineage:

import tdfs4ds

# Document every process and dataset exactly once, topo-sorted (leaves first)
# so shared upstream views are used as context, not re-documented.
tdfs4ds.rebuild_documentation()

# Force regenerate everything (overwrites existing descriptions).
tdfs4ds.rebuild_documentation(force_update=True)

The rebuild uses the persisted graph as the source of truth — it topologically sorts the edges, then walks them with a shared visited-set so:

  • Each process and dataset is documented exactly once.
  • Intermediate views (non-process, non-dataset) are documented as side effects of the processes they feed, not as top-level targets.
  • The LLM reuses earlier descriptions as context, building a coherent narrative.

This is orders of magnitude faster than re-deriving the graph with SHOW VIEW for every node. See Process documentation and Dataset documentation for what gets documented.

docs.title: Scoped refresh (after editing a view)
docs.text:
When you edit a non-process view (an intermediate view that feeds processes but isn't itself registered), you don't need a full rebuild. Refresh just that view's edges and check the impact:

import tdfs4ds

# 1. Refresh just this view's edges (no global wipe).
# Also runs EXPLAIN on downstream processes to verify they still compile.
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=['vw_aggregate_features'], check=True)

# 2. Re-document the impact set (the view + everything downstream).
tdfs4ds.rebuild_documentation(objects=['vw_aggregate_features'])

docs.title: Name matching is safe
docs.text:
Object names are normalized and verified:

# These all refer to the same view — quotes, case, and schema are normalized.
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=['vw_agg'])
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=['VW_AGG'])
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=['my_db.vw_agg'])
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=['"my_db"."VW_AGG"'])

A typo raises ValueError — never a silent no-op.

docs.title: Standalone impact check
docs.text:
To check if downstream processes still compile without updating the graph:

result = tdfs4ds.check_downstream_processes(['vw_aggregate_features'])
print(f"checked: {result['checked']} | ok: {len(result['ok'])} | broken: {len(result['broken'])}")

for broken in result['broken']:
    print(f"  BROKEN: {broken['view']} -> {broken['error']}")

This runs a cheap EXPLAIN SELECT * on every registered process downstream — enough to verify the SQL compiles (a changed view's dropped columns can silently break consumers).

docs.title: Interactive HTML (pyvis network diagram)
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import plot_lineage_pyvis, get_persisted_graph

# Whole graph
plot_lineage_pyvis(get_persisted_graph(), output_path='lineage.html')

# Subgraph: one object + impact + upstream
plot_lineage_pyvis(
    get_persisted_graph(seed='MY_DB.DS_CHURN_RISK'),
    output_path='impact.html'
)

The HTML is self-contained (vis.js bundled) — open in a browser, pan/zoom, and click nodes to inspect them. A color legend is shown in the bottom-left corner:

Color Type Orange Registered process view (feature-engineering view in FS_V_PROCESS_CATALOG) Blue Plain / intermediate view (not a registered process) Green Permanent source table Purple Dataset node

Hover over any node to see its full qualified name and type label.

docs.title: Web UI
docs.text:
When running the Docker container or the python -m tdfs4ds.serve server, the lineage graph is also served as an interactive web panel:

http://localhost:8000/lineage

Optionally scope it to a single object with the seed query parameter:

http://localhost:8000/lineage?seed=MY_DB.DS_CHURN_RISK

See the Deployment guide (section: Dependency graph / lineage) for details.

docs.title: Sankey diagram
docs.text:
For a flow-oriented view (from sources to consumers), use the Sankey:

from tdfs4ds.lineage import plot_lineage_sankey

plot_lineage_sankey(get_persisted_graph(), output_path='lineage.html')

See Sankey visualisation for more.

docs.title: Rebuild documentation with cascade
docs.text:
When a process's documentation changes, you can automatically propagate the new meaning downstream to all dependent processes and datasets:

tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    ...,
    document=True,
    cascade_redocumentation=True,  # re-document all downstream
)

This is opt-in because it costs one LLM pass per affected descendant. It only cascades when the process's documentation actually changed; unchanged processes are skipped. The impact set is determined from the persisted lineage graph, so the graph must be current.

See Process documentation for details.

docs.title: Understanding the edge structure
docs.text:
Each row in FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH represents one directed edge:

Column Meaning PARENT_DATABASE_NAME Source object's database PARENT_OBJECT_NAME Source object's name PARENT_OBJECT_TYPE 'T' (table), 'V' (view), or 'D' (dataset) CHILD_DATABASE_NAME Target object's database CHILD_OBJECT_NAME Target object's name CHILD_OBJECT_TYPE 'T', 'V', or 'D'

Edge direction: parent → child means parent depends on child. So if you have an edge (MY_DB, V_AGG, V) → (MY_DB, T_RAW, T), it means the view V_AGG depends on the table T_RAW.

No PROCESS_ID on edges: an edge connects two objects, and most objects are not processes. To find the process that computes an object, use:

from tdfs4ds.lineage import resolve_process_id_for_object

process_id = resolve_process_id_for_object(db='MY_DB', obj='V_AGG')
# process_id is None if the view is not a registered process

docs.title: Related topics
docs.text: Topic Purpose Dependency graph On-the-fly graph builder (no persistence); useful for one-off analysis. Sankey visualisation Flow diagram showing sources → consumers. Migration manifest Export the graph as JSON for migration planning. Process documentation Auto-document the processes that compute features. Dataset documentation Auto-document the datasets that combine features. fs-lineage-graph skill For Claude Code: rebuild lineage, document the store, check impact. fs-lineage skill For Claude Code: one-off dependency graph + Sankey (no persistence).

docs.title: Sankey visualisation
docs.text: plot_lineage_sankey renders any dependency graph as an interactive Plotly Sankey diagram — orange flow lines on a navy background, matching the Teradata visual identity.

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import build_teradata_dependency_graph, plot_lineage_sankey

graph = build_teradata_dependency_graph(sql_query=sql)
fig   = plot_lineage_sankey(graph, title='Customer Dataset Lineage')
fig.show()                       # inline in a Jupyter notebook

docs.title: Save to a self-contained HTML
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import show_plotly_robust

show_plotly_robust(fig, html_path='lineage_customer_dataset.html')

show_plotly_robust writes a standalone HTML file that requires no Python or Jupyter — share it with stakeholders directly.

docs.title: Customise the figure
docs.text:
plot_lineage_sankey is a thin Plotly wrapper, so you can post-process the figure:

fig.update_layout(
    title_text='Customer Dataset Lineage',
    font=dict(family='Inter', size=12),
    paper_bgcolor='#FFFFFF',
)
fig.write_html('lineage.html')

docs.title: Reading the diagram
docs.text:
Visual Meaning Left → Right Upstream → Downstream Node width Cumulative weight (number of incoming edges) Edge colour Constant orange (Teradata brand) Hover text Object name + type (table / view / dataset)

The graph is directed but Sankey rendering is symmetric — read it as a flow of derived columns rather than as physical I/O.

docs.title: Combining with the consumer agent
docs.text:
The agent's LINEAGE intent calls build_teradata_dependency_graph under the hood and synthesises a plain-language summary alongside a depth-ordered dependency list:

from tdfs4ds.agent import consumer_agent
print(consumer_agent("Where does total_amount come from?", thread_id="x"))

For a richer multi-process view, build the graph yourself and use plot_lineage_sankey — it scales to dozens of nodes without losing readability.

docs.title: Recipes
docs.text:
Short, copy-pasteable end-to-end snippets for common situations.

One-line bootstrap

Connect, setup, and activate a domain in three lines using .env defaults.

Upload & document in one go

Combine upload_features(document=True, optimize_query=True, update_view=True).

Hybrid date × partition filter

Iterate over (date × region) combinations with a single FilterManager.

Launch the chatbot

Build the vector index and start the Gradio UI in two cells.

docs.title: Hybrid date × partition filter
docs.text:
Iterate one (date, region) combination at a time. The hybrid FilterManager view exposes both BUSINESS_DATE (the time column) and the partition column.

from tdfs4ds.utils.filter_management import FilterManager
from tdfs4ds.utils.lineage          import crystallize_view

source_data = tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema('source_db', 'TRANSACTIONS'))

# 1. Build the hybrid manager (5 most-recent combinations for validation)
fm = FilterManager(table_name='FS_FILTER_REGION_DATE', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
fm.load_filter(
    tdml.DataFrame.from_query("""
        SELECT REGION, TX_DATE
        FROM (
            SELECT DISTINCT REGION, TX_DATE
            FROM source_db.TRANSACTIONS
            QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY TX_DATE DESC, REGION) <= 5
        ) t
    """),
    time_column='TX_DATE',
)
print('Combinations:', fm.nb_filters)

# 2. Feature view uses scalar subqueries on both filter columns
df_features_raw = tdml.DataFrame.from_query(f"""
    SELECT A.*
    FROM source_db.TRANSACTIONS A
    WHERE A.TX_DATE = (SELECT BUSINESS_DATE FROM {fm.schema_name}.{fm.view_name})
      AND A.REGION  = (SELECT REGION        FROM {fm.schema_name}.{fm.view_name})
""")

df_features_proc = crystallize_view(
    df_features_raw.groupby('CUSTOMER_ID').agg({'AMOUNT': ['sum', 'mean']}),
    view_name   = 'FEAT_ENG_TX_HYBRID',
    schema_name = tdfs4ds.SCHEMA,
)

# 3. Register with the FilterManager attached — iterates automatically
tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df_features_proc,
    entity_id     = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    feature_names = ['sum_AMOUNT', 'mean_AMOUNT'],
    filtermanager = fm,
)

Why scalar subqueries: the filter view always exposes exactly one row, so the Teradata optimiser resolves the partition value before scanning the source table. This is what unlocks partition elimination at the storage level.

docs.title: Launch the consumer agent chatbot
docs.text:
Two cells — once the business dictionary is populated and the LLM / embedding settings are in .env:

import teradataml as tdml, tdfs4ds, os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()
tdml.create_context(host=os.getenv('TDML_HOST'),
                    user=os.getenv('TDML_USERNAME'),
                    password=os.getenv('TDML_PASSWORD'))
tdfs4ds.connect(database=os.getenv('TDML_DATABASE'), create_if_missing=True)
tdfs4ds.select_data_domain('MY_PROJECT')

from tdfs4ds.agent import launch_chatbot_with_index

demo = launch_chatbot_with_index(port=7860, force_rebuild=False)
# Opens http://localhost:7860 with the Gradio chatbot + agent trace panel

If the dictionary is empty, fix it first:

from tdfs4ds.genai import document_feature_store_incremental
document_feature_store_incremental(language='English', upload=True)

then re-call launch_chatbot_with_index. The function is idempotent — it detects an existing index, rebuilds incrementally, and reuses an existing chatbot if one is already running.

docs.title: One-line bootstrap
docs.text:
When TDFS4DS_* and TDML_* are set in a .env next to your notebook, the preamble fits in three lines.

import teradataml as tdml, tdfs4ds, os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()
tdml.create_context(host=os.getenv('TDML_HOST'),
                    user=os.getenv('TDML_USERNAME'),
                    password=os.getenv('TDML_PASSWORD'))
tdfs4ds.connect(database=os.getenv('TDML_DATABASE'), create_if_missing=True)

tdfs4ds reads TDFS4DS_SCHEMA, TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN, and the LLM / embedding config from the same .env automatically — no further setup needed before calling upload_features, document_process, or launching the consumer agent.

docs.title: Upload, optimise, document — in one go
docs.text:
upload_features accepts three AI toggles. When an instruct model is configured, you can register a feature engineering view, let the query optimizer try to rewrite it, replace the view if the score improves, and generate the documentation — all from a single call.

tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df,
    entity_id     = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    feature_names = ['nb_transactions', 'total_amount', 'avg_amount'],
    metadata      = {'project': 'customer_analytics'},

    # AI add-ons (silently skipped when no instruct model is configured)
    optimize_query = True,    # run the 9-step optimiser
    update_view    = True,    # REPLACE VIEW with the winner when score improves
    document       = True,    # populate process docs + EXPLAIN score
    max_candidates = 5,       # override tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES

    # Optional dataset build + documentation
    dataset_view_name = 'ds_customer_spend',
)

What runs (in order):

  1. Register entity, features, and process.
  2. Persist register_process_view SQL (process catalog, data distribution, optional FilterManager).
  3. skill_optimize_query — generate candidates, score, optionally REPLACE VIEW.
  4. document_process_incremental — business logic + per-feature + EXPLAIN docs.
  5. run(process_id) — ingest values via the (now-optimized) view.
  6. If dataset_view_name set: build the dataset view, then document_dataset_incremental.

Reports for the optimiser land under {QUERY_OPTIMIZER_REPORT_DIR}/{DATA_DOMAIN}/. Failures in steps 3 / 4 / 6 are non-fatal — the ingestion result is never affected.

When None it defaults to tdfs4ds.SCHEMA_VIEW if a dedicated view database is configured, else to schema_name (co-located with the tables — byte-identical to the historical single-database behaviour). existing_view_objects : set, optional Pre-computed set of lower-cased view names present in schema_name_view. Only consulted when the view database differs from the table database.

docs.title: FilterManager
docs.text:
A utility for managing dynamic, versioned filter sets as database-backed views.

The FilterManager enables lightweight scenario management by storing multiple filter definitions in a hidden Teradata table and exposing a public view that dynamically switches between them by filter_id. Each row in the hidden table represents a complete filter configuration. The active configuration is controlled by updating the view definition rather than rewriting table data.

Key Features
  • Store multiple filter states (scenarios) indexed by filter_id
  • Switch filter states instantly by updating a view
  • Optionally include time-based slicing using a BUSINESS_DATE column
  • Clone filters between managers (soft or hard clone modes)
  • Prune obsolete filters to control table size
  • Retrieve current and historical filter definitions
Workflow Overview
  1. Create a FilterManager pointing to a target view name.
  2. Load one or more filter definitions using load_filter().
  3. Switch active filters using update(filter_id).
  4. Inspect the active filter via display() or view DDL.
  5. Optionally prune or clone filters as needed.
How It Works Internally
  • A hidden table named <view_name>_HIDDEN stores filter definitions.
  • A Teradata view named <view_name> exposes only the active filter row.
  • Each filter automatically receives a sequential filter_id (ROW_NUMBER() ordering ensures deterministic assignment).
  • If time-based filtering is used via time_column, a BUSINESS_DATE column is added and projected in all operations.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default table_name str

Public view name to manage or create.

required schema_name str

Teradata schema where artifacts will be created.

required filter_id_name str

Name of the filter ID column. Defaults to 'filter_id'.

'filter_id' time_column str

Optional name of a timestamp column from input DataFrames that maps to a BUSINESS_DATE column for time-aware filters.

None

Attributes:

Name Type Description schema_name str

Target schema for view and hidden table.

table_name str

Name of hidden table storing filters (auto-suffixed with _HIDDEN).

view_name str

Name of public view pointing to current filter.

filter_id_name str

Column containing filter ID.

nb_filters int | None

Number of stored filters (None until initialized).

col_names list[str] | None

Columns projected by the view (data columns only).

time_filtering bool | None

True if time-based filtering enabled.

Notes
  • Database objects are only created when load_filter() is first called.
  • Safe for iterative pipeline runs—auto-detects existing artifacts.
  • Designed for large production tables and Teradata-native workflows.

docs.title: __init__
docs.text:
__init__(table_name, schema_name, filter_id_name='filter_id', time_column=None, storage=None, ofs_map='TD_MAP2')

Initialize the FilterManager.

If the hidden table/view already exist, metadata (column names, maximum filter id, and time filtering status) are detected and cached. If they do not exist yet, attributes are initialized but no objects are created until load_filter() is called.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default storage

Where to create the hidden backing table when this manager (re)creates it. None (default) = system default (block storage, unchanged DDL); 'OFS' = Object File System on Vantage Cloud Lake (MAP = <ofs_map>, STORAGE = TD_OFSSTORAGE). Per-manager, so block and OFS managers can coexist in one session. Only affects table creation; reading an existing table is unchanged. NOTE: for a tdstone2-Apply view, storage='OFS' is necessary but not sufficient on its own — the ACC also rejects scalar subqueries against this filter (Error 6881); reference it via a JOIN instead. OFS + JOIN scores; OFS + scalar subquery still 6881s (see tdfs4ds.utils.storage).

None ofs_map

Map paired with STORAGE = TD_OFSSTORAGE when storage='OFS'.

'TD_MAP2'

docs.title: load_filter
docs.text:
load_filter(df, primary_index=None, time_column=None)

Load a new filter set into the hidden table and (re)point the public view at filter_id=1.

Each row in df is assigned a deterministic filter_id based on ROW_NUMBER() over the ordered set of its columns (plus BUSINESS_DATE when time filtering is enabled). If time_column is provided, values from that column are copied into BUSINESS_DATE and the view will include that time dimension.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default df DataFrame

Incoming filter definitions (one row per filter).

required primary_index list[str]

Primary index columns for the hidden table. Defaults to ['filter_id'] when omitted.

None time_column str

Name of the time column in df to map into BUSINESS_DATE. If provided, time-based filtering is enabled.

None

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If time_column is provided but not present in df.

Retrieve the currently applied filter condition from the database.

This method executes a SQL query that aggregates the filter columns from a filter manager view and returns the result either as a Python dictionary or a raw JSON string.

Parameters:

Format of the returned filter condition. - "dict": Returns a Python dictionary (default). - "str": Returns a raw JSON string.

Example

Generate a SQL query to retrieve records for available entity IDs based on selected features.

Can be a list, dictionary, or string. selected_features (dict): A dictionary of selected features with their versions. other (optional): An additional DataFrame object for more complex queries. Defaults to None. time_column (str, optional): The name of the time column to use for temporal conditions. Defaults to None.

docs.title: write_where_clause_filter
docs.text:
write_where_clause_filter(filtermanager, filter_conditions, list_entity_id, filter_alias='FILTER', table_alias='A')

Constructs a SQL WHERE clause based on filter criteria.

This function generates a SQL WHERE clause for filtering records in a database. The filter_conditions parameter is a dictionary containing key-value pairs, where each key corresponds to a column name in filtermanager.col_names, and each value is an entity ID or filter value. The clause is constructed using aliases for the table and filter provided through filter_alias and table_alias.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default filtermanager object

An object that contains a col_names attribute, which is a list of valid column names used for validation against the keys in the filter dictionary.

required filter_conditions dict

A dictionary where keys represent column names that must match filtermanager.col_names, and values represent entity IDs or specific filter values.

required list_entity_id list

A list of entity IDs used to validate or restrict values in the filter dictionary.

required filter_alias str

The alias used for the filter table in the SQL query. Defaults to 'FILTER'.

'FILTER' table_alias str

The alias used for the main table in the SQL query. Defaults to 'A'.

'A'

Returns:

Name Type Description str

A SQL WHERE clause string that joins each column and entity ID in filter using the format <filter_alias>.<column> = <table_alias>.<value> with 'AND' operators.

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If any key in filter is not found in filtermanager.col_names or if any value in filter is not in list_entity_id.

Example

filtermanager.col_names = ['column1', 'column2'] filter_conditions = {'column1': 'entity_id1', 'column2': 'entity_id2'} list_entity_id = ['entity_id1', 'entity_id2'] write_where_clause_filter(filtermanager, filter_conditions, list_entity_id) 'FILTER.column1=A.entity_id1 AND FILTER.column2=A.entity_id2'

docs.title: get_feature_versions
docs.text:
get_feature_versions(entity_name, features, domain=None)

Retrieve version identifiers for one or more features belonging to a given entity.

The function queries the underlying metadata tables to find the process (i.e., feature‑version) records that match the supplied entity and feature names. It returns a mapping from each requested feature name to either:

  • None – if no matching rows were found.
  • A single UUID string – if exactly one matching row exists for the feature.
  • A list of dictionaries – if more than one matching row is found; each dictionary contains: process_id – the UUID of the process that produced the version, process_view_name – the human‑readable view name associated with that process.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
entity_name : str | list[str] The name (or names) of the entity whose features we are querying. If a single string is supplied it is treated as a singleton list.

str | list[str]

One or more feature names to look up. Accepts a single string or an iterable of strings; if a single string is provided it is wrapped in a list internally.

str, optional

The data‑domain partition to filter on. If omitted the default tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN constant is used.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, str | None | list[dict]] A dictionary keyed by feature name. Each value is either: * None – no records were found for that feature. * str – a single UUID string when exactly one row matched. * list[dict] – multiple matches; each dict has keys process_id and process_view_name.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The query joins the feature catalog view with the process catalog (specifically the “feature split” view) on data domain, entity ID, and feature name.
  • SQL string literals are escaped by doubling single quotes; this is a lightweight escape that suffices for the current use‑case.
  • The function preserves insertion order of features in the returned dictionary (Python 3.7+ guarantees dict order).
  • When tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE is true, the generated SQL statement is printed to stdout – useful for troubleshooting.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: get_feature_versions('user', ['age', 'income'])

docs.title: get_list_entity
docs.text:
get_list_entity(domain=None)

Retrieve a list of unique entity names from a specified data domain.

This function executes a database query to extract distinct entity names from a feature catalog, filtered by the provided data domain. If no domain is specified, it defaults to a predefined data domain.

domain (str, optional): The data domain to filter the entity names. Defaults to None, in which case a predefined domain is used.

Returns: DataFrame: A pandas-like DataFrame containing the unique entity names.

docs.title: get_list_features
docs.text:
get_list_features(entity_name, domain=None)

Retrieve a list of feature names associated with a specific entity or entities from a given data domain.

This function constructs and executes a database query to extract feature names for the specified entity or entities from a feature catalog, filtered by the provided data domain. If no domain is specified, it defaults to a predefined data domain.

entity_name (str or list): The name of the entity or a list of entity names to fetch features for. domain (str, optional): The data domain to filter the feature names. Defaults to None, where a predefined domain is used.

Returns: DataFrame: A pandas-like DataFrame containing the feature names associated with the given entity or entities.

docs.title: follow_up_report
docs.text:
follow_up_report(filtermanager: Optional[object] = None, process_id: Optional[str] = None)

Return a follow-up report as a tdml.DataFrame, optionally filtered by process_id and/or a filtermanager's applied filter.

Behavior by arguments
  • process_id is None and filtermanager is None: Return all rows from SCHEMA.FOLLOW_UP_NAME, sorted by START_DATETIME desc.
  • process_id is not None and filtermanager is None: Return rows for the given PROCESS_ID.
  • process_id is not None and filtermanager is not None: Return rows for the given PROCESS_ID whose APPLIED_FILTER matches the JSON_AGG of filtermanager's columns coming from its schema/view.
  • process_id is None and filtermanager is not None: Return rows whose APPLIED_FILTER matches the JSON_AGG of filtermanager (no PROCESS_ID constraint).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default filtermanager Optional[object]

An object exposing col_names, schema_name, and view_name. Its columns are aggregated via JSON_AGG(col1, col2, ...) to compare against A.APPLIED_FILTER.

None process_id Optional[str]

Optional process identifier used to filter by PROCESS_ID.

None

Returns:

Type Description

tdml.DataFrame: The resulting dataframe sorted by START_DATETIME (descending).

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If filtermanager is provided but is missing required attributes or has an empty col_names list.

RuntimeError

If the query fails.

docs.title: _cfg_load
docs.text:
_cfg_load(module)

Called automatically during import tdfs4ds.

Load order (lowest → highest priority): JSON config file → .env file → OS env vars

docs.title: load_config
docs.text:
load_config(path=None, dotenv_path=None)

(Re-)load configuration from external sources.

Useful for pointing at non-default config files or refreshing values mid-session. Applies sources in priority order (lowest first):

  1. JSON config file (at path or auto-discovered)
  2. .env file (at dotenv_path or auto-discovered)
  3. OS TDFS4DS_* environment variables (always applied)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: path : str or pathlib.Path, optional Explicit path to a JSON config file. Skips auto-discovery when given. dotenv_path : str or pathlib.Path, optional Explicit path to a .env file. Skips auto-discovery when given.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Auto-discover all sources::

import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.load_config()

Explicit paths::

tdfs4ds.load_config(
    path='/project/configs/feature_store.json',
    dotenv_path='/project/.env.local',
)

JSON config format (tdfs4ds.json)::

{
    "schema": "MY_DATABASE",
    "data_domain": "MY_PROJECT",
    "display_logs": true,
    "store_feature": "MERGE",
    "varchar_size": 1024,
    "instruct_model_provider": "openai",
    "instruct_model_model": "gpt-4o"
}

Keys are case-insensitive. instruct_model_api_key is rejected from JSON config — use a .env file or OS env var instead.

.env file format::

TDFS4DS_SCHEMA=MY_DATABASE
TDFS4DS_SCHEMA_VIEW=MY_VIEWS_DATABASE
TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN=MY_PROJECT
TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY=sk-...

Only TDFS4DS_* variables are read from the .env file. Add .env to .gitignore to keep secrets out of source control.

docs.title: crystallize_view
docs.text:
crystallize_view(tddf, view_name, schema_name, output_view=True)

Crystallizes a TeradataML DataFrame into a database view.

This function takes a TeradataML DataFrame (tddf) and converts its SQL representation into a database view. It analyzes dependencies, creates sub-views if needed, and constructs the final view with the specified view_name and schema_name. The resulting view can optionally be returned as a TeradataML DataFrame.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default tddf DataFrame

The TeradataML DataFrame to be converted into a view.

required view_name str

The name of the main view to be created or replaced in the database.

required schema_name str

The schema where the view will be created.

required output_view bool

If True, returns a TeradataML DataFrame representing the created view. If False, the function performs the operation without returning a DataFrame. Default is True.

True

Returns:

Type Description

teradataml.DataFrame: A TeradataML DataFrame representation of the created or replaced view, if output_view is True. Otherwise, returns None.

Raises:

Type Description Exception

If there is an error during SQL execution for creating sub-views or the main view.

Notes
  • The tddf parameter must be a valid TeradataML DataFrame.
  • The function generates a dependency graph for the SQL query representing the input DataFrame. Sub-views are created or replaced with new names based on this graph.
  • Column names are replaced case-insensitively to ensure consistency in SQL queries.
  • The function replaces "CREATE VIEW" with "REPLACE VIEW" to handle updates.
Example

To crystallize a TeradataML DataFrame into a view:

result_df = crystallize_view(my_teradataml_df, 'my_view', 'my_schema')

reconnect_teradata(raise_error: bool = False) -> bool

Removes any stale context, then opens a fresh one. Returns True on success, False on failure (or raises when raise_error=True). A no-op-safe call: if the credentials are not configured it logs a warning and returns False.

docs.title: rebuild_lineage_graph
docs.text:
rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=None, all_data_domains: bool = True, check: bool = True, prune: bool = True, extra_views=None) -> int

Rebuild the persisted lineage graph — fully, or scoped to a list of objects.

Use this to initialise FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH on a store that already has registered processes/datasets, to refresh a graph whose edges have drifted, or to scoped-refresh just the objects you edited. The incremental write hook (:func:record_process_lineage, called from upload_features) keeps lineage current going forward; this function recomputes on demand.

The table is valid-time temporal: every write is diff-based, so re-running on an unchanged store touches zero rows (no history churn). There is no blind delete+insert — :func:record_process_lineage leaves unchanged edges alone, inserts new ones, and closes (CURRENT VALIDTIME DELETE) ones that disappeared.

Full refresh (objects=None):

  1. Ensure the table/view exist (idempotent).
  2. Seed from every registered process view and every dataset (all data domains by default), re-deriving each subgraph via :func:record_process_lineage (diff-based — unchanged edges are untouched).
  3. When prune=True (default), edges whose parent object is no longer a seed (e.g. a since-dropped process) are closed with CURRENT VALIDTIME DELETE. This is a targeted prune of stale parents, not a wipe — it does not re-open the edges that are still valid.

Scoped refresh (objects=[...]): no prune, no catalog-wide seed. The names are validated by :func:resolve_objects (raises on any unmatched name — case / quoting / db.name safe), then record_process_lineage refreshes each object's own outgoing edges (diff-based). Use this when a (possibly non-process) view was edited. When check=True, :func:check_downstream_processes runs afterwards.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] | None When None (default), full refresh. Otherwise refresh only these objects. all_data_domains : bool Full-refresh only: all DATA_DOMAINs (default) or the active one. check : bool Scoped refresh only: run the downstream EXPLAIN compile check afterwards. prune : bool Full-refresh only: close edges of parents that are no longer registered (default True). Set False to never delete (purely additive refresh). extra_views : str | Iterable[str] | None Full-refresh only: additional standalone view names to seed into the graph alongside every registered process view + dataset. Use this to capture a non-process / non-dataset view (e.g. a hand-built or crystallized reporting view) that nothing registered references — it would otherwise never enter FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH. Each name is validated via :func:resolve_objects (case / quoting / db.name safe; raises on any unmatched name). There is no automatic schema scan — only the names you pass.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: int Number of edge changes written (0 when nothing changed).

docs.title: check_downstream_processes
docs.text:
check_downstream_processes(objects)

Deterministic compile check for the process views downstream of changed objects.

When a non-process view changes its result columns, the process views that read it can silently break. This runs EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM <process_view> on every registered process downstream of the given object(s) — EXPLAIN only verifies the query compiles (all columns resolve), it does not execute it. Same compile-validity idiom as the pre-check in upload_features.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] Changed object name(s); resolved via :func:resolve_objects.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict {"checked": int, "ok": [view, ...], "broken": [{"view", "process_id", "error"}, ...]}. Broken processes are also logged as a warning.

docs.title: resolve_objects
docs.text:
resolve_objects(objects, *, require_exists: bool = True, graph=None)

Normalise and verify a user-supplied list of object names so case / " quoting / db.name variance never causes a silent mismatch.

Each input ("db"."NAME", db.name, NAME, any case) is parsed with the same _parse_node_name + _norm helpers record_process_lineage uses to write the table, so a resolved name compares equal to stored rows. An object is considered to exist if it is a node in the persisted lineage graph, or (fallback, for a freshly-created view not yet in the graph) present in DBC.TablesV.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] One name or a list of names. require_exists : bool When True (default), raise ValueError naming every unmatched input. When False, unmatched inputs are logged and dropped. graph : networkx.DiGraph, optional Pre-loaded lineage graph (avoids a reload when the caller already has one).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: (resolved, unmatched) resolved is a list of canonical (DB_UPPER, OBJECT_UPPER) tuples (default DB filled from tdfs4ds.SCHEMA when the input was unqualified); unmatched is the list of original input strings that resolved to nothing.

dataset_catalog()

Retrieve a Teradata DataFrame of all datasets registered in the dataset catalog.

Cross-store federation (v0.3.1.148): when read-only stores are attached, browses a UNION ALL of every store's dataset-catalog view (with a FEATURE_STORE provenance column); otherwise byte-identical. docs.title: get_dataset_entity

get_dataset_entity(dataset_id=None)

Return the entity definition (columns and types) for the given dataset ID. If dataset_id is None, returns entity info for all registered datasets. Federated across read-only stores when attached (dataset IDs are globally unique UUIDs, so the dataset_id filter selects the owning store's rows).
