docs.title: get_process_id
docs.text:
get_process_id(view_name)

Retrieves the process ID associated with a given view name in a database.

This function is designed to obtain the process ID for a specified view within a database. Initially, it formats the provided view_name to conform to the database's naming conventions, including handling scenarios where the view name is prefixed with a database name. After formatting the view name, the function queries a list of processes using an assumedly predefined function list_processes. It then filters this list to find the process corresponding to the formatted view name and returns the associated process ID. The function assumes specific column names ('VIEW_NAME' and 'PROCESS_ID') in the data structure returned by list_processes and that these columns are in a specific format (e.g., uppercase for view names).

view_name (str): The name of the view for which the process ID is to be retrieved. This can be either just the view name or a combination of database name and view name, formatted as 'database.view'.

int: The process ID associated with the given view name. This ID is retrieved from a list of processes, where it is associated with the view name that matches the input.

Raises: IndexError: If no process is found with the given view name.

docs.title: remove_process
docs.text:
remove_process(process_id, nonsequence_validtime=True)

Deletes a process from the feature store's process catalog based on the given process ID.

Also removes all associated documentation (business logic, features, EXPLAIN analysis, and business dictionary entries) before deleting the catalog rows. Documentation cleanup is non-fatal: a failure on any documentation table is logged as a warning and does not prevent the process from being removed.

process_id (str): The unique identifier of the process to be removed. This ID is used to specify which process should be deleted from the process catalog. nonsequence_validtime (bool): When True (default), uses NONSEQUENCED VALIDTIME DELETE to remove all temporal versions from the catalog.

list: SQL query strings that, when executed, delete the specified process from the process catalog and data distribution table.

Note: The actual execution of the SQL queries is handled by the execute_query_wrapper decorator.

docs.title: remove_entity
docs.text:
remove_entity(entity, data_domain=None)

Removes all database entities (tables and views) associated with a given entity name within a specific data domain, and then removes the entity's metadata from the feature catalog.

This function first constructs a SQL query to select distinct table and view names associated with the given entity name and data domain from the feature catalog. It then iterates over the list of tables and views, attempting to drop each one. Progress and status of these operations are tracked using a progress bar. If an error occurs during the drop operation, the error message is displayed in the progress bar description. Finally, the function constructs and executes another SQL query to delete the entity's metadata from the feature catalog.

Parameters: - entity (str): The name of the entity to be removed. - data_domain (str, optional): The data domain where the entity is located. If not provided, the default data domain from tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN is used.

The function utilizes the tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE flag to control the printing of SQL queries for debugging purposes. If DEBUG_MODE is set to True, the queries for selecting and deleting entities are printed to the console.

Dependencies: - Requires the tdfs4ds module for configuration constants like DATA_DOMAIN, SCHEMA, and FEATURE_CATALOG_NAME, as well as the DEBUG_MODE flag. - Requires the tdml module or object with an execute_sql method for database operations. - Uses the tqdm module to display a progress bar for the operations.

Note: - This function assumes that the necessary permissions are in place for dropping tables and views, as well as deleting metadata entries from the feature catalog. - Exception handling within the dropping operations is limited to displaying the first line of any encountered error messages in the progress bar description. Further error handling or cleanup may be necessary depending on the specific requirements and database configuration.

  • None. The primary effect of this function is to alter the state of the database by removing entities and their metadata, not to return data to the caller.

docs.title: delete_feature
docs.text:
delete_feature(feature_name, entity_id, data_domain=None)

Delete the values of a specific feature for given entities from the feature table within a specified data domain.

This function constructs and executes two SQL queries against a Teradata database to remove a feature specified by its name and entity identifiers. The first query retrieves the table name where the feature resides, based on the feature name, entity, and data domain. The second query deletes the feature values from the identified table.

Parameters: - feature_name (str): The name of the feature to be removed. - entity_id (str or list of str): Entity identifier(s). If a string is provided, it will be converted to a single-element list. The list is always sorted alphabetically before use. - data_domain (str, optional): The data domain where the feature is located. If not specified, the function uses the default data domain defined in tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN.

Behavior: - The function checks if the DEBUG_MODE flag in the tdfs4ds module is set to True. If so, it prints the generated SQL queries and the resolved table name for debugging. - If the feature table cannot be resolved, the function returns without executing a delete query.

Returns: - None

Note: - The function assumes the presence of a module tdfs4ds with predefined constants such as DATA_DOMAIN, SCHEMA, FEATURE_CATALOG_NAME_VIEW, and a flag DEBUG_MODE. - It also assumes a tdml module or object with an execute_sql method capable of executing SQL queries against a Teradata database and fetching the results.

  • Exceptions related to SQL execution or connection issues may be raised but are not explicitly handled, except for printing the error message.

docs.title: remove_feature
docs.text:
remove_feature(feature_name, entity_id, data_domain=None)

Attempts to remove a specific feature from the feature catalog and any associated data, within a given data domain in the tdfs4ds package.

This function performs two main operations to remove a feature: 1. Attempts to delete the feature's data using a helper function delete_feature, which handles the specific logic for removing feature data from its storage location. If this operation encounters any exceptions, the function catches these, logs the first word of the exception message, and exits. 2. If the delete_feature operation succeeds, the function constructs and executes a SQL query to remove the feature's metadata entry from the feature catalog, cleaning up the catalog by removing the reference to the now-deleted feature.

Parameters: - feature_name (str): The name of the feature to be removed. - entity_id (str or list of str): Entity identifier(s). If a string is provided, it will be converted to a single-element list. The list is always sorted alphabetically before use. - data_domain (str, optional): The data domain where the feature is located. If not provided, the function uses the default data domain from the tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN setting.

This function utilizes a DEBUG_MODE flag from the tdfs4ds module to control the logging of SQL queries for debugging purposes. If DEBUG_MODE is set to True, the deletion query is printed to the console.

  • None. The function's primary goal is to modify the database state by removing entries, and it does not return any value.

Note: - The function is part of the tdfs4ds package and relies on external modules tdfs4ds for configuration constants and tdml for database interaction. It also assumes the existence of a delete_feature function responsible for the initial attempt to delete the feature's data. - Exception handling within this function is limited to logging the first word of any exception message encountered during the delete_feature call. Further error handling, especially for the SQL deletion operation, may be necessary depending on application requirements.

Raises: - Exceptions from the delete_feature function are caught and logged, but not re-raised. - SQL execution or connection exceptions might occur but are not explicitly handled by this function.

docs.title: drop_dataset
docs.text:
drop_dataset(dataset_id: str, schema_name: str = None, purge: bool = False) -> None

Remove a dataset entry (catalog + entity + feature rows) by DATASET_ID.

purge=True physically removes all temporal rows (history included); the default soft-deletes by valid-time-closing the currently-valid rows.

docs.title: run
docs.text:
run(process_id, return_dataset=False, force_compute=False, force_varchar_length=None, dataset_view_name=None, grouped=False)

Execute a specific process from the feature store identified by the process ID. Uses global logger for diagnostics (gated by tdfs4ds.DISPLAY_LOGS).

grouped is forwarded to build_dataset whenever a dataset view is (re)built. See upload_features for details. Default: False (classic multi-join, unchanged behaviour). Note: not persisted in the process catalog — re-running an existing process_id without passing grouped=True again reverts to the classic join.

docs.title: upload_features
docs.text:
upload_features(df, entity_id, feature_names, metadata={}, primary_index=None, partitioning='', filtermanager=None, entity_null_substitute={}, force_compute=True, force_varchar_length=1024, dataset_view_name=None, optimize_query=False, update_view=True, document=True, max_candidates=None, cascade_redocumentation=False, vector_indexing=False, sql_query=None, target_view_name=None, art_sql=None, art_layers=None, grouped=False)

Upload feature data to the feature store for a specified entity. All diagnostics go through logger_safe() which respects tdfs4ds.DISPLAY_LOGS.

The feature source can be supplied in three ways:

  1. A teradataml DataFrame (df=...) — the classic path.
  2. A fully-qualified view name (df='SCHEMA.view') — for SQL users who already have a view. The schema is mandatory; a bare view name (no schema) is rejected. The view must already exist and be executable.
  3. A SQL query (sql_query='SELECT ...') together with a mandatory target_view_name='SCHEMA.view'. The query is validated with EXPLAIN, a view is created at target_view_name (the schema is mandatory; the view must NOT already exist), and that view — which is permanent and becomes the process view re-executed by run(process_id) — is used as the feature source. When sql_query is given it takes precedence over df.

Recoverable input problems (missing schema, target view already exists, SQL or view fails to compile / is not executable) do not raise a traceback: a clear message with a fix is logged via logger_safe('error', …) and the function returns None. Check the log when a call returns None.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: sql_query : str, optional A SQL SELECT defining the feature source. Requires target_view_name. The view is created from this query (after an EXPLAIN check) and kept. target_view_name : str, optional 'SCHEMA.view' — where to create the view for sql_query (mandatory when sql_query is given; schema required; must not already exist). art_sql : list of str, optional Opt-in UAF ART view mode. A list of solitary SQL statements that materialise a Teradata UAF operator's Analytical Result Table (ART) using EXECUTE FUNCTION ... INTO VOLATILE ART(<name>). When provided, the process is registered with PROCESS_TYPE='uaf art view' and, on every run(), these statements are executed in list order, per FilterManager partition, immediately before the ingestion CTAS (same session, so the session-scoped volatile ART is visible to the CTAS). This unlocks features whose value lives in a secondary ART layer (e.g. ARIMA fit-metrics R_SQUARE/MAPE/AIC in ARTFITMETADATA, residual tests over ARTFITRESIDUALS, TD_PACF) — which a single ingestion statement cannot reach. df/sql_query+target_view_name still define the plain feature view (the one reading the layer via TD_EXTRACT_RESULTS(ART_SPEC(..., LAYER(...)))); art_sql is stored in the process METADATA and never turned into a view (a view cannot contain INTO VOLATILE ART). Order matters — a later statement may read an ART an earlier one created. For this mode optimize_query is force-disabled and the view EXPLAIN in documentation is skipped (the ART does not exist at optimize/document time). Requires art_layers. Default: None (classic 'denormalized view' path, unchanged). art_layers : list of str, optional Required when art_sql is given: the declared LAYER(...) name(s) the feature view reads (e.g. ['ARTFITMETADATA']). Used for documentation and lineage only — it is not validated against the view DDL (the view is authoritative for what is computed). Default: None. entity_id : str or list (recommended), or dict (advanced override) Entity / primary-index key(s). Pass a string for a single-column key or a list for a composite key, e.g. 'CUSTOMER_ID' or ['CUSTOMER_ID', 'EVENT_DATE'] — this is the recommended form. You do not need to specify SQL data types: they are inferred from the source DataFrame with their exact length and character set preserved (a CHAR(14) key stays CHAR(14) in the created feature table, not truncated to CHAR(1)). Only pass a dict (e.g. {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT', 'EVENT_DATE': 'DATE'}) in the rare case where you need to override an inferred type; the supplied type strings are then used verbatim. entity_null_substitute : dict, optional Per-column replacement values for NULLs in entity keys (e.g. {'CLI_CD': '-'}), applied via COALESCE. NULL values in an entity key column that is NOT covered here will raise a ValueError during ingestion: a NULL key produces a cartesian-product MERGE (severe skew / storage blow-up). Filter the NULL rows upstream or provide a substitute. Default: {}. optimize_query : bool, optional If True and an instruct model is configured, run the query optimizer on the registered process after ingestion. The full report is saved to a file named tdfs4ds_optim_{timestamp}_{view}_{process_id}.md in the current working directory. Only the score summary is emitted to the log. Default: False. update_view : bool, optional If True and query optimization finds an improvement, replace the process view DDL with the optimized SQL. Default: True. document : bool, optional If True and an instruct model is configured, run incremental documentation on the registered process (and after any view update). If dataset_view_name is also provided, document_dataset_incremental is called after run() so that the dataset-level documentation can propagate the already-generated feature descriptions bottom-up. Default: True. max_candidates : int, optional Maximum number of SQL rewrite candidates to generate during query optimization. Overrides tdfs4ds.QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES for this call only. Default: None (uses the global value). cascade_redocumentation : bool, optional If True (and document=True with an instruct model configured), re-document every downstream process/dataset that depends on this process — but only when this process's documentation actually changed. The descendant set is read from the persisted lineage graph (FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH). This propagates a changed parent meaning down to everything that consumes it, at the cost of one extra LLM pass per affected descendant; hence it is opt-in. Default: False. vector_indexing : bool, optional If True (and document=True with an instruct model configured), rebuild the vector index after documentation is written so the consumer agent's search results reflect the new/updated business descriptions immediately. Uses an additive upsert (force_rebuild=False) — fast but stale entries from deleted/renamed objects persist until a full rebuild_vector_indexing() is run. Non-fatal: a failure logs a warning without aborting the ingestion. Default: False. grouped : bool, optional Forwarded to build_dataset whenever a dataset view is built (i.e. when dataset_view_name is set or tdfs4ds.BUILD_DATASET_AT_UPLOAD is True). If False (default), uses one sub-query per feature joined together — the classic multi-join approach; every feature sub-query can be targeted by a PI-equality filter, keeping single-entity lookups single-AMP. If True, groups features sharing the same storage table and process version into one sub-query with MAX(CASE WHEN …) pivoting, reducing JOIN count from N_features to N_distinct_(table, version) pairs — recommended for wide datasets with many features from the same source table. See build_dataset for the full explanation. Default: False (unchanged behaviour). Not persisted in the process catalog — a later run(process_id) without grouped=True reverts to the classic join for any dataset rebuild.

docs.title: _upload_features
docs.text:
_upload_features(df, entity_id, feature_names, feature_versions=_FEATURE_VERSION_DEFAULT, primary_index=None, partitioning='', filtermanager=None, entity_null_substitute={}, process_id=None, force_compute=False, force_varchar_length=None, dataset_view_name=None, grouped=False, art_sql=None)

Uploads a set of features into the Feature Store for a given entity.

This function registers an entity and its associated features in the feature catalog if they are not already defined, prepares the data for ingestion, and stores it in the feature store. It also supports incremental feature computation and conditional execution depending on prior runs.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: df : pandas.DataFrame Input dataframe containing entity keys and feature columns to upload. entity_id : str, list, or dict Identifier(s) for the entity. List/str inputs have their SQL types inferred from df with exact length + character set preserved (e.g. CHAR(14) keys are not truncated); a dict is used verbatim. feature_names : list of str List of feature column names to upload from df. feature_versions : dict or str, optional Feature version(s). Default is _FEATURE_VERSION_DEFAULT ('dev.0.0'). primary_index : str or list, optional Primary index for Teradata feature tables. partitioning : str, optional Partitioning clause. Default is ''. filtermanager : FilterManager, optional If provided, features are built iteratively per filter step. entity_null_substitute : dict, optional Replacement values for nulls in entity keys (applied via COALESCE). A NULL in an entity key column not listed here raises a ValueError during ingestion (NULL keys cause a cartesian-product MERGE). process_id : str, optional Process execution identifier for follow-up logging. force_compute : bool, optional If True, forces recomputation even if already completed. Default is False. force_varchar_length : int, optional If provided, all VARCHAR feature columns are resized to this length. dataset_view_name : str, optional Name of the output dataset view to create after ingestion. grouped : bool, optional Forwarded to build_dataset whenever a dataset view is built. See upload_features for details. Default: False (classic multi-join). art_sql : list of str, optional UAF ART view mode. Forwarded to every _compute_features_batch call so the volatile ART(s) are materialised per partition before ingestion. None for the classic path. See upload_features / _materialise_art.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pandas.DataFrame or None If BUILD_DATASET_AT_UPLOAD is enabled, returns the built dataset. Otherwise None.

docs.title: _compute_features_batch
docs.text:
_compute_features_batch(df, entity_id, feature_names, selected_features, primary_index, partitioning, entity_null_substitute, process_id, filtermanager, dataset_view_name, dataset_already_created, grouped=False, art_sql=None)

Execute one feature ingestion batch: followup_open → [materialise ART] → prepare → store → followup_close (COMPLETED) → optional dataset build.

Returns (features_infos, dataset_or_None) on success. On failure: logs the error, attempts to record FAILED in the followup table, then re-raises. Callers that want to continue after a failure (e.g. the filtermanager loop) should wrap this call in their own try/except and use continue rather than re-raising.

Does NOT call apply_collect_stats — the caller is responsible for that.

grouped is forwarded to build_dataset — see upload_features for details.

art_sql (UAF ART view mode only): a list of INTO VOLATILE ART statements materialised before ingestion, in this same session — so the session-scoped volatile ART is visible to the ingestion CTAS. Runs after filtermanager.update() (done by the caller), so the series-input view's scalar subquery already points at the current partition. None/empty for the classic path (a no-op).

docs.title: upload_tdstone2_scores
docs.text:
upload_tdstone2_scores(model)

Upload features from a tdstone2 model's predictions to the feature store.

Handles the full workflow: extract feature names/types, register in the feature catalog, prepare for ingestion, store in the feature store, and build a dataset view.

Parameters: - model: The tdstone2 model object whose predictions contain features to upload.

Returns: - DataFrame: A DataFrame representing the dataset view created in the feature store.

docs.title: roll_out
docs.text:
roll_out(process_list, time_manager, time_id_start=1, time_id_end=None, force_compute=False, force_display_logs=False)

Execute a series of processes for each date in a time manager, managing time, computation settings, and logging.

Iterates over a range of time steps, updating the TimeManager with each step, and executes all processes in process_list for that time step.

Parameters: - process_list (list): List of process IDs to execute for each time step. - time_manager (TimeManager): Object that manages time-related operations. - time_id_start (int, optional): Starting time step ID. Default is 1. - time_id_end (int, optional): Ending time step ID. If None, runs until last step. - force_compute (bool, optional): Force recomputation even if results exist. Default False. - force_display_logs (bool, optional): Force log display during rollout. Default False.

docs.title: build_dataset
docs.text:
build_dataset(entity_id, selected_features, view_name, schema_name=None, comment=None, return_query=False, feature_store_time=False, join_type='INNER', grouped=False, document=False, vector_indexing=False, record_lineage=False)

Generate a SQL query and create a Teradata view to retrieve records for the specified entity IDs based on selected features.

docs.title: Parameters:
docs.text:
entity_id : list, dict, or str The entity IDs for which records are needed. Can be a list, dictionary, or string. - If list: contains multiple entity IDs. - If dict: keys are the entity IDs. - If str: a single entity ID.

dict

A dictionary where the keys are feature table names, and the values are lists of tuples (feature_id, feature_version, feature_name) specifying the features to retrieve. NOTE: feature_version may be either: - a single UUID string, or - a list of dicts like:

str

The name of the view to be created in the database.

str, optional

The name of the database schema where the view will be created. Defaults to the global tdfs4ds.SCHEMA if not provided.

str, optional

A comment to attach to the created view for documentation purposes. Defaults to None.

bool, optional

If True, the function will return the SQL query string instead of executing it. Defaults to False.

bool, optional

If True, the query will use a specific VALIDTIME based on tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME. If False, the query will use CURRENT VALIDTIME. Defaults to False.

str, optional

Specifies the type of join to use between features: 'INNER' (default) or 'FULL OUTER'.

bool, optional

If False (default), uses one sub-query per feature joined together — the classic multi-join approach. Every individual feature sub-query can be targeted by a PI-equality filter pushed from the outer WHERE, making single-entity lookups single-AMP. If True, groups features that share the same storage table and process version into a single sub-query with MAX(CASE WHEN …) pivoting, reducing the number of JOINs from N_features to N_distinct_(table, version) pairs. A HAVING clause enforces non-NULL for every feature in the group when join_type='INNER', preserving the same missing-feature semantics as the classic approach. PI push-down is preserved for INNER JOIN (outer SELECT uses A1.entity); for FULL OUTER the COALESCE in the outer SELECT prevents push-down, same as the classic approach.

bool, optional

If True, generate and persist business-dictionary documentation for the newly built dataset via document_dataset_incremental (LLM-powered). Requires tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER and tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL to be configured; silently skipped (with an info log) otherwise. Failures are non-fatal — the dataset view is always created/returned regardless. Defaults to False (opt-in). Mirrors upload_features. Leave False when the dataset is built via upload_features/run() — that path already documents it, so enabling it here would double-document.

bool, optional

If True (and document=True and an instruct model is configured), refresh the consumer-agent vector index via build_vector_index(force_rebuild=False) after documentation so the new dataset is searchable immediately. Non-fatal. Defaults to False (opt-in).

bool, optional

If True, persist the dataset view's lineage subgraph into FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH via record_process_lineage (no LLM required). Non-fatal. A standalone dataset with no upstream processes records an isolated node. Defaults to False (opt-in).

docs.title: Returns:
docs.text: str or tdml.DataFrame - If return_query is True, returns the generated SQL query as a string. - Otherwise, returns a Teradata DataFrame containing the results.

docs.title: Raises:
docs.text: ValueError If an invalid join_type is provided.

docs.title: build_dataset_opt
docs.text: build_dataset_opt(entity_id, selected_features, view_name=None, schema_name=None, comment='dataset', no_temporal=False, time_manager=None, query_only=False, entity_null_substitute={}, other=None, time_column=None, filtermanager=None, filter_conditions=None)

docs.title: augment_source_with_features
docs.text:
augment_source_with_features(source_schema, source_name, entity_id, feature_selection, output_view_schema, output_view_name, join_type='LEFT JOIN')

Augment a source table with selected features by creating a view that joins the source data with feature tables based on specified entity identifiers and feature selections.

The join type can be specified as either 'LEFT JOIN' or 'INNER JOIN': - 'LEFT JOIN': Retains all rows from the source table, with missing values for unmatched features. - 'INNER JOIN': Returns only rows where all specified features have matching, non-missing values.

Parameters: source_schema (str): The schema of the source table to augment. source_name (str): The name of the source table to augment. entity_id (str or list of str): The entity ID(s) for joining the source data with feature tables. feature_selection (list): A list of features to select and join with the source data. output_view_schema (str): The schema in which to create the output view. output_view_name (str): The name of the output view to create with augmented features. join_type (str, optional): 'LEFT JOIN' or 'INNER JOIN'. Defaults to 'LEFT JOIN'.

Returns: tdml.DataFrame: A DataFrame object that references the newly created view.

docs.title: retrieve_batch
docs.text:
retrieve_batch(dataset, list_entity_keys, entity_cols=None, *, columns=None, method='auto', volatile_threshold=DEFAULT_VOLATILE_THRESHOLD, conn=None, dedup=True)

Retrieve rows of dataset for a list of entity-key tuples.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: dataset : str Fully-qualified DB.dataset_view to read from. list_entity_keys : list[tuple] Entity-key tuples in the order of entity_cols (each tuple one row's key). entity_cols : list[str] | None Grain/entity key columns. When None they (and their SQL types) are auto-derived from the dataset catalog (needs an active teradataml context). columns : list[str] | None Columns to project; None selects all. method : {'auto','in_list','volatile_join'} 'in_list' builds a WHERE predicate (single-col IN / composite OR-of-ANDs); 'volatile_join' loads the keys into a volatile table co-located on the feature-store primary index and joins; 'auto' (default) uses the volatile join when the unique-key count reaches volatile_threshold OR when the in_list predicate would exceed the parser limit, else the in_list predicate. volatile_threshold : int Key count at/above which 'auto' switches to the volatile join. conn : teradatasql connection | None Optional raw connection (benchmark reuse). None uses the teradataml context. dedup : bool Drop duplicate key tuples first (required for the volatile join; also shrinks the in_list predicate).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[tuple] The fetched rows.

docs.title: resolve_dataset_pi
docs.text:
resolve_dataset_pi(dataset, entity_cols, conn=None)

Resolve the REAL primary index / partitioning of a dataset's feature tables.

Returns a dict::

{"primary_index": [...], "partitioning": "...",
 "source": "DBC.IndicesV" | "fallback_entity_cols",
 "feature_tables": ["db.FS_T_…", …]}

The volatile key table should use primary_index so the join co-locates with the feature storage tables. Fail-open: returns entity_cols when the real distribution can't be read (restricted DBC, view-only, no match).

docs.title: dataset_entity_columns
docs.text:
dataset_entity_columns(dataset)

Return the dataset grain as [(column_name, sql_type), …].

Reads ENTITY (name) + ENTITY_TYPE (type) from the dataset catalog's entity table. Requires an active teradataml context. Returns [] when the dataset can't be resolved.

docs.title: run_benchmark
docs.text:
run_benchmark(dataset, entity_cols=None, *, sample_size=10000, skip=(), batch_sizes=(1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500), large_sizes=(10000, 100000, 1000000), strategy_sizes=(500, 5000, 50000), concurrency=(1, 2, 4), time_travel_dates=(), max_in_list_keys=None, conn_kwargs=None, progress=None)

Run the benchmark against dataset and return the report dict.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
dataset : str Fully-qualified DB.dataset_view to benchmark. entity_cols : list[str] | None Grain/entity key columns. When None, auto-derived from the tdfs4ds dataset catalog (needs an active teradataml context). skip : set[str] Any of {'explain','warm','cold','batch','large_batch', 'batch_strategies','concurrent','time_travel','server_timing'} to skip. strategy_sizes : tuple[int] Batch sizes at which the batch_strategies phase compares the in_list predicate vs the volatile_join retrieval side-by-side. max_in_list_keys : int | None Maximum number of keys put into an in_list predicate. When None it is inferred from the entity-column count as max(50, 500 // n_entity_cols) — wide composite keys produce large per-key disjuncts whose parse time dominates the measurement, so the cap keeps in_list batches cheap. It clamps batch_sizes and the in_list arm of batch_strategies ONLY; the volatile_join arm and large_batch keep their full sizes. conn_kwargs : dict | None Raw teradatasql kwargs; defaults to the TDFS4DS_TD_* environment. progress : callable(str) | None Receives one human-readable line per test phase (for a UI bar).

Returns the {"metadata":…, "results":…} report dict.

docs.title: build_benchmark_html
docs.text: build_benchmark_html(report)

docs.title: write_benchmark_report
docs.text:
write_benchmark_report(report, out_path=None)

Render report to HTML and write it to disk; return the file path.

When out_path is None, writes under {tdfs4ds.BENCHMARK_REPORT_DIR}/ with a timestamped, dataset-derived filename (directory created on demand).

docs.title: analyze_parallel_plan
docs.text:
analyze_parallel_plan(seeds=None, all_domains: bool = True, time_managers=None) -> Dict[str, Any]

Compute a parallel-execution plan for feature-engineering processes.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: seeds : list[str] | None Mixed list of process (id or view name) / dataset / feature names. The plan is the upstream closure of the seeds' producing processes (everything needed to (re)build them). None = the whole store. all_domains : bool Read every data domain (default). False restricts to the active tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN. time_managers : ignored placeholder Reserved for supplying explicit TimeManagers to the content-equivalence check when the catalog has no TIME_* association yet.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict seeds, resolved_seeds, unresolved_seeds, closure, processes (per-pid metadata incl. filter/time manager + wave index + lineage_unknown), waves (ordered list of parallel batches), wave_groups (per-wave grouping by (filter, time) manager), serialize_together (write-conflict groups), unify_candidates (equivalent-but-distinct managers), edges (process dependency edges within the closure), cycle (None or a cycle), plus counts.

docs.title: generate_execution_script
docs.text:
generate_execution_script(plan: Dict[str, Any], *, out_path: Optional[str] = None, mode: str = 'process_pool', force_compute: bool = False, max_workers: Optional[int] = None) -> str

Turn an :func:analyze_parallel_plan result into a runnable .py script.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: plan : dict The plan returned by :func:tdfs4ds.analyze_parallel_plan. out_path : str | None If given, also write the script there (see also :func:write_execution_script). mode : {'process_pool', 'sequential'} 'process_pool' (default) parallelises each wave across processes; 'sequential' runs everything in order (debugging). force_compute : bool Passed to run/roll_out in the generated worker. max_workers : int | None Cap on parallel workers per wave (None = one per unit).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: str The script source.

docs.title: write_execution_script
docs.text:
write_execution_script(plan: Dict[str, Any], out_path: Optional[str] = None, **kwargs) -> str

Generate the script and write it to disk, returning the path.

out_path defaults to {tdfs4ds.SCHEDULING_SCRIPT_DIR}/tdfs4ds_execution_<ts>.py. Extra keyword args are forwarded to :func:generate_execution_script.

extend_read_stores(databases, validate=True)

Attach one or more feature stores to the session in READ-ONLY mode.

docs.title: Parameters

Each is either a string ('DB_B' ⇒ schema == schema_view == 'DB_B', default vector prefix) or a dict descriptor ({'schema':..., 'schema_view':..., 'vector_prefix':...}) for a table/view split or a custom vector prefix. validate : bool When True (default), each store is probed for the catalog views + SELECT access + column compatibility; a store that fails is logged and skipped (the good ones still attach).

docs.title: install_skills
docs.text:
install_skills(target_dir: Optional[Union[str, Path]] = None, *, overwrite: bool = True, skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, verbose: bool = True) -> List[Path]

Copy bundled SKILL.md files to target_dir.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: target_dir : str or Path, optional Destination root. Defaults to ~/.claude/skills/ (Claude Code user-level skills directory). overwrite : bool, default True Replace existing SKILL.md files. Set to False to skip skills that already exist at the destination. skills : list[str], optional Subset of skill names to install. Defaults to all bundled skills. verbose : bool, default True Print one line per installed skill plus a final summary.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[pathlib.Path] Destination paths of every SKILL.md that was written (skipped files are not included).

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The destination directory layout mirrors the package source — one directory per skill, each containing a SKILL.md::

target_dir/
  fs-setup/SKILL.md
  fs-upload/SKILL.md
  ...

docs.title: install_skills_global
docs.text:
install_skills_global(*, overwrite: bool = True, skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, verbose: bool = True) -> List[Path]

Install bundled SKILL.md files to the global Claude Code skills directory.

Convenience wrapper around :func:install_skills that targets ~/.claude/skills/ — the default Claude Code user-level skills location shared across all local projects.

Use this when you want to make tdfs4ds skills available to Claude Code globally (in your home directory), so they appear in the IDE extensions, CLI, and web app without repeating the installation per project.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: overwrite : bool, default True Replace existing SKILL.md files. Set to False to skip skills that have already been installed. skills : list[str], optional Subset of skill names to install. Defaults to all bundled skills. Example: skills=['fs-upload', 'fs-dataset']. verbose : bool, default True Print progress — one line per installed skill and a final summary.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[pathlib.Path] Destination paths of every SKILL.md file that was written. Skipped files (when overwrite=False and already present) are not included in the return value.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
This is equivalent to calling:

.. code-block:: python

install_skills(
    target_dir=Path.home() / ".claude" / "skills",
    overwrite=overwrite,
    skills=skills,
    verbose=verbose,
)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Install all bundled skills globally (one-time setup):

import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.install_skills_global() installed fs-agent -> /Users/you/.claude/skills/fs-agent/SKILL.md installed fs-analyze -> /Users/you/.claude/skills/fs-analyze/SKILL.md ... 19 skill(s) installed to /Users/you/.claude/skills

Install only a few specific skills:

tdfs4ds.install_skills_global( ... skills=['fs-upload', 'fs-dataset', 'ca-search'], ... verbose=True ... )

Reinstall a skill (replace if already present):

tdfs4ds.install_skills_global( ... skills=['fs-upload'], ... overwrite=True ... )

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: install_skills_local : Install to a local project directory instead. install_skills : Lower-level function accepting arbitrary target paths.

docs.title: install_skills_local
docs.text:
install_skills_local(*, overwrite: bool = True, skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, verbose: bool = True) -> List[Path]

Install bundled SKILL.md files to the local project's Claude Code directory.

Convenience wrapper around :func:install_skills that targets ./.claude/skills/ — the project-level skills directory for Claude Code in the current working directory.

Use this when you want to version-control tdfs4ds skills alongside your project code (e.g. in a monorepo or framework where custom agents may modify or extend the skill set). Skills installed locally override the global installation for this project.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: overwrite : bool, default True Replace existing SKILL.md files. Set to False to skip skills that have already been installed locally. skills : list[str], optional Subset of skill names to install. Defaults to all bundled skills. Example: skills=['fs-upload', 'ca-explain']. verbose : bool, default True Print progress — one line per installed skill and a final summary.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[pathlib.Path] Destination paths of every SKILL.md file that was written. Skipped files (when overwrite=False and already present) are not included in the return value.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
This is equivalent to calling:

.. code-block:: python

install_skills(
    target_dir=Path.cwd() / ".claude" / "skills",
    overwrite=overwrite,
    skills=skills,
    verbose=verbose,
)

The local skills directory is created automatically if it does not exist.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Install all bundled skills locally (one-time setup in a project):

import tdfs4ds import os os.chdir('/path/to/my/project') tdfs4ds.install_skills_local() installed fs-agent -> /path/to/my/project/.claude/skills/fs-agent/SKILL.md installed fs-analyze -> /path/to/my/project/.claude/skills/fs-analyze/SKILL.md ... 19 skill(s) installed to /path/to/my/project/.claude/skills

Install only consumer-agent reference skills locally:

tdfs4ds.install_skills_local( ... skills=[ ... 'ca-search', 'ca-definition', 'ca-explain', 'ca-lineage', ... 'ca-summary', 'ca-usage', 'ca-freshness', 'ca-dataset' ... ] ... )

Add new skills to an existing local installation:

tdfs4ds.install_skills_local( ... skills=['fs-new-workflow'], ... overwrite=False # skip if already installed ... )

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: install_skills_global : Install to the global Claude Code directory instead. install_skills : Lower-level function accepting arbitrary target paths.

docs.title: export_skills
docs.text:
export_skills(target_dir: Union[str, Path], *, overwrite: bool = False, skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, verbose: bool = True) -> List[Path]

Export bundled SKILL.md files to target_dir, skipping any that already exist there.

Same behaviour as :func:install_skills but with a mandatory destination and overwrite=False by default — use this when copying skills into a target directory and you only want to add missing ones.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: target_dir : str or Path Destination root. overwrite : bool, default False When False (default), skills already present at the destination are left untouched. Pass True to replace them unconditionally. skills, verbose See :func:install_skills.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[pathlib.Path] Destination paths of every SKILL.md that was written (skipped files are not included).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.export_skills('./my_agent/skills') # skip existing tdfs4ds.export_skills('./my_agent/skills', overwrite=True) # replace all tdfs4ds.export_skills('/srv/skills', skills=['fs-setup', 'fs-upload'])

list_skills() -> List[str]

Return the sorted list of bundled skill names.

docs.title: skill_path
docs.text:
skill_path(name: str) -> Path

Return the absolute path to the bundled SKILL.md (or prompt .md) for name.

Searches in two locations:

  1. tdfs4ds/skills/<name>/SKILL.md — top-level skill directory (ca-*, qo-skill-*, etc.)
  2. tdfs4ds/skills/<skill>/prompts/<name>.md — flat prompt file nested inside a skill's prompts/ subfolder (e.g. qo-skill-optimize/prompts/qo-strategies.md).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: name : str Skill or prompt identifier (e.g. 'fs-upload', 'qo-strategies').

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pathlib.Path Path to the .md file.

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: FileNotFoundError If no bundled skill or prompt with that name exists.

docs.title: skill_prompt
docs.text:
skill_prompt(name: str) -> str

Return the prompt template body (after frontmatter) for skill name.

The returned string may contain {variable} placeholders intended for use with meth:str.format (pure-Python prompts) or passed directly to ChatPromptTemplate.from_template (LangChain prompts).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: name : str Skill identifier, e.g. 'qo-candidates'.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: str Prompt body with frontmatter stripped.

docs.title: skill_catalog
docs.text:
skill_catalog() -> Dict[str, Dict[str, str]]

Return a mapping of every bundled skill to its frontmatter metadata.

Each value contains description (one-line summary used by agent dispatchers) and path (absolute SKILL.md path). Frontmatter is parsed without a YAML dependency — only top-level key: value lines inside a leading --- block are extracted.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, dict[str, str]]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.skill_catalog()['fs-upload']['description'][:60] 'Generate Python code to engineer features ...'

docs.title: consumer_agent_skill_catalog
docs.text:
consumer_agent_skill_catalog() -> Dict[str, Dict[str, str]]

Return metadata for all consumer-agent skills, keyed by skill name.

Consumer-agent skills are identified by the ca- name prefix convention. They are installed to ~/.claude/skills/ alongside other skills and are also used by the LangGraph consumer agent to build its intent classifier and skill registry dynamically.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, dict[str, str]] Skill name → frontmatter metadata (includes intent, description, and path).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.consumer_agent_skill_catalog().keys() dict_keys(['ca-dataset', 'ca-definition', 'ca-explain', ...])

docs.title: query_optimizer_skill_catalog
docs.text:
query_optimizer_skill_catalog() -> Dict[str, Dict[str, str]]

Return metadata for all query-optimizer skills, keyed by skill name.

Query-optimizer skills are identified by the qo-skill- name prefix and must have an intent field in their frontmatter. They are used by the LangGraph query optimizer agent to build its intent classifier and skill registry dynamically.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, dict[str, str]] Skill name → frontmatter metadata (includes intent, description, and path).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.query_optimizer_skill_catalog().keys() dict_keys(['qo-skill-explain', 'qo-skill-optimize', 'qo-skill-simplify'])

docs.title: tdfs4ds.catalog
docs.text:
Read-only catalog accessor functions for the tdfs4ds feature store.

These functions let you browse what is registered in the feature store: features, processes, and datasets. For discovery of entity IDs and feature versions before calling build_dataset(), the lower-level helpers from feature_store.feature_query_retrieval are also re-exported here.

docs.text: __init__(schema_name=None, name='DATASET', existing_objects=None, schema_name_view=None, existing_view_objects=None)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: existing_objects : set, optional A pre-computed set of lower-cased table/view names already present in schema_name. When provided, the constructor skips its own db_list_tables() call (saves one round-trip). Pass the set returned by connect()'s DBC.TablesV query. schema_name_view : str, optional Database that holds the catalog VIEW objects. When None it defaults to tdfs4ds.SCHEMA_VIEW if a dedicated view database is configured, else to schema_name (co-located with the tables — byte-identical to the historical single-database behaviour). existing_view_objects : set, optional Pre-computed set of lower-cased view names present in schema_name_view. Only consulted when the view database differs from the table database.

When True, a NONSEQUENCED VALIDTIME DELETE physically removes every temporal row for the dataset — use this to fully reset a dataset that carries stale/closed history before re-registering it.

docs.title: get_list_entity
docs.text:
get_list_entity(domain=None)

Retrieve a list of unique entity names from a specified data domain.

This function executes a database query to extract distinct entity names from a feature catalog, filtered by the provided data domain. If no domain is specified, it defaults to a predefined data domain.

domain (str, optional): The data domain to filter the entity names. Defaults to None, in which case a predefined domain is used.

Returns: DataFrame: A pandas-like DataFrame containing the unique entity names.

docs.title: get_available_features
docs.text:
get_available_features(entity_id, display_details=False)

Retrieves the names of available features for a given entity ID from a feature store.

This function constructs and executes a SQL query to select feature names from a specific catalog within a schema for a given entity ID, optionally using a specific valid time if set in tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME. The function supports querying features as of a certain timestamp or the current valid time if no timestamp is specified. It also provides an option to display detailed information about the features.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default entity_id str, list, or dict

The ID of the entity for which to retrieve available features. This can be a single ID (str), a list of IDs, or a dictionary of IDs where keys are entity names and values are their corresponding IDs.

required display_details bool

If True, prints detailed information about all features in the catalog. Defaults to False.

False

Returns:

Name Type Description list

A list of feature names available for the specified entity ID.

Note
  • tdfs4ds is assumed to be a configuration module or object containing settings for the feature store, including FEATURE_STORE_TIME, SCHEMA, FEATURE_CATALOG_NAME, DATA_DOMAIN, and DEBUG_MODE.
  • The entity ID(s) are used to filter the features specific to the entity. If entity_id is a dictionary, the keys are considered as entity names, and their values are IDs. If it's a list, it's assumed to be a list of entity names or IDs. For a single string, it is treated as a single entity name or ID.
  • FEATURE_STORE_TIME is used to determine the temporal context for querying the feature data. If it is None, the query defaults to the current valid time.
  • If DEBUG_MODE is enabled in tdfs4ds, the constructed query is printed to the console.
  • The function uses tdml.DataFrame.from_query to execute the SQL query and to_pandas to convert the result into a pandas DataFrame for further processing.

docs.title: get_feature_versions
docs.text:
get_feature_versions(entity_name, features, domain=None)

Retrieve version identifiers for one or more features belonging to a given entity.

The function queries the underlying metadata tables to find the process (i.e., feature‑version) records that match the supplied entity and feature names. It returns a mapping from each requested feature name to either:

  • None – if no matching rows were found.
  • A single UUID string – if exactly one matching row exists for the feature.
  • A list of dictionaries – if more than one matching row is found; each dictionary contains: process_id – the UUID of the process that produced the version, process_view_name – the human‑readable view name associated with that process.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
entity_name : str | list[str] The name (or names) of the entity whose features we are querying. If a single string is supplied it is treated as a singleton list.

str | list[str]

One or more feature names to look up. Accepts a single string or an iterable of strings; if a single string is provided it is wrapped in a list internally.

str, optional

The data‑domain partition to filter on. If omitted the default tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN constant is used.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, str | None | list[dict]] A dictionary keyed by feature name. Each value is either: * None – no records were found for that feature. * str – a single UUID string when exactly one row matched. * list[dict] – multiple matches; each dict has keys process_id and process_view_name.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The query joins the feature catalog view with the process catalog (specifically the “feature split” view) on data domain, entity ID, and feature name.
  • SQL string literals are escaped by doubling single quotes; this is a lightweight escape that suffices for the current use‑case.
  • The function preserves insertion order of features in the returned dictionary (Python 3.7+ guarantees dict order).
  • When tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE is true, the generated SQL statement is printed to stdout – useful for troubleshooting.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: get_feature_versions('user', ['age', 'income'])

docs.title: dataset_catalog
docs.text:
dataset_catalog()

Retrieve a Teradata DataFrame of all datasets registered in the dataset catalog.

Cross-store federation (v0.3.1.148): when read-only stores are attached, browses a UNION ALL of every store's dataset-catalog view (with a FEATURE_STORE provenance column); otherwise byte-identical. Federating here — at the read-only accessor — keeps DatasetCatalog's own write/registration logic primary-only.

docs.title: get_dataset_entity
docs.text:
get_dataset_entity(dataset_id=None)

Return the entity definition (columns and types) for the given dataset ID. If dataset_id is None, returns entity info for all registered datasets. Federated across read-only stores when attached (dataset IDs are globally unique UUIDs, so the dataset_id filter selects the owning store's rows).

docs.title: get_dataset_features
docs.text:
get_dataset_features(dataset_id=None)

Return the feature list for the given dataset ID. If dataset_id is None, returns feature info for all registered datasets. Federated across read-only stores when attached (see get_dataset_entity).

docs.title: tdfs4ds.cli
docs.text:
Command-line interface and programmatic skill management for tdfs4ds.

Entry points registered in setup.py: tdfs4ds-install-skills -> cli.install_skills_cmd

Programmatic API (re-exported from tdfs4ds): - tdfs4ds.list_skills() List bundled skill names. - tdfs4ds.skill_path(name) Path to a bundled SKILL.md. - tdfs4ds.install_skills(dir) Copy SKILL.md files into a target directory. - tdfs4ds.export_skills(dir) Alias of install_skills (clearer intent when used outside Claude Code).

docs.text:
get_plugin_enrichment_knowledge(intent: str, base_text: str = '') -> list

Return Enrichment Knowledge blocks from plugin skills matching intent and base_text.

Scans $TDFS4DS_SKILLS_FOLDER for SKILL.md files whose enrichment-for field matches intent. When enrichment-match is also set, at least one of its comma-separated tokens must appear (case-insensitive) in base_text for the block to be included. Returns a list of ## Enrichment Knowledge body strings (one per matching skill).

Called by node_skill_definition after the base grounded explanation is produced, to optionally extend the answer with approved domain knowledge.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: intent : str Skill intent to match against (e.g. 'DEFINITION'). base_text : str The grounded base explanation already produced by the skill. Used for keyword matching when enrichment-match is set.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[str] Approved knowledge blocks to append (may be empty).

docs.title: skill_prompt
docs.text:
skill_prompt(name: str) -> str

Return the prompt template body (after frontmatter) for skill name.

The returned string may contain {variable} placeholders intended for use with meth:str.format (pure-Python prompts) or passed directly to ChatPromptTemplate.from_template (LangChain prompts).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: name : str Skill identifier, e.g. 'qo-candidates'.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: str Prompt body with frontmatter stripped.

docs.title: install_skills
docs.text:
install_skills(target_dir: Optional[Union[str, Path]] = None, *, overwrite: bool = True, skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, verbose: bool = True) -> List[Path]

Copy bundled SKILL.md files to target_dir.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: target_dir : str or Path, optional Destination root. Defaults to ~/.claude/skills/ (Claude Code user-level skills directory). overwrite : bool, default True Replace existing SKILL.md files. Set to False to skip skills that already exist at the destination. skills : list[str], optional Subset of skill names to install. Defaults to all bundled skills. verbose : bool, default True Print one line per installed skill plus a final summary.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[pathlib.Path] Destination paths of every SKILL.md that was written (skipped files are not included).

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The destination directory layout mirrors the package source — one directory per skill, each containing a SKILL.md::

target_dir/
  fs-setup/SKILL.md
  fs-upload/SKILL.md
  ...

docs.title: export_skills
docs.text:
export_skills(target_dir: Union[str, Path], *, overwrite: bool = False, skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, verbose: bool = True) -> List[Path]

Export bundled SKILL.md files to target_dir, skipping any that already exist there.

Same behaviour as :func:install_skills but with a mandatory destination and overwrite=False by default — use this when copying skills into a target directory and you only want to add missing ones.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: target_dir : str or Path Destination root. overwrite : bool, default False When False (default), skills already present at the destination are left untouched. Pass True to replace them unconditionally. skills, verbose See :func:install_skills.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[pathlib.Path] Destination paths of every SKILL.md that was written (skipped files are not included).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.export_skills('./my_agent/skills') # skip existing tdfs4ds.export_skills('./my_agent/skills', overwrite=True) # replace all tdfs4ds.export_skills('/srv/skills', skills=['fs-setup', 'fs-upload'])

docs.title: install_skills_global
docs.text:
install_skills_global(*, overwrite: bool = True, skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, verbose: bool = True) -> List[Path]

Install bundled SKILL.md files to the global Claude Code skills directory.

Convenience wrapper around :func:install_skills that targets ~/.claude/skills/ — the default Claude Code user-level skills location shared across all local projects.

Use this when you want to make tdfs4ds skills available to Claude Code globally (in your home directory), so they appear in the IDE extensions, CLI, and web app without repeating the installation per project.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: overwrite : bool, default True Replace existing SKILL.md files. Set to False to skip skills that have already been installed. skills : list[str], optional Subset of skill names to install. Defaults to all bundled skills. Example: skills=['fs-upload', 'fs-dataset']. verbose : bool, default True Print progress — one line per installed skill and a final summary.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[pathlib.Path] Destination paths of every SKILL.md file that was written. Skipped files (when overwrite=False and already present) are not included in the return value.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
This is equivalent to calling:

.. code-block:: python

install_skills(
    target_dir=Path.home() / ".claude" / "skills",
    overwrite=overwrite,
    skills=skills,
    verbose=verbose,
)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Install all bundled skills globally (one-time setup):

import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.install_skills_global() installed fs-agent -> /Users/you/.claude/skills/fs-agent/SKILL.md installed fs-analyze -> /Users/you/.claude/skills/fs-analyze/SKILL.md ... 19 skill(s) installed to /Users/you/.claude/skills

Install only a few specific skills:

tdfs4ds.install_skills_global( ... skills=['fs-upload', 'fs-dataset', 'ca-search'], ... verbose=True ... )

Reinstall a skill (replace if already present):

tdfs4ds.install_skills_global( ... skills=['fs-upload'], ... overwrite=True ... )

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: install_skills_local : Install to a local project directory instead. install_skills : Lower-level function accepting arbitrary target paths.

docs.title: install_skills_local
docs.text:
install_skills_local(*, overwrite: bool = True, skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, verbose: bool = True) -> List[Path]

Install bundled SKILL.md files to the local project's Claude Code directory.

Convenience wrapper around :func:install_skills that targets ./.claude/skills/ — the project-level skills directory for Claude Code in the current working directory.

Use this when you want to version-control tdfs4ds skills alongside your project code (e.g. in a monorepo or framework where custom agents may modify or extend the skill set). Skills installed locally override the global installation for this project.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: overwrite : bool, default True Replace existing SKILL.md files. Set to False to skip skills that have already been installed locally. skills : list[str], optional Subset of skill names to install. Defaults to all bundled skills. Example: skills=['fs-upload', 'ca-explain']. verbose : bool, default True Print progress — one line per installed skill and a final summary.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[pathlib.Path] Destination paths of every SKILL.md file that was written. Skipped files (when overwrite=False and already present) are not included in the return value.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
This is equivalent to calling:

.. code-block:: python

install_skills(
    target_dir=Path.cwd() / ".claude" / "skills",
    overwrite=overwrite,
    skills=skills,
    verbose=verbose,
)

The local skills directory is created automatically if it does not exist.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Install all bundled skills locally (one-time setup in a project):

import tdfs4ds import os os.chdir('/path/to/my/project') tdfs4ds.install_skills_local() installed fs-agent -> /path/to/my/project/.claude/skills/fs-agent/SKILL.md installed fs-analyze -> /path/to/my/project/.claude/skills/fs-analyze/SKILL.md ... 19 skill(s) installed to /path/to/my/project/.claude/skills

Install only consumer-agent reference skills locally:

tdfs4ds.install_skills_local( ... skills=[ ... 'ca-search', 'ca-definition', 'ca-explain', 'ca-lineage', ... 'ca-summary', 'ca-usage', 'ca-freshness', 'ca-dataset' ... ] ... )

Add new skills to an existing local installation:

tdfs4ds.install_skills_local( ... skills=['fs-new-workflow'], ... overwrite=False # skip if already installed ... )

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: install_skills_global : Install to the global Claude Code directory instead. install_skills : Lower-level function accepting arbitrary target paths.

docs.title: skill_catalog
docs.text:
skill_catalog() -> Dict[str, Dict[str, str]]

Return a mapping of every bundled skill to its frontmatter metadata.

Each value contains description (one-line summary used by agent dispatchers) and path (absolute SKILL.md path). Frontmatter is parsed without a YAML dependency — only top-level key: value lines inside a leading --- block are extracted.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, dict[str, str]]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.skill_catalog()['fs-upload']['description'][:60] 'Generate Python code to engineer features ...'

docs.title: consumer_agent_skill_catalog
docs.text:
consumer_agent_skill_catalog() -> Dict[str, Dict[str, str]]

Return metadata for all consumer-agent skills, keyed by skill name.

Consumer-agent skills are identified by the ca- name prefix convention. They are installed to ~/.claude/skills/ alongside other skills and are also used by the LangGraph consumer agent to build its intent classifier and skill registry dynamically.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, dict[str, str]] Skill name → frontmatter metadata (includes intent, description, and path).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.consumer_agent_skill_catalog().keys() dict_keys(['ca-dataset', 'ca-definition', 'ca-explain', ...])

docs.title: query_optimizer_skill_catalog
docs.text:
query_optimizer_skill_catalog() -> Dict[str, Dict[str, str]]

Return metadata for all query-optimizer skills, keyed by skill name.

Query-optimizer skills are identified by the qo-skill- name prefix and must have an intent field in their frontmatter. They are used by the LangGraph query optimizer agent to build its intent classifier and skill registry dynamically.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, dict[str, str]] Skill name → frontmatter metadata (includes intent, description, and path).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: import tdfs4ds tdfs4ds.query_optimizer_skill_catalog().keys() dict_keys(['qo-skill-explain', 'qo-skill-optimize', 'qo-skill-simplify'])

docs.title: install_skills_cmd
docs.text:
install_skills_cmd()

Entry point: tdfs4ds-install-skills.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.config
docs.text:
Configuration loading for tdfs4ds.

Three external configuration sources are supported, applied in this order so that higher-priority sources always win:

  1. JSON config file — project defaults, searched in order:
  2. ./tdfs4ds.json (project-local)

  3. ~/.tdfs4ds/config.json (user-global)

  4. .env file — local environment overrides, searched in order:

  5. ./.env (project-local)

  6. ~/.tdfs4ds/.env (user-global) Only TDFS4DS_* variables are read. Module-config vars are set on the tdfs4ds module; pass-through vars (connection TDFS4DS_TD_*, serve toggles TDFS4DS_SERVE_*, TDFS4DS_SKILLS_FOLDER) are exported to os.environ because the serve layer reads them from there directly. Unlike the JSON config, credentials are accepted here (add .env to .gitignore to keep secrets out of source control).

  7. OS environment variables — prefix TDFS4DS_, e.g. TDFS4DS_SCHEMA, TDFS4DS_SCHEMA_VIEW, TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN.

Credentials (INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY) are rejected from the JSON config file to prevent accidental commits; use a .env file or an OS env var.

Full priority chain (highest first): programmatic > OS env var > .env file > JSON config > built-in defaults

docs.title: logger module-attribute
docs.text: logger = getLogger(__name__)

docs.title: _BOOL_VARS module-attribute
docs.text: _BOOL_VARS = frozenset({'DISPLAY_LOGS', 'DEBUG_MODE', 'USE_VOLATILE_TABLE', 'BUILD_DATASET_AT_UPLOAD'})

docs.title: _INT_VARS module-attribute

docs.title: _FLOAT_VARS module-attribute

docs.title: _LIST_VARS module-attribute

docs.title: _FILE_VARS module-attribute
docs.text: _FILE_VARS = frozenset({'SCHEMA', 'SCHEMA_VIEW', 'SCHEMA_READONLY', 'DATA_DOMAIN', 'FEATURE_STORE_TIME', 'DISPLAY_LOGS', 'DEBUG_MODE', 'STORE_FEATURE', 'REGISTER_FEATURE', 'REGISTER_PROCESS', 'USE_VOLATILE_TABLE', 'BUILD_DATASET_AT_UPLOAD', 'VARCHAR_SIZE', 'FEATURE_PARTITION_N', 'FEATURE_PARTITION_EACH', 'INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER', 'INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL', 'INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL', 'INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROXY', 'EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER', 'EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL', 'EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL', 'EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROXY', 'EMBEDDING_MODEL_DIM', 'EMBEDDING_MODEL_MAX_TOKENS', 'EMBEDDING_BATCH_DELAY', 'CHROMA_MODE', 'CHROMA_PATH', 'CHROMA_HOST', 'CHROMA_PORT', 'VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND', 'TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX', 'TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE', 'TERADATA_AI_MODEL_NAME', 'TERADATA_AI_REGION', 'TERADATA_AI_API_BASE', 'MCP_SERVER_URL', 'MCP_SERVER_TRANSPORT'})

docs.title: _PASSTHROUGH_ENV_VARS module-attribute
docs.text: _PASSTHROUGH_ENV_VARS = frozenset({'TD_HOST', 'TD_USERNAME', 'TD_PASSWORD', 'TD_LOGMECH', 'TD_DATABASE', 'TD_ENCRYPT', 'SERVE_PORT', 'SERVE_BUILD_INDEX', 'SERVE_CHATBOT', 'SERVE_CHATBOT_PORT', 'SERVE_DOCS', 'SERVE_DOCS_PORT', 'SERVE_ROOT_PATH', 'SKILLS_FOLDER', 'VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_BASE_URL', 'VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_PAT_TOKEN', 'VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_PEM_FILE', 'VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_USERNAME', 'VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_PASSWORD', 'VECTOR_STORE_AUTH_CLIENT_ID'})

_coerce(name, value)

Cast value to the correct Python type for name.

docs.title: _apply_file
docs.text:
_apply_file(module, path)

Read path (JSON) and apply matching keys to module.

docs.title: _parse_dotenv
docs.text:
_parse_dotenv(path)

Parse path as a .env file and return a {ATTR: raw_value} dict for every TDFS4DS_* variable found.

Handles: - KEY=VALUE - KEY="VALUE" / KEY='VALUE' - export KEY=VALUE - # comments and blank lines

docs.title: _apply_dotenv
docs.text:
_apply_dotenv(module, path)

Parse path as a .env file and apply TDFS4DS_* entries.

Module-config vars are set on module; pass-through vars (connection / serve / skills — read straight from os.environ by the serve layer) are exported to os.environ so a .env works regardless of how it is loaded.

docs.title: _apply_env
docs.text:
_apply_env(module)

Apply TDFS4DS_* OS environment variables to module.

docs.title: _load_config
docs.text:
_load_config(module)

Called automatically during import tdfs4ds.

Load order (lowest → highest priority): JSON config file → .env file → OS env vars

docs.title: load_config
docs.text:
load_config(path=None, dotenv_path=None)

(Re-)load configuration from external sources.

Useful for pointing at non-default config files or refreshing values mid-session. Applies sources in priority order (lowest first):

  1. JSON config file (at path or auto-discovered)
  2. .env file (at dotenv_path or auto-discovered)
  3. OS TDFS4DS_* environment variables (always applied)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: path : str or pathlib.Path, optional Explicit path to a JSON config file. Skips auto-discovery when given. dotenv_path : str or pathlib.Path, optional Explicit path to a .env file. Skips auto-discovery when given.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Auto-discover all sources::

import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.load_config()

Explicit paths::

tdfs4ds.load_config(
    path='/project/configs/feature_store.json',
    dotenv_path='/project/.env.local',
)

JSON config format (tdfs4ds.json)::

{
    "schema": "MY_DATABASE",
    "data_domain": "MY_PROJECT",
    "display_logs": true,
    "store_feature": "MERGE",
    "varchar_size": 1024,
    "instruct_model_provider": "openai",
    "instruct_model_model": "gpt-4o"
}

Keys are case-insensitive. instruct_model_api_key is rejected from JSON config — use a .env file or OS env var instead.

.env file format::

TDFS4DS_SCHEMA=MY_DATABASE
TDFS4DS_SCHEMA_VIEW=MY_VIEWS_DATABASE
TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN=MY_PROJECT
TDFS4DS_INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY=sk-...

Only TDFS4DS_* variables are read from the .env file. Add .env to .gitignore to keep secrets out of source control.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.data_domain
docs.text:
Data domain management for the tdfs4ds feature store.

A data domain is a logical namespace that isolates features by project or business unit. Use these functions to list, select, or create data domains within the feature store.

docs.title: execute_query
docs.text:
execute_query(query, raise_error=False, fetch_results=False)

Execute a SQL query or a list of queries using the tdml module.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default query str or list

A single SQL query string or a list of SQL query strings.

required raise_error bool

If True, re-raise exceptions after printing them. Default is False.

False fetch_results bool

If True, fetch and return the results of the SQL execution. Default is False.

False

Returns: The result of the SQL execution if a single query is passed. None if a list of queries is passed or an exception occurs.

docs.title: get_data_domains
docs.text:
get_data_domains(verbose=True)

Retrieve and display all data domains available in the feature store.

Queries both the process catalog and the feature catalog for distinct DATA_DOMAIN values, then prints them with the currently active domain marked with an asterisk.

Parameters: - verbose (bool): If True, prints the list of data domains with the current one marked.

  • list or None: When verbose=False, returns the list of data domain strings. When verbose=True, prints the list and returns None.

docs.title: select_data_domain
docs.text:
select_data_domain(data_domain)

Set the active data domain for feature store operations.

All subsequent feature queries, registrations, and interactions with the feature store are scoped to this domain.

Parameters: - data_domain (str): The name of the data domain to set as active.

Raises: - ValueError: If the data domain does not exist in the feature store.

docs.title: create_data_domain
docs.text:
create_data_domain(data_domain)

Create a new data domain in the feature store.

A data domain serves as a logical grouping for features, isolating them by project, business unit, or data type. The domain is activated locally; features uploaded afterward will be tagged with this domain.

Parameters: - data_domain (str): The name of the new data domain to be created.

docs.title: outstanding_amounts_dataset_filename module-attribute
docs.text: outstanding_amounts_dataset_filename = 'curves.csv'

docs.title: outstanding_amounts_dataset
docs.text:
outstanding_amounts_dataset()

Load and return a dataset of outstanding amounts from a CSV file.

This function reads a CSV file named by the variable outstanding_amounts_dataset_filename located in the "data" subdirectory of the directory specified by package_dir. It parses the 'OUTSTANDING_DATE' column in the dataset as dates.

Returns:

Type Description

pandas.DataFrame: A DataFrame containing the dataset of outstanding amounts. The 'OUTSTANDING_DATE' column

is parsed as datetime objects.

Note
  • This function requires the pandas library to be imported as pd.
  • The variables package_dir and outstanding_amounts_dataset_filename must be defined in the scope where this function is called.
  • The CSV file must have a column named 'OUTSTANDING_DATE' for the date parsing to work correctly.

docs.title: upload_outstanding_amounts_dataset
docs.text:
upload_outstanding_amounts_dataset(table_name='outstanding_amount_dataset', **kwargs)

Uploads the outstanding amounts dataset to a database table and returns the table as a DataFrame.

This function uploads the dataset returned by outstanding_amounts_dataset() to a specified table in a database. If a 'schema_name' is provided via keyword arguments, it uploads the data to that schema; otherwise, it uses the default schema. It prints to the console where the dataset is uploaded. After uploading, it retrieves the newly uploaded table into a DataFrame and returns it.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default table_name str

The name of the database table where the dataset will be uploaded. Defaults to 'outstanding_amount_dataset'.

'outstanding_amount_dataset' **kwargs

Arbitrary keyword arguments. Can be used to specify 'schema_name' and other parameters that tdml.copy_to_sql() might accept, such as connection details.

{}

Returns:

Type Description

tdml.DataFrame: A DataFrame representing the database table into which the dataset was uploaded. This DataFrame is created either in the specified schema (if 'schema_name' is provided) or the default schema.

Note
  • This function requires the tdml library to be available and correctly configured to interact with the target database.
  • The outstanding_amounts_dataset() function is called within this function to obtain the dataset to upload.
  • If 'schema_name' is provided in kwargs, it is used for both uploading the dataset and retrieving it into a DataFrame. If not provided, a notice is printed, and the default schema is used.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.execution
docs.text:
Feature ingestion and process execution for the tdfs4ds feature store.

Core execution call chain:

upload_features(df, entity_id, feature_names, ...)
  → register_process_view.__wrapped__(...)
  → execute_query(query_insert / query_insert_dist / query_insert_filtermanager)
  → [if optimize_query=True and instruct model available]
      → skill_optimize_query(process_id=process_id)
          → saves full Markdown report to tdfs4ds_optim_{timestamp}_{view}_{process_id}.md
          → if improvement found and update_view=True: REPLACE VIEW {view_name} AS {best_sql}
  → [if document=True and instruct model available]
      → document_process_incremental(process_id=process_id, display=False, upload=True)
  → run(process_id)          # ingests from the (now-optimized) view; builds dataset view if dataset_view_name set
  → [if document=True and instruct model available and dataset_view_name is not None]
      → document_dataset_incremental(dataset_id)   # bottom-up dataset docs after feature docs exist
      → SELECT * FROM process_catalog WHERE PROCESS_ID = ?
      → _upload_features(df, entity_id, feature_names, feature_versions=process_id, ...)
          → Gettdtypes / update_varchar_length / validate_feature_types
          → register_entity / register_features
          → _compute_features_batch(...)  [once, or per-filter loop]
              → followup_open
              → prepare_feature_ingestion   # unpivots df into volatile table
              → store_feature               # MERGE/UPDATE_INSERT into feature tables
              → followup_close (COMPLETED)
          → apply_collect_stats(...)        # called once after all batches

roll_out(process_list, time_manager, ...)
  → iterates time steps → run(process_id) per step

docs.title: ACC_6881_HINT module-attribute
docs.text: ACC_6881_HINT = "Error 6881 (not eligible for the Analytical Compute Cluster): for a tdstone2-Apply view that references a TimeManager/FilterManager, TWO things are needed — (a) the manager table on OFS (storage='OFS'), so the ACC can read it, AND (b) the filter written as a JOIN to the manager view, NOT a correlated scalar subquery '(SELECT BUSINESS_DATE FROM <view>)', which the ACC rejects. OFS + JOIN scores; OFS + scalar subquery still 6881s. The filter shape is the user's responsibility. Also: run the model's score() BEFORE upload_features — roll_out/run() cannot drive an Apply model (its ingestion CTAS also hits 6881)."

docs.title: _FEATURE_VERSION_DEFAULT module-attribute
docs.text: _FEATURE_VERSION_DEFAULT = 'dev.0.0'

docs.title: _INTO_VOLATILE_ART_RE module-attribute
docs.text: _INTO_VOLATILE_ART_RE = compile('INTO\\s+VOLATILE\\s+ART\\s*\\(\\s*([A-Za-z_\\"][\\w.\\"]*)', IGNORECASE)

docs.title: FilterManager
docs.text:
A utility for managing dynamic, versioned filter sets as database-backed views.

The FilterManager enables lightweight scenario management by storing multiple filter definitions in a hidden Teradata table and exposing a public view that dynamically switches between them by filter_id. Each row in the hidden table represents a complete filter configuration. The active configuration is controlled by updating the view definition rather than rewriting table data.

Key Features
  • Store multiple filter states (scenarios) indexed by filter_id
  • Switch filter states instantly by updating a view
  • Optionally include time-based slicing using a BUSINESS_DATE column
  • Clone filters between managers (soft or hard clone modes)
  • Prune obsolete filters to control table size
  • Retrieve current and historical filter definitions
Workflow Overview
  1. Create a FilterManager pointing to a target view name.
  2. Load one or more filter definitions using load_filter().
  3. Switch active filters using update(filter_id).
  4. Inspect the active filter via display() or view DDL.
  5. Optionally prune or clone filters as needed.
How It Works Internally
  • A hidden table named <view_name>_HIDDEN stores filter definitions.
  • A Teradata view named <view_name> exposes only the active filter row.
  • Each filter automatically receives a sequential filter_id (ROW_NUMBER() ordering ensures deterministic assignment).
  • If time-based filtering is used via time_column, a BUSINESS_DATE column is added and projected in all operations.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default table_name str

Public view name to manage or create.

required schema_name str

Teradata schema where artifacts will be created.

required filter_id_name str

Name of the filter ID column. Defaults to 'filter_id'.

'filter_id' time_column str

Optional name of a timestamp column from input DataFrames that maps to a BUSINESS_DATE column for time-aware filters.

None

Attributes:

Name Type Description schema_name str

Target schema for view and hidden table.

table_name str

Name of hidden table storing filters (auto-suffixed with _HIDDEN).

view_name str

Name of public view pointing to current filter.

filter_id_name str

Column containing filter ID.

nb_filters int | None

Number of stored filters (None until initialized).

col_names list[str] | None

Columns projected by the view (data columns only).

time_filtering bool | None

True if time-based filtering enabled.

Notes
  • Database objects are only created when load_filter() is first called.
  • Safe for iterative pipeline runs—auto-detects existing artifacts.
  • Designed for large production tables and Teradata-native workflows.
