docs.title: _turn_cache_reset
docs.text:
_turn_cache_reset() -> None

Clear the per-turn memo. Call once at the start of each user turn.

docs.title: _turn_cached
docs.text:
_turn_cached(key, fn)

Return the cached materialized frame for key or compute+store it.

fn must return the already-materialized object (e.g. a pandas DataFrame); caching the lazy tdml.DataFrame would save nothing because re-materializing re-runs the query. key must encode the request scope (id/name set + the active DATA_DOMAIN) so a domain switch mid-graph cannot return a wrong slice. On failure the exception propagates and nothing is cached.

docs.title: _esc_sql_literal
docs.text:
_esc_sql_literal(s: str) -> str

Escape a single value for use inside a SQL string literal (double quotes).

docs.title: _in_clause
docs.text:
_in_clause(col: str, values) -> Optional[str]

Build an UPPER(col) IN ('A','B',…) predicate from a str or list.

Returns None when values is falsy / empty so callers can skip the clause. De-duplicates (case-insensitively) and escapes single quotes. A single string still produces a one-element IN list, which is equivalent to =.

docs.title: _query_bd_sections
docs.text:
_query_bd_sections(section_names: Optional[list[str]] = None, object_name: Optional[str] = None, object_names: Optional[list[str]] = None) -> pd.DataFrame

Query FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS with optional filters.

Uses execute_sql (not from_query) because CURRENT VALIDTIME must be at the outermost level — from_query wraps in a subquery which breaks it.

object_name (singular, back-compat) and object_names (list, batched) are merged into a single UPPER(OBJECT_NAME) IN (…) predicate so callers can fetch many objects in one round-trip instead of looping.

docs.title: _query_bd_objects
docs.text:
_query_bd_objects(object_name: Optional[str] = None, object_names: Optional[list[str]] = None) -> pd.DataFrame

Query FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS with optional filter.

object_name (singular, back-compat) and object_names (list, batched) merge into one UPPER(OBJECT_NAME) IN (…) predicate.

docs.title: _query_bd_columns
docs.text:
_query_bd_columns(table_name: Optional[str] = None, column_name: Optional[str] = None, table_names: Optional[list[str]] = None, column_names: Optional[list[str]] = None) -> pd.DataFrame

Query FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS with optional filter.

Singular kwargs (table_name / column_name, back-compat) and list kwargs (table_names / column_names, batched) merge into UPPER(TABLE_NAME) IN (…) AND UPPER(COLUMN_NAME) IN (…) so all features for a set of views can be fetched in one round-trip; the caller slices the result per (view, column) in pandas.

docs.title: _all_processes_cached
docs.text:
_all_processes_cached() -> pd.DataFrame

Per-turn-memoized process_catalog(all_data_domains=True).to_pandas().

Some lookups genuinely need to scan every process view across all domains (find-object-by-name, build the global allow/forbid set) — they cannot be scoped to a few ids. This accessor still avoids re-materializing the same frame multiple times within a single turn. Cross-turn freshness is preserved by _turn_cache_reset at the start of each turn.

docs.title: _all_features_cached
docs.text:
_all_features_cached(all_data_domains: bool = True) -> pd.DataFrame

Per-turn-memoized feature_catalog(...).to_pandas() for whole-store scans.

docs.title: _processes_by_id
docs.text:
_processes_by_id(process_ids) -> pd.DataFrame

Return PROCESS_ID → VIEW_NAME (+ ENTITY_ID, FEATURE_NAMES) for given ids.

Scoped, memoized replacement for process_catalog(all_data_domains=True) .to_pandas() used only to map a handful of process_ids to their view name. Issues one WHERE PROCESS_ID IN (…) query against the public process catalog view (which already applies VALIDTIME), so it materializes only the turn's rows. Cached per-turn on the id-set. Fail-open: returns an empty frame on error (callers already tolerate a missing view name).

docs.title: _distinct_feature_count
docs.text:
_distinct_feature_count(df, group_by: Optional[str] = None)

Count distinct (FEATURE_NAME, ENTITY_NAME) feature identities.

A feature's identity in tdfs4ds is the pair (FEATURE_NAME, ENTITY_NAME): the same name registered under two entities is two distinct features. This helper is robust to duplicate rows (e.g. a feature appearing in several datasets) and to frames that expose ENTITY_ID instead of ENTITY_NAME.

Cross-store federation limit (v0.3.1.148): the identity key does NOT include a store dimension. With read-only stores holding DISJOINT domains/entities/names (the common case) this is exact. Only when two stores independently reuse the SAME (DATA_DOMAIN, ENTITY_NAME, FEATURE_NAME) do their rows collapse to one — an undercount. Threading the FEATURE_STORE provenance column into the key here (and into domain resolution / the context-graph keys) is the documented follow-up for full collision-proof multi-store counting.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default df

pandas DataFrame containing at least FEATURE_NAME and an entity column (ENTITY_NAME preferred, ENTITY_ID accepted).

required group_by Optional[str]

When given, return a {group_value: count} dict keyed by that column.

None

Returns:

Type Description

int when group_by is None, else a dict.

docs.title: _get_feature_catalog_with_docs
docs.text:
_get_feature_catalog_with_docs(feature_filter: Optional[str] = None, entity_filter: Optional[str] = None) -> pd.DataFrame

Return a DataFrame of features enriched with their process-level documentation.

Resolution chain (batched — see below): 1. Feature catalog (active domain) → all (ENTITY_NAME, FEATURE_NAME) pairs in one materialization; filters applied in pandas. 2. get_feature_versions(entity, features) once per entity (each call already batches all the entity's features via FEATURE_NAME IN (…)) → process_id. 3. _processes_by_id(all_process_ids) — one scoped PROCESS_ID IN (…) query → VIEW_NAME (replaces the old full all-domains process_catalog load). 4. _query_bd_columns(table_names=all_views, column_names=all_features) — one scoped query → BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION, sliced per (VIEW_NAME, FEATURE_NAME) in pandas.

The previous implementation issued one get_list_features per entity and one _query_bd_columns per feature (plus a whole all-domains process_catalog materialization), i.e. tens of round-trips for a multi-entity domain. This version is ~3 scoped round-trips total.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default feature_filter Optional[str]

If given, only return rows for this exact feature name (case-insensitive).

None entity_filter Optional[str]

If given, only process this exact entity name (case-insensitive). Speeds up entity-scoped lookups.

None

Returns:

Type Description DataFrame

DataFrame with columns:

DataFrame

FEATURE_NAME, ENTITY_NAME, PROCESS_ID, VIEW_NAME, BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION

docs.title: _build_multi_object_context
docs.text:
_build_multi_object_context(feature_names: list, hint_entity_name: Optional[str] = None, accumulated_context: Optional[dict] = None) -> dict

Build a shared context graph for multiple features in one pass.

Calls _get_feature_catalog_with_docs() once (no filter) then: - Resolves each name to (entity, process_id, view_name, description) - Fetches BD_SECTIONS + all BD_COLUMNS for each unique VIEW_NAME (once each) - Fetches dataset membership for each feature via get_dataset_features() - Fetches BD_OBJECTS + BD_SECTIONS for each unique dataset (once each)

Returns:

Type Description dict

{ "features": [{name, entity_name, process_id, view_name, description, found_in_catalog, has_description, dataset_names}], "shared_views": {view_name: {"sections": [...], "columns": [...]}}, "shared_datasets": {ds_name: {"database", "bd_description", "sections"}}, "entity_names": [str],

dict

}

docs.title: _recognize_object_type
docs.text:
_recognize_object_type(name: str) -> dict

Identify what type of object name is by querying catalogs.

Returns a dict with keys: type ("feature"|"dataset"|"entity"|"data_domain"|"process"|"unknown"), entity_name, view_name, process_id.

docs.title: _known_catalog_names
docs.text:
_known_catalog_names() -> set

Return the union of registered feature, dataset and BD object names (uppercase).

Used to filter vector-search hits so that fabricated names (embedding-similar but not actually registered) never reach the LLM context. Empty set on failure means no filtering is applied (fail-open).

docs.title: _vector_search_context
docs.text:
_vector_search_context(query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> tuple[str, Optional[str]]

Build LLM context from vector search results when SQL exact match fails.

Vector hits whose OBJECT_NAME is not present in the feature / dataset / process catalogs are dropped — embedding similarity alone is not enough proof that an object exists, and surfacing unverified names leads to fabricated feature suggestions.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default query str

Free-text search query (object name or natural language question).

required top_k int

Maximum results per collection.

5

Returns:

Type Description tuple[str, Optional[str]]

Tuple of (context_string, resolved_object_name). Both may be empty/None.

docs.title: _feature_dataset_names_map
docs.text:
_feature_dataset_names_map() -> tuple

Return (by_entity, by_name) feature→dataset maps for the current domain.

Reverse of the dataset catalog: maps every registered feature to the datasets that expose it, so the knowledge graph can draw feature→dataset edges (otherwise features render as isolated nodes after a SUMMARY).

A feature's identity is the pair (FEATURE_NAME, ENTITY_NAME) — the same name registered under two entities is two distinct features. The dataset _FEATURES table carries FEATURE_ID (the feature's identity), which the feature catalog resolves to the authoritative ENTITY_NAME. We therefore key the primary map on (ENTITY_UPPER, FEATURE_NAME_UPPER) so a dataset is linked only to the entity it actually exposes — never to a same-named feature under a different entity.

Returns a tuple
  • by_entity — {(ENTITY_UPPER, FEATURE_NAME_UPPER): [dataset_name, ...]}
  • by_name — {FEATURE_NAME_UPPER: [dataset_name, ...]} fallback, used only for features whose FEATURE_ID has no catalog match (entity-less / cross-domain).

Three bounded materializations (dataset features, feature catalog, dataset catalog), joined on FEATURE_ID in pandas (no per-row DB query); memoized per turn; fail-open to empty maps (no dataset linkage).

docs.title: _resolve_dataset
docs.text:
_resolve_dataset(object_name: str) -> Optional[dict]

Look up object_name in the dataset catalog.

Returns {dataset_id, dataset_name, dataset_database} or None.

docs.title: _tokenize_question

Return lowercase non-stop-word tokens from the question (used to pre-filter catalog).

docs.title: _depluralize
docs.text:
_depluralize(token: str) -> str

Cheap, dependency-free English depluralization for catalog token matching.

'pharmacies'→'pharmacy', 'providers'→'provider', 'boxes'→'box', 'claims'→'claim'. Leaves 'ss' words (e.g. 'class') and short words unchanged.

docs.title: _normalize_tokens
docs.text:

Return the tokens plus their depluralized singular forms (both kept).

docs.title: _row_token_set
docs.text:
_row_token_set(feature_name: str, domain: str = '', entity: str = '') -> 'set[str]'

Split a catalog row's name/domain/entity into lowercase word tokens.

Names use _ separators (pharmacy_z_score → {pharmacy, z, score}); the full joined name is kept too so substring/prefix checks still work.

docs.title: _question_matches_row
docs.text:
_question_matches_row(question_tokens_norm: 'set[str]', row_tokens: 'set[str]') -> int

Number of distinct normalized question tokens that match a row's tokens.

A token matches when it equals a row token, or is a prefix of one / has one as a prefix (bidirectional, e.g. provider ↔ provider_id). Tokens of length ≤2 only count on exact equality to avoid noise.

docs.title: _truncate_on_word
docs.text:
_truncate_on_word(text: str, max_chars: int) -> str

Truncate text to ~max_chars, cutting on a word boundary and appending '…'.

Returns the text unchanged when it already fits or max_chars <= 0 (disabled).

Find features or datasets matching a business need expressed in natural language.

required

docs.title: _description_claims_higher_is_riskier
docs.text:
_description_claims_higher_is_riskier(business_description: str) -> bool

True when the description asserts that higher values mean more risk.

Deterministic regex over the stored business description; used to decide whether an inverted-score formula contradicts the prose.

docs.title: _direction_correction_note
docs.text:
_direction_correction_note(business_description: str, calculation_sql: Optional[str]) -> Optional[str]

Return a clarification note when the stored description's risk direction contradicts an inverted calculation formula, else None.

The DEFINITION skill trusts the stored business_description; when the actual SQL inverts the score (1 - (...), so a lower value means higher risk) but the prose claims "higher = riskier", we append an authoritative correction rather than silently surfacing the contradiction (which EXPLAIN, reading the live SQL, would expose).

docs.title: skill_definition
docs.text:
skill_definition(question: str, object_name: str, hint_entity_name: Optional[str] = None, hint_table_name: Optional[str] = None, accumulated_context: Optional[dict] = None, _fuzzy_corrected: bool = False) -> dict

Explain what a feature, column, or dataset is in plain business language.

Name Type Description Default question str

User question.

required object_name str

Name of the feature, column, or dataset to define.

required hint_entity_name Optional[str]

Entity name remembered from a previous turn — used to auto-disambiguate when the same feature name exists across entities.

None hint_table_name Optional[str]

Source table/view remembered from a prior EXPLAIN turn — used to narrow column-level BD lookups to the right table when the column is not a registered feature.

None

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with object_name, explanation, and columns.

docs.title: skill_definition_multi
docs.text:
skill_definition_multi(question: str, feature_names: list, hint_entity_name: Optional[str] = None, accumulated_context: Optional[dict] = None) -> dict

Unified multi-feature definition using a shared context graph.

Calls _build_multi_object_context() once to resolve all features and fetch shared documentation (process views, datasets, entities), then makes a single LLM call with the full unified context. No consolidation pass needed.

docs.title: skill_usage
docs.text:
skill_usage(question: str, object_name: str, accumulated_context: Optional[dict] = None, entity_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Advise a consumer on how to safely use a feature in their work.

Enumerates ALL datasets that expose the feature (not just one) and emits a paste-ready SELECT snippet for each so the consumer can pick the right view for their dashboard.

Implements fast-path caching: queries accumulated_context_graph first (cache hit → return immediately), falls back to catalog scans (cache miss).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question about usage (Tableau, Excel, regulatory, etc.).

required object_name str

Name of the feature or dataset.

required accumulated_context Optional[dict]

The accumulated_context_graph from state (used for cache).

None entity_name Optional[str]

Entity name for disambiguation (used in cache lookups).

None

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with object_name, guidance, datasets (list of

dict

{dataset_name, database, dataset_id, select_sql, entity_keys}),

dict

_from_cache (bool), and docs_sources.

docs.title: skill_freshness_for_feature_list
docs.text:
skill_freshness_for_feature_list(question: str, feature_names: list, entity_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Aggregate freshness across a list of feature names referenced by a group pronoun.

Used when the user says "are these features up to date?" without naming any of them explicitly. Looks each feature up in the catalog, collects its process_id, pulls follow_up_report for each, and returns a combined last-successful-run / days-since / failure verdict.

docs.title: _freshness_segments
docs.text:
_freshness_segments(followup) -> list

Per-segment freshness when a process runs segmented (FilterManager).

Groups the follow-up rows by their APPLIED_FILTER / VALIDTIME_DATE segment and returns one freshness record per distinct segment: [{segment, last_successful_run, days_since_update, has_recent_failure}], sorted stalest-first. Returns [] when there is a single segment / no FilterManager (caller keeps the existing global aggregation unchanged).

User question about freshness / reliability.

required object_name str

None

Dict with object_name, last_successful_run, days_since_update,

has_recent_failure, and reliability_assessment.

docs.title: _summary_cache_hit
docs.text:
_summary_cache_hit(acc: Optional[dict], domain: Optional[str], entity_filter: Optional[str], object_name: Optional[str]) -> Optional[dict]

Return the prior turn's cached summary payload when it covers THIS request.

A hit requires: no explicit object named this turn (a named dataset/entity must re-resolve), and the prior _last_summary was taken for the same (domain, entity_filter) scope. Returns the cached payload dict or None.

docs.title: skill_summary
docs.text:
skill_summary(question: str, domain: str = None, object_name: str = None, entity_filter: str = None, accumulated_context: dict = None) -> dict

Give a high-level summary of available features for a domain or a specific dataset.

When object_name resolves to a registered dataset, the response is scoped to that dataset's feature list and the resolved dataset context is returned in the result so the caller can persist it in agent state for follow-up EXPLAIN turns.

When entity_filter is given (or object_name resolves to an entity type), the feature list is scoped to that single entity — answering "what features belong to X?".

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question.

required domain str

Therapeutic domain to filter by (optional).

None object_name str

Dataset, entity, or feature name mentioned by the user (optional).

None entity_filter str

Entity name to scope the feature list (optional).

None

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with domain, total_features, by_granularity, themes_summary.

docs.title: skill_help
docs.text:
skill_help(question: str = '') -> dict

Explain the feature store's own conceptual model (direct-answer, no DB query).

For a confused user asking "what is a feature vs a non-feature?", "what is a data domain?", "how is the feature store organized?", "what can I ask you?". Returns a fixed, grounded explanation (EN/FR) anchored on the certainty distinction between a feature (rich, governed column with a known entity key) and a non-feature column (free; relationships only documentation-inferable, never certain).

docs.title: skill_recap
docs.text:
skill_recap(accumulated_context: dict) -> dict

Recap the features, entities, and datasets from the current conversation.

Reads directly from the accumulated_context_graph (MemorySaver-persisted state) instead of querying Teradata. Returns a structured dict grouping features by domain and entity for synthesis into markdown.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default accumulated_context dict

The accumulated_context_graph dict from agent state.

required

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with by_domain, dataset_names, total_features, etc.

docs.title: _get_process_view
docs.text:
_get_process_view(object_name: str)

Return the process VIEW_NAME registered for object_name (feature or view).

Resolution order: 1. _get_feature_catalog_with_docs chain (join feature → process catalog) → VIEW_NAME 2. process_catalog (all domains) → FEATURE_NAMES comma list match → VIEW_NAME 3. process_catalog (all domains) → exact VIEW_NAME match

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default object_name str

Feature name or view name.

required

Returns:

Type Description
  • str VIEW_NAME on a unique match.
  • list[str] of ENTITY_NAME values when the feature matches multiple entities (caller must handle as an ambiguous-feature error).
  • None if not found at all.

docs.title: _extract_formula_identifiers
docs.text:
_extract_formula_identifiers(formula_sql: str) -> list

Extract distinct column/alias identifiers from a SQL expression.

Strips literals, SQL keywords, function names and numeric tokens to return only identifiers that look like column or alias references. Used to cross-check the LLM's source_columns list for completeness.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default formula_sql str

SQL expression string (e.g. the body of a CASE WHEN).

required

Returns:

Type Description list

Sorted list of distinct identifier strings (case-preserved as in formula).

docs.title: _extract_ddl_tables
docs.text:
_extract_ddl_tables(ddl: str) -> set

Extract all base table/view identifiers referenced from FROM/JOIN clauses in ddl.

Returns a set of bare (unquoted, uppercase) identifiers — used as a cheap sanity check that the LLM's source_table claims correspond to a real relation the view actually reads from.

docs.title: _extract_ddl_table_aliases
docs.text:
_extract_ddl_table_aliases(ddl: str) -> dict

Map {alias_lower: REAL_TABLE_UPPER} for FROM/JOIN <table> <alias> clauses.

ONLY records an alias when the relation after FROM/JOIN is a real (schema-qualified or bare) table — NOT a subquery. A subquery wrapper (FROM ( … ) alias) has a ( after FROM/JOIN, so it is skipped and its alias (e.g. ps, phs, and the outer base_data/flags wrappers) is deliberately NOT mapped to any table. Used to resolve an LLM-claimed source_table that is actually a table alias back to the real underlying table, and to detect subquery aliases (absent here) so a bogus "(from )" annotation can be dropped.

docs.title: _resolve_source_table_alias
docs.text:
_resolve_source_table_alias(alias: Optional[str], ddl: Optional[str]) -> Optional[str]

Resolve an LLM-claimed source_table to the real base table, or signal "drop".

Returns:

Type Description Optional[str]
  • the input unchanged when there is no DDL (fail-open — can't prove it bogus);
Optional[str]
  • the same name (uppercased bare) when it IS a real FROM/JOIN base table;
Optional[str]
  • the mapped real table when it is a table alias (FROM claims_raw c → c);
Optional[str]
  • None when it is a subquery/wrapper alias (e.g. flags, base_data, ps) — the caller should drop the "(from )" annotation.

docs.title: _extract_ddl_columns
docs.text:
_extract_ddl_columns(ddl: str) -> set

Extract identifiers that look like real columns referenced in ddl.

Keeps the column in alias.col (and drops the bare alias), then returns the set of remaining whole-word identifiers (uppercased). Used to decide whether a formula identifier missing from the LLM's source_columns is a genuine base/output column (worth surfacing) or a subquery-derived temporary / alias that only exists in an intermediate scope (drop it).

docs.title: _validate_source_columns
docs.text:
_validate_source_columns(source_columns: list, formula_sql: Optional[str], ddl: Optional[str]) -> tuple[list, list]

Validate LLM-produced source_columns against the formula and DDL.

Returns (augmented, missing_identifiers) where: * augmented = source_columns annotated with source_confidence ('high' when the source_table appears in the DDL's FROM/JOIN set, 'low' otherwise) * missing_identifiers = formula identifiers that are NOT present in source_columns AND plausibly are real columns (so the caller can surface them as UNKNOWN). Single/double-character tokens (SQL aliases like p, s) and subquery-derived temporaries that never appear in the DDL body (e.g. mn/mx computed inside an inner SELECT) are dropped so they are not mis-reported as drillable source variables.

docs.title: _split_select_items
docs.text:
_split_select_items(select_body: str) -> list

Split a SELECT list into items at top-level commas (paren-depth aware).

Nested CASE/window/function expressions contain commas; only commas at paren depth 0 separate select items. Used to build the <expr> AS <alias> map for the formula-column closure.

docs.title: _governing_clause_identifiers
docs.text:
_governing_clause_identifiers(expr: str) -> list

Identifiers from the columns that select WHICH ROWS / ORDERING a derived feature is computed over — i.e. window PARTITION BY / ORDER BY keys and GROUP BY keys.

These are the inputs that are NOT visible from the feature's arithmetic yet materially change its value (e.g. a duplicate-detection flag defined via ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY patient_id, drug_code ORDER BY claim_date) — the grain keys decide what counts as a duplicate). It deliberately does NOT pull CASE/WHERE threshold operands: those re-reference the same arithmetic as the derived feature itself (already listed as a variable), so surfacing them would re-introduce the over-listing this prune exists to remove.

docs.title: _build_alias_expr_map
docs.text:
_build_alias_expr_map(ddl: str) -> dict

Map {alias_lower: defining_expression} for every <expr> AS <alias> in ddl.

Scans all SELECT segments (paren-aware) so derived columns at any nesting level are captured. Used by the formula-column closure to walk from a derived feature to the governing columns of its definition.

docs.title: _formula_column_closure
docs.text:
_formula_column_closure(calculation_sql: Optional[str], ddl: Optional[str])

Transitive closure of the columns that materially feed calculation_sql.

Starts from the identifiers in the formula, then for each identifier that is a DERIVED column (<expr> AS <alias> in the DDL) pulls in the identifiers of its GOVERNING clauses (window/CASE/WHERE/GROUP BY/HAVING) — iterating to a fixpoint. This keeps columns that legitimately drive the formula through clauses (e.g. patient_id, drug_code, claim_date behind a ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY patient_id, drug_code ORDER BY claim_date) flag) while NOT pulling in the plain arithmetic operands of an already-listed derived feature.

Returns a set of lowercased column names, or None (= "do not prune") when either input is falsy — fail-open so a missing formula/DDL never hides variables.

docs.title: _prune_source_columns
docs.text:
_prune_source_columns(source_columns: list, closure) -> list

Keep only source_columns whose name is in closure (None ⇒ keep all).

Fail-open: when closure is None (no formula/DDL) the list is returned unchanged, so pruning never removes a legitimately-listed variable.

docs.title: _best_name_similarity
docs.text:
_best_name_similarity(tokens: list, known_names) -> dict

Return {token: (best_score, best_name)} — the closest catalog name and its similarity score for each token.

Size-adaptive backend (the candidate set drives the choice, per design): - small set (len(known_names) <= tdfs4ds.FUZZY_INDB_THRESHOLD): Python difflib.SequenceMatcher ratio — no DB round-trip, ideal for the small per-turn-cached catalogs the consumer agent uses (e.g. 52 names); - large set: Teradata in-database StringSimilarity (jaro_winkler), scored set-based in one query — scales to thousands of names.

known_names is an iterable of catalog names (any case). Matching is case-insensitive. Fail-open: on any error returns {} (caller treats as no fuzzy match, i.e. exact-only).

docs.title: _best_name_similarity_difflib
docs.text:
_best_name_similarity_difflib(tokens: list, names: list) -> dict

Python-difflib backend for _best_name_similarity (small candidate sets).

docs.title: _best_name_similarity_indb
docs.text:
_best_name_similarity_indb(tokens: list, names: list) -> dict

Teradata StringSimilarity (jaro_winkler) backend for large candidate sets.

Builds an inline (token, name) cross-product as the table-operator input and keeps the max jaro_winkler score per token. Falls back to difflib on any SQL error (fail-open via the caller's try/except).

docs.title: _entity_name_pool
docs.text:
_entity_name_pool() -> list

Return the distinct ENTITY_NAME values across all data domains (cache-backed).

Reads the per-turn-cached feature catalog (_all_features_cached) and returns the unique non-empty ENTITY_NAME values. Fail-open: empty list on any error.

docs.title: _canonicalize_name
docs.text:
_canonicalize_name(name: str, kind: str) -> str

Return the canonical catalog spelling of name, correcting a single typo.

Conservative, single-high-confidence typo correction used at the classify seam so a misspelled name never propagates into the context graph (mints a duplicate node). The name pool depends on kind:

  • "domain" → registered DATA_DOMAIN names (get_data_domain())
  • "entity" → distinct ENTITY_NAME values (_entity_name_pool())
  • "object" / anything else → the union of feature / dataset / process-view names (_known_catalog_names()) PLUS domain names (an object_name can itself be a typo'd domain).

Resolution order: blank → returned as-is; exact case-insensitive hit → the canonical-cased pool name; else a single fuzzy match with score >= tdfs4ds.FUZZY_NAME_MATCH_CUTOFF (default 0.90) → the matched name; otherwise name is returned unchanged so the skills' did_you_mean clarification still owns genuine multi-candidate ambiguity.

Fail-open: never raises; any error returns name unchanged. Memoized per turn.

docs.title: _fuzzy_match_feature
docs.text:
_fuzzy_match_feature(object_name: str) -> list

Return catalog feature names that are close (fuzzy) matches for object_name.

Uses :func:difflib.get_close_matches against the full feature catalog (all data domains). Only names with a similarity score >= 0.75 are returned, which catches single-character differences (missing/extra letter, transposition) without being too permissive.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default object_name str

The name as typed by the user (may contain a typo).

required

Returns:

Type Description list

List of up to 3 feature names from the catalog in their original case,

list

ordered by similarity. Empty list when no close match is found.

docs.title: _fuzzy_match_dataset
docs.text:
_fuzzy_match_dataset(object_name: str) -> list

Return dataset names that are close (fuzzy) matches for object_name.

Mirrors :func:_fuzzy_match_feature against the dataset catalog so a near-miss like 'ds_investigation_que' resolves instead of silently failing.

Returns:

Type Description list

List of up to 3 dataset names in their original case, ordered by

list

similarity. Empty list when no close match is found.

docs.title: _available_dataset_names
docs.text:
_available_dataset_names() -> list

Return all dataset names registered in the dataset catalog (original case).

docs.title: _question_is_french
docs.text:
_question_is_french(question: str) -> bool

Cheap token-based French detection for deterministic (non-LLM) replies.

Only used where the answer is templated rather than LLM-generated — the LLM paths handle language matching natively via the system prompt.

docs.title: _entity_from_question
docs.text:
_entity_from_question(question: str, entities: list) -> Optional[str]

Deterministically resolve an ambiguous feature's entity from the question text.

When a feature exists under several entities the user may have already named the one they mean ("total_amount for pharmacy_id") — asking a clarification in that case is a dead turn. Scans the question for the candidate entity names (case-insensitive, _/space-normalised; multi-key entities like claim_id,region match on any key component) and returns the candidate when exactly ONE matches. Zero or multiple matches return None so the normal clarification flow runs.

docs.title: _fetch_view_ddl
docs.text:
_fetch_view_ddl(view_name: str) -> Optional[str]

Fetch the DDL of a view via SHOW VIEW.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default view_name str

Bare view name (no schema prefix needed — uses tdfs4ds.SCHEMA).

required

Returns:

Type Description Optional[str]

DDL text, or None on failure.

docs.title: _feat_cache_key
docs.text:
_feat_cache_key(feature_name: str, entity_name: str = '', data_domain: Optional[str] = None) -> str

Return the composite cache key used in accumulated_context_graph.features.

A feature's identity is the TRIPLET (DATA_DOMAIN, ENTITY_NAME, FEATURE_NAME). Because the active domain can change mid-conversation, the domain is part of the key so a cached feature from domain A is never served for domain B.

Format: DOMAIN::ENTITY::FEATURE_NAME (all uppercased). When entity/domain are unknown/blank those segments are empty so the key never collides with a concrete entry and stays findable by suffix scan. data_domain defaults to the active tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN.

Cross-store federation limit (v0.3.1.148): the triplet has NO store segment, so two read-only stores sharing a (domain, entity, feature) triplet would map to the same key (last-write-wins in accumulated_context_graph.features). This is a no-op for stores with disjoint domains/names (the common case); adding a store segment here is the documented follow-up for collision-proof multi-store memory.

docs.title: _feat_cache_lookup
docs.text:
_feat_cache_lookup(features_dict: dict, feature_name: str, entity_name: str = '', data_domain: Optional[str] = None) -> Optional[dict]

Look up a feature entry in accumulated_context_graph.features.

Triplet-aware (DOMAIN::ENTITY::NAME) with graceful fallback to older snapshots that used NAME::ENTITY or a plain NAME key. Tries in order: 1. Exact triplet key (when domain+entity known) → exact. 2. Active-domain + entity, or active-domain + any entity (suffix scan). 3. Any key ending in ::NAME (legacy DOMAIN::ENTITY::NAME or new) — first match (ambiguous; callers needing precision pass entity+domain). 4. Legacy NAME::ENTITY / NAME:: prefix and plain NAME keys.

docs.title: _query_accumulated_context_for_lineage

_query_accumulated_context_for_lineage(feature_name: str, entity_name: str, accumulated_context: Optional[dict]) -> Optional[dict]

Returns the edges and nodes found in the graph for this feature, or None if the feature is not cached (cache miss). On cache hit, includes depth estimate.

Parameters:

The accumulated_context_graph from state.

Returns:

Name Type Description Optional[dict]

Dict with keys: {"edges": [...], "nodes": {...}, "depth": int} or None (cache miss).

Edges Optional[dict]

[{"source": "...", "target": "...", "relation": "depends_on"}]

Nodes Optional[dict]

{"name": {"type": "feature|view|table|dataset", ...}}

docs.title: _resolve_owning_object_for_column
docs.text:
_resolve_owning_object_for_column(column_name: str, hint_table_name: Optional[str] = None) -> Optional[dict]

Resolve a non-feature column to its owning object via FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS.

Returns {"column": COL, "object": TABLE_NAME, "database": DB, "description": ...} or None when the name is not a documented column. When hint_table_name is supplied and the column appears under several objects, that object is preferred.

docs.title: _lineage_direction
docs.text:
_lineage_direction(question: str) -> str

Infer the asked lineage direction: 'downstream' | 'upstream' | 'both'.

docs.title: _position_object_from_graph
docs.text:
_position_object_from_graph(question: str, object_name: str, *, column_note: Optional[str] = None, correction_note: Optional[str] = None, direction: Optional[str] = None) -> Optional[dict]

Position ANY documented object in the persisted lineage graph (FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH).

Used as the fallback when object_name is not a feature/dataset/process view but is a documented table/plain-view/dataset node — so the user can ask "where does the RAW_CLAIMS table sit / what depends on it?" for non-process objects too.

Returns a lineage result dict (same shape as skill_lineage's slow path), or None when the object is not a node in the graph (caller then falls through to the normal not-found path). When the node exists but has no edges, returns a lineage_empty result (no confabulation), with the object's BD description if any.

docs.title: skill_lineage
docs.text:
skill_lineage(question: str, object_name: str, hint_view_name: Optional[str] = None, accumulated_context: Optional[dict] = None, entity_name: Optional[str] = None, hint_table_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Return the upstream data dependencies of a feature, object, or column.

Walks the dependency graph starting from the registered process view, and returns a structured list of upstream nodes (tables and views) with their types. Does not require LLM — the graph is deterministic.

When object_name is not a feature/dataset/process view (e.g. a plain documented table, view, or a non-feature column), it falls back to the persisted lineage graph (FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH) to position ANY documented object. A column is first resolved to its owning object (TABLE_NAME) and that object is positioned instead — see _position_object_from_graph.

Implements fast-path caching: queries accumulated_context_graph first (cache hit → return immediately), falls back to expensive DDL scans (cache miss → rebuild and merge results for next turn).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question (used to determine response language).

required object_name str

Feature, object (table/view/dataset), or column name.

required hint_view_name Optional[str]

Pre-resolved view name from a prior turn (skips catalog lookup).

None accumulated_context Optional[dict]

The accumulated_context_graph from state (used for cache).

None entity_name Optional[str]

Entity name for disambiguation (used in cache lookups).

None hint_table_name Optional[str]

Owning object for a column (from a prior EXPLAIN/DEFINITION turn via resolved_column_sources) — positions that object directly.

None

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with object_name, process_view, dependencies (list of

dict

{name, type, depth}), plain_summary (LLM plain-language answer),

dict

_from_cache (bool), and resolved_triplet when resolution succeeded.

docs.title: _skill_explain_multi
docs.text:
_skill_explain_multi(question: str, features_list: list) -> dict

Explain how multiple features are calculated in a single LLM call.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question.

required features_list list

List of {feature_name, entity_name, view_name, process_id, description} dicts.

required

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with explanation (plain-text multi-feature answer), features_explained,

dict

context_graph (for context accumulation), and optionally resolved_triplet.

docs.title: _hint_violation_note
docs.text:
_hint_violation_note(hints: dict) -> Optional[str]

Authoritative one-line clarification(s) for the active interpretation hints.

Deterministic flag→sentence map, used as the EXPLAIN backstop when the LLM explanation contradicts a hint (see :func:_find_hint_violations). Generalises DEFINITION's inverted-only _direction_correction_note to every flag.

docs.title: _explain_input_column_context
docs.text:
_explain_input_column_context(identifiers: list, max_cols: int = 6, max_chars: int = 300) -> str

Resolve business descriptions for a formula's input columns (bounded).

Grounds the EXPLAIN LLM in what the inputs mean (so it can derive the output's direction/meaning instead of guessing from the top-level arithmetic). Deterministic, capped, and fail-open: any lookup failure for a column is skipped silently.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default identifiers list

candidate real column names (already alias/temporary-filtered).

required max_cols int

cap on the number of columns resolved (prompt-size / round-trip bound).

6 max_chars int

per-description truncation.

300

Returns:

Type Description str

A ## Input column meanings prompt section, or "" when nothing resolves.

docs.title: _is_sto_backed
docs.text:
_is_sto_backed(ddl: Optional[str]) -> bool

True when the view DDL references a Script Table Operator / tdstone2 STO view.

docs.title: _parse_sto_mapper_uuid
docs.text:
_parse_sto_mapper_uuid(ddl: Optional[str]) -> Optional[str]

Extract the tdstone2 scoring-mapper UUID from a process view's DDL.

The process view of an ML feature selects FROM TDS_STO_SCORING_LIVE_<uuid> (or TDS_STO_SCORING_<uuid>); the <uuid> is the tdstone2 ID_MAPPER_SCORING. Teradata table names use _ in place of the UUID's -, so we normalise back to hyphenated canonical form.

Returns None when no STO scoring reference is present (i.e. not an ML feature).

docs.title: _extract_packages_from_code
docs.text:
_extract_packages_from_code(code_text: Optional[str]) -> list

Return the distinct top-level Python packages imported by code_text.

Scans import X / from X import … lines, keeps the top-level package, drops standard-library modules, dedupes preserving first-seen order, and surfaces sklearn first when present (it is the headline ML library).

docs.title: _is_sentinel_timestamp
docs.text:
_is_sentinel_timestamp(value) -> bool

True when value is the open-end sentinel (year >= 9999) or unparseable-as-real.

tdstone2's trained-models table can carry an open-period sentinel (9999-01-01) in TD_TIMECODE instead of a real training time. Treat such a value as "training date not recorded". A None/blank value is also treated as a sentinel (no usable date).

docs.title: _build_distinct_model_join_sql
docs.text:
_build_distinct_model_join_sql(trained_models_table: str, schema: str, cap: int = 20) -> str

Build the capped DISTINCT trained-model→code resolution query.

From the trained-models table, take DISTINCT ID_TRAINED_MODEL and join TDS_MODEL_REPOSITORY to resolve ID_CODE + ARGUMENTS in one round trip, capped at cap rows. In practice all partitions share one ID_MODEL/ID_CODE so the result collapses to a single row.

docs.title: _read_code_blob
docs.text:
_read_code_blob(schema: str, code_id: str, max_chars: int) -> str

Read the Python source of a tdstone2 code repository entry.

The CODE column is a BLOB; CAST(CODE AS VARCHAR) errors ("Conversion between BYTE data and other types is illegal"), so it must be read via FROM_BYTES(CODE,'ascii'), chunked with SUBSTR for long sources. Returns the decoded text truncated to max_chars (a marker is appended on truncation).

docs.title: _read_trained_models_summary
docs.text:
_read_trained_models_summary(schema: str, trained_models_table: str) -> dict

Summarise a tdstone2 trained-models table: partitions, status, freshness.

Returns {n_partitions, n_successful, model_type, training_time_available}. training_time_available is False when the max TD_TIMECODE is the 9999 open-end sentinel (or unreadable). Fail-open: returns zeros on query failure.

docs.title: _algo_question_wants_code
docs.text:
_algo_question_wants_code(question: str) -> bool

True when the question is about the code / packages / algorithm internals.

docs.title: skill_algorithm
docs.text:
skill_algorithm(question: str, object_name: str, hint_view_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Explain the machine-learning model behind a feature.

Walks the tdfs4ds → tdstone2 chain: resolve the feature's process view, detect the Script-Table-Operator scoring view in its DDL, parse the scoring-mapper UUID, resolve the trained model → model arguments → Python code, and report the algorithm, packages, hyperparameters, model inputs and training-partition status in business language.

Distinct from :func:skill_explain (which explains the SQL formula): this skill explains the ML model (algorithm / code / packages / hyperparameters).

Returns a dict carrying answer (so the synthesiser returns it directly) plus structured fields (algorithm, packages, hyperparameters, model_inputs, n_trained_partitions, training_time_available …) and error on the failure paths.

docs.title: _parse_sto_invocation
docs.text:
_parse_sto_invocation(ddl: Optional[str]) -> dict

Parse a tdstone2 Script-Table-Operator view DDL into its run-time invocation.

Extracts the reproduction-relevant pieces of the FROM Script(...) clause: partition_by / order_by columns, the script_command string, the returns output signature (list), and charset. Regex-based; fail-open — returns {} (or partial) when a piece is absent.

docs.title: _algorithm_system_prompt
docs.text:
_algorithm_system_prompt(facts: dict, model_code: Optional[str], wants_code: bool, input_meanings: str = '') -> str

Assemble the ALGORITHM skill's LLM system prompt (DB-free testable seam).

Injects the deterministic facts (algorithm/packages/hyperparameters/STO config/ structure/invocation), optional input-column business meanings, and the model source (only when the user asked for code). Frames the answer as a tdstone2 reproduction recipe without inventing API the package doesn't have.

The raw STO training view DDL (_raw_ddl inside sto_invocation) is extracted and injected as a separate authoritative block so the LLM can quote it verbatim instead of reconstructing SQL syntax.

docs.title: _infer_algorithm
docs.text:
_infer_algorithm(hyperparameters: dict, code_text: Optional[str]) -> Optional[str]

Best-effort algorithm name from hyperparameter key prefixes or code text.

docs.title: _strip_and_parse_json
docs.text:
_strip_and_parse_json(raw)

Parse a JSON value that may arrive as a dict, str, or fenced str.

tdstone2's ARGUMENTS/METADATA columns are JSON; depending on the driver they come back as a dict already or as a (possibly markdown-fenced) string.

docs.title: skill_explain
docs.text:
skill_explain(question: str, object_name: str, hint_view_name: Optional[str] = None, hint_dataset_features: Optional[list] = None) -> dict

Explain how a feature is calculated by reading its process view DDL.

Fetches the SQL definition of the registered process view and asks the LLM to translate the logic into plain business language — no SQL jargon.

When hint_dataset_features is provided and object_name is empty, explains all features from the current dataset context (up to 10) in a single LLM call.

When object_name contains comma-separated feature names, explains each one.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question.

required object_name str

Feature or view name. May be comma-separated for multiple features.

required hint_view_name Optional[str]

Pre-resolved view name from a prior turn (skips catalog lookup).

None hint_dataset_features Optional[list]

Feature list from a prior SUMMARY turn on a dataset.

None

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with object_name, process_view, explanation, and resolved_triplet.

docs.title: _extract_feature_list_from_question
docs.text:
_extract_feature_list_from_question(question: str, available_features: list, available_entities: Optional[list] = None) -> tuple

Ask the LLM to extract feature names AND entity-key concepts from a multi-feature request.

Used when the user asks something like "show top 50 highest-risk claims with risk band, top driver, provider and pharmacy" — the question references several feature concepts plus entity keys (provider, pharmacy) needed for the SELECT projection.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question text.

required available_features list

List of registered feature names in the active domain.

required available_entities Optional[list]

List of entity-key tokens (e.g. provider_id, pharmacy_id) parsed from the feature catalog. Optional.

None

Returns:

Type Description tuple

(feature_names, entity_keys) — both subsets of the inputs.

tuple

Returns ([], []) when the LLM call fails.

docs.title: _is_numeric_feature_type
docs.text:
_is_numeric_feature_type(feature_type: 'str | None') -> 'bool | None'

Best-effort: True/False from a SQL type string, None when unknown.

docs.title: _choose_ranking_metric
docs.text:
_choose_ranking_metric(feature_cols: list, feature_meta: dict, inverted_features: 'set | None' = None) -> dict

Pick a sortable ranking column for a dashboard top-N query.

Never orders by a categorical band with a bare DESC. Prefers a numeric risk/score column; falls back to a deterministic CASE over a band label.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default feature_cols list

requested feature/column names.

required feature_meta dict

{NAME_UPPER: {"feature_type": str, "is_numeric": bool|None}}.

required inverted_features 'set | None'

names (any case) whose formula inverts the score (lower = higher risk) → ranked ASC.

None

Returns {"metric", "direction", "order_expr", "note"} (order_expr may be a CASE expression; metric/order_expr are None when no sortable column exists).

docs.title: _cover_features_with_datasets
docs.text:
_cover_features_with_datasets(feature_names: list, per_feature: list, dataset_entity_keys: dict, entity_targets: 'list | None' = None) -> dict

Choose the minimal set of datasets covering the requested features (and the requested entity columns) and the join keys to combine them — deterministic, no LLM, no fabrication.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default feature_names list

requested feature names.

required per_feature list

skill_dataset per-feature list (each has datasets: [{dataset_name, database, dataset_id}]).

required dataset_entity_keys dict

{fq_dataset_name: [entity_col, ...]}.

required entity_targets 'list | None'

entity columns the user asked to project (e.g. provider_id, pharmacy_id). When a chosen dataset does not already expose one, an additional dataset whose entity-key list includes it is pulled in so the JOIN brings it along.

None

Returns {"datasets": [fq, ...], "join_keys": [col, ...], "uncovered": [...]}. uncovered lists requested features AND entity targets that no registered dataset exposes.

docs.title: _select_dashboard_datasets
docs.text:
_select_dashboard_datasets(dataset_model: dict, feature_cols: list, entity_keys: list) -> dict

Pick the dataset(s) for a dashboard query from the authoritative dataset model — deterministic, no LLM, no fabrication.

A dataset projects exactly its ENTITY key columns + its registered FEATURE columns. The primary dataset projects the most requested columns; a second dataset is JOINed only when it shares ≥1 entity key AND adds projectable columns. Columns no chosen dataset can project are returned as unavailable.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default dataset_model dict

{fq: {"entity_keys": [...], "features": {UP: {...}}}}.

required feature_cols list

requested feature names.

required entity_keys list

requested entity-id columns (provider_id, …).

required

Returns {"datasets", "join_keys", "proj_entities", "proj_features", "unavailable"}.

docs.title: _build_dataset_model
docs.text:
_build_dataset_model() -> dict

Build the authoritative dataset model from the three dataset-catalog views (CATALOG / ENTITY / FEATURES), per the tdfs4ds data model.

Returns {fq_dataset_name: {"dataset_id", "entity_keys": [...], "features": {FEATURE_NAME_UPPER: {"name", "feature_type", "is_numeric"}}}}.

This is the single source of truth for the dashboard composition: what each dataset actually exposes (its ENTITY key columns + its registered FEATURE columns, with types) — not reconstructed from the global feature catalog. Best-effort: returns {} on failure.

docs.title: _build_dataset_entity_keys
docs.text:
_build_dataset_entity_keys() -> dict

Return {fq_dataset_name: [entity_col, ...]} for all registered datasets.

Entity columns come from get_dataset_entity (parsing the ENTITY_ID / column form). Used to derive join keys for multi-dataset dashboard queries. Best-effort: returns {} on failure.

docs.title: _detect_inverted_features
docs.text:
_detect_inverted_features(feature_names: list) -> set

Return the subset of feature_names whose process-view formula inverts the score (1 - …, so lower = higher risk). Best-effort; bounded to the small requested set. Failures are swallowed (feature simply treated as non-inverted).

docs.title: skill_compose_dashboard
docs.text:
skill_compose_dashboard(question: str) -> dict

Multi-feature dataset composition for dashboard-style requests.

Decomposes the question into a feature list, then re-uses :func:skill_dataset multi-feature logic to find datasets exposing all of them (or report a JOIN gap when none does).

Returns the same shape as multi-feature skill_dataset plus the extracted feature_names so the synthesiser can confirm what was matched.

docs.title: skill_dataset
docs.text:
skill_dataset(question: str, object_name: str, entity_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Find datasets that expose one or more features.

When object_name contains comma-separated feature names, returns per-feature dataset lists plus an intersection of datasets that contain all features.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question.

required object_name str

Feature name(s) to look up, comma-separated for multi-feature.

required

Returns:

Type Description dict

Single-feature: {status, object_name, datasets} as before.

dict

Multi-feature: {status, object_names, per_feature, intersection}.

dict

Status values: 'found', 'partial' (some features have no dataset),

dict

'no_dataset'.

docs.title: _skill_dataset_single
docs.text:
_skill_dataset_single(object_name: str, entity_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Single-feature dataset lookup (original logic).

Feature identity is the TRIPLET, so when the dataset-features frame carries an ENTITY_NAME and the feature name exists under multiple entities, constrain by entity_name so "which datasets expose ?" doesn't merge two entities' datasets (returns ambiguous_feature when the entity is needed but unknown).

docs.title: _skill_dataset_no_match
docs.text:
_skill_dataset_no_match(object_name: str) -> dict

Build the 'no_dataset' result, enriched with entity and sibling features.

docs.title: skill_propose_features
docs.text:
skill_propose_features(use_case: str, entity_name: str, all_features: list, top_n: int = 20) -> dict

Propose the most relevant features for a given use case.

When all_features has more than top_n entries the LLM selects the most relevant ones. When there are top_n or fewer, all are returned directly without an LLM call.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default use_case str

Free-text description of the business need (user's message).

required entity_name str

Entity the features belong to (context for the LLM).

required all_features list

Full list of {name, description} dicts.

required top_n int

Maximum features to propose.

20

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with status='features_proposed', proposed_features,

dict

feature_count (total available), and include_all_offered (bool).

docs.title: _rank_documented_objects
docs.text:
_rank_documented_objects(use_case: str, documented_objects: list, documented_columns: list, diagnostic: list) -> tuple

Pick the use-case-relevant documented datasets / objects / columns.

One bounded LLM pass over the documented (EFS-governed) objects + non-feature columns. Returns (relevant_datasets, relevant_objects, relevant_columns) where datasets are the kind=='dataset' subset. Fail-open: on any error, returns small head slices so the feasibility answer still lists something.

docs.title: skill_feasibility
docs.text:
skill_feasibility(use_case: str) -> dict

Assess a use case WITHOUT creating a dataset (read-only feasibility check).

Scoped strictly to the data governed and documented by the feature store — the registered feature catalog (+ column business descriptions) and the business-dictionary documentation tables (objects + columns + sections). It does NOT inspect physical source tables / external schemas.

Two halves:

  1. Relevant existing assets — ranks the registered features (:func:skill_propose_features) AND the other documented objects: datasets (BD_OBJECTS type 'D'), tables/views ('T'/'V'), and non-feature documented columns (BD_COLUMNS), via :func:_rank_documented_objects. Features are the finest grain but any documented object that helps the use case is surfaced.
  2. Missing capabilities — given the use case, the documented feature coverage (feature names + descriptions, grouped by entity) and the domain business-dictionary themes, the LLM names up to 6 capabilities a complete solution would need that the feature store does NOT yet document. Each is framed against the existing coverage (e.g. "no temporal trend feature at the provider grain"). No dataset is built; nothing outside the EFS is read.

Returns a direct-answer dict (its own answer) — no second LLM pass.

docs.title: skill_check_dataset_name
docs.text:
skill_check_dataset_name(proposed_name: str, feature_name: str) -> dict

Check whether a proposed dataset name is already taken in the catalog.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default proposed_name str

Dataset name proposed by the user.

required feature_name str

Feature for which the dataset is to be created (context only).

required

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with status ('name_available' or 'name_taken'),

dict

proposed_name, and feature_name. When taken, also includes

dict

existing (list of {database, name}).

Resolve a list of {name, description} feature dicts to the selected_features format.

Calls get_feature_versions() and splits results into clean (single-version) and conflict (multi-version) groups.

Parameters:

[{feature_name, versions: [{process_id, process_view_name}]}].

Create a Teradata dataset view and auto-document it.

docs.title: _render_sources_footer

Render a small provenance footer for FRESHNESS and LINEAGE answers.

docs.title: _get_forbidden_objects
docs.text:
_get_forbidden_objects() -> tuple

Return (forbidden_set, dataset_list, feat_names) from the live feature store catalogs.

forbidden_set contains uppercase bare names and DATABASE.TABLE forms for: - all registered process views - all feature storage tables

dataset_list contains dataset names the MCP sub-agent is allowed to query. feat_names is a sorted list of all known feature names (reused by the async skill to avoid a second feature_catalog round-trip). Returns (set(), [], []) on any catalog error.

docs.title: _rewrite_select_top20
docs.text:
_rewrite_select_top20(sql: str) -> str

Rewrite SELECT/SEL to SELECT TOP 20 / SEL TOP 20 when no TOP N is present.

docs.title: _make_guarded_tools
docs.text:
_make_guarded_tools(tools: list, forbidden_objects: set) -> list

Wrap MCP tools with feature-store query guards and a TOP 20 row cap.

Returns new StructuredTool objects — StructuredTool is a Pydantic model so monkey-patching invoke/ainvoke is not possible; we create proper wrappers.

Guards applied on every tool invocation: 1. Reject inputs that reference forbidden feature store objects. 2. Rewrite SELECT queries to SELECT TOP 20 when not already limited. 3. Append a notice when the result appears to hit the 20-row limit.

docs.title: _extract_chart_from_tool_output
docs.text:
_extract_chart_from_tool_output(tool_name: str, output) -> Optional[dict]

Recover a renderable chart from an MCP tool's output, if present.

teradata-mcp-server's plot_* tools return Chart.js JSON, which langchain-mcp-adapters surfaces as the ToolMessage .artifact ({"structured_content": {...}}). Returns {"title", "html"} for the chatbot to embed, or None when the output carries no chart.

docs.title: _skill_mcp_tools_async async
docs.text: _skill_mcp_tools_async(question: str, server_url: str, forbidden_set: set, dataset_list: list, known_features: list = None, transport: str = 'streamable_http') -> dict

docs.title: skill_mcp_tools
docs.text:
skill_mcp_tools(question: str, server_url: str, transport: str = 'streamable_http') -> dict

Delegate a question to external MCP server tools.

Enforces feature-store query guards: - process views and feature storage tables are forbidden - SELECT queries are automatically capped to TOP 20 - dataset catalog is offered as the approved data source

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question.

required server_url str

MCP server endpoint URL.

required transport str

MCP transport protocol — 'streamable_http' (default, modern) or 'sse'.

'streamable_http'

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with answer (str) and error_type (None or 'mcp_error').

docs.title: node_skill_mcp_tools
docs.text:
node_skill_mcp_tools(state: dict) -> dict

LangGraph node: delegate to MCP server tools.

Dashboard-shaped questions are short-circuited to the grounded :func:skill_compose_dashboard path FIRST — even with MCP enabled — so the answer is composed from verified datasets/columns instead of free-form MCP SQL that can fabricate column names. Falls through to MCP when the question does not decompose into a feature composition.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.agent.embedding
docs.text:
Embedding model factory for the tdfs4ds consumer agent.

Uses dedicated EMBEDDING_MODEL_* configuration when set, otherwise falls back to INSTRUCT_MODEL_* variables:

  • 'openai' -> langchain_openai.OpenAIEmbeddings
  • 'bedrock' -> langchain_aws.BedrockEmbeddings (amazon.titan-embed-text-v2:0)
  • 'mistralai' -> langchain_mistralai.MistralAIEmbeddings

Typical LiteLLM setup (vLLM for instruct, TEI for embeddings behind a proxy)::

tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL  = 'https://my-proxy/v1'       # chat completions
tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL = 'https://my-proxy/v1/e5'    # embeddings (custom sub-path)

AWS Bedrock setup requires AWS credentials and region configuration::

tdfs4ds.EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER = 'bedrock'
# Set AWS_REGION or AWS_DEFAULT_REGION environment variable
# AWS credentials can be provided via AWS_PROFILE, IAM role, or ~/.aws/credentials

docs.title: _resolve_proxy
docs.text:
_resolve_proxy(*, for_embedding: bool = False) -> 'str | None'

Return the configured outbound-proxy URL for LLM/embedding HTTP calls.

Embedding calls use EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROXY and fall back to INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROXY; instruct calls use INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROXY only. Returns None (direct connection) when nothing is set.

docs.title: _proxy_http_client
docs.text:
_proxy_http_client(*, for_embedding: bool = False, is_async: bool = False)

Build an httpx client bound to the configured proxy, or None.

The proxy URL's scheme selects the protocol (http:// or socks5://; SOCKS5 requires the httpx[socks] extra). Returning None lets the OpenAI-family constructors use their default client — i.e. this is a no-op when no proxy is configured. Only OpenAI-family providers accept an http_client; Bedrock/GCP use their own SDK proxy mechanisms.

docs.title: _inject_provider_env
docs.text:
_inject_provider_env(*, for_embedding: bool = False) -> None

Inject tdfs4ds config variables into the process environment so that LangChain / boto3 / google-cloud clients pick them up automatically.

The active provider is read from
  • EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER (when for_embedding=True) → falls back to INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER
  • INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER (when for_embedding=False)
Resolution order for setting values
  • EMBEDDING_MODEL_ → INSTRUCT_MODEL_ (when for_embedding=True)
  • INSTRUCT_MODEL_* only (when for_embedding=False)

docs.title: get_embeddings
docs.text:
get_embeddings() -> Embeddings

Return a LangChain Embeddings instance based on the active provider.

Resolution order for each setting: - EMBEDDING_MODEL_PROVIDER -> falls back to INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER - EMBEDDING_MODEL_API_KEY -> falls back to INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY - EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL -> falls back to INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL - EMBEDDING_MODEL_MODEL -> used as-is (default: text-embedding-3-small)

Injects tdfs4ds config variables into environment so that boto3, Azure, and Google Cloud clients pick up credentials and endpoints automatically.

Returns:

Name Type Description Embeddings Embeddings

A configured LangChain embedding model.

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If the resolved provider is not supported.

docs.title: get_vector_store_embedding
docs.text:
get_vector_store_embedding()

Return the embedding object for the active vector-store backend.

Selection rule
  • tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE set -> teradatagenai.TeradataAI(...) (in-database embedding; used by the 'teradata' backend).
  • otherwise -> get_embeddings() (LangChain Embeddings; works with vLLM / TEI / OpenAI-compatible setups and is also accepted as embedding= by TeradataVectorStore).

The ChromaDB backend always uses get_embeddings() regardless of this setting — TeradataAI is a Vantage-side construct and is only meaningful when the vector store also lives in Vantage.

docs.title: list_embedding_models
docs.text:
list_embedding_models(sub_paths: list[str] | None = None) -> 'pd.DataFrame'

List available embedding models from the configured provider.

For LiteLLM setups with multiple TEI backends (e.g. /v1/e5, /v1/code), pass the sub-path names to query each one::

list_embedding_models(sub_paths=['e5', 'code'])

Each sub-path is appended to INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL and queried for /models. Results are merged into a single DataFrame with a SUB_PATH column.

When sub_paths is None, the function derives the sub-path from EMBEDDING_MODEL_URL (if set) or queries INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL directly.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default sub_paths list[str] | None

List of LiteLLM route prefixes to query (e.g. ['e5', 'code']).

None

Returns:

Type Description 'pd.DataFrame'

DataFrame with columns: MODEL_ID, OWNED_BY, SUB_PATH (OpenAI-compatible) or

'pd.DataFrame'

MODEL_ID, PROVIDER, MODEL_NAME (Bedrock).

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If the provider is not recognized or not configured.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.agent.evaluation
docs.text:
Agent evaluation: scenario generation, run, LLM-as-judge, and reporting.

Self-contained evaluation harness for the tdfs4ds consumer agent. It does NOT use an external eval framework (RAGAS etc.) — the judge reuses the configured instruct model via the agent's own _llm_call seam, and scenario "expected answers" are MCP/Teradata-grounded structured facts (catalog rows), not gold prose.

docs.title: Public surface
docs.text:
• :func:generate_scenarios — build a scenario set grounded in the live catalog.
  • :func:run_evaluation — run the agent over every turn and judge each.
  • :func:build_report — render a Markdown or HTML synthesis + detail report.
  • :func:evaluate_agent — programmatic entry point: load a scenario file, run, judge, and write the report. Re-exported as tdfs4ds.evaluate_agent.
  • :func:load_scenarios / :func:save_scenarios / :func:merge_scenarios — import/export helpers (replace vs append).

The on-disk results schema mirrors the existing evaluation_*.json format: {type, timestamp, model, tdfs4ds_version, n_scenarios, n_turns, summary[], turns[]} with five 1–5 metrics (faithfulness, completeness, relevance, robustness, efficiency).

docs.title: _generate_fact_first
docs.text:
_generate_fact_first(facts: dict, *, facts_per_kind: int = 2, multi_turn: bool = True, start_id: int = 0) -> list

Fact-first ("Jeopardy") scenarios for non-feature OBJECTS and COLUMNS.

Harvests the answer (a grounded fact) first, then writes the question around it: object/column DEFINITION + LINEAGE, plus a column→object→lineage COMPOUND turn. Grounded in BD_OBJECTS / BD_COLUMNS + FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH neighbors. Returns a list of scenario dicts; empty when no non-feature objects/columns are documented.

docs.title: _verified_names_from_context
docs.text:
_verified_names_from_context(graph_context: dict) -> set

Collect every real object/feature/view/dataset name from a turn's accumulated graph context, so the judge can tell real-but-unlisted names apart from fabrications.

The agent records what it actually grounded on in accumulated_context_graph (features/datasets/objects/columns + per-feature view_name). Those names are authoritative — expected_facts is only a focal spot-check, not the complete truth, so anything here must NOT be judged a hallucination. Returns an UPPER-cased set; empty when no context (fail-open).

docs.title: _judge_turn
docs.text:
_judge_turn(user: str, answer: str, expected_facts: dict, expected_intent: str, actual_intent: str, tokens: int, graph_context: Optional[dict] = None) -> dict

Score one turn on the five metrics with an LLM-as-judge grounded on facts.

Deterministic signals (intent match, fact presence in the answer) are computed first and injected into the judge prompt so the LLM grades against ground truth rather than its own priors.

graph_context is the turn's accumulated_context_graph — the names the agent actually grounded on. It is the second authoritative source: a name the answer mentions that is in EITHER expected_facts OR this verified set is real, not a hallucination. expected_facts alone is a focal spot-check, not a closed-world checklist (so set-returning skills — SUMMARY/FEASIBILITY/DATASET — are not penalised for correctly enumerating many real assets).

Classify a user question into one of the available intents.

None intent_descriptions dict

docs.title: detect_data_domain_for_object
docs.text:
detect_data_domain_for_object(object_name: str, prefer_domain: Optional[str] = None) -> Optional[str]

Return the DATA_DOMAIN that contains object_name as a feature name.

Searches the full feature catalog (all data domains). Falls back to the process catalog (VIEW_NAME) if the feature is not found by name.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default object_name str

Feature or view name to locate.

required prefer_domain Optional[str]

When the object exists in multiple domains, return this one if it is among the matches. Used to prevent silent context switches on ambiguous feature names (e.g. "Claims" appearing in multiple feature stores).

None

Returns:

Type Description Optional[str]

Matching DATA_DOMAIN string, or None if not found.

Cross-store federation limit (v0.3.1.148): the catalog scanned here is the federated UNION across the primary + read-only stores, but resolution returns a bare DATA_DOMAIN with NO store tag (and _with_domain only switches tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN, not the active store). With DISJOINT stores a domain uniquely identifies its store, so this is correct. If two stores share a domain name, _pick returns the first match (primary rows sort first → primary wins); making resolution store-aware is the documented follow-up.

Enumerates ALL datasets that expose the feature (not just one) and emits a paste-ready SELECT snippet for each so the consumer can pick the right view for their dashboard.

skill_freshness_for_feature_list(question: str, feature_names: list, entity_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Aggregate freshness across a list of feature names referenced by a group pronoun.

Used when the user says "are these features up to date?" without naming any of them explicitly. Looks each feature up in the catalog, collects its process_id, pulls follow_up_report for each, and returns a combined last-successful-run / days-since / failure verdict.

docs.title: skill_summary
docs.text:
skill_summary(question: str, domain: str = None, object_name: str = None, entity_filter: str = None, accumulated_context: dict = None) -> dict

Give a high-level summary of available features for a domain or a specific dataset.

When object_name resolves to a registered dataset, the response is scoped to that dataset's feature list and the resolved dataset context is returned in the result so the caller can persist it in agent state for follow-up EXPLAIN turns.

When entity_filter is given (or object_name resolves to an entity type), the feature list is scoped to that single entity — answering "what features belong to X?".

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question.

required domain str

Therapeutic domain to filter by (optional).

None object_name str

Dataset, entity, or feature name mentioned by the user (optional).

None entity_filter str

Entity name to scope the feature list (optional).

None

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with domain, total_features, by_granularity, themes_summary.

docs.title: skill_lineage
docs.text:
skill_lineage(question: str, object_name: str, hint_view_name: Optional[str] = None, accumulated_context: Optional[dict] = None, entity_name: Optional[str] = None, hint_table_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Return the upstream data dependencies of a feature, object, or column.

Walks the dependency graph starting from the registered process view, and returns a structured list of upstream nodes (tables and views) with their types. Does not require LLM — the graph is deterministic.

When object_name is not a feature/dataset/process view (e.g. a plain documented table, view, or a non-feature column), it falls back to the persisted lineage graph (FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH) to position ANY documented object. A column is first resolved to its owning object (TABLE_NAME) and that object is positioned instead — see _position_object_from_graph.

Implements fast-path caching: queries accumulated_context_graph first (cache hit → return immediately), falls back to expensive DDL scans (cache miss → rebuild and merge results for next turn).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question (used to determine response language).

required object_name str

Feature, object (table/view/dataset), or column name.

required hint_view_name Optional[str]

Pre-resolved view name from a prior turn (skips catalog lookup).

None accumulated_context Optional[dict]

The accumulated_context_graph from state (used for cache).

None entity_name Optional[str]

Entity name for disambiguation (used in cache lookups).

None hint_table_name Optional[str]

Owning object for a column (from a prior EXPLAIN/DEFINITION turn via resolved_column_sources) — positions that object directly.

None

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with object_name, process_view, dependencies (list of

dict

{name, type, depth}), plain_summary (LLM plain-language answer),

dict

_from_cache (bool), and resolved_triplet when resolution succeeded.

docs.title: skill_explain
docs.text:
skill_explain(question: str, object_name: str, hint_view_name: Optional[str] = None, hint_dataset_features: Optional[list] = None) -> dict

Explain how a feature is calculated by reading its process view DDL.

Fetches the SQL definition of the registered process view and asks the LLM to translate the logic into plain business language — no SQL jargon.

When hint_dataset_features is provided and object_name is empty, explains all features from the current dataset context (up to 10) in a single LLM call.

When object_name contains comma-separated feature names, explains each one.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question.

required object_name str

Feature or view name. May be comma-separated for multiple features.

required hint_view_name Optional[str]

Pre-resolved view name from a prior turn (skips catalog lookup).

None hint_dataset_features Optional[list]

Feature list from a prior SUMMARY turn on a dataset.

None

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with object_name, process_view, explanation, and resolved_triplet.

docs.title: skill_algorithm
docs.text:
skill_algorithm(question: str, object_name: str, hint_view_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Explain the machine-learning model behind a feature.

Walks the tdfs4ds → tdstone2 chain: resolve the feature's process view, detect the Script-Table-Operator scoring view in its DDL, parse the scoring-mapper UUID, resolve the trained model → model arguments → Python code, and report the algorithm, packages, hyperparameters, model inputs and training-partition status in business language.

Distinct from :func:skill_explain (which explains the SQL formula): this skill explains the ML model (algorithm / code / packages / hyperparameters).

Returns a dict carrying answer (so the synthesiser returns it directly) plus structured fields (algorithm, packages, hyperparameters, model_inputs, n_trained_partitions, training_time_available …) and error on the failure paths.

docs.title: skill_dataset
docs.text:
skill_dataset(question: str, object_name: str, entity_name: Optional[str] = None) -> dict

Find datasets that expose one or more features.

When object_name contains comma-separated feature names, returns per-feature dataset lists plus an intersection of datasets that contain all features.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question.

required object_name str

Feature name(s) to look up, comma-separated for multi-feature.

required

Returns:

Type Description dict

Single-feature: {status, object_name, datasets} as before.

dict

Multi-feature: {status, object_names, per_feature, intersection}.

dict

Status values: 'found', 'partial' (some features have no dataset),

dict

'no_dataset'.

docs.title: skill_propose_features
docs.text:
skill_propose_features(use_case: str, entity_name: str, all_features: list, top_n: int = 20) -> dict

Propose the most relevant features for a given use case.

When all_features has more than top_n entries the LLM selects the most relevant ones. When there are top_n or fewer, all are returned directly without an LLM call.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default use_case str

Free-text description of the business need (user's message).

required entity_name str

Entity the features belong to (context for the LLM).

required all_features list

Full list of {name, description} dicts.

required top_n int

Maximum features to propose.

20

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dict with status='features_proposed', proposed_features,

dict

feature_count (total available), and include_all_offered (bool).

docs.title: skill_check_dataset_name
docs.text:
skill_check_dataset_name(proposed_name: str, feature_name: str) -> dict

Check whether a proposed dataset name is already taken in the catalog.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default proposed_name str

Dataset name proposed by the user.

required feature_name str

required

Dict with status ('name_available' or 'name_taken'),

existing (list of {database, name}).

Create a Teradata dataset view and auto-document it.

dict

'query_returned'), dataset_name, schema, feature_count,

dict

and doc_status ('success', 'failed', or 'skipped').

docs.title: resolve_features_for_dataset
docs.text:
resolve_features_for_dataset(entity_name: str, feature_list: list) -> tuple

Resolve a list of {name, description} feature dicts to the selected_features format.

Calls get_feature_versions() and splits results into clean (single-version) and conflict (multi-version) groups.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default entity_name str

Entity the features belong to.

required feature_list list

List of {name, description} dicts (as produced by skill_propose_features).

required

Returns:

Name Type Description tuple

Tuple of (selected_features_dict, conflicts_list).
