[{feature_name, versions: [{process_id, process_view_name}]}].

docs.title: synthesize_answer
docs.text:
synthesize_answer(question: str, skill_result: dict, intent: str, resolved_feature_list: Optional[list] = None, accumulated_context: Optional[dict] = None) -> str

Convert structured skill output into a plain-language response.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

Original user question.

required skill_result dict

Dict returned by the skill function.

required intent str

The classified intent (SEARCH, DEFINITION, etc.).

required resolved_feature_list Optional[list]

Optional list of feature names from current/prior turns (for group context).

None accumulated_context Optional[dict]

Optional accumulated context graph with cached metadata.

None

Returns:

Type Description str

A natural language answer suitable for a non-technical business user.

docs.title: node_skill_mcp_tools
docs.text:
node_skill_mcp_tools(state: dict) -> dict

LangGraph node: delegate to MCP server tools.

Dashboard-shaped questions are short-circuited to the grounded :func:skill_compose_dashboard path FIRST — even with MCP enabled — so the answer is composed from verified datasets/columns instead of free-form MCP SQL that can fabricate column names. Falls through to MCP when the question does not decompose into a feature composition.

docs.title: _recognize_object_type
docs.text:
_recognize_object_type(name: str) -> dict

Identify what type of object name is by querying catalogs.

Returns a dict with keys: type ("feature"|"dataset"|"entity"|"data_domain"|"process"|"unknown"), entity_name, view_name, process_id.

docs.title: _known_catalog_names
docs.text:
_known_catalog_names() -> set

Return the union of registered feature, dataset and BD object names (uppercase).

Used to filter vector-search hits so that fabricated names (embedding-similar but not actually registered) never reach the LLM context. Empty set on failure means no filtering is applied (fail-open).

docs.title: _best_name_similarity
docs.text:
_best_name_similarity(tokens: list, known_names) -> dict

Return {token: (best_score, best_name)} — the closest catalog name and its similarity score for each token.

Size-adaptive backend (the candidate set drives the choice, per design): - small set (len(known_names) <= tdfs4ds.FUZZY_INDB_THRESHOLD): Python difflib.SequenceMatcher ratio — no DB round-trip, ideal for the small per-turn-cached catalogs the consumer agent uses (e.g. 52 names); - large set: Teradata in-database StringSimilarity (jaro_winkler), scored set-based in one query — scales to thousands of names.

known_names is an iterable of catalog names (any case). Matching is case-insensitive. Fail-open: on any error returns {} (caller treats as no fuzzy match, i.e. exact-only).

docs.title: _looks_like_group_reference
docs.text: _looks_like_group_reference(question: str) -> bool

docs.title: _looks_like_algorithm_question
docs.text:
_looks_like_algorithm_question(question: str) -> bool

True when the question is about the ML model/algorithm/packages/hyperparameters.

docs.title: _resolve_compound_sub_intents
docs.text:
_resolve_compound_sub_intents(result, question: str, object_name, entity_name)

Return an ordered list of >=2 sub-intent dicts for a COMPOUND question, else [].

Prefers the classifier's own sub_intents. Falls back to a deterministic backstop for the common pairs (DEFINITION/EXPLAIN/FRESHNESS + LINEAGE, EXPLAIN + ALGORITHM) so a small model that emits a single intent still produces the compound. Validates each sub-intent's name; collapses to [] when only one distinct sub-intent survives (single-purpose question — unchanged behaviour).

docs.title: _rescue_out_of_scope
docs.text:
_rescue_out_of_scope(question: str)

Rescue an in-scope question the classifier wrongly sent to OUT_OF_SCOPE.

Returns (object_name, intent) when the question names a KNOWN catalog feature/object, else (None, None) (leave OUT_OF_SCOPE as-is). Intent is EXPLAIN for a "how/formula" shape, otherwise DEFINITION. Deterministic and grounded in _known_catalog_names so genuine off-topic questions still refuse.

docs.title: _normalize_source_tables
docs.text:
_normalize_source_tables(tables, view_sql)

Drop source-table entries that are not real FROM/JOIN base tables of view_sql.

Prevents subquery aliases (e.g. flags, base_data) from being stored in the accumulated context graph as real tables. Fail-open: returns the input unchanged when view_sql is missing or the real-table set can't be derived. Handles both a list of names and a list of {name: …} dicts.

docs.title: _merge_accumulated_context
docs.text:
_merge_accumulated_context(current: Optional[dict], updates: dict) -> dict

Non-destructive deep merge of structural relationship updates into the accumulated graph.

Each section is a flat dict keyed by an uppercase identifier. New entries overwrite existing ones so the map always reflects the latest resolution. Focus pointers (_focus, _focus_dataset, _focus_entity) and user_context are updated atomically. User preferences are merged, with new values overriding old ones. Freshness timelines and search results are merged entry-by-entry (new overwrites old).

docs.title: _build_context_update
docs.text:
_build_context_update(skill_result: dict, domain: Optional[str], current_acc: Optional[dict] = None) -> dict

Extract structural relationships and discoverable payload from a skill result.

Reads resolved_triplet (single-feature path) and context_graph.features (multi-feature path) to populate features, processes, datasets, and entities. Also extracts and merges discoverable payload: - From EXPLAIN: calculation_sql, source_columns, explanation, docs_sources - From LINEAGE: view_sql, source_tables, node metadata (type, depth, features) - From USAGE: entity_keys, select_template - From FRESHNESS: last_successful_run, days_since_update, has_recent_failure

current_acc is the accumulated context graph before this update. When provided, existing feature-entry fields (e.g. dataset_names, business_description) are inherited so that skills which do not return those fields (e.g. EXPLAIN) do not accidentally overwrite them with empty values.

docs.title: _resolve_clarification_choice
docs.text:
_resolve_clarification_choice(reply: str, interpretations: list[dict]) -> Optional[dict]

Match the user's reply against the stored clarification options.

Accepts: - A 1-based index ("1", "2", "option 2", "the second one") - A fuzzy label/object match (substring or difflib similarity)

Returns the matched interpretation dict, or None when unresolvable.

docs.title: _ambiguous_feature_pending
docs.text:
_ambiguous_feature_pending(result: dict, intent: str) -> dict

Build the state-update dict for an ambiguous-feature entity clarification.

Called by node_skill_definition when skill_definition returns error='ambiguous_feature'. Stores the entity list, original object name, and origin intent so that the next turn's classify can resolve the reply deterministically without an LLM round-trip.

Factored into a helper so other skills (USAGE, FRESHNESS, …) can trigger the same pending flow with a single call when they encounter the same error.

docs.title: _fuzzy_resolve_from_column_sources
docs.text:
_fuzzy_resolve_from_column_sources(question: str, col_sources: dict) -> Optional[str]

Try to resolve a vague column/table reference from a prior EXPLAIN result.

Splits the question into tokens and checks whether any token appears as a substring in a known column name or source table name. Returns the matched name only when exactly one candidate matches (to avoid false positives on common words).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

Raw user question.

required col_sources dict

resolved_column_sources dict (COLUMN_UPPER → source_table).

required

Returns:

Type Description Optional[str]

The matched column name or source table name, or None.

docs.title: _clean_object_name
docs.text:
_clean_object_name(name)

Strip markdown/quote wrappers and a trailing annotation around a name.

The intent classifier (or user) sometimes wraps the object name in markdown backticks/quotes (`provider_z_score`) or appends a parenthetical annotation (provider_z_score (from flags)). Catalog lookups are exact, case-insensitive matches, so either form matches nothing and DEFINITION wrongly falls through to "feature not found / did you mean …".

Strips, in order: surrounding backticks/quotes/whitespace, then ONE trailing (...) group, then surrounding wrappers again. Only the surrounding wrappers and a trailing parenthetical are removed — interior characters are never touched (feature names contain _ and digits; composite entities use commas like claim_id,region; names never contain parentheses). Falls back to the wrapper-only result if stripping the parenthetical would empty the name.

Returns the input unchanged when falsy (None / "").

docs.title: _split_object_names
docs.text:
_split_object_names(obj)

Split a multi-object string into a clean list of names.

Handles the "A and B" / "A & B" / "A, B" forms the classifier emits for multi-feature/-dataset/-domain questions, normalising the conjunctions to commas, then cleaning each element with :func:_clean_object_name (strips wrappers + a trailing parenthetical per element — the classify-node clean runs on the whole comma-joined string and cannot reach per-element annotations). Mirrors the inline idiom used in node_skill_definition so multi-name parsing is consistent across skills.

Returns [] for a falsy input.

docs.title: _extract_feature_list_for_routing
docs.text:
_extract_feature_list_for_routing(question, known_names, fuzzy_fn=None)

Extract the catalog feature names a question lists, for RECAP→DEFINITION re-routing.

Tokenizes the raw question (commas / newlines / whitespace), cleans each token (_clean_object_name strips wrappers + a trailing (from flags) style annotation), drops stop-words and tokens shorter than 3 chars, and keeps a token when it EXACTLY matches a known catalog name OR fuzzy-matches one with high confidence (>= tdfs4ds.FUZZY_NAME_MATCH_CUTOFF, default 0.90 — a real typo, not a coincidental word).

Typo'd tokens are returned as the user typed them (after cleaning), so the downstream skill_definition_multi per-element fuzzy path emits the "did you mean …?" correction rather than the guard auto-correcting silently.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question

Raw user question text.

required known_names

Iterable of catalog names (any case).

required fuzzy_fn

Optional tokens, names -> {token: (score, name)} (injected for tests). Defaults to the size-adaptive _best_name_similarity.

None

Returns:

Type Description

(matched_tokens, had_colon) — matched_tokens is the list of name-like

tokens in question order (deduped, as-typed); had_colon is True when the

question contains a ':' before the list (e.g. "what are these features: …").

Empty list when nothing matches (caller leaves intent unchanged).

docs.title: _node_classify_impl
docs.text:
_node_classify_impl(state: AgentState, intent_model, intent_descriptions: dict, mcp_enabled: Optional[bool] = None) -> dict

Core classify logic — extracted so it can be closed over a dynamic model.

Short-circuits the LLM classifier for all dataset-creation pending states so that the user's reply is routed directly to the dataset skill without being misclassified.

When the intent is DEFINITION but no object_name was extracted, attempts a fuzzy match against resolved_column_sources (populated by a prior EXPLAIN turn) so that vague follow-ups like "what does the date mean?" resolve to the correct column or source table without requiring the user to quote the exact name.

Extracts user preferences (e.g., currency, output format) and merges them into accumulated_preferences for all downstream skills to access.

docs.title: node_classify
docs.text:
node_classify(state: AgentState) -> dict

Run intent classification using the hardwired full-intent model (legacy path).

docs.title: node_detect_domain
docs.text:
node_detect_domain(state: AgentState) -> dict

Detect the DATA_DOMAIN for the requested object and list all available domains.

DATA_DOMAIN is sticky: once resolved for the thread, it is only changed when the current turn extracts an explicit object_name AND detect_data_domain_for_object returns a domain that actually owns it. This prevents silent context switches on vague follow-up questions.

docs.title: _with_domain
docs.text:
_with_domain(domain: Optional[str])

Context manager that temporarily sets tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN.

docs.title: _extract_triplet
docs.text:
_extract_triplet(skill_result: Optional[dict]) -> Optional[dict]

Pull resolved_triplet from a skill result if present and non-empty.

docs.title: _skill_with_triplet
docs.text:
_skill_with_triplet(skill_result: dict, current_acc: Optional[dict] = None) -> dict

Build state-update dict from a skill result, propagating triplet and dataset context when resolved.

Focus pointers (_focus, _focus_entity, _focus_dataset) are written into the accumulated_context_graph rather than as top-level state fields.

docs.title: _hint_view
docs.text:
_hint_view(state: AgentState) -> Optional[str]

Return the remembered view_name only when this turn has no explicit object.

When the user names something explicitly, we re-resolve rather than reuse the stale hint so that a new feature mentioned in the same thread is handled correctly.

docs.title: _effective_object
docs.text:
_effective_object(state: AgentState) -> str

Return the object name: explicit this turn > last focus in accumulated graph.

docs.title: _get_entity_name_from_state
docs.text:
_get_entity_name_from_state(state: AgentState) -> Optional[str]

Extract entity name from state for fast-path queries (skills can use this for disambiguation).

docs.title: _looks_like_dashboard_request
docs.text: _looks_like_dashboard_request(question: str) -> bool

docs.title: _capture_feature_group_from_search
docs.text:
_capture_feature_group_from_search(updates: dict) -> dict

Populate resolved_feature_list / resolved_entity_name from a SEARCH or compose-dashboard skill result.

Walks the skill result for feature_names (composition path) or matches (regular search) and lifts the feature names into resolved_feature_list. When all matched features share the same entity in the catalog, also remembers resolved_entity_name. This keeps multi-turn group-pronoun questions ("are these features fresh?") in lockstep with the SUMMARY path.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default updates dict

Dict containing the skill_result key.

required

Returns:

Type Description dict

The same updates dict, augmented with state keys when applicable.

docs.title: _make_feature_group
docs.text:
_make_feature_group(entity: Optional[str], feature_names: list, question: str) -> Optional[dict]

Build a _recent_feature_group record for the focus history.

Only returns a record for genuine multi-feature groups (>1 feature); a single-feature turn is not a group and must never evict the history. The label_tokens carry words from the question that qualify the group (e.g. "pharmacy", "provider") so a later group-pronoun reference can match on the qualifier even when no entity was resolved.

docs.title: _entity_group_from_question
docs.text:
_entity_group_from_question(question: str) -> Optional[tuple]

When the question names a known entity ("...for pharmacies", "provider behaviour"), return (entity_name, [all feature names for that entity]) from the live catalog.

SEARCH match sets vary with the LLM, so a group reference like "the pharmacy features I just saw" is far more robustly grounded against the full catalog feature set for the matched entity than against the LLM's selected subset. Returns None when no single entity is clearly named.

docs.title: node_skill_search
docs.text:
node_skill_search(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the search skill.

When the question looks like a dashboard-composition request (multiple feature concepts + dashboard/SELECT/top-N tokens), runs :func:skill_compose_dashboard first to find a dataset exposing the whole set. If decomposition yields fewer than 2 features, falls back to the regular SEARCH path.

Also captures the resulting feature names into resolved_feature_list so follow-up group-pronoun questions ("are these features fresh?") can aggregate freshness without the user having to re-state the names.

docs.title: _apply_definition_enrichment
docs.text:
_apply_definition_enrichment(result: dict, question: str) -> None

Mutate result in-place: extend explanation with approved plugin knowledge.

Scans $TDFS4DS_SKILLS_FOLDER for SKILL.md files that carry an enrichment-for: DEFINITION field and whose enrichment-match keywords appear in the base explanation. When matches are found, makes a second LLM call that presents the grounded base answer followed by the approved domain knowledge. The enriched text replaces result["explanation"] and "PLUGIN_ENRICHMENT" is added to result["docs_sources"].

This function is a no-op when: - $TDFS4DS_SKILLS_FOLDER is not set or contains no enrichment skills. - The result has no "explanation" key or is a cached answer. - Any exception occurs (always non-fatal — the base answer is preserved).

docs.title: node_skill_definition
docs.text:
node_skill_definition(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the definition skill.

For comma-separated object names (multi-object question — features, datasets, or a mix), calls skill_definition once per name and combines the explanations into a single answer with per-object section headers. The last successfully resolved triplet is propagated to state for follow-up turns.

docs.title: node_skill_usage
docs.text:
node_skill_usage(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the usage skill with fast-path caching.

docs.title: _resolve_group_from_history
docs.text:
_resolve_group_from_history(question: str, acc: Optional[dict]) -> Optional[dict]

Resolve a qualified group reference against the focus history.

For "the pharmacy-level features I just saw", the live _focus_feature_list may have been overwritten by an intervening single-feature turn. This scans _recent_feature_groups (most-recent first) and returns the group whose entity or label tokens match a qualifier word in the question. Returns {"features": [...], "entity": ...} on a unique match, else None.

docs.title: _question_has_group_qualifier
docs.text:
_question_has_group_qualifier(question: str, acc: Optional[dict]) -> bool

True when a group reference is scoped by an entity/qualifier word.

"the features I just saw" is unqualified; "the pharmacy-level features I just saw" is qualified. A qualifier is a token from a known entity name (from the focus history or accumulated entities). Qualified-but-unresolvable group references must NOT fall back to a sticky single feature.

docs.title: node_skill_freshness
docs.text:
node_skill_freshness(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the freshness skill.

When the user's question is a group pronoun ("are these features up to date", "the pharmacy features I just saw") and a feature list is remembered from a previous turn, aggregates freshness across the whole group instead of trying to resolve a single object.

docs.title: node_skill_summary
docs.text:
node_skill_summary(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the summary skill.

docs.title: node_skill_feasibility
docs.text:
node_skill_feasibility(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the FEASIBILITY skill — read-only use-case capability assessment.

Identifies relevant existing features and missing-but-derivable capabilities for a described use case, WITHOUT creating a dataset. Direct-answer skill.

docs.title: node_skill_data_domains
docs.text:
node_skill_data_domains(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the DATA_DOMAINS skill — list/describe feature-store data domains.

No domain named → inventory all DATA_DOMAINs. One or more named (comma/and separated) → per-domain feature count + entity breakdown, typo-tolerant. Direct-answer skill (the skill builds its own answer).

docs.title: node_skill_help
docs.text:
node_skill_help(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the HELP skill — explain the feature store's conceptual model.

Direct-answer (no Teradata query); the skill builds its own answer.

docs.title: node_skill_compound
docs.text:
node_skill_compound(state: AgentState, intent_node_map: dict) -> dict

Orchestrate a COMPOUND (multi-purpose) question — run each sub-intent in order.

Generalises the hardcoded EXPLAIN+ALGORITHM dual-intent pattern: for every sub-intent the classifier emitted, dispatch to the SAME skill node it would normally route to, synthesize that sub-answer to text, and join the parts with a --- divider. Sequential sub-intents (depends_on_prev) thread the prior sub-question's resolved owning object forward (column→object→lineage), reusing skill_lineage's hint_table_name via resolved_column_sources. Accumulated context is merged from every sub-result so both subjects stay pronoun-referenceable.

docs.title: node_skill_recap
docs.text:
node_skill_recap(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the RECAP skill — recap features/datasets from conversation context.

Reads directly from accumulated_context_graph; no Teradata queries.

docs.title: node_skill_out_of_scope
docs.text:
node_skill_out_of_scope(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the OUT_OF_SCOPE skill — polite refusal for off-topic questions.

No-op node; the answer is rendered directly in synthesize_answer.

docs.title: node_skill_lineage
docs.text:
node_skill_lineage(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the lineage skill with fast-path caching.

Supports multi-feature comma-separated lineage via context_graph consolidation.

docs.title: node_skill_explain
docs.text:
node_skill_explain(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the explain skill and persist column→source-table mapping for follow-up DEFINITION turns.

docs.title: node_pre_skill_explain
docs.text:
node_pre_skill_explain(state: AgentState) -> dict

No-op node emitted before node_skill_explain to show a live trace step immediately.

docs.title: node_skill_algorithm
docs.text:
node_skill_algorithm(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the ALGORITHM skill (explain the ML model behind a feature).

Mirrors node_skill_explain: resolves the effective object, runs the skill in the active domain, and merges the resolved feature triplet + context update. The skill returns its final answer directly, so no second LLM pass.

Dual-intent (EXPLAIN + ALGORITHM): when the question asks both how the formula works ("how is X computed?") AND about the ML model ("what algorithm is behind it?"), this node runs EXPLAIN first and prepends its answer. This handles compound questions without changing the graph structure.

docs.title: node_skill_dataset
docs.text:
node_skill_dataset(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the dataset availability skill — handles a 4-step creation flow.

Step 0 (no pending): look up datasets that expose the feature. Step 1 (awaiting_feature_selection): user described their use case → LLM proposes top-20 relevant features (or all if ≤ 20). Step 2 (awaiting_feature_confirmation): user replied 'ok' or 'all' → record selection and ask for a dataset name. Step 3 (awaiting_dataset_name): user provided a name → validate it.

docs.title: node_skill_build_dataset
docs.text:
node_skill_build_dataset(state: AgentState) -> dict

Execute the BUILD_DATASET autonomous creation flow.

Step 0 (no pending): Locate entity from catalog, immediately propose features. Step 1 (awaiting_feature_confirmation): User confirms 'ok' or 'all' → resolve versions. If conflicts → awaiting_version_selection. Else → awaiting_dataset_name. Step 2 (awaiting_version_selection): User picks version per conflict. When all resolved → awaiting_dataset_name. Step 3 (awaiting_dataset_name): Validate name; if available → create + document immediately.

docs.title: node_recognize_entity
docs.text:
node_recognize_entity(state: AgentState) -> dict

Identify the catalog type of each named object before skill dispatch.

Populates recognized_entities (feature/dataset/process/unknown per name) and sets secondary_intent = "DATASET" when a DEFINITION question also asks about dataset availability.

node_synthesize(state: AgentState) -> dict

When a secondary DATASET intent was detected by node_recognize_entity, also runs skill_dataset and appends a dataset-availability section to the answer. When the classifier flagged low confidence with multiple interpretations, renders a clarification prompt instead and sets the pending_action for the next turn.

docs.title: consumer_agent
docs.text:
consumer_agent(question: str, thread_id: str = 'default', return_trace: bool = False, return_context: bool = False, skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, mcp_enabled: bool = False) -> Union[str, tuple]

Run the LangGraph agent for a single conversational turn.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

User question in natural language (French or English).

required thread_id str

Conversation thread ID for multi-turn memory.

'default' return_trace bool

When True, includes trace_md in the return tuple.

False return_context bool

When True, includes accumulated_context_graph in the return tuple.

False skills Optional[List[str]]

Optional list of intent names (e.g. ['SEARCH', 'DEFINITION']) to restrict the agent to a subset of skills. When None (default), all installed consumer-agent skills are used. Threads are isolated per skill subset — mixing skills values in the same thread_id is not recommended.

None mcp_enabled bool

When True and tdfs4ds.MCP_SERVER_URL is set, the agent can route questions to the MCP server's tools. The toggle changes the graph variant (different cache key) so it is safe to flip mid-conversation — but note that conversation history is not shared between the MCP-on and MCP-off graph instances.

False

Returns:

Type Description Union[str, tuple]
  • answer: str when both return_trace and return_context are False (default).
Union[str, tuple]
  • (answer, trace_md) when return_trace=True and return_context=False.
Union[str, tuple]
  • (answer, accumulated_context_graph) when return_trace=False and return_context=True.
Union[str, tuple]
  • (answer, trace_md, accumulated_context_graph) when both are True.

docs.title: consumer_agent_stream
docs.text:
consumer_agent_stream(question: str, thread_id: str = 'default', skills: Optional[List[str]] = None, mcp_enabled: bool = False)

Generator variant of consumer_agent for real-time step streaming.

Yields:

Type Description

("step", node_name, partial_state) after each graph node completes,

where partial_state is the state accumulated so far (all fields set by

nodes that have already run).

("done", answer, trace_md) as the final event with the complete result.

The MemorySaver checkpointer behaves identically with .stream() as with .invoke() — multi-turn memory is fully preserved.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.agent.query_optimizer
docs.text: Query Optimizer Agent — structured multi-step pipeline built around a self-contained skill.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The optimization logic lives entirely in skill_optimize_query, a synchronous function following the same pattern as the consumer agent skills (skill_search, skill_definition etc.). The LangGraph StateGraph is a thin multi-turn wrapper that calls the skill and persists conversation state across turns sharing the same thread_id.

Process-aware entry point ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ When a process_id is supplied, the skill calls tdfs4ds.process_store.process_store_catalog_management.get_process_info to retrieve: - The registered SQL view (PROCESS_SQL) - Pre-existing EXPLAIN documentation (EXPLAIN_ANALYSIS, USER_SCORE, OPTIMIZATION_SCORE, EXPLAIN_WARNINGS, EXPLAIN_RECOMMENDATIONS) - Entity and feature column metadata

This avoids re-running the EXPLAIN LLM chain when an up-to-date analysis is already stored.

EXPLAIN analysis responsibility split ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ tdfs4ds.genai.documentation.document_sql_query_explain returns a structured dict: - explanation — plain-language plan description - user_score — 1–5, quality of what the SQL author controls - global_score — 1–5, overall execution quality including infrastructure - warnings — prefixed [You] or [Data Engineer / DBA] - recommendations— same prefixes

Only the [You]-prefixed items are actionable by the SQL author. The optimizer uses this split to focus candidate rewrites on author-controllable improvements.

Native Teradata tools (no MCP required) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ Seven read-only metadata functions replicate the tools exposed by the teradata-mcp-minimal server as direct Python calls over the active teradataml connection. No subprocess, no extra dependencies beyond teradataml.

Pipeline (strategy-based, executed inside skill_optimize_query) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ 1. Gather process context (get_process_info — skipped if no process_id) 2. Analyze original EXPLAIN (document_sql_query_explain — or reuse stored) 3. Build lineage graph (tdfs4ds.build_teradata_dependency_graph) → Primary Index + partition columns per underlying object 4. Fetch DDL for referenced objects (_td_get_table_ddl / _td_get_view_ddl) 5. Plan strategies — combinatorial with technique variants (no LLM call): For each [You] item, extracts technique hints from the recommendation text (e.g. "CTE", "scalar subquery" → precompute category → CTE, CROSS JOIN, and scalar variants). Then adds pairwise/group combinations. Capped at QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES (default 10). 6. Execute each strategy — For each strategy sequentially: a. Generate 1 candidate implementing the strategy (_generate_for_strategy_sync) b. Analyze EXPLAIN → extract remaining [You] items c. Refinement loop (up to QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_REFINE_ROUNDS, default 3): each round asks the LLM to fix the remaining [You] items from the previous round (_refine_candidate_sync) d. Early stop the entire pipeline when no [You] items remain (5/5 score) e. Compaction pass (on 5/5): asks the LLM to flatten unnecessary nesting and simplify the SQL structure; accepts only if the score holds. 7. LLM selects best plan (structured output: PlanComparison) 8. FilterManager check (pure Python, tdfs4ds.FilterManager)

Quick start::

import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER = "vllm"
tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL    = "..."

# requires an active tdml.create_context() connection
result = tdfs4ds.query_optimizer(
    "SELECT * FROM dw.orders o JOIN dw.customers c ON o.cust_id = c.id",
    thread_id="session-01",
)
print(result["answer"])
print("Best SQL:", result["best_sql"])

# Process-aware: pull SQL + stored analysis from the process catalog
result = tdfs4ds.query_optimizer(
    process_id="550e8400-...",
    thread_id="session-02",
)

docs.title: _reindent_cte_blocks
docs.text:
_reindent_cte_blocks(formatted: str) -> str

Re-indent CTE body blocks to use consistent 4-space indentation.

Detects lines ending with AS (CTE header) followed by a ( on the next line. The body between ( and the matching ) is re-indented to 4 spaces while preserving relative indentation within the block (e.g. CASE/WHEN nesting).

Non-CTE queries pass through unchanged.

docs.title: _format_sql
docs.text:
_format_sql(sql: str) -> str

Pretty-print SQL using sqlparse with post-processing for CTE readability.

sqlparse produces poor CTE formatting: deep alignment for continuation CTEs, REPLACE VIEW ... AS WITH on a single line. Post-processing fixes:

  1. Newline after */ block comments.
  2. REPLACE VIEW ... AS WITH → ... AS\nWITH.
  3. Deep CTE alignment (50+ spaces) collapsed to root level.
  4. CTE body blocks re-indented to consistent 4-space indent while preserving relative indentation within each block (e.g. CASE/WHEN).

docs.title: _fix_answer_fences
docs.text:
_fix_answer_fences(text: str) -> str

Close any fenced code block the LLM forgot to close before a new section.

Some LLMs write prose text (Key Changes, section headings) inside the opening sql ... block instead of after it. This function inserts a closing ` whenever a Markdown section heading (## /# ``) is encountered while a fence is still open, and appends a final closing fence if the text ends inside one.

_analyze_explain(sql_query: str, classify: bool = False) -> dict

Run document_sql_query_explain for structured EXPLAIN analysis. Falls back to raw EXPLAIN text on any error.

classify=False by default: the optimizer uses raw [You] counts so that structural issues (redundant scans, nesting) drive simplification and candidate strategies. The LLM classification runs only on the final public-facing score (via document_process_incremental).

Generate exactly one candidate SQL rewrite for a specific strategy.

Unlike _generate_candidates_sync (generic prompt, up to N candidates), this function constrains the LLM to produce one rewrite that strictly follows the given strategy description and targets.

Refine a candidate SQL to fix remaining [You] items without changing the strategy.

remaining_you_items should already have the [You] prefix stripped. lineage_info provides PI + partition columns so the LLM can make informed fixes for index-related warnings.

docs.title: _simplify_sql_sync
docs.text:
_simplify_sql_sync(sql_query: str, ddls: dict[str, str]) -> Optional[CandidateRewrite]

Ask the LLM to produce the most compact, readable form of the SQL.

Flattens unnecessary nesting, merges derived-table layers into CTEs, inlines date arithmetic, and removes identity wrappers — while preserving exact business logic (same result set).

Used as the LLM pass in skill_simplify_query. Returns None if the LLM cannot simplify further.

docs.title: _generate_report_sync
docs.text:
_generate_report_sync(sql_query: str, user_request: str, original_analysis: dict, lineage_info: dict, candidate_analyses: list[dict], comparison: PlanComparison, best_sql: str, fm_suggestion: dict, process_info: Optional[dict], score_delta: Optional[dict] = None, strategy_plan: Optional[StrategiesOutput] = None, simplify_result: Optional[dict] = None, original_raw_sql: Optional[str] = None, token_usage: Optional[dict] = None) -> str

Assemble the Markdown optimisation report from structured data.

Only the ## Summary section uses a short LLM call (~100 tokens output). All other sections are built deterministically from the pipeline outputs, avoiding the token-limit failures that occur when the full report is LLM-generated.

docs.title: _normalize_sql
docs.text:
_normalize_sql(sql: str) -> str

Collapse whitespace and lowercase for similarity comparison.

docs.title: _split_select_items
docs.text:
_split_select_items(col_str: str) -> list

Split a comma-separated SELECT column list, respecting paren depth and quotes.

docs.title: _is_from_keyword
docs.text:
_is_from_keyword(text: str, pos: int) -> bool

Return True when position pos in text is the start of a standalone FROM keyword.

docs.title: _walk_to_from
docs.text:
_walk_to_from(text: str, start: int) -> int

Return the index of the first depth-0 FROM keyword starting at start, or -1.

docs.title: _sanitize_sql_identifiers
docs.text:
_sanitize_sql_identifiers(sql: str) -> str

Replace MySQL/generic backtick identifiers with Teradata double-quote identifiers.

LLMs occasionally produce `column_name` style quoting (MySQL habit) which Teradata rejects with Error 3707. A simple regex substitution is safe here because we never generate SQL that contains backtick characters for any other purpose.

docs.title: _fix_scalar_subquery_to_direct_ref
docs.text:
_fix_scalar_subquery_to_direct_ref(sql: str) -> str

Replace (SELECT col FROM derived_alias) → derived_alias.col.

Teradata resolves the FROM clause of a scalar subquery against the database catalog, not against derived-table aliases visible in the enclosing FROM clause. The correct pattern is to reference the column directly: derived_alias.col_name.

Algorithm
  1. Collect all derived-table alias names defined in the SQL (pattern: ) alias_name followed by whitespace or a SQL keyword).
  2. Replace every (SELECT single_col FROM alias) where alias is a known derived-table alias with alias.single_col.

Complements _fix_reserved_word_alias: both are applied together so that e.g. (SELECT threshold FROM hvt) is first rewritten to hvt.threshold and then threshold as a reserved-word alias is renamed if needed.

docs.title: _fix_reserved_word_alias
docs.text:
_fix_reserved_word_alias(sql: str, error_message: str) -> Optional[str]

Deterministically rename a Teradata reserved word used as a column alias.

Teradata Error 3706 sometimes reads

"Syntax error: expected something between '' and the '' keyword."

when a reserved word (e.g. THRESHOLD, DATE, PERIOD) is used as a bare alias.

This function extracts the offending word from the error message and renames every occurrence (alias definition AND bare column references) to <word>_col. The rename is a global word-boundary substitution so it covers both ... AS threshold and Transaction_Amount > threshold.

Returns the repaired SQL, or None if the error pattern does not match.

docs.title: _fix_syntax_error_sync
docs.text:
_fix_syntax_error_sync(sql: str, error_message: str, llm, max_fix_attempts: int = 3) -> Optional[str]

Repair a Teradata SQL syntax error, first with deterministic checks then via LLM.

Deterministic pass (no LLM cost): 1. Reserved-word alias — detects the "expected … and the '' keyword" pattern and renames the offending alias globally.

LLM pass (up to max_fix_attempts retries): Asks the LLM to fix only the syntax, not the optimization logic. The deterministic post-processing (backtick sanitization, alias injection) is applied to the LLM's output before returning so the caller can go straight to EXPLAIN.

Returns the repaired SQL string, or None if all attempts fail or raise.

docs.title: _add_missing_derived_table_aliases
docs.text:
_add_missing_derived_table_aliases(sql: str) -> str

Add missing aliases to unaliased derived tables in FROM/JOIN clauses.

Teradata requires every inline view (subquery used as a derived table) to have an alias immediately after the closing parenthesis. The LLM sometimes omits the alias when simplifying nested queries, causing Error 3707: "expected something like a name … between ')' and ';'".

Algorithm: walk the SQL tracking parenthesis depth (skipping string literals), record every ( that immediately follows FROM or JOIN, then when the matching ) is found check whether it is followed by an SQL keyword or end-of-input instead of an identifier. If so, inject _dt0, _dt1, … as the alias.

docs.title: _try_flatten_identity_wrapper
docs.text:
_try_flatten_identity_wrapper(sql: str) -> str

Deterministically collapse a redundant identity-wrapper SELECT.

Targets the pattern::

[DDL] SELECT col1 [AS "col1"], ..., 'lit' AS extra
      FROM ( <inner_query> ) AS <alias>

where the outer SELECT has no WHERE / GROUP BY / HAVING / ORDER BY and every column is either a same-name alias ("x" AS "x") or a literal constant ('v' AS extra).

Literal injection into an aggregation query is safe: SQL constants do not belong in GROUP BY and injecting them at the END of the SELECT list does not shift the positional indices used by any GROUP BY 1, 2, … already present in the inner query.

Returns the original SQL unchanged when the pattern is not clearly recognized.

docs.title: _is_near_duplicate
docs.text:
_is_near_duplicate(sql_a: str, sql_b: str, threshold: float = 0.97) -> bool

Return True when two SQL strings are essentially the same query.

Uses exact match after normalisation first (fast path), then falls back to difflib similarity ratio so cosmetic reformats (added aliases, re-indented subqueries) are also detected.

docs.title: _sql_structure_preview
docs.text:
_sql_structure_preview(sql: str, max_chars: int = 400) -> str

Return the structural skeleton of a SQL string for context feedback.

Skips the SELECT column list (which is identical across all candidates and wastes tokens) and returns instead: - The WITH block if present (CTE names + first line of each CTE body) - Everything from the first top-level FROM keyword onwards

This makes the preview directly comparable across candidates at the level where structural differences actually exist.

docs.title: _build_attempt_context
docs.text:
_build_attempt_context(attempt_history: list[dict], original_user_score: Optional[int] = None) -> str

Format per-attempt feedback lines into a compact context block.

Improving candidates (score > original) are NOT added to the forbidden list — instead they are surfaced as 'current best' with their remaining [You] issues so the next attempt can build on them rather than abandoning the approach.

Non-improving candidates (score <= original), near-duplicates, and EXPLAIN failures ARE added to the forbidden list.

docs.title: _extract_you_items
docs.text:
_extract_you_items(analysis: dict) -> list[str]

Extract [You]-prefixed warnings + recommendations, with the prefix stripped.

docs.title: _deduplicate_you_items
docs.text:
_deduplicate_you_items(items: list[str], threshold: float = 0.5) -> list[str]

Remove near-duplicate [You] items using token-overlap similarity.

EXPLAIN warnings and recommendations often describe the same issue in slightly different wording (e.g. a warning says "correlated subquery forces recomputation" and the recommendation says "replace the correlated subquery with a CTE"). Keeping both produces duplicate strategies that waste LLM calls.

For each pair, strip SQL code blocks (which add noise), tokenize the prose, and compute the overlap coefficient (|A∩B| / min(|A|, |B|)). This metric works well for short-vs-long pairs (a brief warning vs. a detailed recommendation). When two items exceed threshold, the shorter one is dropped (the longer one typically carries more context).

docs.title: _strip_sql_comments
docs.text:
_strip_sql_comments(sql: str) -> str

Remove block comments (/* ... */) and line comments (-- ...) from SQL.

The LLM is instructed to produce comment-free SQL but sometimes ignores that rule. Stripping comments prevents formatting issues (sqlparse jams */ onto the same line as the next token) and avoids polluting the EXPLAIN analysis with irrelevant text.

docs.title: _fix_reserved_aliases_proactive
docs.text:
_fix_reserved_aliases_proactive(sql: str) -> str

Rename Teradata reserved words used as bare identifiers.

Detects reserved words in three contexts: - Column alias: AS threshold - CTE name: WITH threshold AS - Derived-table alias: ) threshold (after closing paren)

When found, renames every occurrence of that word globally (word-boundary safe) to <word>_val.

Word-boundary matching ensures compound identifiers (e.g. Date_transaction, CURRENT_DATE) are not affected.

docs.title: _sanitize_candidate_sql
docs.text:
_sanitize_candidate_sql(sql: str, label: str) -> str

Apply all deterministic SQL sanitization passes in sequence.

  1. Strip block/line comments (LLM sometimes adds them despite the prompt)
  2. Proactively rename reserved-word aliases (AS threshold → AS threshold_val)
  3. Backtick identifiers → double-quote (Teradata-safe)
  4. Missing derived-table aliases injected
  5. Identity wrapper flattened
  6. Scalar subquery → direct derived-table reference

docs.title: _attempt_explain_with_fix
docs.text:
_attempt_explain_with_fix(sql: str, label: str) -> tuple[str, dict]

Analyze EXPLAIN; if syntax error, attempt repair and re-analyze.

Returns (final_sql, analysis) — analysis["user_score"] is None when both the original and the repaired SQL failed.

docs.title: _extract_technique_hints
docs.text:
_extract_technique_hints(text: str) -> list[tuple[str, str]]

Extract SQL technique variants from a [You] item's text.

When the text mentions a technique in a category (e.g. "CTE" triggers the precompute category), all techniques in that category are returned so the strategy generator can offer multiple structural approaches.

Returns a list of (suffix, hint_description) tuples. An empty list means no actionable technique was detected — the strategy should be generated without a technique constraint.

docs.title: _generate_combinatorial_strategies
docs.text:
_generate_combinatorial_strategies(you_items: list[str], max_strategies: int) -> list['StrategySpec']

Build strategies by combinatorially addressing [You] items.

Produces strategies in three tiers:

  • Tier 1 — technique variants (singles): for each [You] item, extract technique hints from its text (e.g. "CTE", "CROSS JOIN", "scalar subquery"). When hints are found, generate one strategy per technique so the LLM produces structurally diverse SQL. When no hints are detected, one unconstrained strategy is generated.
  • Tier 2 — pairwise: one strategy per pair of [You] items — fix two simultaneously.
  • Tier 3+ — groups: triplets, quads, … up to fix-all.

The function stops as soon as max_strategies is reached, prioritising technique diversity in the singles tier.

Refinement rounds within each strategy will address any remaining [You] items that don't disappear after the targeted fix.

docs.title: skill_generate_candidates
docs.text:
skill_generate_candidates(sql_query: str, user_request: str, original_analysis: dict, ddls: dict, lineage_info: dict, history: Optional[list]) -> tuple[list[dict], list[dict], Optional['StrategiesOutput']]

Strategy-based candidate generation with multi-round refinement.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
1. Plan strategies — combinatorial: one strategy per [You] item (sequential), then one per pair (simultaneous), then triplets, etc. No LLM call needed; capped at QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES.
  2. Execute each strategy — For each strategy sequentially: a. Generate one candidate implementing that strategy. b. EXPLAIN the candidate → extract remaining [You] items. c. If [You] items remain, refine the candidate (up to QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_REFINE_ROUNDS rounds), each round targeting the [You] items from the previous round. d. Early stop the entire pipeline when a strategy achieves user_score = 5/5 (no [You] items remaining).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: candidate_analyses : list[dict] One entry per strategy executed. Each has index, sql, strategy, strategy_description, strategy_targets, rationale, analysis, and rounds (list of per-round dicts). steps : list[dict] Pipeline step records for skill_optimize_query. strategy_plan : StrategiesOutput | None The raw plan returned by the strategy-planning LLM call (None when the query was already optimal and no strategies were generated).

docs.title: skill_simplify_query
docs.text:
skill_simplify_query(sql_query: Optional[str] = None, process_id: Optional[str] = None, max_fix_attempts: int = 3) -> dict

Simplify a Teradata SQL query by removing unnecessary nesting.

Applies deterministic passes (comment stripping, identity-wrapper flattening, reserved-word alias fixes) followed by an LLM pass that merges derived-table layers into CTEs and inlines date arithmetic.

The simplified SQL is validated with EXPLAIN; if the score regresses below the original, the deterministic-only version is returned.

Can be used standalone or as a pre-step in skill_optimize_query.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default sql_query Optional[str]

Raw Teradata SQL to simplify.

None process_id Optional[str]

UUID of a registered tdfs4ds process (fetches SQL from catalog).

None max_fix_attempts int

Max syntax-error repair retries for the LLM output.

3

Returns:

Type Description dict

dict with keys simplified_sql, original_sql, simplified,

dict

original_score, simplified_score.

skill_optimize_query(sql_query: Optional[str] = None, process_id: Optional[str] = None, user_request: str = 'Optimize this Teradata SQL query for performance.', history: Optional[list] = None) -> dict

Analyse and rewrite a Teradata SQL query for better performance.

Either sql_query or process_id must be provided. When process_id is given, the SQL and any existing EXPLAIN documentation are pulled from the tdfs4ds process catalog, avoiding a redundant LLM call.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default sql_query Optional[str]

Raw Teradata SELECT statement to optimise.

None process_id Optional[str]

UUID of a registered tdfs4ds process. When supplied, the SQL view DDL and stored EXPLAIN analysis are fetched from the catalog. If sql_query is also given it takes precedence.

None user_request str

Optional context sentence describing the optimisation goal (surfaced in the report and fed to the LLM).

'Optimize this Teradata SQL query for performance.' history Optional[list]

Prior LangGraph messages for multi-turn context.

None

Returns:

Type Description dict

dict with keys: answer — Structured Markdown optimisation report. best_sql — Recommended SQL (original if already optimal). steps — List of {step, ...} dicts for each pipeline step. filtermanager_applicable — True if a FilterManager loop was recommended. original_analysis — Structured EXPLAIN analysis of the original query. candidates — List of candidate dicts (sql, strategy, analysis). comparison — Plan comparison result. process_info — Process catalog info dict (or None).

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If neither sql_query nor process_id is provided.

LangGraph-based orchestration for the tdfs4ds query optimizer agent.

START -> node_classify -> [conditional on intent] -> node_skill_* -> node_synthesize -> END

docs.title: query_optimizer_agent
docs.text:
query_optimizer_agent(question: str, sql_query: Optional[str] = None, process_id: Optional[str] = None, thread_id: str = 'default', return_trace: bool = False) -> Union[str, tuple]

Run the LangGraph query optimizer agent for a single conversational turn.

The agent classifies the question into one of three intents (EXPLAIN / SIMPLIFY / OPTIMIZE) and routes to the corresponding skill. Multi-turn context (sql_query, process_id) is persisted across calls that share the same thread_id.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default question str

Natural language description of the desired action, e.g. 'Analyze this query for performance issues' or 'Optimize this SQL'.

required sql_query Optional[str]

Optional SQL string. When provided it is remembered for follow-up turns on the same thread_id.

None process_id Optional[str]

Optional process UUID (looked up in the process catalog to retrieve the SQL automatically). Remembered across turns.

None thread_id str

Conversation thread ID for multi-turn memory.

'default' return_trace bool

When True, returns (answer, skill_result) instead of just the answer string.

False

Returns:

Type Description Union[str, tuple]

Answer string, or (answer, skill_result) tuple when

Union[str, tuple]

return_trace=True.

docs.text:
_upsert_documents_batched(store: Chroma, docs: list[Document], ids: list[str]) -> None

Upsert documents into a Chroma store in batches (add new, update existing).

Respects embedding API batch size and inter-batch delay (for vLLM/TEI overload prevention). Delay is applied between batches, not after the final batch.

docs.title: build_vector_index
docs.text:
build_vector_index(force_rebuild: bool = False) -> None

Build or refresh the three vector-store collections from the business dictionary tables.

Dispatches to the configured backend (tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND): 'chroma' (default) or 'teradata'.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default force_rebuild bool

If True, delete existing collections and re-index from scratch (Chroma backend only — no admin privileges required locally). The Teradata backend does NOT delete on force_rebuild: it always reuses/upserts into the existing collection instead, because deleting there requires admin privileges that may not be available (see vector_store_teradata.build_teradata_index's docstring). Use tdfs4ds.agent.vector_store_teradata.reset_teradata_store() directly for a true destroy-and-recreate on Teradata when you have admin rights and want stale rows purged. If False, upsert all rows (add new, update existing) on both backends.

False

docs.title: search_vector_index
docs.text:
search_vector_index(query: str, sources: list[str] = ('objects', 'sections', 'columns'), section_names: Optional[List[str]] = None, top_k: int = 10, k: Optional[int] = None) -> pd.DataFrame

Search the requested Chroma collections using similarity search.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default query str

Natural language search query.

required sources list[str]

Which collections to search (subset of 'objects', 'sections', 'columns').

('objects', 'sections', 'columns') section_names Optional[List[str]]

If provided and 'sections' is in sources, filter by SECTION_NAME.

None top_k int

Number of results per collection.

10 k Optional[int]

Alias for top_k — accepted because k is the near-universal convention in the surrounding ecosystem (Chroma, FAISS, LangChain retrievers), unlike tdfs4ds's own top_k naming. If both are given and differ, k wins and a warning is logged.

None

Returns:

Type Description DataFrame

DataFrame with columns: SOURCE, OBJECT_NAME, SECTION_NAME, COLUMN_NAME, TEXT, SCORE.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.agent.vector_store_teradata
docs.text:
Teradata Enterprise Vector Store backend for the tdfs4ds business dictionary.

Provides the same public surface as the Chroma backend (:func:tdfs4ds.agent.vector_index.build_vector_index and :func:tdfs4ds.agent.vector_index.search_vector_index) but persists the three collections as Teradata vector stores via langchain_teradata:

  • {prefix}_objects -> FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS
  • {prefix}_sections -> FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS
  • {prefix}_columns -> FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS

Embeddings are produced by :func:tdfs4ds.agent.embedding.get_vector_store_embedding which selects either teradatagenai.TeradataAI (when tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE is set) or the standard LangChain Embeddings — TeradataVectorStore accepts both at the embedding= argument.

Indexing source (v0.3.1.131): documents are supplied to TeradataVectorStore.from_datasets/add_datasets reading from a permanent CURRENT VALIDTIME view (FS_V_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_*, created by genai.documentation.documentation_tables_creation()) — NOT via from_documents/add_documents (LangChain Document list input). The Document-list path has a confirmed bug: it internally stages the input through teradataml.DataFrame.from_dict(..., persist=True) / DataFrame.materialize(), which takes no database argument and may materialize as a volatile table (session-scoped) or a view — either way teradataml itself documents this choice as internal/opaque ("decided and taken care by teradataml"). A volatile table is invisible to the separate Vector Store REST session, causing TDML_2412: Object ... does not exist regardless of target_database (which only scopes where the FINAL collection metadata is registered, not where the input data is read from).

Deeper pitfall found on live VCL2 testing (v0.3.1.14x): reading from the permanent view is necessary but not sufficient on its own — the way the view is handed to from_datasets/add_datasets matters too. _from_datasets_kwargs() used to wrap the view in tdml.DataFrame.from_query("SELECT ... FROM <view>") before v0.3.1.14x. Confirmed live: DataFrame.from_query(...) sets _table_name to a freshly materialized, ephemeral ml___frmqry_v_<id> view at construction time (before teradatagenai is even called), and teradatagenai bakes whatever _table_name it receives permanently into the collection's _inputdata view DDL (confirmed via SHOW VIEW on a live collection: ... FROM "data_scientist"."ml___frmqry_v_<id>"). Once Vantage garbage-collects that ephemeral view (observed to happen almost immediately — confirmed absent from DBC.TablesV within the same session that created it), _inputdata — and collectionV_<name>, which joins through it — is permanently broken: every query touching it, from any client (similarity_search, a plain teradataml query, an unrelated MCP session), fails with Error 3807 / TDML_2412: Object ... does not exist, forever, not transiently (confirmed: 3/3 retries fail identically). The fix: data= is now passed as a plain fully-qualified "schema.view" string naming the permanent view directly (see _from_datasets_kwargs) — never a from_query() result, which guarantees no ephemeral materialization ever happens.

No row-level filtering of data= — ever. Neither from_datasets nor add_datasets exposes any filter/predicate/where-clause parameter (checked directly against the teradatagenai signatures/docstrings). Pointing data= at a filtered view instead of the full source view was considered and rejected: since _inputdata is a live view (re-evaluated on every query, not a frozen snapshot), a filtered data= would permanently narrow what the whole collection can ever return — hiding previously-indexed rows outside the filter from search forever. Separately, _index (the real embeddings table) is keyed by TD_ID, which _inputdata assigns via ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY <text column>) — recomputed fresh on every evaluation of whatever data= points to, so any change in row count/ordering between the _index write and a later _inputdata evaluation risks silently misaligning embeddings with the wrong row's text/metadata. Always pass the entire, unfiltered source view.

Reaching this error means either none of those groups were configured, or set_auth_token itself failed — check the connect()/setup() log for a 'Vector Store auth ... failed' warning line for the underlying reason. Detail: {detail}"

Call tdfs4ds.agent.vector_store_teradata.reset_teradata_store(sources=['{source}']) (requires admin privileges) to destroy and recreate it from the current business dictionary, then rebuild the index."
