docs.title: get_vector_store_embedding
docs.text:
get_vector_store_embedding()

Return the embedding object for the active vector-store backend.

Selection rule
  • tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE set -> teradatagenai.TeradataAI(...) (in-database embedding; used by the 'teradata' backend).
  • otherwise -> get_embeddings() (LangChain Embeddings; works with vLLM / TEI / OpenAI-compatible setups and is also accepted as embedding= by TeradataVectorStore).

The ChromaDB backend always uses get_embeddings() regardless of this setting — TeradataAI is a Vantage-side construct and is only meaningful when the vector store also lives in Vantage.

docs.title: _store_name
docs.text: _store_name(source: str) -> str

docs.title: _import_vector_store
docs.text: _import_vector_store()

docs.title: _raise_service_unavailable
docs.text: _raise_service_unavailable(exc: Exception)

docs.title: _raise_not_configured
docs.text: _raise_not_configured(exc: Exception)

docs.title: _is_not_ready_error
docs.text:
_is_not_ready_error(exc: Exception) -> bool

True when exc is the transient "Collection ... cannot be updated as it is not in a ready state" 409 — the collection is still processing a prior async operation (its own creation, or a previous update), NOT a real failure. Distinguished from a generic 409/HTML service-down error (which also contains "Response Code:") by requiring the more specific "not in a ready state" phrase; must be checked BEFORE _reraise_if_service_down's generic "response code:" branch, which would otherwise misclassify this transient, retryable condition as "service unreachable/unlicensed."

docs.title: _is_transient_staging_object_error
docs.text:
_is_transient_staging_object_error(exc: Exception) -> bool

True when exc is a 400 "Object ... does not exist" from similarity_search/ask — confirmed live: the Enterprise Vector Store REST backend materializes a short-lived per-query staging view (ml___frmqry_v_<id>) to run the search, and that object can be gone (dropped/never-committed/session mismatch) by the time the search reads it back — a platform-side race, not a licensing/activation problem. Must be checked BEFORE _reraise_if_service_down's generic "response code:" branch, which otherwise misclassifies this as "service unreachable/unlicensed" (every TeradataMlException from this API embeds "Response Code:", so that check alone can't distinguish a genuine gateway failure from this per-query staging race).

docs.title: _is_transient_gateway_error
docs.text:
_is_transient_gateway_error(exc: Exception) -> bool

True when exc is a bare HTTP 5xx gateway error (502/503/504) — confirmed live on a VCL2 test instance: the Vector Store REST gateway occasionally returns a raw <html>...502 Bad Gateway...</html> body (or 503/504) with no relation to any code path or configuration issue — pure transient infrastructure flakiness that resolves on retry within seconds. Distinguished from a genuine "service unreachable/unlicensed" condition only by being retryable: unlike a persistent misconfiguration (missing auth, wrong URL, licensing), a gateway 502/503/504 is by definition a transient proxy/gateway-layer failure, so retrying a few times before giving up is safe and appropriate. Checked BEFORE _reraise_if_service_down's generic "<html"/"response code:" branch so a transient gateway blip doesn't immediately surface as the terminal _SERVICE_DOWN_MSG on the very first retry-able occurrence.

docs.title: _retry_if_transient_gateway_error
docs.text:
_retry_if_transient_gateway_error(fn, label: str, timeout_seconds: float = 60.0, poll_interval: float = 5.0)

Call fn(), retrying while it raises a transient gateway 5xx (see _is_transient_gateway_error). Re-raises the original exception once timeout_seconds elapses, or immediately for any other exception shape.

A short default timeout (60s, vs. the 120s/1800s used for genuine async "not ready" waits) is deliberate — a gateway 502/503/504 either clears in a few seconds or reflects a real outage, in which case failing fast and surfacing the clear _SERVICE_DOWN_MSG (via the caller's own _reraise_if_service_down) is more useful than a long silent wait.

docs.title: _reraise_if_service_down
docs.text:
_reraise_if_service_down(exc: Exception)

Inspect exc and raise a clear RuntimeError when it looks like either:

  • the Vector Store client itself is unconfigured (e.g. requests' MissingSchema/InvalidURL from a request built with a None base URL — "Invalid URL 'None/api/v2/...': No scheme supplied" — meaning set_auth_token/session auth was never set up). This is distinguished from "service down" because the fix is entirely client-side (call set_auth_token), not a licensing/activation problem on the Vantage instance.
  • the Vector Store REST gateway itself is unreachable/not enabled (any other transport-level failure, or a reachable-but-wrong endpoint returning a raw HTML/status body — e.g. a gateway 502 — instead of a JSON error).

Returns normally (does nothing) for any other exception, leaving it to the caller — in particular for the transient "not in a ready state" 409 (see _is_not_ready_error), the transient per-query staging-object race (see _is_transient_staging_object_error), and a transient gateway 502/503/504 (see _is_transient_gateway_error), which callers should retry rather than treat as fatal, so none of the three is ever raised as service-down here.

Shared by every call site that talks to teradatagenai/langchain_teradata directly (_lookup_store_database, build_teradata_index's from_datasets/add_datasets/status calls) so neither failure mode is ever mistaken for "store doesn't exist yet, fall back to rebuild" — which would just fail again later with a much less clear message.

docs.title: _collection_manager
docs.text:
_collection_manager()

Import and instantiate teradatagenai's CollectionManager.

Raises a clear RuntimeError (mirroring _import_vector_store) if the package is missing — the Teradata backend depends on it transitively via langchain_teradata, so a missing import here means a broken install.

docs.title: _lookup_store_info
docs.text:
_lookup_store_info(name: str, target_database: Optional[str] = None) -> Optional[dict]

Return {"target_database": ..., "collection_status": ...} for a named vector store, or None if no store with that name exists.

target_database (cross-store federation, v0.3.1.148): when given, a row matches only if its own target_database also matches — because two feature stores using the same TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX have identically-named collections (e.g. tdfs4ds_objects) in different databases, and a name-only match would return the wrong store's status. Default None ⇒ name-only match (unchanged for existing callers).

Uses CollectionManager.list() — the same list-and-match pattern teradatagenai uses internally (Collection.exists, VectorStore.__init__) — so a caller can discover where a store lives before attaching, instead of assuming it matches tdfs4ds.SCHEMA. This catches the case where a store was created before a target_database fix (or a schema change) and now sits in a different database than expected.

collection_status is the authoritative signal for whether a write actually succeeded — confirmed live to take values like 'ready', 'update_failed', 'create_failed' (see _is_failed_status). A write call (from_datasets/add_datasets) returning without an exception only means "the request was accepted" (these operations are asynchronous) — it does NOT mean the operation succeeded; only collection_status reflects the true server-side outcome.

Raises RuntimeError (via _raise_service_unavailable) when the Vector Store service itself is unreachable/not enabled — distinguished from "store not found", which returns None normally. A transient gateway 502/503/504 (confirmed live on a VCL2 test instance — pure infrastructure flakiness, unrelated to code/config) is retried a few times first via _retry_if_transient_gateway_error before falling through to that service-down classification.

docs.title: _lookup_store_database
docs.text:
_lookup_store_database(name: str) -> Optional[str]

Backward-compatible wrapper over :func:_lookup_store_info returning just the target_database (existing callers only need this).

docs.title: _is_failed_status
docs.text:
_is_failed_status(status) -> bool

True when status (a collection_status value from :func:_lookup_store_info) is a terminal failure — e.g. update_failed, create_failed. Matches any ..._failed suffix rather than hardcoding the two observed strings, in case other failure variants exist.

A failed collection will NOT recover by waiting or retrying — it requires an explicit :func:reset_teradata_store (destroy + recreate).

docs.title: _raise_collection_failed
docs.text: _raise_collection_failed(name: str, status, source: str)

docs.title: _from_datasets_kwargs
docs.text:
_from_datasets_kwargs(source: str) -> dict

Build the data/data_columns/key_columns/metadata_columns kwargs for TeradataVectorStore.from_datasets/add_datasets.

data is passed as a plain fully-qualified "schema.view" string pointing directly at the permanent FS_V_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_* view — NOT a tdml.DataFrame.from_query(...) result. Confirmed live: DataFrame.from_query(...) sets _table_name to a freshly materialized, ephemeral ml___frmqry_v_<id> view at construction time — before teradatagenai ever sees it — and teradatagenai bakes whatever _table_name it receives permanently into the collection's _inputdata view DDL. Once Vantage garbage-collects that ephemeral view (observed to happen almost immediately), _inputdata (and anything joining through it, including similarity_search) is permanently broken (see module docstring). The view already exposes exactly the columns needed, so no projection/SELECT is needed here — data_columns/key_columns/metadata_columns tell from_datasets/add_datasets which columns of the named view to use.

docs.title: _has_rows
docs.text:
_has_rows(source: str) -> bool

Cheap existence check — avoids calling from_datasets/add_datasets on an empty source (mirrors the old if not docs: continue guard).

docs.title: _target_database_kwargs
docs.text:
_target_database_kwargs() -> dict

target_database= kwarg for from_datasets/add_datasets, scoped to tdfs4ds.SCHEMA — where the Vantage REST API registers the final collection's metadata.

docs.title: _safe_destroy
docs.text:
_safe_destroy(vs_class, name: str) -> None

Destroy a named vector store if it already exists.

Looks up the store's ACTUAL database via CollectionManager.list() first, rather than assuming it lives at tdfs4ds.SCHEMA — a store created before a target_database/schema change can be sitting in a different database, and attaching only at the expected database would silently miss it, leaving an orphaned duplicate once a fresh store is created elsewhere on force_rebuild.

Calls destroy() unconditionally once existence is confirmed — do NOT gate on status() first. Collection.status() reports the async create/destroy/update OPERATION history, not current existence; a store that exists but has no reported operation history returns an empty DataFrame, which previously made this function conclude "nothing to destroy" and skip the call — leaving the real collection in place. The subsequent from_datasets() call then collided with a TeradataGenAIException: Collection ... already exists (Collection.create() does its own authoritative self.exists check), silently caused by a swallowed destroy() failure below (e.g. non-admin session — destroy() requires admin privileges per its docstring) going unnoticed because this function used to only log a warning and return.

A destroy() failure is therefore re-raised (not swallowed): silently continuing after a failed destroy just defers the same collision to the from_datasets()/add_datasets() call with a far less clear error.

docs.title: _wait_until_collection_ready
docs.text:
_wait_until_collection_ready(collection, name: str, timeout_seconds: float = 1800.0, poll_interval: float = 5.0) -> None

Poll collection.get_details() — a read-only method — every poll_interval seconds until it succeeds, treating any exception as "the collection is still processing a prior async operation" (Collection create/update/destroy are all asynchronous: the server reports a request as "accepted and in progress" well before the operation actually completes, so a write issued right after can hit the collection while it's still busy and fail with a 409 "not in a ready state").

Deliberately read-only: unlike retrying the write itself, this probe can't create additional server-side contention while checking. Raises the last exception observed if the collection is not ready within timeout_seconds (a genuinely stuck/failed operation should surface clearly rather than hang the caller forever).

docs.title: _verify_write_succeeded
docs.text:
_verify_write_succeeded(name: str, source: str, timeout_seconds: float = 300.0, poll_interval: float = 5.0) -> None

After a write call (from_datasets/add_datasets) returns without raising, confirm the operation actually completed by polling collection_status (via :func:_lookup_store_info) until it reports 'ready'.

A write call returning cleanly only means "the request was accepted" (Collection writes are asynchronous) — NOT that the operation succeeded. Confirmed live: a store can log "indexed successfully" here while collection_status is actually update_failed/ create_failed and the collection holds no searchable data.

If collection_status ever reports a *_failed status while polling, raises immediately via :func:_raise_collection_failed (no point waiting further — it's terminal). Raises a plain RuntimeError on timeout if it never resolves to 'ready' or a failed status.

docs.title: build_teradata_index
docs.text:
build_teradata_index(force_rebuild: bool = False) -> None

Build or refresh the three Teradata vector stores.

Always reuses an existing collection via add_datasets (which creates the collection on first use, and upserts/appends rows keyed on ID on subsequent calls) rather than destroying and recreating it. destroy() requires admin privileges ("Only admin users can use this method" per Collection.destroy()'s own docstring) and previously ran as a routine step of every force_rebuild=True call — a non-admin session, or any other destroy failure, was silently swallowed, leaving the stale collection in place so the following from_datasets() call then failed with TeradataGenAIException: Collection ... already exists (raised by Collection.create()'s own authoritative existence check). Reusing the collection sidesteps the admin requirement entirely and matches how the Enterprise Vector Store is meant to be refreshed incrementally.

Known limitation, confirmed live: add_datasets() actually rejects re-adding an object name already attached to the collection ("You can only add object names that do not exist in the collection", TDML_2412 400) — and _from_datasets_kwargs() always names the SAME FS_V_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_* view, so this call fails on every second-and-later invocation once the source is already attached (i.e. calling build_teradata_index() again after new rows are documented does not currently pick them up via this path). The documented alternative, Collection.update(data_refresh=True) ("refresh/ regenerate vectors ... using existing backing/source tables"), was investigated as a replacement — it succeeded once in isolated testing, then failed consistently on every subsequent attempt (fresh instance, genuinely new unindexed row present) with "At least one of collection_index, collection_parameters, collection_description, or new_collection_name must be provided for update" — an apparently intermittent/stateful server-side issue in teradatagenai, not something reliably reproducible or fixable from tdfs4ds's side. Left as add_datasets() rather than trade a known-broken path for a differently-broken one; a real fix needs a dedicated follow-up once data_refresh's behavior is better understood.

force_rebuild is now purely informational for logging — both branches behave identically. Note this means rows whose source business-dictionary entry was deleted are NOT purged from the index by this call (no admin-free per-id deletion API is available yet — see Collection.get_ids, which is currently an unimplemented stub upstream); only genuinely new/changed rows are added or refreshed.

docs.title: _retry_if_not_ready
docs.text:
_retry_if_not_ready(fn, name: str, timeout_seconds: float = 120.0, poll_interval: float = 5.0)

Call fn(), retrying while it raises the transient "not in a ready state" 409 (see _is_not_ready_error) — the collection is still processing a prior async operation (its own creation, or a previous update/add_datasets call). Every Collection write operation is asynchronous ("... request is accepted and in progress"); calling another write immediately after can hit the collection while it's still busy.

Re-raises the original exception once timeout_seconds elapses, or immediately for any other exception shape (not a not-ready 409).

docs.title: _wait_until_destroyed
docs.text:
_wait_until_destroyed(name: str, timeout_seconds: float = 60.0, poll_interval: float = 2.0) -> bool

Poll _lookup_store_database(name) until it reports the collection gone, or timeout_seconds elapses.

Collection.destroy() is asynchronous — it returns as soon as the request is accepted ("Collection destroy request is accepted and in progress"), not once the collection is actually gone. Calling from_datasets() (create) immediately afterward is a race: the collection can still exist server-side, so create collides with TeradataGenAIException: Collection ... already exists even though destroy() itself reported success.

Returns True if the collection was confirmed gone, False on timeout (caller decides whether that is fatal).

docs.title: reset_teradata_store
docs.text:
reset_teradata_store(sources: Optional[List[str]] = None) -> None

Destroy and recreate the named Teradata vector store collection(s) from scratch — the true "clean rebuild" that :func:build_teradata_index no longer performs (it always reuses/upserts, see its docstring), so stale rows (business-dictionary entries deleted since the last index) are never purged there.

Requires the connected user to have admin privileges — destroy() (via :func:_safe_destroy) raises otherwise (see Collection.destroy()'s docstring: "Only admin users can use this method"). Call this explicitly when you know you have admin rights and want stale rows gone; the routine build_vector_index()/rebuild_vector_indexing() path deliberately does not call this, so it keeps working for non-admin sessions.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: sources: Subset of "objects", "sections", "columns" to reset. None (default) resets all three.

docs.title: _parse_one
docs.text:
_parse_one(source: str, hit) -> Optional[dict]

Normalise a single similarity-search hit into the standard row schema.

Confirmed live: a Teradata Vector Store hit's dict shape is flattened — the text lives directly under the source's real business-dictionary column name (business_description for objects/columns, section_content for sections), NOT a generic content/text key, and metadata (object_name, section_name, column_name, database_name, table_name) is flattened into the same dict, not nested under a metadata key. Before business_description/ section_content were added to _TEXT_KEYS, every hit's text stayed empty and was silently dropped by the if not text.strip() guard below — search_teradata_index would return 0 rows even when the underlying similarity_search call itself returned real, relevant hits.

docs.title: _iter_results
docs.text:
_iter_results(raw)

Yield individual hits from whatever TeradataVectorStore.similarity_search returns (json string, list of Documents, list of dicts, object with .similar_objects attribute, etc.).

docs.title: search_teradata_index
docs.text:
search_teradata_index(query: str, sources: list[str] = ('objects', 'sections', 'columns'), section_names: Optional[List[str]] = None, top_k: int = 10) -> pd.DataFrame

Search the Teradata vector stores using similarity search.

Cross-store federation (v0.3.1.148): searches the primary store's collections AND, when read-only stores are attached (tdfs4ds.read_stores()), each read-only store's OWN materialized collections — each store keeps its own collections in its own database, so this needs no re-index, only additional queries scoped by (vector_prefix, target_database) per store. Hits are merged, tagged with FEATURE_STORE provenance (only when >1 store), deduped on (OBJECT_NAME, FEATURE_STORE) and run through the shared distance gate. Fail-open: a store whose collections are absent/failed is skipped with a warning (the agent still answers it via the catalog / business-dictionary paths). A single store (the default) is byte-identical to the historical read. Chroma is a single local index and does NOT federate (see search_vector_index).

docs.title: tdfs4ds.benchmark
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.benchmark — dataset serving-latency benchmark + HTML report.

run_benchmark(dataset) measures client-perceived latency / throughput of a tdfs4ds dataset view (single-key, batch, large-batch, concurrency, time-travel, DBQL server-side), using raw teradatasql; build_benchmark_html / write_benchmark_report render a self-contained HTML report.

docs.title: __all__ module-attribute
docs.text: __all__ = ['run_benchmark', 'get_dataset_schema', 'build_benchmark_html', 'write_benchmark_report']

docs.title: run_benchmark
docs.text:
run_benchmark(dataset, entity_cols=None, *, sample_size=10000, skip=(), batch_sizes=(1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500), large_sizes=(10000, 100000, 1000000), strategy_sizes=(500, 5000, 50000), concurrency=(1, 2, 4), time_travel_dates=(), max_in_list_keys=None, conn_kwargs=None, progress=None)

Run the benchmark against dataset and return the report dict.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
dataset : str Fully-qualified DB.dataset_view to benchmark. entity_cols : list[str] | None Grain/entity key columns. When None, auto-derived from the tdfs4ds dataset catalog (needs an active teradataml context). skip : set[str] Any of {'explain','warm','cold','batch','large_batch', 'batch_strategies','concurrent','time_travel','server_timing'} to skip. strategy_sizes : tuple[int] Batch sizes at which the batch_strategies phase compares the in_list predicate vs the volatile_join retrieval side-by-side. max_in_list_keys : int | None Maximum number of keys put into an in_list predicate. When None it is inferred from the entity-column count as max(50, 500 // n_entity_cols) — wide composite keys produce large per-key disjuncts whose parse time dominates the measurement, so the cap keeps in_list batches cheap. It clamps batch_sizes and the in_list arm of batch_strategies ONLY; the volatile_join arm and large_batch keep their full sizes. conn_kwargs : dict | None Raw teradatasql kwargs; defaults to the TDFS4DS_TD_* environment. progress : callable(str) | None Receives one human-readable line per test phase (for a UI bar).

Returns the {"metadata":…, "results":…} report dict.

docs.title: get_dataset_schema
docs.text:
get_dataset_schema(dataset, entity_cols)

Probe the dataset's resolved columns → feature count + per-column types.

Resolution order: HELP COLUMN first (authoritative for VIEWS), DBC.ColumnsV fallback. 'features' are the non-entity columns; entity columns are the grain keys. Returns a metadata dict or {error:…}.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.benchmark.report
docs.text:
Render a compelling, self-contained HTML report from the dict produced by :func:tdfs4ds.benchmark.run_benchmark (or its saved JSON).

The HTML is fully offline — inline SVG charts + CSS, no external JS — so it renders identically in any browser and embeds directly in the admin panel.

:func:build_benchmark_html, :func:write_benchmark_report. Also a CLI:

python -m tdfs4ds.benchmark.report report.json [-o out.html][--open]

docs.title: _GUIDE module-attribute
docs.text: _GUIDE = {'schema': '<p>The dataset is a pre-joined view. Each lookup returns one row spanning all these columns, so <b>row width drives transfer cost</b>: many wide <code>VARCHAR</code>/<code>DECIMAL</code> features mean more bytes per lookup and a higher latency floor, independent of how fast the index seek is.</p><ul><li><b>Features</b> = the non-key columns served. <b>Entity key</b> = the grain you look up by.</li><li>If latency looks high but the plan is single-AMP, suspect a wide row (large bulk-read bytes), not the seek.</li></ul>', 'explain': "<p>This is the <b>structural proof</b> behind every latency number — it inspects the query plan, not a stopwatch.</p><ul><li><span class='g'>✓ Single-AMP access</span> / <span class='g'>✓ Unique primary index</span> — ideal: the row is found on one AMP via the primary index. This is what makes point lookups fast.</li><li><span class='b'>✗ All-AMPs retrieve</span> / <span class='b'>✗ All-rows scan</span> — the engine touches every AMP / scans the whole table. Acceptable for bulk reads, but a red flag for single-key serving — latency will not scale and will rise with table size.</li></ul><p>If the latency tables look good <i>and</i> this is single-AMP, the numbers are trustworthy. If lookups are single-AMP-clean but still slow, the cost is network/transfer, not the plan.</p>", 'single_key': "<p>The core serving pattern: one entity key → one feature row. Read the <b>percentiles, not the mean</b> — p50 is the typical experience, p95/p99 are the tail your slowest users feel.</p><ul><li><b>Warm</b> rotates a small hot key set, so the row/index pages are cached — this is your <i>best-case</i> served latency.</li><li><b>Cold</b> spreads across the whole key space, forcing disk/cache misses — closer to <i>real production</i> where any key can be requested.</li><li>A big warm→cold gap means you are I/O-bound on cache misses; a small gap means the working set fits in memory.</li><li>Colors: <span class='g'>green <150 ms</span>, <span class='y'>amber 150–500 ms</span>, <span class='r'>red >500 ms</span>. These include network + driver overhead.</li></ul>", 'batch_strategies': "<p>Two ways to fetch many keys in one shot, measured side-by-side:</p><ul><li><b>in_list</b> — a single <code>SELECT … WHERE (a=… AND b=…) OR …</code> predicate. Simple, but the <b>SQL text grows with the key count</b>: a wide composite-key batch hits Teradata's ~1 MB request parser limit (Error 5639) and is shown here as <i>skipped (parser limit)</i>.</li><li><b>volatile_join</b> — the keys are bulk-loaded into a session <b>volatile table</b> whose <code>PRIMARY INDEX</code> matches the feature-store storage tables, then <code>INNER JOIN</code>ed. Keys travel as <b>data, not SQL text</b>, so there is no parser limit, and the join is AMP-local (co-located).</li></ul><p>Rule of thumb: in_list wins for small batches (no table setup); volatile_join wins as the key count grows and is the <b>only</b> option once the predicate would exceed the parser limit.</p>", 'batch': '<p>The API-scoring pattern: N keys fetched in <b>one</b> round-trip. Compare the per-batch latency to N separate single-key lookups — batching amortizes the network round-trip, so a batch of 100 should be far cheaper than 100 individual calls.</p><ul><li>Watch how p50 grows with batch size. Sub-linear growth = batching is paying off.</li><li>For a composite key the predicate is an <code>OR</code> of <code>AND</code> groups; very large batches can add parse/optimize time — if the biggest batch jumps disproportionately, that parse cost (not retrieval) is usually why.</li></ul>', 'time_travel': "<p>Cost of querying the dataset <b>AS OF a historical instant</b> vs. the current-time baseline. The baseline is the unmodified <code>CURRENT VALIDTIME</code> view; each <code>AS OF</code> row re-binds the view's validtime to a past timestamp.</p><ul><li>Compare each <code>AS OF</code> p50 against the <b>baseline</b> row — the difference is the time-travel overhead.</li><li>A small delta means historical queries are about as cheap as current ones; a large delta means scanning historical row versions is materially more expensive.</li></ul>", 'large_batch': '<p>The MPP sweet spot: large scans pulled fully to the client. Here the headline is <b>throughput (rows/sec)</b>, not per-query latency.</p><ul><li>Higher rows/sec is better. A plateau across batch sizes means you have hit a bottleneck — usually client-side fetch/deserialize or network bandwidth, not the database.</li><li>If throughput is flat while latency climbs with size, you are transfer-bound: the engine produced rows fast but the client drained them slowly.</li></ul>', 'concurrent': '<p>How the system behaves under parallel clients — the <b>QPS ceiling</b> and how latency degrades as load rises.</p><ul><li>Ideally QPS rises roughly linearly with threads while p50 stays flat. When QPS stops climbing but latency keeps rising, you have found <b>saturation</b> — adding clients only queues work.</li><li>On a shared / trial system this ceiling can be low (limited AMPs/sessions); a drop in QPS at higher concurrency usually reflects the test box, not the dataset design.</li><li>Each thread opens its own session, so high concurrency also tests session-setup capacity.</li></ul>', 'server_side': "<p>Splits client-perceived latency into <b>server execution time</b> (from DBQL: <code>FirstRespTime − StartTime</code>) vs. everything else (network round-trip + driver).</p><ul><li><b>Server p50</b> is the database's own time for the <i>same single-key lookups</i> as the warm/cold tests — not a mix of all your queries.</li><li><b>Overhead = client p50 − server p50</b> is the network + driver tax. Large overhead with small server time means you are far from the database (run the benchmark closer / same region).</li><li><b>AMP CPU</b> = compute the AMPs spent; <b>Parser CPU</b> = optimize/parse cost (high parser CPU on tiny lookups hints at plan-cache misses).</li><li>A negative or flagged-unreliable overhead means DBQL could not be scoped to the lookups — ignore the number, not the percentiles.</li></ul>"}

docs.text:
CSS = '
:root{
  --bg:#0b1020; --panel:#141b2e; --panel2:#1b2540; --ink:#e8edf7; --muted:#93a0bd;
  --line:#26314f; --accent:#ff8a3d; --accent2:#4f8cff;
  --ok:#34d399; --warn:#f87171; --good:#38bdf8; --fair:#fbbf24; --poor:#fb7185;
  --excellent:#34d399;
}
*{box-sizing:border-box}
body{margin:0;background:linear-gradient(180deg,#0b1020,#0d1426 40%,#0b1020);
  color:var(--ink);font:15px/1.55 -apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif}
a{color:var(--accent2)}
.wrap{max-width:1080px;margin:0 auto;padding:32px 22px 80px}
header.hero{background:radial-gradient(1200px 400px at 10% -20%,rgba(255,138,61,.22),transparent 60%),
  linear-gradient(135deg,#16213e,#0e1730);border:1px solid var(--line);
  border-radius:18px;padding:30px 32px;margin-bottom:26px;position:relative;overflow:hidden}
header.hero:before{content:"";position:absolute;right:-60px;top:-60px;width:240px;height:240px;
  background:radial-gradient(circle,rgba(79,140,255,.25),transparent 65%)}
.kicker{letter-spacing:.16em;text-transform:uppercase;font-size:12px;color:var(--accent);font-weight:700}
h1{margin:.25em 0 .15em;font-size:30px;letter-spacing:-.02em}
.dataset{font-size:18px;color:var(--ink);font-weight:600}
.meta-line{color:var(--muted);font-size:13.5px;margin-top:10px;display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:6px 18px}
.meta-line b{color:var(--ink);font-weight:600}
.note{margin-top:14px;padding:10px 14px;background:rgba(79,140,255,.10);
  border:1px solid rgba(79,140,255,.30);border-radius:10px;color:#cdd9f5;font-size:13px}

.kpi-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fit,minmax(165px,1fr));gap:14px;margin:4px 0 6px}
.kpi{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:14px;padding:16px 16px 14px;position:relative}
.kpi-label{font-size:12px;color:var(--muted);text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.06em}
.kpi-value{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;margin-top:6px;letter-spacing:-.02em}
.kpi-sub{font-size:12px;color:var(--muted);margin-top:4px}
.kpi:before{content:"";position:absolute;left:0;top:14px;bottom:14px;width:3px;border-radius:3px;background:var(--accent2)}
.kpi-excellent:before,.kpi-good:before{background:var(--ok)}
.kpi-fair:before{background:var(--fair)}
.kpi-poor:before{background:var(--poor)}

section.card{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:16px;
  padding:22px 24px;margin:20px 0}
h2{margin:0 0 4px;font-size:20px;letter-spacing:-.01em}
h4{margin:18px 0 8px;font-size:13px;color:var(--muted);text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.06em}
.muted{color:var(--muted);margin:.2em 0 14px;font-size:13.5px}

table.data{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;margin-top:6px;font-size:13.5px}
table.data th{ text-align:right;color:var(--muted);font-weight:600;font-size:12px;
  text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.04em;padding:6px 10px;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
table.data th:first-child{text-align:left}
table.data td{text-align:right;padding:8px 10px;border-bottom:1px solid rgba(38,49,79,.5)}
table.data td.name{text-align:left;font-weight:600;color:var(--ink)}
table.data tr:hover td{background:rgba(79,140,255,.06)}

.lat{font-weight:700;padding:2px 8px;border-radius:7px;font-size:12.5px}
.lat-excellent{background:rgba(52,211,153,.16);color:#6ee7b7}
.lat-good{background:rgba(56,189,248,.16);color:#7dd3fc}
.lat-fair{background:rgba(251,191,36,.16);color:#fcd34d}
.lat-poor{background:rgba(251,113,133,.16);color:#fda4af}
.lat-na{background:#26314f;color:var(--muted)}

.chips{display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:10px;margin-top:6px}
.chip{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:7px;padding:7px 13px;border-radius:999px;
  font-size:13px;font-weight:600;border:1px solid var(--line);background:var(--panel2)}
.chip b{font-size:14px}
.chip-ok{border-color:rgba(52,211,153,.45);color:#6ee7b7;background:rgba(52,211,153,.10)}
.chip-warn{border-color:rgba(248,113,113,.45);color:#fca5a5;background:rgba(248,113,113,.10)}
.chip-neutral{border-color:rgba(79,140,255,.4);color:#bcd4ff;background:rgba(79,140,255,.10);font-weight:600}
.bar-type{fill:var(--accent2)}
td.mono,.mono{font-family:ui-monospace,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;font-size:12.5px;color:#bcd4ff}
.muted .t{font-family:ui-monospace,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;color:#bcd4ff;font-size:12.5px}

.verdict{margin:14px 0 4px;padding:11px 14px;border-radius:10px;background:var(--panel2);
  border:1px solid var(--line);font-size:14px}
.verdict-ok{border-color:rgba(52,211,153,.4);background:rgba(52,211,153,.08)}
.verdict-warn{border-color:rgba(248,113,113,.4);background:rgba(248,113,113,.08)}

details.plan{margin-top:14px}
details.plan summary{cursor:pointer;color:var(--accent2);font-size:13px;font-weight:600}
details.plan pre{white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word;background:#0a1224;border:1px solid var(--line);
  border-radius:10px;padding:14px;margin-top:10px;font-size:12px;color:#bcd}

.chart-wrap{margin-top:14px}
svg.barchart{width:100%;height:auto;display:block}
.bar-label{fill:var(--muted);font-size:13px}
.bar-track{fill:rgba(38,49,79,.55)}
.bar{fill:var(--accent2)}
.bar-excellent,.bar-good{fill:var(--ok)}
.bar-fair{fill:var(--fair)}
.bar-poor{fill:var(--poor)}
.bar-value{fill:var(--ink);font-size:12.5px;font-weight:600}
.bar-value-in{fill:#0b1020}
.bar-na{fill:var(--muted);font-style:italic}

.dual{display:grid;grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr;gap:22px}
@media(max-width:760px){.dual{grid-template-columns:1fr}}

.errors{margin-top:16px;border-top:1px solid var(--line);padding-top:12px}
.errors h4{color:var(--warn)}
.errors ul{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
.errors li{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;gap:16px;padding:6px 0;
  border-bottom:1px solid rgba(38,49,79,.5);font-size:13px}
.errors .name{color:var(--ink);font-weight:600}
.err-msg{color:var(--muted);text-align:right;font-family:ui-monospace,Menlo,monospace;font-size:12px}

.notice{padding:11px 14px;border-radius:10px;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);font-size:13.5px;color:var(--muted)}
.notice-warn{border-color:rgba(248,113,113,.4);background:rgba(248,113,113,.08);color:#fca5a5}

details.explain{margin-top:16px;border-top:1px dashed var(--line);padding-top:12px}
details.explain>summary{cursor:pointer;color:var(--accent);font-size:13px;font-weight:600;
  list-style:none;display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:7px;user-select:none}
details.explain>summary::-webkit-details-marker{display:none}
details.explain>summary:before{content:"?";display:inline-flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;
  width:18px;height:18px;border-radius:50%;background:rgba(255,138,61,.16);color:var(--accent);
  font-size:12px;font-weight:700}
details.explain[open]>summary{margin-bottom:8px}
.explain-body{font-size:13.5px;color:#c4d0ea;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
  border-radius:10px;padding:14px 16px}
.explain-body p{margin:.2em 0 .7em}
.explain-body p:last-child{margin-bottom:0}
.explain-body ul{margin:.3em 0 .5em;padding-left:20px}
.explain-body li{margin:5px 0}
.explain-body code{background:#0a1224;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:5px;
  padding:1px 6px;font-family:ui-monospace,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;font-size:12px;color:#bcd4ff}
.explain-body .g{color:#6ee7b7;font-weight:600}
.explain-body .b{color:#fca5a5;font-weight:600}
.explain-body .y{color:#fcd34d;font-weight:600}
.explain-body .r{color:#fda4af;font-weight:600}

footer{color:var(--muted);font-size:12.5px;text-align:center;margin-top:34px;line-height:1.7}
.legend{display:flex;gap:14px;flex-wrap:wrap;justify-content:center;margin-top:8px}
.legend span{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:6px}
.dot{width:10px;height:10px;border-radius:3px;display:inline-block}
'

docs.title: tdfs4ds.benchmark.runner
docs.text:
End-to-end latency / throughput benchmark for a tdfs4ds feature dataset view.

Uses raw teradatasql (NOT teradataml) on purpose: teradataml routes every statement through a single shared SQLAlchemy connection pool, which (a) corrupts under the concurrent-QPS thread fan-out ("N is not a valid connection pool handle") and (b) adds per-call wrapping latency that pollutes a latency benchmark. Connections are opened from the same TDFS4DS_TD_* environment the serve layer uses (tdfs4ds.utils.reconnect._connect_kwargs).

docs.title: WHAT THIS MEASURES
docs.text:
CLIENT-PERCEIVED latency — wall-clock around cursor.execute() — which includes driver overhead, network round-trip, server parse/plan/AMP execution and result serialization. The optional DBQL pass isolates pure server time.

Two entry points
  • programmatic — :func:run_benchmark (returns the report dict), and
  • CLI — python -m tdfs4ds.benchmark.runner --host … --dataset … --entity-cols ….

docs.title: _connect
docs.text:

docs.title: test_batch_strategies
docs.text:
test_batch_strategies(dataset, entity_cols, all_keys, sizes, n_runs=5, max_in_list_keys=None)

Compare the two batch-retrieval strategies side-by-side, per size.

For each size, time BOTH retrieve_batch(method='in_list') and retrieve_batch(method='volatile_join') over the same key sample, reusing the raw benchmark connection (_MAIN_CONN) so no teradataml pooling latency enters the measurement. An in_list predicate that would exceed the parser limit is recorded as {"error":"skipped_parser_limit"} rather than crashing — exactly the case the volatile join is meant to cover.

max_in_list_keys caps only the in_list arm: above it the in_list arm is recorded as {"error":"skipped_in_list_cap","cap":…} (a wide composite predicate's parse time would dominate the measurement) while the volatile_join arm still runs at the full size.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
dataset : str Fully-qualified DB.dataset_view to benchmark. entity_cols : list[str] | None Grain/entity key columns. When None, auto-derived from the tdfs4ds dataset catalog (needs an active teradataml context). skip : set[str] Any of {'explain','warm','cold','batch','large_batch', 'batch_strategies','concurrent','time_travel','server_timing'} to skip. strategy_sizes : tuple[int] Batch sizes at which the batch_strategies phase compares the in_list predicate vs the volatile_join retrieval side-by-side. max_in_list_keys : int | None Maximum number of keys put into an in_list predicate. When None it is inferred from the entity-column count as max(50, 500 // n_entity_cols) — wide composite keys produce large per-key disjuncts whose parse time dominates the measurement, so the cap keeps in_list batches cheap. It clamps batch_sizes and the in_list arm of batch_strategies ONLY; the volatile_join arm and large_batch keep their full sizes. conn_kwargs : dict | None Raw teradatasql kwargs; defaults to the TDFS4DS_TD_* environment. progress : callable(str) | None Receives one human-readable line per test phase (for a UI bar).

Returns the {"metadata":…, "results":…} report dict.

docs.title: build_dataset
docs.text:
build_dataset(entity_id, selected_features, view_name, schema_name=None, comment=None, return_query=False, feature_store_time=False, join_type='INNER', grouped=False, document=False, vector_indexing=False, record_lineage=False)

Generate a SQL query and create a Teradata view to retrieve records for the specified entity IDs based on selected features.

docs.title: Parameters:
docs.text:
entity_id : list, dict, or str The entity IDs for which records are needed. Can be a list, dictionary, or string. - If list: contains multiple entity IDs. - If dict: keys are the entity IDs. - If str: a single entity ID.

dict

A dictionary where the keys are feature table names, and the values are lists of tuples (feature_id, feature_version, feature_name) specifying the features to retrieve. NOTE: feature_version may be either: - a single UUID string, or - a list of dicts like:

str

The name of the view to be created in the database.

str, optional

The name of the database schema where the view will be created. Defaults to the global tdfs4ds.SCHEMA if not provided.

str, optional

A comment to attach to the created view for documentation purposes. Defaults to None.

bool, optional

If True, the function will return the SQL query string instead of executing it. Defaults to False.

bool, optional

If True, the query will use a specific VALIDTIME based on tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME. If False, the query will use CURRENT VALIDTIME. Defaults to False.

str, optional

Specifies the type of join to use between features: 'INNER' (default) or 'FULL OUTER'.

bool, optional

If False (default), uses one sub-query per feature joined together — the classic multi-join approach. Every individual feature sub-query can be targeted by a PI-equality filter pushed from the outer WHERE, making single-entity lookups single-AMP. If True, groups features that share the same storage table and process version into a single sub-query with MAX(CASE WHEN …) pivoting, reducing the number of JOINs from N_features to N_distinct_(table, version) pairs. A HAVING clause enforces non-NULL for every feature in the group when join_type='INNER', preserving the same missing-feature semantics as the classic approach. PI push-down is preserved for INNER JOIN (outer SELECT uses A1.entity); for FULL OUTER the COALESCE in the outer SELECT prevents push-down, same as the classic approach.

bool, optional

If True, generate and persist business-dictionary documentation for the newly built dataset via document_dataset_incremental (LLM-powered). Requires tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER and tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL to be configured; silently skipped (with an info log) otherwise. Failures are non-fatal — the dataset view is always created/returned regardless. Defaults to False (opt-in). Mirrors upload_features. Leave False when the dataset is built via upload_features/run() — that path already documents it, so enabling it here would double-document.

bool, optional

If True (and document=True and an instruct model is configured), refresh the consumer-agent vector index via build_vector_index(force_rebuild=False) after documentation so the new dataset is searchable immediately. Non-fatal. Defaults to False (opt-in).

bool, optional

If True, persist the dataset view's lineage subgraph into FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH via record_process_lineage (no LLM required). Non-fatal. A standalone dataset with no upstream processes records an isolated node. Defaults to False (opt-in).

docs.title: Returns:
docs.text: str or tdml.DataFrame - If return_query is True, returns the generated SQL query as a string. - Otherwise, returns a Teradata DataFrame containing the results.

docs.title: Raises:
docs.text: ValueError If an invalid join_type is provided.

docs.title: augment_source_with_features
docs.text:
augment_source_with_features(source_schema, source_name, entity_id, feature_selection, output_view_schema, output_view_name, join_type='LEFT JOIN')

Augment a source table with selected features by creating a view that joins the source data with feature tables based on specified entity identifiers and feature selections.

The join type can be specified as either 'LEFT JOIN' or 'INNER JOIN': - 'LEFT JOIN': Retains all rows from the source table, with missing values for unmatched features. - 'INNER JOIN': Returns only rows where all specified features have matching, non-missing values.

Parameters: source_schema (str): The schema of the source table to augment. source_name (str): The name of the source table to augment. entity_id (str or list of str): The entity ID(s) for joining the source data with feature tables. feature_selection (list): A list of features to select and join with the source data. output_view_schema (str): The schema in which to create the output view. output_view_name (str): The name of the output view to create with augmented features. join_type (str, optional): 'LEFT JOIN' or 'INNER JOIN'. Defaults to 'LEFT JOIN'.

Returns: tdml.DataFrame: A DataFrame object that references the newly created view.

docs.title: retrieve_batch
docs.text:
retrieve_batch(dataset, list_entity_keys, entity_cols=None, *, columns=None, method='auto', volatile_threshold=DEFAULT_VOLATILE_THRESHOLD, conn=None, dedup=True)

Retrieve rows of dataset for a list of entity-key tuples.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: dataset : str Fully-qualified DB.dataset_view to read from. list_entity_keys : list[tuple] Entity-key tuples in the order of entity_cols (each tuple one row's key). entity_cols : list[str] | None Grain/entity key columns. When None they (and their SQL types) are auto-derived from the dataset catalog (needs an active teradataml context). columns : list[str] | None Columns to project; None selects all. method : {'auto','in_list','volatile_join'} 'in_list' builds a WHERE predicate (single-col IN / composite OR-of-ANDs); 'volatile_join' loads the keys into a volatile table co-located on the feature-store primary index and joins; 'auto' (default) uses the volatile join when the unique-key count reaches volatile_threshold OR when the in_list predicate would exceed the parser limit, else the in_list predicate. volatile_threshold : int Key count at/above which 'auto' switches to the volatile join. conn : teradatasql connection | None Optional raw connection (benchmark reuse). None uses the teradataml context. dedup : bool Drop duplicate key tuples first (required for the volatile join; also shrinks the in_list predicate).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[tuple] The fetched rows.

docs.title: resolve_dataset_pi
docs.text:
resolve_dataset_pi(dataset, entity_cols, conn=None)

Resolve the REAL primary index / partitioning of a dataset's feature tables.

Returns a dict::

{"primary_index": [...], "partitioning": "...",
 "source": "DBC.IndicesV" | "fallback_entity_cols",
 "feature_tables": ["db.FS_T_…", …]}

The volatile key table should use primary_index so the join co-locates with the feature storage tables. Fail-open: returns entity_cols when the real distribution can't be read (restricted DBC, view-only, no match).

  • builder

docs.title: tdfs4ds.dataset.batch_retrieval
docs.text:
Parser-limit-safe batch retrieval of dataset rows for a list of entity keys.

A naive batch lookup builds SELECT * FROM ds WHERE (a=1 AND b=2) OR … whose text grows with the number of keys and, for many / wide composite keys, exceeds Teradata's ~1 MB request parser limit (Error 5639). The robust strategy is a volatile-table join: load the (deduplicated) keys into a volatile table whose PRIMARY INDEX matches the actual distribution of the feature-store technical tables the dataset joins, then INNER JOIN — keys travel as data, not SQL text, so there is no parser limit and the join is AMP-local.

docs.title: Public API
docs.text:
• :func:retrieve_batch — fetch rows for a list of entity-key tuples, choosing the in_list predicate or the volatile_join strategy (method='auto').
  • :func:resolve_dataset_pi — read the real PRIMARY INDEX / partitioning of the feature tables a dataset joins (so the volatile table co-locates with them).
  • :func:dataset_entity_columns — the dataset grain as [(name, type), …].

docs.title: Connection seam
docs.text: Every helper takes an optional raw teradatasql conn. When conn is None the active teradataml context is used via tdml.execute_sql — so package users get the zero-config path and the benchmark can reuse its own raw per-thread connection (avoiding teradataml's pooling latency).

docs.title: compute_dataset_split
docs.text:
compute_dataset_split(entity_id, selected_features)

Compute the AUTHORITATIVE entity/feature split a dataset view will have, using exactly the column aliases _build_dataset_multijoin generates.

This is what build_dataset already knows at build time — the role of a column (entity vs feature) is dataset-specific and must NOT be re-derived from the global catalog (the same name can be a feature in one process and an entity in another) nor from fragile DDL regex.

Returns (entity_cols, feature_map) where: * entity_cols — list of entity column names (the dataset grain), * feature_map — {ALIAS_UPPER: {id, version, database, view}} keyed by the feature's final column alias in the view (uppercased). type is filled in later from the built view's column types.

docs.title: _dataset_with_known_split
docs.text:
_dataset_with_known_split(view_name, schema_name, entity_id, selected_features)

Construct a Dataset using the build-time entity/feature split.

Types are sourced from the built view's columns (authoritative for what the view actually emits). Falls back to the regex-based Dataset(view_name=...) if the split can't be computed, so registration never hard-fails.

docs.title: _build_dataset_multijoin
docs.text:
_build_dataset_multijoin(entity_id, selected_features, view_name, schema_name, feature_store_time, join_type)

Build the REPLACE VIEW DDL using one sub-query per feature (classic approach).

Each feature gets its own sub-query; the outer query chains them with JOINs. Teradata pushes a PI-equality filter on the entity column directly into every sub-query, making single-entity lookups single-AMP.

Returns the full DDL string, or None when no features are available.

docs.title: _build_dataset_grouped
docs.text:
_build_dataset_grouped(entity_id, selected_features, view_name, schema_name, feature_store_time, join_type)

Build the REPLACE VIEW DDL using one sub-query per (table, version) group.

Features sharing the same storage table and process version are pivoted into a single sub-query with MAX(CASE WHEN FEATURE_ID=X THEN FEATURE_VALUE END) per feature and GROUP BY on the entity columns. This reduces the number of JOINs from N_features down to N_distinct_(table, version) pairs.

For INNER JOIN a HAVING clause filters out entities missing any feature in the group, preserving the same strict semantics as the per-feature approach. For FULL OUTER no HAVING is added so partial entities survive with NULLs.

PI-equality filters on the entity column are pushed through the GROUP BY into the base-table scan when the outer WHERE references the entity column directly. For INNER JOIN the outer SELECT uses A1.entity (enables push-down); for FULL OUTER it uses COALESCE (push-down is not possible, same as classic approach).

Returns the full DDL string, or None when no features are available.

docs.title: build_dataset
docs.text:
build_dataset(entity_id, selected_features, view_name, schema_name=None, comment=None, return_query=False, feature_store_time=False, join_type='INNER', grouped=False, document=False, vector_indexing=False, record_lineage=False)

Generate a SQL query and create a Teradata view to retrieve records for the specified entity IDs based on selected features.

docs.title: Parameters:
docs.text:
entity_id : list, dict, or str The entity IDs for which records are needed. Can be a list, dictionary, or string. - If list: contains multiple entity IDs. - If dict: keys are the entity IDs. - If str: a single entity ID.

dict

A dictionary where the keys are feature table names, and the values are lists of tuples (feature_id, feature_version, feature_name) specifying the features to retrieve. NOTE: feature_version may be either: - a single UUID string, or - a list of dicts like:

str

The name of the view to be created in the database.

str, optional

The name of the database schema where the view will be created. Defaults to the global tdfs4ds.SCHEMA if not provided.

str, optional

A comment to attach to the created view for documentation purposes. Defaults to None.

bool, optional

If True, the function will return the SQL query string instead of executing it. Defaults to False.

bool, optional

If True, the query will use a specific VALIDTIME based on tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME. If False, the query will use CURRENT VALIDTIME. Defaults to False.

str, optional

Specifies the type of join to use between features: 'INNER' (default) or 'FULL OUTER'.

bool, optional

If False (default), uses one sub-query per feature joined together — the classic multi-join approach. Every individual feature sub-query can be targeted by a PI-equality filter pushed from the outer WHERE, making single-entity lookups single-AMP. If True, groups features that share the same storage table and process version into a single sub-query with MAX(CASE WHEN …) pivoting, reducing the number of JOINs from N_features to N_distinct_(table, version) pairs. A HAVING clause enforces non-NULL for every feature in the group when join_type='INNER', preserving the same missing-feature semantics as the classic approach. PI push-down is preserved for INNER JOIN (outer SELECT uses A1.entity); for FULL OUTER the COALESCE in the outer SELECT prevents push-down, same as the classic approach.

bool, optional

If True, generate and persist business-dictionary documentation for the newly built dataset via document_dataset_incremental (LLM-powered). Requires tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER and tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL to be configured; silently skipped (with an info log) otherwise. Failures are non-fatal — the dataset view is always created/returned regardless. Defaults to False (opt-in). Mirrors upload_features. Leave False when the dataset is built via upload_features/run() — that path already documents it, so enabling it here would double-document.

bool, optional

If True (and document=True and an instruct model is configured), refresh the consumer-agent vector index via build_vector_index(force_rebuild=False) after documentation so the new dataset is searchable immediately. Non-fatal. Defaults to False (opt-in).

bool, optional

If True, persist the dataset view's lineage subgraph into FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH via record_process_lineage (no LLM required). Non-fatal. A standalone dataset with no upstream processes records an isolated node. Defaults to False (opt-in).

docs.title: Returns:
docs.text: str or tdml.DataFrame - If return_query is True, returns the generated SQL query as a string. - Otherwise, returns a Teradata DataFrame containing the results.

docs.title: Raises:
docs.text: ValueError If an invalid join_type is provided.

docs.title: augment_source_with_features
docs.text:
augment_source_with_features(source_schema, source_name, entity_id, feature_selection, output_view_schema, output_view_name, join_type='LEFT JOIN')

Augment a source table with selected features by creating a view that joins the source data with feature tables based on specified entity identifiers and feature selections.

The join type can be specified as either 'LEFT JOIN' or 'INNER JOIN': - 'LEFT JOIN': Retains all rows from the source table, with missing values for unmatched features. - 'INNER JOIN': Returns only rows where all specified features have matching, non-missing values.

Parameters: source_schema (str): The schema of the source table to augment. source_name (str): The name of the source table to augment. entity_id (str or list of str): The entity ID(s) for joining the source data with feature tables. feature_selection (list): A list of features to select and join with the source data. output_view_schema (str): The schema in which to create the output view. output_view_name (str): The name of the output view to create with augmented features. join_type (str, optional): 'LEFT JOIN' or 'INNER JOIN'. Defaults to 'LEFT JOIN'.

Returns: tdml.DataFrame: A DataFrame object that references the newly created view.

docs.title: get_feature_types_sql_format
docs.text:
get_feature_types_sql_format(tddf, columns=None)

Retrieve the SQL data types of specified columns from a Teradata dataframe.

This function executes a query to fetch the SQL data types of the given columns and returns them in a dictionary format.

Parameters: tddf (tdml.DataFrame): A Teradata dataframe. columns (list): List of column names to retrieve their data types.

Returns: dict: A dictionary where keys are column names and values are their corresponding SQL data types.

Example:

tdml.load_example_data("GLM", ["admissions_train"]) df = tdml.DataFrame("admissions_train") get_feature_types_sql_format(df, columns=df.columns) {'id': 'INTEGER', 'masters': 'VARCHAR(5)', 'gpa': 'FLOAT', 'stats': 'VARCHAR(30)', 'programming': 'VARCHAR(30)', 'admitted': 'INTEGER'}

df.to_sql(table_name='test_admission_train', types={'stats': tdml.VARCHAR(length=40, charset='UNICODE')}, if_exists='replace') df = tdml.DataFrame("test_admission_train") get_feature_types_sql_format(df, columns=df.columns) {'id': 'INTEGER', 'masters': 'VARCHAR(5)', 'gpa': 'FLOAT', 'stats': 'VARCHAR(40) CHARACTER SET UNICODE', 'programming': 'VARCHAR(30)', 'admitted': 'INTEGER'}

docs.title: feature_store_table_creation
docs.text:
feature_store_table_creation(entity_id, feature_type, if_exists='fail', primary_index=None, partitioning='')

Creates a table and a corresponding view for feature storage in a Teradata database schema, based on specified entity ID and feature type.

docs.title: register_entity
docs.text:
register_entity(entity_id, feature_types, primary_index=None, partitioning='')

Registers an entity within the feature store by facilitating the creation of feature store tables across multiple data types.

This function leverages the feature_store_table_creation function to create feature store tables tailored for different data types: 'FLOAT', 'BIGINT', 'VARCHAR_UNICODE', and 'VARCHAR_LATIN'. By doing so, it ensures a comprehensive setup for the specified entity, allowing for a diverse range of feature data types to be stored efficiently.

Parameters: - entity_id (str): Unique identifier for the entity. This ID is utilized to construct table names and associate them with the corresponding entity within the feature store. - primary_index (list, optional): Specifies the columns to be used as the primary index for the tables. A well-chosen primary index can significantly enhance query performance. - partitioning (str, optional): Defines the partitioning strategy for the tables, which can help manage and optimize the storage and retrieval of large datasets.

Returns: tuple: Contains the names of the created feature store tables. This tuple includes names for tables designed to store 'FLOAT', 'BIGINT', 'VARCHAR_UNICODE', and 'VARCHAR_LATIN' feature types, in the order they were created.

Example Usage:

register_entity("customer123") ('customer123_float_table', 'customer123_bigint_table', 'customer123_varchar_unicode_table', 'customer123_varchar_latin_table')

Note: - This function is integral to initializing the feature storage for a new entity, ensuring that data of various types can be accommodated. - The function assumes that the feature_store_table_creation function handles the intricacies of table creation, including dealing with existing tables based on the 'if_exists' parameter and applying specified partitioning and indexing strategies.

docs.title: tdstone2_entity_id
docs.text:
tdstone2_entity_id(existing_model)

Generate a dictionary mapping entity IDs to their respective data types in a given tdstone2 model.

This function analyzes the provided tdstone2 model object to determine the type of the model ('model scoring' or 'feature engineering'). Depending on the model type, it retrieves the list of entity IDs from the appropriate mapper attribute ('mapper_scoring' or 'mapper'). It then constructs a dictionary where each key is an entity ID, and its corresponding value is the data type of that entity ID, as specified in the model's mapper attributes ('types').

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default existing_model object

A model object created using the tdstone2 package. This object should contain the necessary mapper attributes ('mapper_scoring' or 'mapper') with 'id_row', 'id_partition', and 'types'.

required

Returns:

Name Type Description dict

A dictionary mapping entity IDs to their data types. Keys are entity IDs, and values are data types (e.g., 'BIGINT').

Raises:

Type Description TypeError

If the relevant ID attribute ('id_row' or 'id_partition') in the model is neither a list nor a single value.

Note
  • The function dynamically determines the type of the model (scoring or feature engineering) based on the presence of specific attributes.
  • 'id_row' or 'id_partition' is converted to a list if it is not already one.
  • This function assumes the model is correctly instantiated and the necessary attributes are properly defined.
Example

model = entity_id_types = tdstone2_entity_id(model)

docs.title: remove_entity
docs.text:
remove_entity(entity, data_domain=None)

Removes all database entities (tables and views) associated with a given entity name within a specific data domain, and then removes the entity's metadata from the feature catalog.

This function first constructs a SQL query to select distinct table and view names associated with the given entity name and data domain from the feature catalog. It then iterates over the list of tables and views, attempting to drop each one. Progress and status of these operations are tracked using a progress bar. If an error occurs during the drop operation, the error message is displayed in the progress bar description. Finally, the function constructs and executes another SQL query to delete the entity's metadata from the feature catalog.

Parameters: - entity (str): The name of the entity to be removed. - data_domain (str, optional): The data domain where the entity is located. If not provided, the default data domain from tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN is used.

The function utilizes the tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE flag to control the printing of SQL queries for debugging purposes. If DEBUG_MODE is set to True, the queries for selecting and deleting entities are printed to the console.

Dependencies: - Requires the tdfs4ds module for configuration constants like DATA_DOMAIN, SCHEMA, and FEATURE_CATALOG_NAME, as well as the DEBUG_MODE flag. - Requires the tdml module or object with an execute_sql method for database operations. - Uses the tqdm module to display a progress bar for the operations.

Note: - This function assumes that the necessary permissions are in place for dropping tables and views, as well as deleting metadata entries from the feature catalog. - Exception handling within the dropping operations is limited to displaying the first line of any encountered error messages in the progress bar description. Further error handling or cleanup may be necessary depending on the specific requirements and database configuration.

  • None. The primary effect of this function is to alter the state of the database by removing entities and their metadata, not to return data to the caller.

docs.title: _get_database_username
docs.text:
_get_database_username()

Return the Teradata database user for volatile-table scoping.

LDAP sessions may surface a dotted login name (e.g. 'DEINF.CARLOSN'). Using it unquoted produces an invalid three-part name (Error 3707). Stripping to the last segment ('CARLOSN') points to the wrong database (Error 3807). Double-quoting makes it a valid single identifier: "DEINF.CARLOSN".table.

docs.title: display_table
docs.text:
display_table(df, max_widths=[25, 55, 10], header=['Feature Database', 'Feature Table', '# rows'])

Display a TeradataML DataFrame as a formatted table in the console.

This function formats each row of the TeradataML DataFrame according to specified maximum column widths. It prints out the DataFrame in a tabular format with a header.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default df DataFrame

The TeradataML DataFrame to be displayed.

required max_widths list of int

Maximum width of each column in characters. Defaults to [25, 55, 10].

[25, 55, 10] header list of str

Column headers to be displayed. Defaults to ["Feature Database", "Feature Table", "# rows"].

['Feature Database', 'Feature Table', '# rows']

Returns:

Name Type Description None

This function only prints the table and does not return anything.

Example

To display a TeradataML DataFrame with custom column widths and headers:

display_table(my_teradataml_dataframe, max_widths=[30, 60, 12], header=["DB Name", "Table Name", "Row Count"])

Note
  • The 'max_widths' list specifies the maximum width of each column in characters.
  • The 'header' list defines the column headers to be displayed.
  • The function formats each row of the TeradataML DataFrame to fit within the specified column widths and prints the table.

docs.title: _check_null_entity_keys
docs.text:
_check_null_entity_keys(null_counts)

Raise if any entity / primary-index key column contains NULL values.

NULL values in an entity (primary-index) key column are invalid: the MERGE ON clause uses plain equality, so an un-substituted NULL key never matches an existing row and the rows fan out into a cartesian-like product (severe skew, runtime and storage blow-up). This mirrors the duplicate-key guard, which also hard-fails rather than silently dropping rows.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: null_counts : dict Mapping of entity key column name -> number of NULL rows (as read from the materialized volatile table, where any column covered by entity_null_substitute is already COALESCE'd and therefore reports 0).

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: ValueError If one or more columns have a NULL count greater than 0. The message names the offending column(s) and their counts.

docs.title: generate_collect_stats
docs.text:
generate_collect_stats(entity_id, primary_index='', partitioning='')

Generate a COLLECT STATISTICS SQL query based on specified entity identifiers, primary index, and partitioning information. The query collects statistics for columns relevant to the data structure, including partitioning and entity identifiers, for optimized query performance.

entity_id (list or dict): A list of entity IDs or a dictionary where keys represent entity IDs. Used to identify the columns requiring statistical collection. primary_index (str or list, optional): The primary index column(s) for the table, provided as a single string or list of strings. Defaults to an empty string. partitioning (str, optional): A string defining partitioning information, used to identify additional columns for statistical collection. Defaults to an empty string.

tuple: A tuple containing two strings: - The base COLLECT STATISTICS SQL query. - The extended COLLECT STATISTICS SQL query with sampling and thresholds applied.

docs.title: prepare_feature_ingestion
docs.text:
prepare_feature_ingestion(df, entity_id, feature_names, feature_versions=None, primary_index=None, partitioning='', entity_null_substitute={}, **kwargs)

Transforms and prepares a DataFrame for feature ingestion into a feature store by unpivoting it.

This function accepts a DataFrame along with the specifications for entity IDs, feature names, and optionally, feature versions. It generates a new DataFrame with the data unpivoted to align with the requirements of a feature store ingestion process. Additionally, it creates a volatile table in the database to facilitate the transformation.

Parameters: - df (tdml.DataFrame): The input DataFrame containing the feature data. - entity_id (list/dict/other): Specifies the entity IDs. If a dictionary, the keys are used as entity IDs. If a list or another type, it is used directly as the entity ID. - feature_names (list): Names of features to be unpivoted. - feature_versions (dict, optional): Maps feature names to their versions. If not provided, a default version is applied. - primary_index (list/str, optional): Primary index(es) for the volatile table. If not specified, entity IDs are used. - **kwargs: Additional keyword arguments for customization.

tuple: A tuple containing: 1. A tdml.DataFrame with the transformed data ready for ingestion. 2. The name of the volatile table created in the database.

Raises: Exception: If the function encounters an issue during the database operations or data transformations.

Note: - The function handles different data types for 'entity_id' to ensure correct SQL query formation. - It assumes a specific structure for 'df' to properly execute SQL commands for data transformation.

Example:

df = tdml.DataFrame(...) entity_id = {'customer_id': 'INTEGER'} feature_names = ['feature1', 'feature2'] transformed_df, volatile_table_name = prepare_feature_ingestion(df, entity_id, feature_names)

docs.title: apply_collect_stats
docs.text:
apply_collect_stats(entity_id: Mapping[str, Any] | Iterable[str], primary_index: Optional[str], partitioning: Optional[str], feature_infos: DataFrame) -> Dict[str, Any]

Run COLLECT STATS on all target feature tables, with fallbacks and timing.

Steps: 1) Determine a stable ordering of entity IDs (for deterministic query gen). 2) Group feature_infos by FEATURE_DATABASE + FEATURE_TABLE to get unique targets. 3) Generate COLLECT STATS statements via generate_collect_stats(...) for fallback use. 4) For each target table: - Try a simple COLLECT STATS ON <db>.<table>. - On failure, retry with generated statements (and optional extension). 5) Log a compact summary (counts + total duration) and return it as a dict.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: entity_id : Mapping[str, Any] | Iterable[str] Entity identifiers used to parameterize collect-stat statements. If a mapping (e.g., dict), its keys are used and sorted. If an iterable (e.g., list/tuple), it’s sorted directly. primary_index : Optional[str] Primary index used by generate_collect_stats (may be None). partitioning : Optional[str] Partitioning clause used by generate_collect_stats (may be None). feature_infos : pd.DataFrame Must contain columns: 'FEATURE_TABLE', 'FEATURE_DATABASE', 'FEATURE_ID'.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Dict[str, Any] Summary with keys: - total_tables: int - ok: int - retried: int - failed: int - duration_seconds: float - duration_hms: str - details: list[dict] # per-table status entries

docs.title: _store_feature_update_insert
docs.text:
_store_feature_update_insert(entity_id, volatile_table_name, entity_null_substitute={}, primary_index=None, partitioning='', features_infos=None, **kwargs)

Stores prepared feature data in specific feature tables within a Teradata database. This function performs updates and inserts on the feature tables based on the provided entity IDs and prepared feature data.

Parameters: - entity_id (dict): A dictionary representing the entity ID, where keys are used to identify the entity. - prepared_features (tdml.DataFrame): A DataFrame containing the prepared feature data for ingestion. - **kwargs: Additional keyword arguments that can include: - schema (str): The schema name where the feature tables are located. - feature_catalog_name (str, optional): The name of the feature catalog table, defaulting to 'FS_FEATURE_CATALOG'.

Returns: None: This function does not return any value but performs database operations.

Note: - The function uses the Teradata temporal feature (VALIDTIME) for handling the valid time of the records. - It constructs SQL queries to update existing feature values and insert new ones based on the entity ID. - The function handles the entity ID, feature IDs, and versions for inserting and updating records. - It utilizes additional settings from the tdfs4ds module for configuring the feature store time and display logs. - This function assumes that the necessary database tables and schema are correctly set up and accessible.

Example Usage:

entity_id_dict = {'customer_id': 'INTEGER'} prepared_features = tdml.DataFrame(...) store_feature(entity_id_dict, prepared_features)

docs.title: prepare_feature_ingestion_tdstone2
docs.text:
prepare_feature_ingestion_tdstone2(df, entity_id)

Prepares a DataFrame for feature ingestion into a tdstone2 feature store by transforming the data structure. This function unpivots the DataFrame and adds necessary columns for entity IDs, feature names, feature values, and a predefined feature version. It creates a volatile table in the database to facilitate this transformation.

  • df (tdml.DataFrame): The input DataFrame containing the feature data. The DataFrame should be structured in a way that is compatible with tdstone2 feature store requirements.
  • entity_id (list/dict/other): A representation of the entity ID. If a dictionary, the keys are used as entity IDs. If a list or another data type, it is used directly as the entity ID.

Returns: - tdml.DataFrame: A transformed DataFrame suitable for feature store ingestion. - str: The name of the volatile table created in the database for storing the transformed data.

Note: - The function requires that the input DataFrame 'df' has a valid table name and is compatible with tdml operations. - It automatically handles the creation and management of a volatile table in the database. - 'ID_PROCESS' is used as the default feature version identifier. - The transformed DataFrame includes columns for each entity ID, 'FEATURE_NAME', 'FEATURE_VALUE', and 'FEATURE_VERSION'.

Example Usage:

input_df = tdml.DataFrame(...) entity_id_dict = {'customer_id': 'INTEGER'} transformed_df, table_name = prepare_feature_ingestion_tdstone2(input_df, entity_id_dict)

docs.title: list_features
docs.text:
list_features()

Retrieves and returns a list of features from a feature store as a DataFrame.

This function constructs a SQL query to fetch all features from a specific catalog view within a schema. The query uses the schema and catalog name configured in the tdfs4ds module. The function executes the query and returns the result as a DataFrame.

Returns:

Type Description

tdml.DataFrame: A DataFrame containing the list of features from the feature store.

Notes
  • The function relies on the tdfs4ds module for configuration, including the SCHEMA and FEATURE_CATALOG_NAME_VIEW settings.
  • The tdml.DataFrame is used to execute the query and retrieve the data.
  • This function does not include temporal logic or filtering. It assumes the default behavior of querying the current valid state of the feature catalog.
  • Cross-store federation (v0.3.1.148): when read-only stores are attached (tdfs4ds.SCHEMA_READONLY), returns a UNION ALL across the primary + read-only stores' feature-catalog views (with a FEATURE_STORE provenance column); otherwise byte-identical to the single-store read.
