docs.title: _is_structured_output_rejection
docs.text:
_is_structured_output_rejection(exc: Exception) -> bool

True when exc looks like the backend refusing our structured-output parameter (guided_json / response_format / format) rather than a genuine model/content error. Covers the 4xx shapes seen from OpenAI-compatible gateways (LiteLLM, Mistral, ...): extra_forbidden / "Extra inputs are not permitted" / "unknown|unrecognized|unsupported field|parameter" naming one of those keys.

A genuine model error (context length, content policy) never names these parameters, so keying on them keeps the one-shot fallback from masking real failures.

docs.title: _build_provider_llm_caller
docs.text:
_build_provider_llm_caller(llm: ChatOpenAI, provider: str, base_schema: Optional[Dict] = None, schema_name: str = 'Output')

Return a callable(messages) -> AIMessage that asks provider for JSON matching base_schema (a plain JSON Schema dict) using that provider's native structured-output mechanism, with a one-shot fallback to an unconstrained call if the backend rejects the structured-output parameter.

This is the single source of truth for how each provider is asked for structured JSON — do not re-branch on provider elsewhere. Mechanisms:

  • vllm -> extra_body={"guided_json": <schema>}
  • openai / azure / mistralai / openai-compatible -> response_format={"type":"json_schema", ...} (strict only for openai/azure — see _STRICT_JSON_SCHEMA_PROVIDERS)
  • ollama -> format=<schema>
  • anything else -> no constraint (prompt-guided JSON only)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: llm : ChatOpenAI The language model interface. provider : str The LLM provider (vllm, openai, azure, mistralai, openai-compatible, ollama, ...). base_schema : dict, optional Plain JSON Schema for the desired output. None -> unconstrained call. schema_name : str Name used inside the OpenAI json_schema wrapper (ignored by other mechanisms).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: callable A function that invokes the LLM with the appropriate structured-output constraint and the one-shot unconstrained fallback.

docs.title: _print_documentation
docs.text:
_print_documentation(documented_sql: str, entity_description: str, documented_entity_columns: Dict[str, str], documented_feature_columns: Dict[str, str], process_id: Optional[str] = None, view_name: Optional[str] = None, explain_analysis: Optional[str] = None, user_score: Optional[int] = None, optimization_score: Optional[int] = None, explain_warnings: Optional[List[str]] = None, explain_recommendations: Optional[List[str]] = None, sql_query: Optional[str] = None, explain_plan: Optional[str] = None, n_steps: Optional[int] = None, n_spool_objects: Optional[int] = None) -> None

Pretty print documentation with context-aware formatting. Uses HTML in notebooks, text format in regular scripts.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: documented_sql : str The query business logic description. entity_description : str The entity description. documented_entity_columns : dict Mapping of entity column names to descriptions. documented_feature_columns : dict Mapping of feature column names to descriptions. process_id : str, optional The process identifier for the title. view_name : str, optional The view name for the title. explain_analysis : str, optional The EXPLAIN plan analysis description. optimization_score : int, optional Optimization score from 1 to 5. explain_warnings : list, optional List of warnings from EXPLAIN analysis. explain_recommendations : list, optional List of recommendations from EXPLAIN analysis. sql_query : str, optional The original SQL query to display. explain_plan : str, optional The raw EXPLAIN plan output to display.

docs.title: build_llm
docs.text:
build_llm(llm_service: str = None, api_key: str = None, model_id: str = None, temperature: float = 0.0, timeout: int = 120, provider: str = None)

Build and return a chat LLM client based on the configured provider.

When called with no arguments, reads from tdfs4ds global configuration: - INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER (provider) - INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL (llm_service) - INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY (api_key) - INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL (model_id) - INSTRUCT_MODEL_REGION (AWS region for Bedrock) - INSTRUCT_MODEL_AZURE_DEPLOYMENT, INSTRUCT_MODEL_AZURE_API_VERSION (Azure) - INSTRUCT_MODEL_GCP_PROJECT, INSTRUCT_MODEL_GCP_LOCATION (GCP)

Injects tdfs4ds config variables into environment so that boto3, Azure, and Google Cloud clients pick up credentials and endpoints automatically.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: llm_service : str, optional Base URL of the LLM service (overrides INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL). api_key : str, optional API key for authentication (overrides INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY). model_id : str, optional Model identifier (overrides INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL). temperature : float Sampling temperature for response diversity. timeout : int Request timeout in seconds. provider : str, optional LLM provider name (overrides INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: BaseChatModel Configured LLM client (ChatOpenAI, AzureChatOpenAI, ChatBedrockConverse, ChatVertexAI, etc.).

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: Exception If LLM client creation fails or provider is not supported.

docs.title: build_documentation_json_schema
docs.text:
build_documentation_json_schema(columns: List[str], provider: str = 'generic') -> Dict[str, Any]

Build a provider-appropriate JSON Schema used to enforce strict JSON output for SQL column documentation across multiple LLM backends.

This function returns different schema shapes depending on the LLM provider, because each ecosystem uses a different structured-output mechanism:

docs.title: Provider Modes
docs.text:
• provider="openai", "azure" Returns the JSON Schema wrapped in OpenAI's response_format={"type": "json_schema", ...} structure. Supported by GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and Azure OpenAI.

  • provider="anthropic", "claude" Returns an Anthropic tool schema definition. Claude 3.x models use tool schemas to enforce strict JSON output.

  • provider="ollama" Returns the raw JSON schema that Ollama expects under the format= parameter of the generate API. (Ollama 0.2+ supports response schemas.)

  • provider="vllm" Returns plain JSON Schema for use with vLLM's guided_json constrained decoding.

  • provider="bedrock" Bedrock Claude follows the Anthropic tool schema format. Bedrock Llama / Titan accept plain JSON schema. This function returns the base schema and leaves the final wrapping to the caller.

  • provider="generic" Returns plain JSON schema. Useful for LLM backends that do not support constrained decoding, prompt-only JSON generation, or post-processing repair.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
columns : list[str] Column names to include as required JSON object keys. Each column will map to a string description generated by the model.

str, optional

The model provider or backend type. Determines the structural format required for constrained generation. One of: "openai", "anthropic", "ollama", "vllm", "bedrock", "generic".

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict A dictionary representing the JSON Schema or provider-specific wrapper used to enforce strict JSON output during LLM generation.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• All schemas require that:

    • the output be a JSON object
    • keys match exactly the column names
    • all values be strings
    • additional properties be disallowed

  • Not all providers enforce schemas equally:

    • OpenAI, Claude, and vLLM offer hard guarantees.
    • Ollama enforces schema reasonably well.
    • Generic models may require post-processing.

docs.title: build_sql_documentation_chain
docs.text:
build_sql_documentation_chain(llm: ChatOpenAI, entity_columns: Sequence[str], feature_columns: Sequence[str], provider: str = 'vllm', json_constraint: bool = True, prompt_only: bool = False) -> Runnable

Build a LangChain Runnable that generates business-focused documentation
for lists of entity and feature columns from a SQL query output, with optional provider-specific JSON
constraints (vLLM, OpenAI, Ollama, etc.).

The resulting chain expects two input variables:
    - sql_query: str         → the SQL query whose output is being documented
    - columns_str: str       → formatted list of entity and feature columns (e.g. "Entity columns:
  • col1

Feature columns: - col2")

Parameters
----------
llm : ChatOpenAI
    The language model interface (may point to vLLM, OpenAI, Ollama, etc.).
entity_columns : Sequence[str]
    List of entity/identifier columns that must appear as keys in the output JSON.
feature_columns : Sequence[str]
    List of feature columns that must appear as keys in the output JSON.
provider : str, optional (default="vllm")
    Indicates which structured-output mechanism to use.
    Supported values:
        - "vllm"               → uses `guided_json` for strict JSON output
        - "openai" / "azure"   → uses OpenAI JSON Schema via `response_format`
        - "mistralai" / "openai-compatible"
                               → OpenAI JSON Schema via `response_format` (NOT
                                 vLLM `guided_json`; that is a vLLM-only extension)
        - "ollama"             → uses Ollama's `format=` schema
        - any other value      → fall back to unconstrained text output
json_constraint : bool, optional (default=True)
    If True:
        - a JSON Schema is generated from the column lists
        - provider-specific constrained decoding is applied
    If False:
        - the chain does not enforce JSON structure at the LLM level
        - the model is only guided by the prompt (weaker guarantees)
prompt_only : bool, optional (default=False)
    If True:
        - returns only the prompt template, without attaching the LLM or parser
        - useful for debugging, testing, or customizing the prompt before execution
    If False:
        - returns the full chain: prompt → LLM (optionally schema-guided) → JSON parser

Returns
-------
Runnable
    If prompt_only=False:
        A LangChain Runnable that executes:
            prompt → LLM (optionally schema-guided) → JSON parser
        When invoked with:
            {
                "sql_query": "...",
                "columns_str": "Entity columns:
  • column1

Feature columns: - column2 ..." } It returns: dict[str, str] A mapping of each requested column name to a short, business-oriented description (≤ 5 sentences), plus a 'query_business_logic' key containing a high-level description of the query's business logic (5-10 sentences), and an 'entity_description' key with a holistic description of the entity (3-5 sentences). If prompt_only=True: The prompt template itself, for inspection or further customization.

Notes
-----
- The chain enforces valid JSON when possible:
    * vLLM → `guided_json`
    * OpenAI → `response_format={"type": "json_schema", ...}`
    * Ollama → `format=<schema>`
- For unsupported providers, the model may emit imperfect JSON.
- Descriptions focus on business meaning, business logic,
  and optionally technical details only when relevant.

docs.title: run_sql_documentation
docs.text:
run_sql_documentation(chain: Runnable, sql_query: str, entity_columns: Sequence[str], feature_columns: Sequence[str], language: str = 'English', upstream_context: str = '') -> Dict[str, str]

Execute a previously constructed SQL-documentation chain and return business-friendly documentation for the specified SQL output columns.

This function prepares the chain inputs (SQL query, formatted column list, target language) and invokes the chain. The chain itself must have been created using build_sql_documentation_chain(), which ensures the model produces structured JSON suitable for parsing.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
chain : Runnable A LangChain Runnable returned by build_sql_documentation_chain(). This Runnable encapsulates: - the prompt template - a provider-specific LLM invocation (with or without JSON constraints) - a JSON output parser

str

The SQL query whose resulting columns should be documented. This query is shown to the model so it can infer business logic, derivation rules, and column meaning.

Sequence[str]

The list of entity/identifier column names that must appear as keys in the output JSON. Only these columns will be documented. The order does not matter.

Sequence[str]

The list of feature column names that must appear as keys in the output JSON. Only these columns will be documented. The order does not matter.

str, optional (default="English")

The target output language for the generated documentation. This value is passed into the prompt’s {language} variable. Examples: "English", "French", "German", "Spanish", "Japanese".

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, str] A dictionary mapping each column name to a human-readable, business-oriented description generated by the model, plus a 'query_business_logic' key with the query's business logic description, and an 'entity_description' key with the holistic entity description. Example: { "customer_id": "Unique customer identifier used for ...", "order_date": "Business date when the order was created ...", "entity_description": "The customer entity represents...", "query_business_logic": "This query provides a view of ..." }

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The output format is determined by the chain's JSON parser. If the model fails to produce valid JSON (e.g., due to unsupported constraints), a OutputParserException may be raised.
  • The resulting descriptions are typically ≤ 5 sentences per column, unless modified in the chain's prompt.

docs.title: detect_score_inversion
docs.text:
detect_score_inversion(sql: str) -> bool

Return True when the SQL inverts a score, i.e. contains a 1 - (...) / 1.0 - X transform (so a lower output value means higher risk).

Single source of truth for the inversion signal, shared by document-time hint generation (:func:_extract_interpretation_hints) and the agent's runtime DEFINITION/EXPLAIN reconciliation guard.

docs.title: _extract_interpretation_hints
docs.text:
_extract_interpretation_hints(sql: str) -> dict

Scan the full SQL text for signals that constrain how column descriptions must read.

Operates on the entire SQL string (including WHERE, GROUP BY, subqueries, CTEs) so it catches patterns that a single-level SELECT AST walk would miss.

Returns a dict of boolean flags. All-False means plain arithmetic — no extra constraints are added to the LLM prompt.

docs.title: _hints_to_context_section
docs.text:
_hints_to_context_section(hints: dict) -> str

Convert interpretation hints into a context snippet appended to upstream_context.

Returns an empty string when all hints are False (no constraints needed).

docs.title: _find_hint_violations
docs.text:
_find_hint_violations(feature_docs: dict, hints: dict) -> list

Check feature descriptions against interpretation hints.

Returns a list of (column_name, violation_reason) tuples for any description that contradicts a hint. Empty list when all descriptions are consistent.

docs.title: document_sql_query_columns
docs.text:
document_sql_query_columns(sql_query: str, entity_columns: Sequence[str], feature_columns: Sequence[str], language: str = 'English', provider: Optional[str] = None, json_constraint: bool = True, prompt_only: bool = False, upstream_context: str = '') -> Dict[str, Any]

Convenience function to generate business-focused documentation for SQL query output columns using the configured instruction model from tdfs4ds settings.

This function automatically builds the LLM client using the tdfs4ds configuration variables (INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL, INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY, INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL), constructs the documentation chain, and executes it to produce column descriptions.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
sql_query : str The SQL query whose resulting columns should be documented. This query is shown to the model so it can infer business logic, derivation rules, and column meaning.

Sequence[str]

The list of entity/identifier column names that must appear as keys in the output JSON. Only these columns will be documented. The order does not matter.

Sequence[str]

The list of feature column names that must appear as keys in the output JSON. Only these columns will be documented. The order does not matter.

str, optional (default="English")

The target output language for the generated documentation. This value is passed into the prompt's {language} variable. Examples: "English", "French", "German", "Spanish", "Japanese".

str, optional (default=None)

Indicates which structured-output mechanism to use for the LLM. If None, uses INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER from tdfs4ds config. Supported values: - "vllm" → uses guided_json for strict JSON output - "openai" / "azure" → uses OpenAI JSON Schema via response_format - "mistralai" / "openai-compatible" → OpenAI JSON Schema via response_format (NOT vLLM guided_json; that is a vLLM-only extension) - "ollama" → uses Ollama's format= schema - any other value → fall back to unconstrained text output

bool, optional (default=True)

If True: - a JSON Schema is generated from the column lists - provider-specific constrained decoding is applied If False: - the chain does not enforce JSON structure at the LLM level - the model is only guided by the prompt (weaker guarantees)

bool, optional (default=False)

If True: - returns only the prompt template, without executing the chain - useful for debugging, testing, or customizing the prompt before execution If False: - executes the full chain and returns structured documentation

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict If prompt_only=False: A dictionary with four keys: - "query_business_logic": str containing the high-level business logic description of the query - "entity_description": str containing the holistic description of the entity - "entity_columns": dict[str, str] mapping each entity column name to its description - "feature_columns": dict[str, str] mapping each feature column name to its description If prompt_only=True: The prompt template itself, for inspection or further customization.

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: ValueError If any of the required tdfs4ds configuration variables (INSTRUCT_MODEL_URL, INSTRUCT_MODEL_API_KEY, INSTRUCT_MODEL_MODEL, INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER) are not set.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This function requires that the tdfs4ds instruction model configuration is properly set.
  • The resulting descriptions are typically ≤ 5 sentences per column, focusing on business meaning and logic.
  • If the model fails to produce valid JSON, an exception will be raised.

docs.title: _classify_you_items
docs.text:
_classify_you_items(you_items: list[str]) -> list[dict]

Classify each [You] item by semantic category using a single LLM call.

Returns a list of dicts {"item": ..., "category": ...} in the same order as the input. Falls back to "OTHER" for every item when the LLM is not available or the call fails.

docs.title: Categories
docs.text: UNCORRELATED_SCALAR_SUBQUERY Flags an uncorrelated scalar subquery (e.g. SELECT AVG(col) FROM t) as causing redundant scans or per-row evaluation. Teradata evaluates these once — this is a false positive. CROSS_JOIN_SINGLE_ROW Flags a CROSS JOIN to a single-row aggregate result as a Cartesian product. 1 x N = N rows, not a real performance problem. INFORMATIONAL The item explicitly states no action is needed, the query is already correct, or contradicts itself (e.g. "Teradata evaluates it once"). PRODUCT_JOIN Real Cartesian product between multi-row tables. REDUNDANT_SCAN A table is scanned multiple times for a non-scalar reason (e.g. self-join). PI_MISMATCH Join on non-primary-index columns forcing data redistribution. TYPE_CAST Implicit type conversion in join or WHERE predicates. OTHER Any issue that does not match the above categories.

docs.title: _compute_user_score_from_you_items
docs.text:
_compute_user_score_from_you_items(warnings: list, recommendations: list, explain_text: str = None, classify: bool = True) -> int

Derive user_score deterministically from [You]-prefixed items.

The LLM identifies issues reliably (factual observations) but assigns numeric scores inconsistently — the same SQL can score 3/5 or 4/5 depending on prompt context. This function replaces the LLM-assigned score with a deterministic mapping based on the number and severity of [You] items the LLM identified:

=== ============================================ 5 No [You] items — no SQL-level issues found 4 1 distinct issue 3 2–3 distinct issues 2 4+ issues or significant structural flaw 1 Critical bug (Cartesian product, wrong results) === ============================================

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
classify : bool, default True When True, runs the LLM semantic classification to filter false-positive [You] items. Set to False during intermediate scoring (e.g. the simplification baseline) where every [You] item should count so the optimizer has the raw signal to drive structural improvements.

Scoring pipeline:

  1. Extract [You]-prefixed warnings and recommendations.
  2. LLM semantic classification (when classify=True) — each item is classified into a category via a single LLM call. Items in false-positive categories are filtered out. Falls back to regex-based filtering when the LLM is unavailable. Skipped entirely when classify=False.
  3. EXPLAIN validation (when explain_text is provided) — items that claim an issue not present in the raw EXPLAIN are filtered.
  4. Severity classification + count mapping — deterministic 1–5 score.

docs.title: _parse_explain_metrics
docs.text:
_parse_explain_metrics(explain_text: str) -> dict

Extract deterministic execution metrics from raw Teradata EXPLAIN text.

Returns a dict with: - n_steps: number of numbered execution steps (e.g. 1) First, ...) - n_spool_objects: count of distinct Spool N references

docs.title: _parse_explain_signals
docs.text:
_parse_explain_signals(explain_text: str) -> dict

Extract concrete execution signals from raw Teradata EXPLAIN text.

Returns a dict of ground-truth signals that can be used to validate LLM-generated [You] items against what the EXPLAIN actually shows.

Keys: - has_product_join: True if the EXPLAIN contains a real product join - table_scan_counts: {TABLE_NAME: n_scans} — how many data-access steps read from each table (Spool reads excluded) - has_duplicate_scans: True if any table is scanned more than once - has_redistribution: True if any step redistributes data by hash

docs.title: build_explain_documentation_chain
docs.text:
build_explain_documentation_chain(llm: ChatOpenAI, provider: str = 'vllm', json_constraint: bool = True, prompt_only: bool = False) -> Runnable

Build a LangChain Runnable that analyzes SQL EXPLAIN plans and generates optimization scores, warnings, and recommendations.

The resulting chain expects two input variables
  • sql_query: str → the original SQL query
  • explain_plan: str → the EXPLAIN output from the database

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: llm : ChatOpenAI The language model interface (may point to vLLM, OpenAI, Ollama, etc.). provider : str, optional (default="vllm") Indicates which structured-output mechanism to use. Supported values: - "vllm" → uses guided_json for strict JSON output - "openai" / "azure" → uses OpenAI JSON Schema via response_format - "mistralai" / "openai-compatible" → OpenAI JSON Schema via response_format (NOT vLLM guided_json; that is a vLLM-only extension) - "ollama" → uses Ollama's format= schema - any other value → fall back to unconstrained text output json_constraint : bool, optional (default=True) If True: a JSON Schema is generated and provider-specific constrained decoding is applied. If False: the chain does not enforce JSON structure at the LLM level.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
Runnable A LangChain Runnable that executes: prompt → LLM (optionally schema-guided) → JSON parser

When invoked with:
    {
        "sql_query": "SELECT ...",
        "explain_plan": "..."
    }

It returns:
    dict with keys:
        - "explanation": str describing the EXPLAIN plan in business terms
        - "optimization_score": int from 1 (poorly optimized) to 5 (well optimized)
        - "warnings": list[str] of potential issues or concerns
        - "recommendations": list[str] of actionable optimization suggestions

docs.title: run_explain_documentation
docs.text:
run_explain_documentation(chain: Runnable, sql_query: str, explain_plan: str, classify: bool = True) -> Dict[str, Any]

Execute an EXPLAIN-documentation chain and return optimization analysis.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: chain : Runnable A LangChain Runnable returned by build_explain_documentation_chain(). sql_query : str The original SQL query. explain_plan : str The EXPLAIN output from the database. classify : bool, default True Pass through to _compute_user_score_from_you_items. Set to False during intermediate scoring (simplification, candidate evaluation) to keep the raw [You] count and avoid filtering useful structural signals.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict A dictionary with keys: "explanation", "optimization_score", "warnings", "recommendations"

docs.title: document_sql_query_explain
docs.text:
document_sql_query_explain(sql_query: str, provider: Optional[str] = None, json_constraint: bool = True, prompt_only: bool = False, classify: bool = True) -> Dict[str, Any]

Analyze a SQL query's EXPLAIN plan and return optimization recommendations.

This function automatically builds the LLM client using tdfs4ds configuration, constructs the EXPLAIN analysis chain, and executes it.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: sql_query : str The original SQL query. explain_plan : str The EXPLAIN output from the database. provider : str, optional (default=None) Indicates which structured-output mechanism to use for the LLM. If None, uses INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER from tdfs4ds config. Supported values: "vllm", "openai", "azure", "ollama", etc. json_constraint : bool, optional (default=True) If True: use provider-specific constrained decoding. If False: rely on prompt guidance only.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict A dictionary with keys: - "explanation": str describing the EXPLAIN plan - "optimization_score": int from 1 to 5 - "warnings": list[str] of potential issues - "recommendations": list[str] of actionable suggestions

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: ValueError If tdfs4ds instruction model configuration is incomplete.

docs.title: documentation_tables_creation
docs.text:
documentation_tables_creation()

Create the necessary documentation tables in the database if they do not already exist. tdml: The tdfs4ds TDML connection object.

docs.title: documentation_business_dictionary_view_creation
docs.text:
documentation_business_dictionary_view_creation()

(Re)create the CURRENT VALIDTIME + LOCKING ROW FOR ACCESS views over the three business dictionary tables — used by the Teradata Enterprise Vector Store backend (vector_store_teradata.py) so a permanent teradataml.DataFrame can be passed directly to from_datasets()/add_datasets(), instead of routing through from_documents()/add_documents()'s Document-conversion path, whose internal staging call (DataFrame.from_dict(persist=True)) has no database argument and may materialize as a volatile table invisible to the Vector Store's separate REST session. REPLACE VIEW keeps this idempotent across re-runs, so it is safe (and intended) to call unconditionally on every connect()/setup(), mirroring feature_store_catalog_view_creation()/process_store_catalog_view_creation() — this avoids depending on a table-existence probe to decide whether the views need (re)creating.

ID mirrors the doc-id convention in agent/vector_index.py's _objects_to_docs / _sections_to_docs / _columns_to_docs (f"{db}__{obj}" etc.) so switching between the Chroma and Teradata backends keeps stable, comparable identifiers. The blank/NULL-description filter mirrors those functions' if not text.strip(): continue guard, pushed into SQL so the view itself excludes rows not worth embedding.

docs.title: migrate_documentation_explain_schema
docs.text:
migrate_documentation_explain_schema()

Ensure the FS_PROCESS_DOCUMENTATION_EXPLAIN table has all expected columns.

Check-first: inspects the live DDL and only runs ALTER TABLE for columns that are actually missing. No-op (and no log at INFO) when already up-to-date.

Columns added over time: - USER_SCORE (v0.2.x) — deterministic score from [You] items - N_STEPS (v0.2.9) — EXPLAIN step count - N_SPOOL_OBJECTS (v0.2.9) — distinct Spool object count

New columns start as NULL for existing rows and are filled on the next document_process() or document_process_incremental() run.

docs.title: migrate_documentation_columns_schema
docs.text:
migrate_documentation_columns_schema()

Ensure FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS has the interpretation-contract columns.

Check-first: inspects live DDL and only runs ALTER TABLE for columns that are actually missing. No-op (and no log at INFO) when already up-to-date.

Columns added over time: - MONOTONIC_DIRECTION (v0.3.1.35) — direction hint derived from formula - VALUE_DOMAIN (v0.3.1.35) — value range hint - IS_ABSOLUTE_VALUE (v0.3.1.35) — True when ABS() detected in formula - FORMULA_CONSISTENCY_WARNING (v0.3.1.35) — set when LLM violated hint after retry

New columns start as NULL for existing rows.

docs.title: document_process
docs.text:
document_process(process_id: str, language: str = 'English', json_constraint: bool = True, show_sql_query: bool = False, show_explain_plan: bool = False, display: bool = True, upload: bool = True, prompt_only=False) -> Optional[Dict[str, Any]]

Generate and store documentation for a data process identified by process_id. This function retrieves the SQL query and output columns for the process, generates business-focused documentation using an LLM, and stores the results in the appropriate documentation tables.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
process_id : str The unique identifier of the data process to document.

str, optional (default="English")

The target output language for the generated documentation. This value is passed into the prompt’s {language} variable. Examples: "English", "French", "German", "Spanish", "Japanese".

provider : str, optional (default=None) Indicates which structured-output mechanism to use for the LLM. If None, uses INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER from tdfs4ds config. Supported values: - "vllm" → uses guided_json for strict JSON output - "openai" / "azure" → uses OpenAI JSON Schema via response_format - "mistralai" / "openai-compatible" → OpenAI JSON Schema via response_format (NOT vLLM guided_json; that is a vLLM-only extension) - "ollama" → uses Ollama's format= schema - any other value → fall back to unconstrained text output json_constraint : bool, optional (default=True) If True: - a JSON Schema is generated from the column list - provider-specific constrained decoding is applied If False: - the chain does not enforce JSON structure at the LLM level - the model is only guided by the prompt (weaker guarantees) show_sql_query : bool, optional (default=False) If True, display the original SQL query at the end of the documentation report. show_explain_plan : bool, optional (default=False) If True, display the raw EXPLAIN plan output at the end of the documentation report. display : bool, optional (default=True) If True, print the generated documentation to the console. upload_documentation : bool, optional (default=True) If True, upload the generated documentation to the documentation tables.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
dict or None A dictionary containing the generated documentation and analysis, or None if an error occurred. The dictionary includes keys: - PROCESS_ID - DOCUMENTED_SQL - ENTITY_DESCRIPTION - DOCUMENTED_ENTITY_COLUMNS - DOCUMENTED_FEATURE_COLUMNS - EXPLAIN_ANALYSIS (if show_explain_plan is True) - OPTIMIZATION_SCORE (if show_explain_plan is True) - EXPLAIN_WARNINGS (if show_explain_plan is True) - EXPLAIN_RECOMMENDATIONS (if show_explain_plan is True) - RAW_EXPLAIN_PLAN (if show_explain_plan is True) Notes

  • This function requires that the tdfs4ds instruction model configuration is properly set.
  • If the model fails to produce valid JSON, an exception will be raised.
  • The resulting descriptions are typically ≤ 5 sentences per column, focusing on business meaning and logic.

docs.title: _sanitize_for_teradata
docs.text:
_sanitize_for_teradata(text: str) -> str

Replace characters outside Teradata LATIN (ISO-8859-1) character set.

LLM-generated text often contains Unicode characters (smart quotes, em dashes, ellipsis, etc.) that trigger Teradata Error 6706 when inserted into LATIN-charset columns. This function replaces the most common offenders with ASCII equivalents, then encodes the remainder as ISO-8859-1, substituting any still-unmappable code points with '?'.

docs.title: feed_process_info_with_prompt_result
docs.text:
feed_process_info_with_prompt_result(process_info, sql_documentation_response=None, sql_explain_response=None, display_info=True, upload_info=True)

Enriches a process_info dictionary with SQL documentation and EXPLAIN plan analysis results, with options to display the results and upload the enriched information.

This function integrates the results of SQL documentation and EXPLAIN plan analysis into the provided process_info dictionary. It extracts and organizes documentation for entity and feature columns, as well as optimization insights, to provide a comprehensive view of the SQL query's business logic, performance, and potential improvements. It also supports optional display of the enriched information and automatic upload of the documentation and EXPLAIN analysis to a backend system.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default process_info dict

A dictionary containing metadata about the SQL process, including: - 'ENTITY_COLUMNS': List of columns representing the entity in the SQL query. - 'FEATURE_COLUMNS': List of columns representing features in the SQL query.

required sql_documentation_response dict

A dictionary containing SQL documentation results, including descriptions for entity/feature columns and query business logic. Expected keys: - 'query_business_logic': Description of the query's purpose and logic. - 'entity_description': Description of the entity represented by the query. - Column names as keys, with their descriptions as values.

None sql_explain_response dict

A dictionary containing SQL EXPLAIN plan analysis results, including: - 'explanation': Detailed analysis of the EXPLAIN plan. - 'optimization_score': Integer score (1-5) indicating query optimization level. - 'warnings': List of potential issues identified in the EXPLAIN plan. - 'recommendations': List of actionable recommendations for query optimization.

None display_info bool

If True, displays the enriched process_info using display_process_info. Defaults to True.

True upload_info bool

If True, uploads the enriched documentation and EXPLAIN analysis to a backend system. Defaults to True.

True

Returns:

Name Type Description dict

The enriched process_info dictionary with the following additional keys (if input responses are provided): - 'DOCUMENTED_SQL': Business logic description of the SQL query. - 'ENTITY_DESCRIPTION': Description of the entity represented by the query. - 'DOCUMENTED_ENTITY_COLUMNS': Dictionary of documented entity columns and their descriptions. - 'DOCUMENTED_FEATURE_COLUMNS': Dictionary of documented feature columns and their descriptions. - 'EXPLAIN_ANALYSIS': Analysis of the EXPLAIN plan. - 'OPTIMIZATION_SCORE': Optimization score (1-5) for the query. - 'EXPLAIN_WARNINGS': List of warnings from the EXPLAIN plan analysis. - 'EXPLAIN_RECOMMENDATIONS': List of optimization recommendations.

Example

process_info = { ... 'ENTITY_COLUMNS': ['customer_id', 'order_id'], ... 'FEATURE_COLUMNS': ['order_amount', 'order_date'] ... } sql_documentation_response = { ... 'query_business_logic': 'This query joins customer and order data...', ... 'entity_description': 'The customer entity represents...', ... 'customer_id': 'Unique identifier for customers.', ... 'order_amount': 'Total amount of the order.' ... } sql_explain_response = { ... 'explanation': 'The EXPLAIN plan shows a nested loop join...', ... 'optimization_score': 3, ... 'warnings': ['Full table scan on orders table'], ... 'recommendations': ['Add index on orders.customer_id'] ... } enriched_info = feed_process_info_with_prompt_result( ... process_info, ... sql_documentation_response, ... sql_explain_response, ... display_info=True, ... upload_info=True ... ) print(enriched_info.keys()) ['ENTITY_COLUMNS', 'FEATURE_COLUMNS', 'DOCUMENTED_SQL', 'ENTITY_DESCRIPTION', 'DOCUMENTED_ENTITY_COLUMNS', 'DOCUMENTED_FEATURE_COLUMNS', 'EXPLAIN_ANALYSIS', 'OPTIMIZATION_SCORE', 'EXPLAIN_WARNINGS', 'EXPLAIN_RECOMMENDATIONS']

docs.title: upload_business_dictionary_objects
docs.text:
upload_business_dictionary_objects(df: DataFrame) -> None

Insert or update object-level descriptions in the business dictionary.

The DataFrame must contain the following columns
  • DATABASE_NAME : str — database (or schema) that owns the object
  • OBJECT_NAME : str — table or view name
  • OBJECT_TYPE : str — 'T' for table, 'V' for view
  • BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION: str — business-oriented description of the object

Rows are merged into FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS using CURRENT VALIDTIME semantics (Teradata temporal). Existing rows matched on (DATABASE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME) are updated; new rows are inserted.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: df : pd.DataFrame DataFrame with columns DATABASE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME, OBJECT_TYPE, BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION.

Insert or update column-level descriptions in the business dictionary.

The DataFrame must contain the following columns

  • TABLE_NAME : str — table or view name
  • COLUMN_NAME : str — column name
  • BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION: str — business-oriented description of the column

Rows are merged into FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS using CURRENT VALIDTIME semantics (Teradata temporal). Existing rows matched on (DATABASE_NAME, TABLE_NAME, COLUMN_NAME) are updated; new rows are inserted.

docs.title: Parameters

Insert or update section-level descriptions in the business dictionary.

The DataFrame must contain the following columns

  • OBJECT_NAME : str — object name (table, view, dataset)
  • SECTION_NAME : str — section identifier (e.g. 'OVERVIEW', 'FEATURE_THEMES')
  • SECTION_CONTENT: str — section text content

Rows are merged into FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS using CURRENT VALIDTIME semantics. Existing rows matched on (DATABASE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME, SECTION_NAME) are updated; new rows are inserted.

docs.title: delete_process_documentation
docs.text:
delete_process_documentation(process_id: str) -> None

Remove all documentation rows for a process from every documentation table.

Deletes from: - FS_PROCESS_DOCUMENTATION_BUSINESS_LOGIC (keyed by PROCESS_ID) - FS_PROCESS_DOCUMENTATION_FEATURES (keyed by PROCESS_ID) - FS_PROCESS_DOCUMENTATION_EXPLAIN (keyed by PROCESS_ID) - FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS (keyed by the process VIEW_NAME) - FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS (keyed by the process VIEW_NAME) - FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS (keyed by the process VIEW_NAME)

Uses NONSEQUENCED VALIDTIME DELETE to fully remove all temporal versions, mirroring the approach used by remove_process for the process catalog.

Each step is independent and non-fatal: a failure on one table is logged as a warning and the remaining tables are still cleaned up.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: process_id : str UUID of the process whose documentation should be removed.

docs.title: delete_business_dictionary_objects

When omitted, all databases are affected (useful to clean up stale entries that were uploaded under a wrong database name).

docs.title: remove_object_documentation
docs.text:
remove_object_documentation(object_name: str, database_name: Optional[str] = None) -> None

Remove all business dictionary documentation for a given object in one call.

Deletes all currently-valid rows keyed on object_name from:

  • FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS — object-level description
  • FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS — structured sections (OVERVIEW, ENTITY, …)
  • FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS — column / feature descriptions

Designed to be called alongside DROP VIEW / DROP TABLE for any documented object: dataset views, process views, source tables, etc. Failures on individual tables are non-fatal warnings so a partial cleanup does not abort the caller.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: object_name : str The OBJECT_NAME / TABLE_NAME to remove (leading/trailing quotes are stripped automatically). database_name : str, optional Restrict deletion to this database. When omitted, all databases are affected (useful when the database is unknown or may vary).

docs.title: _document_intermediate_view
docs.text:
_document_intermediate_view(node_name: str, node_meta: Dict[str, Any], graph: Dict[str, Any], language: str = 'English', entity_columns: List[str] = []) -> None

Generate and store documentation for a non-feature-store intermediate view, using its direct children's already-resolved context as upstream anchors.

docs.title: document_process_incremental
docs.text:
document_process_incremental(process_id: str, language: str = 'English', json_constraint: bool = True, show_sql_query: bool = False, show_explain_plan: bool = False, display: bool = True, upload: bool = True, force_update: bool = False, _visited_processes: Optional[set] = None, _visited_views: Optional[set] = None) -> Optional[Dict[str, Any]]

Generate documentation for a process incrementally and bottom-up.

Traverses the full dependency graph of the process SQL. At each level: - Internal feature store tables (in FS_FEATURE_CATALOG or _HIDDEN suffix) are silently skipped. - Base tables with no business dictionary entry trigger a warning; the process continues best-effort without their context. - Regular intermediate views with no documentation are automatically documented (their own sources resolved first before proceeding upward). - Feature store views (type 'F' in FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS, or detected via FS_V_PROCESS_CATALOG) are treated as leaves; if undocumented, a warning is emitted and they are omitted from context.

The current process is documented last, using the fully resolved upstream context from its direct source objects as ground truth anchors for the LLM.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: process_id : str UUID of the process to document. language : str Output language for LLM-generated descriptions. json_constraint : bool Whether to enforce JSON schema in the LLM output. show_sql_query : bool Include original SQL in the display output. show_explain_plan : bool Include raw EXPLAIN plan in the display output. display : bool Whether to print/render the documentation. upload : bool Whether to persist the documentation to the catalog tables. force_update : bool If True, regenerate documentation even for already-documented nodes. _visited_processes : set, optional Internal recursion guard — do not pass manually. _visited_views : set, optional Internal recursion guard — do not pass manually.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict or None Same structure as document_process().

docs.title: cascade_redocumentation
docs.text:
cascade_redocumentation(process_id: str, language: str = 'English') -> List[str]

Re-document every downstream process/dataset that depends on process_id.

Walks the persisted lineage graph (FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH) in-memory via networkx to find the descendants of the process's view, resolves each descendant object to a registered process or dataset, and re-runs document_process_incremental / document_dataset_incremental with force_update=True. A single shared _visited_processes / _visited_views set is threaded across all descendant calls so shared upstream views are documented at most once (avoiding an LLM-cost explosion).

Intended to be called only when the parent's documentation actually changed (the _doc_changed gate in upload_features). Objects in the descendant path that are not registered processes/datasets (plain intermediate views) are skipped as documentation targets — they are re-documented as a side effect of their owning process's walk.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: process_id : str The process whose documentation just changed. language : str Output language passed through to the re-documentation calls.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[str] Identifiers (process_id / dataset_id) that were re-documented.

docs.title: _extract_feature_process_map_from_ddl
docs.text:
_extract_feature_process_map_from_ddl(ddl: str) -> Dict[str, str]

Parse a dataset view DDL and return a mapping of short feature name (upper) to PROCESS_ID.

Dataset column aliases use a qualified format to disambiguate the same feature appearing from multiple processes: B1.FEATURE_VALUE AS {short_name}__{database}___{view_name}_

This function strips the qualifier to return the short business name so it matches what retrieve_documentation() returns from the feature catalog. When the same short name appears in multiple process subqueries, the first encountered process wins (any documented process provides the same description).

docs.title: _get_dataset_info
docs.text:
_get_dataset_info(dataset_id: str) -> Optional[Dict[str, Any]]

Resolve a dataset_id to its metadata: name, database, DDL, entity columns, and feature-to-process_id mapping.

Returns None if the dataset is not found in the catalog.

docs.title: _propagate_docs_from_processes
docs.text:
_propagate_docs_from_processes(feature_process_map: Dict[str, str], entity_columns: List[str]) -> Dict[str, Any]

Propagate feature and entity documentation from process docs.

For each feature, retrieves the process documentation and copies the matching feature description. Also collects entity descriptions and process-level business logic descriptions.

Returns a dict with keys
  • documented_feature_columns: {feature_name: description}
  • documented_entity_columns: {entity_col: description}
  • entity_description: str
  • process_descriptions: {process_id: business_logic_description}
  • missing_features: [feature_names with no doc found]

docs.title: _generate_dataset_description
docs.text:
_generate_dataset_description(dataset_name: str, entity_description: str, entity_column_docs: Dict[str, str], feature_docs: Dict[str, str], process_descriptions: Dict[str, str], language: str = 'English') -> Dict[str, str]

Generate a structured business description for a dataset using a single LLM call with JSON-constrained output.

Returns a dict with one key per section, each independently storable as a RAG document in FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS:

  • OVERVIEW — what the dataset represents, at what grain, primary purpose
  • ENTITY — business meaning of each entity column
  • FEATURE_THEMES — features grouped by logical themes
  • BUSINESS_QUESTIONS — 3-5 concrete questions the dataset answers
  • INTENDED_AUDIENCE — who benefits and in what workflows

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, str] Mapping of section name to section content. Empty dict on LLM failure.

docs.title: _store_dataset_doc
docs.text:
_store_dataset_doc(db: str, obj: str, sections: Dict[str, str], column_docs: Dict[str, str]) -> None

Persist generated documentation for a dataset to the business dictionary.

Stores: - Object-level entry in FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS (type 'D') with the OVERVIEW section as BUSINESS_DESCRIPTION. - One row per section in FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS. - One row per column in FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS.

docs.title: _print_dataset_documentation
docs.text:
_print_dataset_documentation(dataset_name: str, dataset_database: str, dataset_id: str, sections: Dict[str, str], documented_entity_columns: Dict[str, str], documented_feature_columns: Dict[str, str]) -> None

Pretty-print dataset documentation with context-aware formatting.

docs.title: document_dataset_incremental
docs.text:
document_dataset_incremental(dataset_id: str, language: str = 'English', display: bool = True, upload: bool = True, force_update: bool = False, _visited_processes: Optional[Set[str]] = None, _visited_views: Optional[Set[str]] = None) -> Optional[Dict[str, Any]]

Generate documentation for a dataset incrementally and bottom-up.

Walks the full lineage graph from the dataset view through its contributing process views down to source tables. At each level:

  • Internal feature store tables (FS_T_*, _HIDDEN) are silently skipped.
  • Base tables with no business dictionary entry trigger a warning.
  • Intermediate views with no documentation are auto-documented via LLM.
  • Process views (feature store type 'F') with no documentation are actively documented via document_process_incremental.
  • The dataset itself is documented last: feature and entity column descriptions are propagated from process documentation (no LLM needed for 1:1 columns), and a single LLM call generates the dataset-level business description.

Documentation is stored in the business dictionary tables (FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS with type 'D', FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: dataset_id : str UUID of the dataset (from dataset_catalog()). language : str Output language for LLM-generated descriptions. display : bool Whether to render the documentation. upload : bool Whether to persist the documentation to the business dictionary. force_update : bool If True, regenerate documentation even for already-documented nodes. _visited_processes : set, optional Shared recursion guard from an outer orchestration call — do not pass manually. When provided, processes already in this set are skipped without re-generating docs. _visited_views : set, optional Shared recursion guard from an outer orchestration call — do not pass manually.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict or None A dictionary with the dataset documentation, or None on error.

docs.title: _document_from_graph
docs.text:
_document_from_graph(graph: Dict[str, Any], proc_df, ds_df, *, all_process_ids: Set[str], all_dataset_ids: Set[str], language: str, json_constraint: bool, display: bool, upload: bool, force_update: bool, log_prefix: str = '_document_from_graph') -> Dict[str, List[str]]

Walk a dependency graph leaves-first and document each registered process / dataset exactly once, sharing visited-sets so common upstream views are documented only once.

Shared back-end of :func:document_feature_store_incremental (on-the-fly combined graph) and :func:rebuild_documentation (persisted graph). Mirrors the original steps 2 (lookup maps), 4–5 (topo + walk) and 6 (fallback).

graph is the {'nodes','edges','roots'} dict; proc_df / ds_df are the process and dataset catalogs; all_process_ids / all_dataset_ids bound the fallback pass.

docs.title: rebuild_documentation
docs.text:
rebuild_documentation(objects=None, force_update: bool = False, all_data_domains: bool = True, language: str = 'English', json_constraint: bool = True, display: bool = False, upload: bool = True, rebuild_lineage: bool = True, rebuild_vector_indexing: bool = True, extra_views=None) -> Dict[str, List[str]]

Document the feature store from the persisted lineage graph (FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH) — no on-the-fly SHOW VIEW re-derivation.

The persisted graph is read once (get_persisted_graph), topologically sorted (leaves first), and walked with shared visited-sets so each process / dataset is documented exactly once and shared upstream views are never re-documented. Seeds are effectively the top consumers of the graph (datasets and process views); base tables / intermediate views / tdstone2 TDS_* objects are connectors, documented only as a side effect of their owning process's walk.

Run :func:tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph first to populate the graph, or set rebuild_lineage=True (the default) to have this function call it automatically.

Scoped to changed objects (objects=[...]): re-document only the impact set — each named object plus everything downstream of it (get_descendants) — because a changed (possibly non-process) view invalidates the upstream context of everything that consumes it. Names are validated by :func:resolve_objects (case / quoting / db.name safe; raises on any unmatched name). Scoped mode forces force_update=True so the stale descendants actually regenerate.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] | None When None (default), document the whole store. Otherwise re-document only the impact set of these changed objects. force_update : bool Whole-store mode: when False (default) already-documented objects are reused as context and only gaps get an LLM call; True regenerates every object. (Scoped mode always forces True.) all_data_domains : bool Include processes from all DATA_DOMAINs (default) or only the active one. rebuild_lineage : bool When True (default), calls :func:tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph before the documentation walk so the persisted graph is up to date. Set to False to skip (e.g. you already called rebuild_lineage_graph() just before). A warning is emitted when False. rebuild_vector_indexing : bool When True (default), calls :func:tdfs4ds.rebuild_vector_indexing after the documentation walk so the vector index reflects the freshly written business dictionary rows. Set to False to skip (e.g. you will call it separately or want to defer indexing). A warning is emitted when False. extra_views : str | Iterable[str] | None Standalone view name(s) (not a process view / dataset) to also capture + document — e.g. a hand-built reporting view nothing registered references. Passed to rebuild_lineage_graph(extra_views=…) so the view enters FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH and then appears as a root for the documentation walk (documented via the intermediate-view path). Only effective when rebuild_lineage=True. Validated (raises on unmatched name); no schema scan. language, json_constraint, display, upload : Passed through to document_process_incremental / document_dataset_incremental.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict Summary with processes_documented / datasets_documented / processes_skipped / datasets_skipped (lists of IDs).

docs.title: rebuild_vector_indexing
docs.text:
rebuild_vector_indexing(force_rebuild: bool = True) -> None

Rebuild the vector index from the current business dictionary.

On the Chroma backend, a full rebuild (force_rebuild=True, the default) destroys and re-creates all three collections so removed or renamed documentation does not linger.

On the Teradata backend, force_rebuild does NOT destroy anything: destroy() requires admin privileges that may not be available, so this always reuses/upserts into the existing collection (create-if-missing, add/update rows otherwise) — see tdfs4ds.agent.vector_store_teradata.build_teradata_index's docstring. Stale rows from deleted business-dictionary entries are NOT purged by this call on Teradata; use tdfs4ds.agent.vector_store_teradata.reset_teradata_store() directly (requires admin rights) for a true destroy-and-recreate there.

Use force_rebuild=False for an additive upsert on either backend (faster on Chroma; identical behavior to force_rebuild=True on Teradata).

Works with both Chroma and Teradata backends (tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND).

docs.title: document_feature_store_incremental
docs.text:
document_feature_store_incremental(language: str = 'English', json_constraint: bool = True, display: bool = True, upload: bool = True, force_update: bool = False, all_data_domains: bool = False) -> Dict[str, Any]

Document all registered processes and datasets in a single optimised pass.

Objects are processed in dependency order — leaves (deepest upstream dependencies) first, roots last — so that upstream context is always available when a higher-level process or dataset is reached. A single pair of visited-sets is shared across every call so each process view is documented at most once, regardless of how many datasets reference it.

docs.title: Strategy
docs.text:
1. Fetch all registered processes (process_catalog) and datasets (dataset_catalog).
  2. Build a combined lineage graph seeded from every dataset DDL (expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=True) and from any orphan process not already covered by a dataset.
  3. Topologically sort the combined graph (leaves first, roots last).
  4. Walk the sorted order, calling document_process_incremental for registered process views and document_dataset_incremental for dataset views, sharing the same _visited_processes / _visited_views sets throughout.
  5. Any registered object absent from the graph (e.g. if its lineage build failed) is documented individually as a fallback.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: language : str Output language for LLM-generated descriptions. json_constraint : bool Whether to enforce JSON schema in the LLM output. display : bool Whether to render each object's documentation as it is produced. upload : bool Whether to persist the documentation to the catalog tables. force_update : bool If True, regenerate documentation even for already-documented objects. all_data_domains : bool If True, include processes from all DATA_DOMAINs; otherwise only the current tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN is included.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict Summary with keys "processes_documented", "datasets_documented", "processes_skipped", "datasets_skipped" (each a list of IDs).

docs.title: document_view_incremental
docs.text:
document_view_incremental(database: str, view_name: str, language: str = 'English', force_update: bool = False, display: bool = True, upload: bool = True, _visited_views: Optional[set] = None) -> None

Document an arbitrary view and all its upstream dependencies.

Mirrors the inner loop of document_process_incremental but seeded from any SELECT * FROM {database}.{view_name} — the view does not need to be registered in the process or dataset catalog.

Useful for custom analytical views, reporting views, or any intermediate objects that are not tracked by the feature store catalogs.

A shared _visited_views set can be passed when calling this function in a loop so that views referenced by multiple seeds are only documented once.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: database : str Database (schema) that owns the view. view_name : str Name of the view to document. language : str Output language for LLM-generated descriptions. force_update : bool If True, regenerate docs even if the view is already documented. display : bool Whether to emit INFO log lines when done. upload : bool Whether to persist docs to the catalog tables (passed through to the underlying calls — currently informational, upload happens inside _document_intermediate_view). _visited_views : set, optional Cross-call deduplication guard. Pass the same set when calling this function for multiple views in a single loop so shared dependencies are only documented once.

docs.title: analyze_sql_query
docs.text:
analyze_sql_query(sql_query: str, *, debug: bool = False) -> Dict[str, List[str]]

Extracts source and target tables/views from a SQL query.

docs.title: analyze_teradata_ddl
docs.text:
analyze_teradata_ddl(ddl: str) -> Dict[str, Any]
Analyse Teradata CREATE TABLE DDL and return

{ 'primary_index_columns': [...], 'partition_columns': [...], 'partitioning_levels': [...], 'partitioning_by_column': {...} }

Critical behavior: - Ignores keyword-like text inside single-quoted literals - Searches PRIMARY INDEX / PARTITION BY only in the table-options tail (after the column-definition block).

docs.title: build_teradata_dependency_graph
docs.text:
build_teradata_dependency_graph(sql_query: str, *, default_database: Optional[str] = None, max_depth: int = 50, store_ddl: bool = False, stop_at_feature_store_datasets: bool = True, expand_datasets_via_process_catalog: bool = True) -> Dict[str, Any]
Builds a dependency graph by

1) analyzing the initial SQL via analyze_sql_query 2) for each discovered source object: - SHOW VIEW and re-run analyze_sql_query on its DDL to expand dependencies - or SHOW TABLE and run analyze_teradata_ddl to attach PI/partition metadata

Feature store dataset views (registered in the FS_V_DATASET_CATALOG) are treated as named "dataset" nodes when stop_at_feature_store_datasets=True (the default). Instead of drilling into their underlying SQL, the graph queries the dataset feature catalog (FS_V_{catalog}_FEATURES) to find which registered process views contribute to the dataset (one process = one view), and adds edges to those views directly. Set expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=False to treat datasets as pure leaves.

Graph edges are parent -> child (parent depends on child).

Returns:

Type Description Dict[str, Any]

{ "nodes": { node_name: { ...NodeInfo... }, ... }, "edges": [ (parent, child), ... ], "roots": [ ...targets from initial SQL... ],

Dict[str, Any]

}

docs.title: graph_to_migration_manifest
docs.text:
graph_to_migration_manifest(graph: Dict[str, Any], *, filter_database: Optional[str] = None) -> Dict[str, Any]

Converts a lineage graph into a migration manifest — a JSON-serialisable dict that lists every view and table discovered, with optional filtering to a single database (e.g. the feature store database).

Useful for planning a feature store migration: objects whose database matches filter_database are the ones that need to be recreated or moved; edges between them describe the recreation order.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: graph : dict Output of build_teradata_dependency_graph. filter_database : str, optional When given, only nodes whose database field matches (case-insensitive) are included. Edges whose source or target falls outside the filter are also excluded. When None every node and edge is returned.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict with keys: "views" : list of dicts {database, name, type} type is "view" or "dataset" "tables" : list of dicts {database, name, type, primary_index, partition_columns} type is "table" "edges" : list of dicts {from, to} (parent depends on child)

plot_lineage_sankey(graph: Dict[str, Any], *, title: str = 'Lineage Sankey', max_label_len: int = 45, show_full_name_in_hover: bool = True) -> go.Figure
Plot an interactive Sankey from a lineage graph like

{ "nodes": {name: {name, object_type, database, object, table_info, error, ...}, ...}, "edges": [(parent, child), ...], # parent depends on child "roots": [...] }

Hover displays relevant info (no DDL). Returns a plotly Figure.

Display Plotly figure robustly across environments.

  • Falls back to browser renderer if nbformat is missing
  • Optionally writes an HTML file

docs.title: lineage_graph_catalog_creation
docs.text:
lineage_graph_catalog_creation() -> str

Create the FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH temporal edge table and its public FS_V_LINEAGE_GRAPH view if they do not already exist.

Idempotent: mirrors documentation_tables_creation — a failed CREATE whose error mentions "already exists" is swallowed.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: str The name of the lineage graph table.

docs.title: record_process_lineage
docs.text:
record_process_lineage(view_name: str, art_sql=None, art_layers=None) -> int

Persist the lineage subgraph rooted at view_name into FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH.

Edges are produced by :func:build_teradata_dependency_graph (the same function used everywhere else, which skips FS-internal technical tables and stops at datasets / process views). Idempotent refresh: every parent node present in the freshly built graph has its existing edge rows deleted (CURRENT VALIDTIME) before the new ones are inserted, so a re-run / rewrite replaces exactly that subgraph and leaves no stale edges.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: view_name : str The (possibly schema-qualified, possibly quoted) process view name, e.g. my_db."V_FEAT" or a DataFrame _table_name. art_sql : list of str, optional UAF ART view mode. The INTO VOLATILE ART materialisation statements of this process. When provided, lineage is traced through the ephemeral volatile ART to the real series-input source: any edge in the view graph that points at a volatile ART named by art_sql is rewritten into a two-hop path feature_view -> <operator/layer node> -> series_input_source (the sources parsed from the art_sql SERIES_SPEC/ART_SPEC TABLE_NAME(...) clauses), so no dangling edge to a non-existent ART remains. Non-fatal: on any parse problem the classic (view-only) graph is persisted unchanged. art_layers : list of str, optional The declared LAYER name(s), used only to label the synthetic operator node (e.g. vw_feat::ARTFITMETADATA). Cosmetic.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: int Number of edges written (0 when the graph has no edges).

docs.title: rebuild_lineage_graph
docs.text:
rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=None, all_data_domains: bool = True, check: bool = True, prune: bool = True, extra_views=None) -> int

Rebuild the persisted lineage graph — fully, or scoped to a list of objects.

Use this to initialise FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH on a store that already has registered processes/datasets, to refresh a graph whose edges have drifted, or to scoped-refresh just the objects you edited. The incremental write hook (:func:record_process_lineage, called from upload_features) keeps lineage current going forward; this function recomputes on demand.

The table is valid-time temporal: every write is diff-based, so re-running on an unchanged store touches zero rows (no history churn). There is no blind delete+insert — :func:record_process_lineage leaves unchanged edges alone, inserts new ones, and closes (CURRENT VALIDTIME DELETE) ones that disappeared.

Full refresh (objects=None):

  1. Ensure the table/view exist (idempotent).
  2. Seed from every registered process view and every dataset (all data domains by default), re-deriving each subgraph via :func:record_process_lineage (diff-based — unchanged edges are untouched).
  3. When prune=True (default), edges whose parent object is no longer a seed (e.g. a since-dropped process) are closed with CURRENT VALIDTIME DELETE. This is a targeted prune of stale parents, not a wipe — it does not re-open the edges that are still valid.

Scoped refresh (objects=[...]): no prune, no catalog-wide seed. The names are validated by :func:resolve_objects (raises on any unmatched name — case / quoting / db.name safe), then record_process_lineage refreshes each object's own outgoing edges (diff-based). Use this when a (possibly non-process) view was edited. When check=True, :func:check_downstream_processes runs afterwards.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] | None When None (default), full refresh. Otherwise refresh only these objects. all_data_domains : bool Full-refresh only: all DATA_DOMAINs (default) or the active one. check : bool Scoped refresh only: run the downstream EXPLAIN compile check afterwards. prune : bool Full-refresh only: close edges of parents that are no longer registered (default True). Set False to never delete (purely additive refresh). extra_views : str | Iterable[str] | None Full-refresh only: additional standalone view names to seed into the graph alongside every registered process view + dataset. Use this to capture a non-process / non-dataset view (e.g. a hand-built or crystallized reporting view) that nothing registered references — it would otherwise never enter FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH. Each name is validated via :func:resolve_objects (case / quoting / db.name safe; raises on any unmatched name). There is no automatic schema scan — only the names you pass.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: int Number of edge changes written (0 when nothing changed).

docs.title: load_lineage_digraph
docs.text:
load_lineage_digraph(auto_build: bool = True)

Load the persisted lineage graph into a networkx.DiGraph with a single read of FS_V_LINEAGE_GRAPH (CURRENT VALIDTIME).

Edge direction is parent -> child (parent depends on child). Each node carries an object_type attribute ('T'/'V'/'D'). All ancestor / descendant traversal is then done in-memory — no recursive SQL.

Fallback: when the persisted table is missing/empty or unreadable and auto_build=True (default), the graph is re-derived on the fly via :func:_build_digraph_on_the_fly so callers still work on a store whose graph was never persisted (run :func:rebuild_lineage_graph to persist it and make subsequent loads fast).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: networkx.DiGraph

docs.title: get_descendants
docs.text:
get_descendants(database: str, object_name: str, graph=None) -> List[str]

Objects that depend (directly or transitively) on database.object_name — the impact / reverse direction (nx.ancestors over a depends-on DiGraph, since edges point parent -> child = downstream -> upstream).

Returns canonical node ids ('DB.OBJECT').

docs.title: get_ancestors
docs.text:
get_ancestors(database: str, object_name: str, graph=None) -> List[str]

Upstream sources that database.object_name depends on (directly or transitively) — nx.descendants over the depends-on DiGraph.

Returns canonical node ids ('DB.OBJECT').

docs.title: get_persisted_graph
docs.text:
get_persisted_graph(seed: Optional[str] = None) -> Dict[str, Any]

Return the persisted lineage in the same {'nodes', 'edges', 'roots'} dict shape produced by :func:build_teradata_dependency_graph, so existing consumers (plot_lineage_sankey, graph_to_migration_manifest, skill_lineage) work unchanged on persisted data.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: seed : str, optional If given ('DB.OBJECT' or 'OBJECT'), restrict to the subgraph of the seed plus all its ancestors and descendants.

docs.title: resolve_process_id_for_object
docs.text:
resolve_process_id_for_object(database: str, object_name: str) -> Optional[str]

Resolve an object node to its registered process PROCESS_ID, or None when the object is not a registered process view (a plain view / table / dataset).

Some views are processes, some are not — the lineage table stores objects only, so the link is resolved here through the authoritative process catalog (FS_V_PROCESS_CATALOG: VIEW_NAME <-> PROCESS_ID). Matching is done on the de-quoted, uppercased (database, object) to be robust to the quoting variance of stored VIEW_NAME values.

docs.title: resolve_objects
docs.text:
resolve_objects(objects, *, require_exists: bool = True, graph=None)

Normalise and verify a user-supplied list of object names so case / " quoting / db.name variance never causes a silent mismatch.

Each input ("db"."NAME", db.name, NAME, any case) is parsed with the same _parse_node_name + _norm helpers record_process_lineage uses to write the table, so a resolved name compares equal to stored rows. An object is considered to exist if it is a node in the persisted lineage graph, or (fallback, for a freshly-created view not yet in the graph) present in DBC.TablesV.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] One name or a list of names. require_exists : bool When True (default), raise ValueError naming every unmatched input. When False, unmatched inputs are logged and dropped. graph : networkx.DiGraph, optional Pre-loaded lineage graph (avoids a reload when the caller already has one).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: (resolved, unmatched) resolved is a list of canonical (DB_UPPER, OBJECT_UPPER) tuples (default DB filled from tdfs4ds.SCHEMA when the input was unqualified); unmatched is the list of original input strings that resolved to nothing.

docs.title: check_downstream_processes
docs.text:
check_downstream_processes(objects)

Deterministic compile check for the process views downstream of changed objects.

When a non-process view changes its result columns, the process views that read it can silently break. This runs EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM <process_view> on every registered process downstream of the given object(s) — EXPLAIN only verifies the query compiles (all columns resolve), it does not execute it. Same compile-validity idiom as the pre-check in upload_features.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] Changed object name(s); resolved via :func:resolve_objects.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict {"checked": int, "ok": [view, ...], "broken": [{"view", "process_id", "error"}, ...]}. Broken processes are also logged as a warning.

docs.title: plot_lineage_pyvis
docs.text:
plot_lineage_pyvis(graph: Dict[str, Any], *, output_path: str = 'tdfs4ds_lineage.html', height: str = '750px', width: str = '100%', notebook: bool = False)

Render a lineage graph as an interactive pyvis network and write it to a self-contained HTML file.

Accepts the same {'nodes', 'edges', 'roots'} dict shape produced by :func:get_persisted_graph / :func:build_teradata_dependency_graph. Node colour encodes object type (view/table/dataset), matching plot_lineage_sankey. Edge direction is parent -> child (depends-on).

pyvis is a tdfs4ds dependency; it is imported lazily so building/persisting lineage never pays the import cost unless a visualisation is requested.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pyvis.network.Network The configured network (already written to output_path).

docs.title: _RE_FROM_JOIN module-attribute
docs.text:
_RE_FROM_JOIN = compile('
    \bFROM\b
    \s+(?P<name>[\w".]+)
    |
    \b(?:INNER\s+JOIN|LEFT\s+JOIN|RIGHT\s+JOIN|FULL\s+OUTER\s+JOIN|CROSS\s+JOIN|JOIN)\b
    \s+(?P<join_name>[\w".]+)
    ', IGNORECASE | VERBOSE)

docs.title: tdfs4ds.lineage.lineage_store
docs.text:
Persisted, temporal lineage graph for tdfs4ds.

Where network.py derives lineage on the fly (SHOW VIEW / SHOW TABLE + regex), this module persists it. At each upload_features call the process view's lineage subgraph is recorded into the FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH temporal table (one row per directed parent -> child object edge). The table is created when missing at setup() / connect(create_if_missing=True).

docs.title: Design notes
docs.text:
• Nodes reuse the business-dictionary object identity (DATABASE_NAME, OBJECT_NAME) + OBJECT_TYPE CHAR(1) ('T'/'V'/'D'), so the lineage graph, the business dictionary and the documentation all speak the same vocabulary.
  • Edges carry no PROCESS_ID — an edge connects two objects, and most objects (source tables, intermediate views, datasets) are not processes. Node -> process resolution is done on demand through the process catalog (resolve_process_id_for_object).
  • Edges are produced by the existing authoritative builder :func:build_teradata_dependency_graph, which intentionally skips the feature store's internal technical tables and stops at the dataset / process-view level. We do not re-parse DDL here.
  • All graph traversal (ancestors / descendants) is done in-memory with networkx after a single full-table load — no recursive SQL, no per-hop round-trips.

docs.title: _OBJECT_TYPE_CODE module-attribute
docs.text: _OBJECT_TYPE_CODE = {'view': 'V', 'table': 'T', 'dataset': 'D', 'unknown': 'V'}

docs.title: _PYVIS_COLOR module-attribute
docs.text: _PYVIS_COLOR = {'view': '#6baed6', 'process': '#e6550d', 'table': '#31a354', 'dataset': '#756bb1', 'unknown': '#bdbdbd'}

docs.text:
logger_safe(level, message, *args, **kwargs)

Wrapper around the global logger that only emits logs when tdfs4ds.DISPLAY_LOGS is True. level is a string like "info", "error", etc.

docs.title: build_teradata_dependency_graph
docs.text:
build_teradata_dependency_graph(sql_query: str, *, default_database: Optional[str] = None, max_depth: int = 50, store_ddl: bool = False, stop_at_feature_store_datasets: bool = True, expand_datasets_via_process_catalog: bool = True) -> Dict[str, Any]
Builds a dependency graph by

1) analyzing the initial SQL via analyze_sql_query 2) for each discovered source object: - SHOW VIEW and re-run analyze_sql_query on its DDL to expand dependencies - or SHOW TABLE and run analyze_teradata_ddl to attach PI/partition metadata

Feature store dataset views (registered in the FS_V_DATASET_CATALOG) are treated as named "dataset" nodes when stop_at_feature_store_datasets=True (the default). Instead of drilling into their underlying SQL, the graph queries the dataset feature catalog (FS_V_{catalog}_FEATURES) to find which registered process views contribute to the dataset (one process = one view), and adds edges to those views directly. Set expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=False to treat datasets as pure leaves.

Graph edges are parent -> child (parent depends on child).

Returns:

Type Description Dict[str, Any]

{ "nodes": { node_name: { ...NodeInfo... }, ... }, "edges": [ (parent, child), ... ], "roots": [ ...targets from initial SQL... ],

Dict[str, Any]

}

docs.text:
_parse_node_name(node_name: str) -> Tuple[Optional[str], str]
Accepts names like
  • '"sales"."orders"'
  • '."orders"'
  • 'sales.orders'
  • 'orders' (rare; treat as object only)

    • Returns (database, object).
