docs.title: _uaf_art_rewrite
docs.text:
_uaf_art_rewrite(rows, parents, schema, art_sql, art_layers, feature_db, feature_obj)

Rewrite the view-graph edges of a UAF ART view so lineage traces through the ephemeral volatile ART to the real series-input source.

The feature view's ART_SPEC(TABLE_NAME(<art>)) yields a dangling edge feature_view -> <art> (the volatile ART is not in any catalog). Using art_sql we know (a) the ART name(s) it declares and (b) the real series source(s) it reads (SERIES_SPEC(TABLE_NAME(<src>))). We drop every edge whose child is such an ART, insert a synthetic operator/layer node <feature_obj>::<layers> (type 'V'), and link feature_view -> op_node -> each series source.

Returns (new_rows, new_parents). Fail-open: on any problem returns the inputs unchanged so the classic (view-only) graph is persisted.

docs.title: _sql_str
docs.text:
_sql_str(s: str) -> str

Escape a value for inlining inside a single-quoted SQL literal.

docs.title: lineage_graph_catalog_creation
docs.text:
lineage_graph_catalog_creation() -> str

Create the FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH temporal edge table and its public FS_V_LINEAGE_GRAPH view if they do not already exist.

Idempotent: mirrors documentation_tables_creation — a failed CREATE whose error mentions "already exists" is swallowed.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: str The name of the lineage graph table.

docs.title: record_process_lineage
docs.text:
record_process_lineage(view_name: str, art_sql=None, art_layers=None) -> int

Persist the lineage subgraph rooted at view_name into FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH.

Edges are produced by :func:build_teradata_dependency_graph (the same function used everywhere else, which skips FS-internal technical tables and stops at datasets / process views). Idempotent refresh: every parent node present in the freshly built graph has its existing edge rows deleted (CURRENT VALIDTIME) before the new ones are inserted, so a re-run / rewrite replaces exactly that subgraph and leaves no stale edges.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: view_name : str The (possibly schema-qualified, possibly quoted) process view name, e.g. my_db."V_FEAT" or a DataFrame _table_name. art_sql : list of str, optional UAF ART view mode. The INTO VOLATILE ART materialisation statements of this process. When provided, lineage is traced through the ephemeral volatile ART to the real series-input source: any edge in the view graph that points at a volatile ART named by art_sql is rewritten into a two-hop path feature_view -> <operator/layer node> -> series_input_source (the sources parsed from the art_sql SERIES_SPEC/ART_SPEC TABLE_NAME(...) clauses), so no dangling edge to a non-existent ART remains. Non-fatal: on any parse problem the classic (view-only) graph is persisted unchanged. art_layers : list of str, optional The declared LAYER name(s), used only to label the synthetic operator node (e.g. vw_feat::ARTFITMETADATA). Cosmetic.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: int Number of edges written (0 when the graph has no edges).

docs.title: rebuild_lineage_graph
docs.text:
rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=None, all_data_domains: bool = True, check: bool = True, prune: bool = True, extra_views=None) -> int

Rebuild the persisted lineage graph — fully, or scoped to a list of objects.

Use this to initialise FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH on a store that already has registered processes/datasets, to refresh a graph whose edges have drifted, or to scoped-refresh just the objects you edited. The incremental write hook (:func:record_process_lineage, called from upload_features) keeps lineage current going forward; this function recomputes on demand.

The table is valid-time temporal: every write is diff-based, so re-running on an unchanged store touches zero rows (no history churn). There is no blind delete+insert — :func:record_process_lineage leaves unchanged edges alone, inserts new ones, and closes (CURRENT VALIDTIME DELETE) ones that disappeared.

Full refresh (objects=None):

  1. Ensure the table/view exist (idempotent).
  2. Seed from every registered process view and every dataset (all data domains by default), re-deriving each subgraph via :func:record_process_lineage (diff-based — unchanged edges are untouched).
  3. When prune=True (default), edges whose parent object is no longer a seed (e.g. a since-dropped process) are closed with CURRENT VALIDTIME DELETE. This is a targeted prune of stale parents, not a wipe — it does not re-open the edges that are still valid.

Scoped refresh (objects=[...]): no prune, no catalog-wide seed. The names are validated by :func:resolve_objects (raises on any unmatched name — case / quoting / db.name safe), then record_process_lineage refreshes each object's own outgoing edges (diff-based). Use this when a (possibly non-process) view was edited. When check=True, :func:check_downstream_processes runs afterwards.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] | None When None (default), full refresh. Otherwise refresh only these objects. all_data_domains : bool Full-refresh only: all DATA_DOMAINs (default) or the active one. check : bool Scoped refresh only: run the downstream EXPLAIN compile check afterwards. prune : bool Full-refresh only: close edges of parents that are no longer registered (default True). Set False to never delete (purely additive refresh). extra_views : str | Iterable[str] | None Full-refresh only: additional standalone view names to seed into the graph alongside every registered process view + dataset. Use this to capture a non-process / non-dataset view (e.g. a hand-built or crystallized reporting view) that nothing registered references — it would otherwise never enter FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH. Each name is validated via :func:resolve_objects (case / quoting / db.name safe; raises on any unmatched name). There is no automatic schema scan — only the names you pass.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: int Number of edge changes written (0 when nothing changed).

docs.title: _build_digraph_on_the_fly
docs.text:
_build_digraph_on_the_fly()

Build the lineage DiGraph without the persisted table — fallback for when FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH is missing/empty (never built) or unreadable.

Re-derives the full graph in memory by running build_teradata_dependency_graph over every registered process view + dataset (the same seed set rebuild_lineage_graph uses) and merging the edges. Slower than reading the table (it runs SHOW VIEW), but it means the query layer (get_descendants / get_ancestors / impact check / pyvis) still works on a store whose graph was never persisted.

docs.title: load_lineage_digraph
docs.text:
load_lineage_digraph(auto_build: bool = True)

Load the persisted lineage graph into a networkx.DiGraph with a single read of FS_V_LINEAGE_GRAPH (CURRENT VALIDTIME).

Edge direction is parent -> child (parent depends on child). Each node carries an object_type attribute ('T'/'V'/'D'). All ancestor / descendant traversal is then done in-memory — no recursive SQL.

Fallback: when the persisted table is missing/empty or unreadable and auto_build=True (default), the graph is re-derived on the fly via :func:_build_digraph_on_the_fly so callers still work on a store whose graph was never persisted (run :func:rebuild_lineage_graph to persist it and make subsequent loads fast).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: networkx.DiGraph

Canonical in-graph node id: 'DB.OBJECT' (uppercased, de-quoted).

get_descendants(database: str, object_name: str, graph=None) -> List[str]

docs.title: get_persisted_graph
docs.text:
get_persisted_graph(seed: Optional[str] = None) -> Dict[str, Any]

Return the persisted lineage in the same {'nodes', 'edges', 'roots'} dict shape produced by :func:build_teradata_dependency_graph, so existing consumers (plot_lineage_sankey, graph_to_migration_manifest, skill_lineage) work unchanged on persisted data.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: seed : str, optional If given ('DB.OBJECT' or 'OBJECT'), restrict to the subgraph of the seed plus all its ancestors and descendants.

docs.title: resolve_process_id_for_object
docs.text:
resolve_process_id_for_object(database: str, object_name: str) -> Optional[str]

Resolve an object node to its registered process PROCESS_ID, or None when the object is not a registered process view (a plain view / table / dataset).

Some views are processes, some are not — the lineage table stores objects only, so the link is resolved here through the authoritative process catalog (FS_V_PROCESS_CATALOG: VIEW_NAME <-> PROCESS_ID). Matching is done on the de-quoted, uppercased (database, object) to be robust to the quoting variance of stored VIEW_NAME values.

docs.title: _dbc_object_exists
docs.text:
_dbc_object_exists(database: str, object_name: str) -> bool

True if (database, object_name) exists in DBC.TablesV (case-insensitive).

docs.title: resolve_objects
docs.text:
resolve_objects(objects, *, require_exists: bool = True, graph=None)

Normalise and verify a user-supplied list of object names so case / " quoting / db.name variance never causes a silent mismatch.

Each input ("db"."NAME", db.name, NAME, any case) is parsed with the same _parse_node_name + _norm helpers record_process_lineage uses to write the table, so a resolved name compares equal to stored rows. An object is considered to exist if it is a node in the persisted lineage graph, or (fallback, for a freshly-created view not yet in the graph) present in DBC.TablesV.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] One name or a list of names. require_exists : bool When True (default), raise ValueError naming every unmatched input. When False, unmatched inputs are logged and dropped. graph : networkx.DiGraph, optional Pre-loaded lineage graph (avoids a reload when the caller already has one).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: (resolved, unmatched) resolved is a list of canonical (DB_UPPER, OBJECT_UPPER) tuples (default DB filled from tdfs4ds.SCHEMA when the input was unqualified); unmatched is the list of original input strings that resolved to nothing.

docs.title: check_downstream_processes
docs.text:
check_downstream_processes(objects)

Deterministic compile check for the process views downstream of changed objects.

When a non-process view changes its result columns, the process views that read it can silently break. This runs EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM <process_view> on every registered process downstream of the given object(s) — EXPLAIN only verifies the query compiles (all columns resolve), it does not execute it. Same compile-validity idiom as the pre-check in upload_features.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: objects : str | Iterable[str] Changed object name(s); resolved via :func:resolve_objects.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict {"checked": int, "ok": [view, ...], "broken": [{"view", "process_id", "error"}, ...]}. Broken processes are also logged as a warning.

docs.title: plot_lineage_pyvis
docs.text:
plot_lineage_pyvis(graph: Dict[str, Any], *, output_path: str = 'tdfs4ds_lineage.html', height: str = '750px', width: str = '100%', notebook: bool = False)

Render a lineage graph as an interactive pyvis network and write it to a self-contained HTML file.

Accepts the same {'nodes', 'edges', 'roots'} dict shape produced by :func:get_persisted_graph / :func:build_teradata_dependency_graph. Node colour encodes object type (view/table/dataset), matching plot_lineage_sankey. Edge direction is parent -> child (depends-on).

pyvis is a tdfs4ds dependency; it is imported lazily so building/persisting lineage never pays the import cost unless a visualisation is requested.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pyvis.network.Network The configured network (already written to output_path).

_fetch_dataset_registry(schema: Optional[str], catalog_view: str) -> Set[Tuple[str, str]]

Pre-fetches all registered feature store datasets as a set of (DATASET_DATABASE.upper(), DATASET_NAME.upper()) tuples. Returns an empty set if the catalog is unreachable.

docs.title: _extract_process_ids_from_dataset_ddl
docs.text:
_extract_process_ids_from_dataset_ddl(ddl: str) -> List[str]

Parses a dataset view DDL and returns the unique FEATURE_VERSION values found in it.

Dataset views embed lines like

WHERE (FEATURE_ID = 1 AND FEATURE_VERSION='3b3e3c87-2f0e-404b-82ae-c598e4addcd1')

FEATURE_VERSION is the PROCESS_ID of the process that produced the feature, so this gives us all process UUIDs whose features are consumed by the dataset. Order-preserving deduplication is applied.

docs.title: _fetch_view_names_for_process_ids
docs.text:
_fetch_view_names_for_process_ids(schema: str, process_catalog_view: str, process_ids: List[str]) -> List[str]

Given a list of PROCESS_IDs, returns the VIEW_NAME for each from the process catalog. VIEW_NAME is the feature-engineering view registered for that process (e.g. '"demo_user"."FEAT_ENG_CUST"'), not the internal feature-store storage tables.

Returns an empty list if the catalog is unreachable or no rows are found.

build_teradata_dependency_graph(sql_query: str, *, default_database: Optional[str] = None, max_depth: int = 50, store_ddl: bool = False, stop_at_feature_store_datasets: bool = True, expand_datasets_via_process_catalog: bool = True) -> Dict[str, Any]
Builds a dependency graph by

1) analyzing the initial SQL via analyze_sql_query 2) for each discovered source object: - SHOW VIEW and re-run analyze_sql_query on its DDL to expand dependencies - or SHOW TABLE and run analyze_teradata_ddl to attach PI/partition metadata

Feature store dataset views (registered in the FS_V_DATASET_CATALOG) are treated as named "dataset" nodes when stop_at_feature_store_datasets=True (the default). Instead of drilling into their underlying SQL, the graph queries the dataset feature catalog (FS_V_{catalog}_FEATURES) to find which registered process views contribute to the dataset (one process = one view), and adds edges to those views directly. Set expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=False to treat datasets as pure leaves.

Graph edges are parent -> child (parent depends on child).

Returns:

Type Description Dict[str, Any]

{ "nodes": { node_name: { ...NodeInfo... }, ... }, "edges": [ (parent, child), ... ], "roots": [ ...targets from initial SQL... ],

docs.title: graph_to_migration_manifest
docs.text:
graph_to_migration_manifest(graph: Dict[str, Any], *, filter_database: Optional[str] = None) -> Dict[str, Any]

Converts a lineage graph into a migration manifest — a JSON-serialisable dict that lists every view and table discovered, with optional filtering to a single database (e.g. the feature store database).

Useful for planning a feature store migration: objects whose database matches filter_database are the ones that need to be recreated or moved; edges between them describe the recreation order.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: graph : dict Output of build_teradata_dependency_graph. filter_database : str, optional When given, only nodes whose database field matches (case-insensitive) are included. Edges whose source or target falls outside the filter are also excluded. When None every node and edge is returned.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict with keys: "views" : list of dicts {database, name, type} type is "view" or "dataset" "tables" : list of dicts {database, name, type, primary_index, partition_columns} type is "table" "edges" : list of dicts {from, to} (parent depends on child)

docs.title: execute_query_wrapper
docs.text:
execute_query_wrapper(f)

Decorator to execute a query. It wraps around the function and adds exception handling.

This decorator is used to wrap around a function that generates a query and execute that query. It handles exceptions during query execution and prints the error message along with the query. The decorator works with both TeradataML's tdml and legacy tdml.get_context() execution methods, depending on the TeradataML version.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default f function

Function to be decorated.

required

Returns:

Name Type Description function

Decorated function.

Example

To decorate a function that generates and executes a query:

@execute_query_wrapper def my_query_function(): return "SELECT * FROM my_table"

This decorator can be applied to functions that generate and execute queries with ease.

docs.title: follow_up_report
docs.text:
follow_up_report(filtermanager: Optional[object] = None, process_id: Optional[str] = None)

Return a follow-up report as a tdml.DataFrame, optionally filtered by process_id and/or a filtermanager's applied filter.

Behavior by arguments
  • process_id is None and filtermanager is None: Return all rows from SCHEMA.FOLLOW_UP_NAME, sorted by START_DATETIME desc.
  • process_id is not None and filtermanager is None: Return rows for the given PROCESS_ID.
  • process_id is not None and filtermanager is not None: Return rows for the given PROCESS_ID whose APPLIED_FILTER matches the JSON_AGG of filtermanager's columns coming from its schema/view.
  • process_id is None and filtermanager is not None: Return rows whose APPLIED_FILTER matches the JSON_AGG of filtermanager (no PROCESS_ID constraint).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default filtermanager Optional[object]

An object exposing col_names, schema_name, and view_name. Its columns are aggregated via JSON_AGG(col1, col2, ...) to compare against A.APPLIED_FILTER.

None process_id Optional[str]

Optional process identifier used to filter by PROCESS_ID.

None

Returns:

Type Description

tdml.DataFrame: The resulting dataframe sorted by START_DATETIME (descending).

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If filtermanager is provided but is missing required attributes or has an empty col_names list.

RuntimeError

If the query fails.

docs.title: follow_up_report_many
docs.text:
follow_up_report_many(process_ids)

Return follow-up rows for several process_ids in a single query.

Batched counterpart to follow_up_report(process_id=…): issues one WHERE PROCESS_ID IN (…) query instead of one round-trip per process. Callers group the result by PROCESS_ID in pandas to reproduce the per-process aggregation. Returns a tdml.DataFrame sorted by START_DATETIME descending.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default process_ids

Iterable of process identifier strings. Empty/None entries are dropped; duplicates are collapsed.

required

Returns:

Type Description

tdml.DataFrame with the same columns as the follow-up table. When the

id list is empty, an empty result (WHERE 1=0) is returned so the

caller's downstream pandas logic still works.

Raises:

Type Description RuntimeError

If the query fails.

docs.title: list_processes
docs.text:
list_processes()

Retrieves and returns a list of all processes from the feature store.

This function constructs and executes a SQL query to fetch details of all processes stored in the feature store. The details include process ID, process type, view name, entity ID, feature names, feature version, data domain, and metadata. The function handles different states of the feature store time, choosing either the current valid time or a specific timestamp based on the configuration. It also optionally prints the constructed SQL query, depending on the configuration. If the query execution fails, the function catches the exception, prints the error message, and the query for debugging purposes.

Returns:

Name Type Description DataFrame

A pandas DataFrame containing the details of all processes in the feature store. The columns of the DataFrame correspond to the process ID, process type, view name, entity ID, feature names, feature version, data domain, and metadata.

Raises:

Type Description Exception

If the query execution fails, the function prints the exception message and the query.

docs.title: remove_process
docs.text:
remove_process(process_id, nonsequence_validtime=True)

Deletes a process from the feature store's process catalog based on the given process ID.

Also removes all associated documentation (business logic, features, EXPLAIN analysis, and business dictionary entries) before deleting the catalog rows. Documentation cleanup is non-fatal: a failure on any documentation table is logged as a warning and does not prevent the process from being removed.

process_id (str): The unique identifier of the process to be removed. This ID is used to specify which process should be deleted from the process catalog. nonsequence_validtime (bool): When True (default), uses NONSEQUENCED VALIDTIME DELETE to remove all temporal versions from the catalog.

list: SQL query strings that, when executed, delete the specified process from the process catalog and data distribution table.

Note: The actual execution of the SQL queries is handled by the execute_query_wrapper decorator.

docs.title: get_process_id
docs.text:
get_process_id(view_name)

Retrieves the process ID associated with a given view name in a database.

This function is designed to obtain the process ID for a specified view within a database. Initially, it formats the provided view_name to conform to the database's naming conventions, including handling scenarios where the view name is prefixed with a database name. After formatting the view name, the function queries a list of processes using an assumedly predefined function list_processes. It then filters this list to find the process corresponding to the formatted view name and returns the associated process ID. The function assumes specific column names ('VIEW_NAME' and 'PROCESS_ID') in the data structure returned by list_processes and that these columns are in a specific format (e.g., uppercase for view names).

view_name (str): The name of the view for which the process ID is to be retrieved. This can be either just the view name or a combination of database name and view name, formatted as 'database.view'.

int: The process ID associated with the given view name. This ID is retrieved from a list of processes, where it is associated with the view name that matches the input.

Raises: IndexError: If no process is found with the given view name.

docs.title: register_process_view
docs.text:
register_process_view(view_name, entity_id, feature_names, metadata={}, entity_null_substitute={}, **kwargs)

Registers a process view in the feature store or updates an existing one, based on global configuration and specified parameters. This function serves as a wrapper that dynamically chooses the registration method (either update-insert or merge) according to the global tdfs4ds.REGISTER_PROCESS setting. It supports registering views with an option to return the process ID for tracking and further operations.

  • view_name (str | DataFrame): The name of the view as a string or a DataFrame object. If a DataFrame is provided, it is expected that the table name will be used as the view name.
  • entity_id (str): The identifier of the entity associated with this view.
  • feature_names (List[str]): A list of the names of features to be included in the view.
  • metadata (dict, optional): Additional metadata related to the view. Defaults to an empty dictionary.
  • **kwargs: Arbitrary keyword arguments. This includes 'with_process_id', which dictates whether the process ID should be returned along with the query.
  • If 'with_process_id' is specified and True, the function returns a tuple containing the main SQL query string, the process ID, the distribution query, and the filter manager query (if applicable). Otherwise, it returns the main SQL query string, the distribution query, and the filter manager query, omitting the process ID.

Note: - The function determines the registration method (update-insert or merge) based on the global configuration. - It delegates the actual registration logic to _register_process_view_update_insert or _register_process_view_merge, depending on the chosen method.

docs.title: _register_process_view_merge
docs.text:
_register_process_view_merge(view_name, entity_id, feature_names, metadata={}, entity_null_substitute={}, **kwargs)

This function registers a new process view or updates an existing one in the feature store. It is versatile, accepting either a view name as a string or a DataFrame, and can handle different time parameters for data validity. The primary operations include generating a unique process ID, constructing SQL queries for inserting or updating view details, and optionally returning the process ID for further use. It leverages global configurations and specific modules for its operations.

  • view_name (str | DataFrame): The name of the view as a string, or a DataFrame object. If a DataFrame is provided, its table name is extracted and used as the view name.
  • entity_id (str): The identifier of the entity to which the view is associated.
  • feature_names (List[str]): The names of features included in the view.
  • metadata (dict, optional): A dictionary of additional metadata related to the view. Defaults to {}.
  • **kwargs: Arbitrary keyword arguments. Includes options like 'with_process_id' to return the process ID, 'filtermanager' for applying a filter manager, 'primary_index', and 'partitioning' for data distribution.
  • If 'with_process_id' is True, returns a tuple containing the main SQL query string, process ID, distribution query, and filter manager query (if applicable). Otherwise, returns the main SQL query string, distribution query, and filter manager query without the process ID.

Note: - Assumes certain global variables and configurations (e.g., tdfs4ds.END_PERIOD, tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME) are set. - Requires 'tdml' module for DataFrame operations and 'uuid' for generating unique identifiers.

docs.title: _register_process_view_update_insert
docs.text:
_register_process_view_update_insert(view_name, entity_id, feature_names, metadata={}, entity_null_substitute={}, **kwargs)

Registers or updates a process view in the feature store. This function supports both the creation of new views and the modification of existing ones. It handles the process based on the type of view name provided (str or DataFrame) and the specified time parameters (current or past).

The function generates a unique process identifier and constructs a SQL query to insert or update the view details in the feature store. Additionally, it handles logging of the process registration.

view_name (str or DataFrame): The name of the view (string) or a DataFrame object representing the view. If a DataFrame is provided, its table name is used as the view name. entity_id (str): The identifier of the entity associated with the view. feature_names (list of str): A list of feature names included in the view. metadata (dict, optional): Additional metadata related to the view. Defaults to an empty dictionary. kwargs: Additional keyword arguments. If 'with_process_id' is provided and set to True, the function also returns the process ID.

tuple or str: If 'with_process_id' is True, returns a tuple containing the SQL query string and the process ID. Otherwise, returns only the SQL query string. The query is for inserting or updating the view details in the feature store.

Note: - The function assumes specific global variables and configurations (like tdfs4ds.END_PERIOD, tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME, etc.) are already set in the environment. - It requires the 'tdml' module for DataFrame operations and 'uuid' for generating unique identifiers.

docs.title: register_process_tdstone
docs.text:
register_process_tdstone(model, metadata={})

Registers a 'tdstone2 view' process in the feature store with specified model details and metadata. This function is designed for registering hyper-segmented models created using the 'tdstone2' Python package. It handles both scenarios where the feature store date is current or in the past.

Parameters: model (Model Object): An instance of the 'tdstone2' model containing necessary details for the registration. metadata (dict, optional): Additional metadata related to the process. Defaults to an empty dictionary.

Returns: str: A SQL query string to insert the process details into the feature store.

Notes: - The 'model' parameter should be an object of the 'tdstone2' model class. - This function generates a unique process identifier for each registration. - It constructs SQL queries for both current valid time and past date scenarios based on 'tdfs4ds' global variables. - The function logs the process registration by printing the process ID.

Dependencies: - 'tdfs4ds' global variables and configurations must be set in the environment. - The 'uuid' and 'json' modules are used for generating unique identifiers and handling metadata, respectively. - This function requires the 'tdstone2' Python package for working with 'tdstone2' models.

docs.title: process_store_catalog_view_creation
docs.text:
process_store_catalog_view_creation()

Constructs SQL queries to replace views related to the process store catalog.

This function generates two SQL queries:

  1. A query to replace a view in the process store catalog with the content of the process catalog table. The query uses the REPLACE VIEW statement and locks rows for access during execution.

  2. A query to replace a view that contains split features from the process catalog table. This query utilizes a text processing function (NGramSplitter) to extract n-grams from a specified text column and creates a view based on the processed data.

Returns:

Name Type Description tuple

A pair of SQL query strings: - query_1: SQL to replace the primary process catalog view. - query_2: SQL to replace the split features view with processed data.

docs.title: process_store_catalog_creation
docs.text:
process_store_catalog_creation(if_exists='replace', comment='this table is a process catalog')

Create or replace a feature store catalog table in Teradata database.

This function creates a catalog table in the specified schema of the Teradata database. The catalog table is used to store information about features, including their names, associated tables, databases, validity periods, etc.

  • if_exists (str, optional): Specifies the behavior if the catalog table already exists. Default is 'replace', which means the existing table will be replaced.
  • comment (str, optional): A comment to describe the catalog table. Default is 'this table is a process catalog'.

Returns: str: The name of the created or replaced catalog table.

Notes: - The schema in which the catalog table will be created should be specified separately. - If the catalog table already exists and if_exists is set to 'replace', the existing table will be dropped and recreated. - This function also creates a secondary index on the 'PROCESS_TYPE' column for faster querying. - The catalog table structure includes columns for process ID, process type, view name, entity ID, feature names, feature version, data domain, metadata, and validity period. - This function relies on external global variables and configurations like 'tdfs4ds' and 'tdml' which should be set in the environment. - It also depends on an 'execute_query' function for executing SQL queries.

Dependencies: - 'tdfs4ds' global variables for schema and table name. - 'tdml' for displaying SQL queries (if configured). - 'execute_query' for executing SQL queries.

docs.title: time_manager_catalog_creation
docs.text:
time_manager_catalog_creation()

Create the FS_TIME_MANAGER catalog table if it does not already exist.

Non-destructive, idempotent counterpart to the FS_TIME_MANAGER block inside :func:process_store_catalog_creation — it NEVER drops an existing table. Used by :func:tdfs4ds.connect to self-heal installs whose process catalog predates v0.3.1.147 (so the FS_V_PROCESS_CATALOG view's LEFT JOIN FS_TIME_MANAGER resolves). The table mirrors FS_FILTER_MANAGER exactly.

Returns:

Name Type Description str

the FS_TIME_MANAGER table name.

docs.title: record_process_time_manager
docs.text:
record_process_time_manager(process_id, time_manager)

Upsert a process → TimeManager association into FS_TIME_MANAGER (v0.3.1.147).

Called from :func:tdfs4ds.roll_out as it drives a time_manager across a process_list — the natural home for the association, since a TimeManager is a roll-out driver (one timeline, many processes), not a registration-time property of a single process like the FilterManager. Running an existing rollout therefore auto-populates the catalog, including for processes registered by tdfs4ds versions predating this table.

Idempotent (UPDATE … ELSE INSERT keyed on PROCESS_ID) and non-fatal — any failure is logged and swallowed so it can never abort a rollout.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.scheduling
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.scheduling — parallel-execution planning for feature-engineering processes (v0.3.1.147).

Public surface:

  • :func:analyze_parallel_plan — seed a list of processes/datasets/features, resolve the upstream process closure, and group it into dependency waves (parallel within a wave, sequential across waves).
  • :func:generate_execution_script / :func:write_execution_script — turn a plan into a standalone, safe (multi-session) Python execution script.
  • :func:unify_filter_managers / :func:unify_time_managers — opt-in repointing of parallel-candidate processes onto a single canonical manager.
  • :func:plot_workflow_pyvis — interactive wave-layered graph of a plan.

docs.title: __all__ module-attribute

docs.title: analyze_parallel_plan
docs.text:
analyze_parallel_plan(seeds=None, all_domains: bool = True, time_managers=None) -> Dict[str, Any]

Compute a parallel-execution plan for feature-engineering processes.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: seeds : list[str] | None Mixed list of process (id or view name) / dataset / feature names. The plan is the upstream closure of the seeds' producing processes (everything needed to (re)build them). None = the whole store. all_domains : bool Read every data domain (default). False restricts to the active tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN. time_managers : ignored placeholder Reserved for supplying explicit TimeManagers to the content-equivalence check when the catalog has no TIME_* association yet.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict seeds, resolved_seeds, unresolved_seeds, closure, processes (per-pid metadata incl. filter/time manager + wave index + lineage_unknown), waves (ordered list of parallel batches), wave_groups (per-wave grouping by (filter, time) manager), serialize_together (write-conflict groups), unify_candidates (equivalent-but-distinct managers), edges (process dependency edges within the closure), cycle (None or a cycle), plus counts.

docs.title: generate_execution_script
docs.text:
generate_execution_script(plan: Dict[str, Any], *, out_path: Optional[str] = None, mode: str = 'process_pool', force_compute: bool = False, max_workers: Optional[int] = None) -> str

Turn an :func:analyze_parallel_plan result into a runnable .py script.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: plan : dict The plan returned by :func:tdfs4ds.analyze_parallel_plan. out_path : str | None If given, also write the script there (see also :func:write_execution_script). mode : {'process_pool', 'sequential'} 'process_pool' (default) parallelises each wave across processes; 'sequential' runs everything in order (debugging). force_compute : bool Passed to run/roll_out in the generated worker. max_workers : int | None Cap on parallel workers per wave (None = one per unit).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: str The script source.

Generate the script and write it to disk, returning the path.

out_path defaults to {tdfs4ds.SCHEDULING_SCRIPT_DIR}/tdfs4ds_execution_<ts>.py. Extra keyword args are forwarded to :func:generate_execution_script.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.scheduling.parallel_plan
docs.text:
Parallel-execution planner for tdfs4ds feature-engineering processes (v0.3.1.147).

Given a seed list of processes / datasets / features, this module resolves each seed to its producing process(es), expands to the upstream process closure (every process the seeds depend on — the self-contained "build set", which doubles as a migration unit), and groups the closure into dependency waves: a topological layering of the process DAG where every process in a wave is mutually independent and can run in parallel, and waves run in order.

The heavy lifting reuses the persisted lineage graph (:func:tdfs4ds.lineage.lineage_store.load_lineage_digraph) and the process / feature / dataset catalogs. The graph algorithms (_build_process_dag / _compute_waves / _upstream_closure) are pure functions over a networkx.DiGraph so they can be unit-tested without a database.

Nothing here mutates the store — see :mod:tdfs4ds.scheduling.unify for the opt-in manager-unification mutators and :mod:tdfs4ds.scheduling.script for turning a plan into an executable script.

docs.title: _process_catalog_df
docs.text:

docs.title: _process_dependency_edges
docs.text:
_process_dependency_edges(G, nodeid_to_pid: Dict[str, str]) -> Set[Tuple[str, str]]

Contract the object lineage graph G onto process nodes.

G edges point parent -> child = downstream -> upstream (a process depends on its children). For each process p we take its transitive upstream objects (networkx.descendants(G, node_p)) and, for every one that is also a process q, emit an edge (q, p) meaning "q is upstream, run q before p". Transitive edges are harmless for the topological layering computed later.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.scheduling.script
docs.text:
Generate a standalone Python script that executes a parallel-execution plan (v0.3.1.147).

The plan produced by :func:tdfs4ds.analyze_parallel_plan is turned into a self-contained .py that runs the closure's processes wave by wave: sequential across waves (dependency order), parallel within a wave. Parallelism uses concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor — one OS process per unit — NOT threads, because tdfs4ds.run and ingestion mutate module-level globals (DATA_DOMAIN / FEATURE_STORE_TIME / RUN_ID / PROCESS_TYPE) and use teradataml's shared SQLAlchemy pool, which corrupts under thread fan-out. Each worker process bootstraps its own Teradata context from the TDFS4DS_TD_* environment, so its global mutations never collide.

generate_execution_script returns the script as a string (inspect without executing — the repo's return_query idiom); write_execution_script saves it under tdfs4ds.SCHEDULING_SCRIPT_DIR.

docs.title: _TEMPLATE module-attribute
docs.text:
_TEMPLATE = '#!/usr/bin/env python
"""
Auto-generated by tdfs4ds {version} on {generated_at}.

Parallel-execution script: {n_processes} process(es) across {n_waves} wave(s).
Waves run sequentially (dependency order); processes within a wave run in
parallel, one OS process each (safe re: tdfs4ds module globals + teradataml
pool). Each worker opens its own Teradata context from TDFS4DS_TD_* env.

Run:   python {basename}
"""
import json
import sys
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor, as_completed

PLAN = json.loads({plan_literal})
FORCE_COMPUTE = {force_compute}
MAX_WORKERS = {max_workers}   # None = one worker per parallel unit
MODE = {mode!r}


def _bootstrap():
    """Open this worker's own Teradata context + tdfs4ds config from env."""
    import tdfs4ds
    from tdfs4ds.utils.reconnect import reconnect_teradata
    ok = reconnect_teradata()
    if not ok:
        raise RuntimeError(
            "reconnect_teradata() failed - set TDFS4DS_TD_HOST/USERNAME/PASSWORD "
            "(and optionally _LOGMECH/_DATABASE) in the environment."
        )
    if PLAN.get("schema"):
        tdfs4ds.SCHEMA = PLAN["schema"]
    if PLAN.get("schema_view"):
        tdfs4ds.SCHEMA_VIEW = PLAN["schema_view"]
    return tdfs4ds


def _execute_pid(pid):
    """Run one process; drive its TimeManager via roll_out when it has one."""
    tdfs4ds = _bootstrap()
    meta = PLAN["processes"].get(pid, {{}})
    dom = meta.get("data_domain")
    if dom:
        tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN = dom
    tm = meta.get("time")
    if tm and tm.get("view"):
        from tdfs4ds.utils.time_management import TimeManager
        tmgr = TimeManager(table_name=tm["view"], schema_name=tm["database"])
        tdfs4ds.roll_out([pid], tmgr, force_compute=FORCE_COMPUTE)
    else:
        tdfs4ds.run(pid, force_compute=FORCE_COMPUTE)
    return pid


def _run_group(pids):
    """Run a serialize-together group sequentially inside a single worker."""
    for pid in pids:
        print("  [proc] running", pid, flush=True)
        _execute_pid(pid)
    return pids


def _wave_units(wave_idx, wave):
    """Group write-conflicting processes into one unit; others are singletons."""
    conflict = {{}}
    for grp in PLAN["serialize_together"]:
        if grp.get("wave") == wave_idx:
            members = [p for p in grp["processes"] if p in wave]
            if len(members) > 1:
                rep = members[0]
                for p in members:
                    conflict[p] = rep
    units, seen = [], set()
    for grp in PLAN["serialize_together"]:
        if grp.get("wave") == wave_idx:
            members = [p for p in grp["processes"] if p in wave and p not in seen]
            if len(members) > 1:
                units.append(members)
                seen.update(members)
    for pid in wave:
        if pid not in seen:
            units.append([pid])
            seen.add(pid)
    return units


def _run_sequential():
    for k, wave in enumerate(PLAN["waves"]):
        print("Wave {{}}: {{}} process(es) (sequential)".format(k, len(wave)), flush=True)
        for pid in wave:
            _execute_pid(pid)
    print("All waves complete.", flush=True)


def _run_parallel():
    for k, wave in enumerate(PLAN["waves"]):
        units = _wave_units(k, wave)
        print("Wave {{}}: {{}} parallel unit(s) / {{}} process(es)".format(k, len(units), len(wave)), flush=True)
        workers = MAX_WORKERS or max(1, len(units))
        with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=workers) as ex:
            futs = {{ex.submit(_run_group, u): u for u in units}}
            for fut in as_completed(futs):
                fut.result()   # re-raise worker failures
    print("All waves complete.", flush=True)


def main():
    print("tdfs4ds parallel execution | {n_waves} wave(s) | {n_processes} process(es)", flush=True)
    if MODE == "sequential":
        _run_sequential()
    else:
        _run_parallel()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
'

docs.title: tdfs4ds.scheduling.unify
docs.text:
Opt-in unification of filter / time managers across parallel-candidate processes (v0.3.1.147).

When :func:tdfs4ds.analyze_parallel_plan reports unify_candidates — distinct FilterManager / TimeManager objects that hold equivalent content — these helpers repoint a set of processes onto a single canonical manager so one driver (one update() / one roll_out timeline) can drive the whole wave.

Repointing a process means two coordinated writes:

  1. Rewrite the process view's manager reference — the process feature view references the manager's public view by name (a scalar subquery or JOIN); _repoint_process_* swaps that fully-qualified reference for the canonical manager's view and re-issues REPLACE VIEW.
  2. Update the catalog row — FS_FILTER_MANAGER / FS_TIME_MANAGER so run() reconstructs the canonical manager.

Everything is opt-in and defaults to dry_run=True (returns the SQL it would run without touching the store). _add_filter_values / _add_time_steps are the "edit an existing manager" primitives used when the managers are compatible (same columns / timeline shape) but hold different rows — they extend the canonical to the union before repointing.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.scheduling.viz
docs.text:
Interactive workflow visualisation for a parallel-execution plan (v0.3.1.147).

plot_workflow_pyvis renders the process DAG of an :func:tdfs4ds.analyze_parallel_plan result as a wave-layered pyvis network: every process node is placed in a band by its wave index (vis.js hierarchical layout, level = wave), so processes in the same band run in parallel and dependency edges run band → band (sequential). This makes "what is parallel vs sequential" visually unambiguous — the exact question the admin "Execution plan" panel answers.

Mirrors :func:tdfs4ds.lineage.lineage_store.plot_lineage_pyvis (lazy pyvis import, self-contained HTML written to output_path).

docs.title: tdfs4ds.serve
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.serve — OpenAI-compatible + LangServe HTTP server for the two agents.

Install the optional dependencies with::

pip install tdfs4ds[serve]

Run the server::

python -m tdfs4ds.serve

See :mod:tdfs4ds.serve.app for the FastAPI factory and :mod:tdfs4ds.serve.openai_router for the OpenAI surface.

Build the FastAPI app.

docs.title: Parameters

Pass False to build the app object for import-only checks (e.g. GET /v1/models needs no database). root_path: External URL path prefix the app is mounted under (e.g. "/myapp" behind a reverse proxy). Defaults to TDFS4DS_SERVE_ROOT_PATH (or empty). FastAPI uses it for OpenAPI/redirects; the admin shell reads it from each request so its assets / fetch calls resolve under the prefix.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.serve.app
docs.text:
FastAPI application factory for the tdfs4ds agent server.

Mounts two surfaces over the same two agents:

  • OpenAI-compatible — /v1/models + /v1/chat/completions (see :mod:tdfs4ds.serve.openai_router). The model field selects the agent.
  • LangServe — /consumer and /query-optimizer with the built-in /invoke, /stream and /playground routes, for interactive debugging.

The consumer / query-optimizer entry points are plain functions, so each is wrapped in a RunnableLambda before being handed to add_routes.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.serve.bootstrap
docs.text:
Runtime bootstrap for the tdfs4ds API server.

Establishes the Teradata connection, lets tdfs4ds load its own TDFS4DS_* configuration (SCHEMA, DATA_DOMAIN, INSTRUCT_MODEL_, EMBEDDING_MODEL_, CHROMA_*, VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND …), and optionally pre-builds the consumer-agent vector index so the first SEARCH does not pay a cold build.

The Teradata connection is not covered by tdfs4ds/config.py — that module loads feature-store / LLM / vector-store settings but never opens a database context. This bootstrap owns the connection and reads it directly from the environment:

TDFS4DS_TD_HOST        Teradata host (required)
TDFS4DS_TD_USERNAME    Teradata user (required)
TDFS4DS_TD_PASSWORD    Teradata password (required)
TDFS4DS_TD_LOGMECH     Logon mechanism (optional, e.g. LDAP / TD2)
TDFS4DS_TD_DATABASE    Default database for the session (optional)
TDFS4DS_TD_ENCRYPT     Enable wire encryption — 'true' or 'false' (optional)

Server-specific flags:

TDFS4DS_SERVE_BUILD_INDEX   'true' (default) builds the vector index at
                            startup; set 'false' for a QO-only deployment.

connect_teradata(host=None, username=None, password=None, logmech=None, database=None, encrypt=None, build_index=None)

Open (or re-open) the Teradata context and run post-connection setup.

Reusable by both the startup bootstrap and the in-app Connect page. Any credential left None falls back to the matching TDFS4DS_TD_* env var. On success, sets the module CONNECTED flag, applies tdfs4ds config and (optionally) builds the vector index. On failure, CONNECTED stays False and LAST_CONNECT_ERROR records the reason — the caller decides whether to raise.

Returns {"connected": bool, "error": str|None}.

docs.title: bootstrap
docs.text:
bootstrap(force: bool = False)

Idempotent, NON-FATAL server bootstrap.

Attempts the Teradata connection from TDFS4DS_TD_* env vars. If it fails (missing creds or unreachable host) the app still boots disconnected — the shell, /health and the in-app Connect page load, and the user can connect at runtime via POST /ui/connect. Safe to call more than once.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.serve.dashboard_report
docs.text:
Render a compelling, self-contained interactive HTML report from a plain data dict describing the feature store (inventory, insight facts, technical storage-table sizes, orphan features, process run-history, freshness alerts, lineage dependency counts).

Same philosophy as :mod:tdfs4ds.benchmark.report: the HTML is fully offline — inline SVG charts + scoped CSS, no JavaScript — so it renders identically in any browser, prints cleanly, embeds directly in the admin panel, and exports to a single .html file. Interactivity is via native <details> expand/collapse.

The builder consumes only JSON-serialisable data (records / numbers), never Plotly figures, so the same document shows on-screen and downloads byte-identical.

Public: :func:build_dashboard_report, :func:write_dashboard_report.

docs.title: _TREEMAP_JS module-attribute
docs.text:
_TREEMAP_JS = '
(function(){
  var wrap = document.currentScript.closest('.tm-wrap');
  if(!wrap || wrap._tmInit) return; wrap._tmInit = true;
  var model = JSON.parse(wrap.querySelector('.tm-data').textContent || '{}');
  var svg = wrap.querySelector('svg.treemap');
  var crumb = wrap.querySelector('.tm-crumb');
  var upBtn = wrap.querySelector('.tm-up');
  var detail = wrap.querySelector('.tm-detail');
  var stack = []; // breadcrumb of {label, nodes, kind}
  function shade(r){ r=Math.max(0,Math.min(1, r||0));
    var a=[255,210,176], b=[194,65,12];
    return 'rgb('+Math.round(a[0]+(b[0]-a[0])*r)+','+Math.round(a[1]+(b[1]-a[1])*r)+','+Math.round(a[2]+(b[2]-a[2])*r)+')'; }
  function esc(s){ return String(s==null?'':s).replace(/&/g,'&').replace(/</g,'<').replace(/>/g,'>').replace(/"/g,'"'); }
  function fmt(n){ try{return Number(n).toLocaleString();}catch(e){return n;} }
  function trunc(s,n){ s=String(s); return s.length<=n?s:s.slice(0,n-1)+'…'; }
  function level(){
    if(stack.length===0) return {kind:'table', nodes:model.tables||[], crumb:'All feature tables'};
    return stack[stack.length-1];
  }
  function render(){
    var L = level(); var nodes=(L.nodes||[]).filter(function(n){return (n.value||0)>0;});
    nodes.sort(function(a,b){return (b.value||0)-(a.value||0);});
    crumb.textContent = L.crumb || '';
    upBtn.disabled = stack.length===0;
    var W=960, H=430, total=nodes.reduce(function(s,n){return s+(n.value||0);},0)||1;
    var nrows=Math.max(1, Math.round(Math.sqrt(nodes.length||1)));
    var per=Math.ceil((nodes.length||1)/nrows);
    var rowsArr=[]; for(var i=0;i<nodes.length;i+=per) rowsArr.push(nodes.slice(i,i+per));
    var out=''; var y=0;
    rowsArr.forEach(function(row){
      var rv=row.reduce(function(s,n){return s+(n.value||0);},0)||1;
      var rh=(rv/total)*H, x=0;
      row.forEach(function(n){
        var w=(n.value/rv)*W; var fits=w>60&&rh>24;
        var canDrill = (L.kind==='table' && (n.processes||[]).length) ||
                       (L.kind==='process' && (n.features||[]).length);
        var fill = n.orphan ? '#fdecea' : shade(n.ratio);
        var stroke = n.orphan ? '#e74c3c' : '#fff';
        var hover = hoverText(L.kind, n);
        out += '<g class="tm-tile'+(canDrill?' tm-can':'')+'" data-i="'+nodes.indexOf(n)+'">'
          + '<rect x="'+x.toFixed(1)+'" y="'+y.toFixed(1)+'" width="'+Math.max(1,w).toFixed(1)
          + '" height="'+Math.max(1,rh).toFixed(1)+'" rx="2" fill="'+fill+'" stroke="'+stroke
          + '" stroke-width="'+(n.orphan?2:1.5)+'"><title>'+esc(hover)+'</title></rect>';
        if(fits){
          out += '<text x="'+(x+7)+'" y="'+(y+18)+'" class="tm-label">'
              + (n.orphan?'⚠ ':'') + esc(trunc(n.label, Math.floor(w/7.5)))+'</text>';
          if(rh>36) out += '<text x="'+(x+7)+'" y="'+(y+34)+'" class="tm-sub">'+fmt(n.value)+(L.kind==='table'?' rows':'')+'</text>';
        }
        out += '</g>';
        x+=w;
      });
      y+=rh;
    });
    svg.innerHTML = out;
    Array.prototype.forEach.call(svg.querySelectorAll('.tm-tile'), function(g){
      g.addEventListener('click', function(){
        var n = nodes[+g.getAttribute('data-i')]; drill(L.kind, n);
      });
    });
    renderDetail(L.kind, nodes);
  }
  function hoverText(kind, n){
    if(kind==='table') return n.label+'\n'+n.table+(n.process_id?('\nprocess '+n.process_id):'')+'\n'+fmt(n.value)+' stored rows';
    if(kind==='process') return n.label+(n.process_id?('\nprocess '+n.process_id):'')+'\n'+(n.features?n.features.length:0)+' features · ~'+fmt(n.value)+' rows';
    return n.label+'\n'+(n.type||'')+(n.entity?(' · '+n.entity):'')+(n.orphan?'\n⚠ orphan (not in catalog)':'')+'\n~'+fmt(n.value)+' rows';
  }
  function drill(kind, n){
    if(kind==='table' && (n.processes||[]).length){
      stack.push({kind:'process', nodes:n.processes, crumb:'Table '+n.label+' › processes'});
      render();
    } else if(kind==='process' && (n.features||[]).length){
      stack.push({kind:'feature', nodes:n.features, crumb:(crumb.textContent)+' › '+n.label+' › features'});
      render();
    }
  }
  function renderDetail(kind, nodes){
    var html='';
    if(kind==='table'){
      html='<table class="data"><thead><tr><th>Process view</th><th>Process id</th><th>Features</th><th>Rows</th></tr></thead><tbody>';
      nodes.forEach(function(n){ html+='<tr><td class="name">'+esc(n.label)+'</td><td class="mono">'+esc(n.process_id||'—')+'</td><td>'+(n.processes?n.processes.reduce(function(s,p){return s+(p.features?p.features.length:0);},0):0)+'</td><td>'+fmt(n.value)+'</td></tr>'; });
      html+='</tbody></table>';
    } else if(kind==='process'){
      html='<table class="data"><thead><tr><th>Process view</th><th>Process id</th><th>#Features</th><th>Rows</th></tr></thead><tbody>';
      nodes.forEach(function(n){ html+='<tr><td class="name">'+esc(n.label)+'</td><td class="mono">'+esc(n.process_id||'—')+'</td><td>'+(n.features?n.features.length:0)+'</td><td>'+fmt(n.value)+'</td></tr>'; });
      html+='</tbody></table>';
    } else {
      html='<table class="data"><thead><tr><th>Feature</th><th>Type</th><th>Entity</th><th>Orphan</th><th>Rows</th></tr></thead><tbody>';
      nodes.forEach(function(n){ html+='<tr><td class="name">'+esc(n.label)+'</td><td>'+esc(n.type||'—')+'</td><td>'+esc(n.entity||'—')+'</td><td>'+(n.orphan?'⚠ yes':'no')+'</td><td>'+fmt(n.value)+'</td></tr>'; });
      html+='</tbody></table>';
    }
    detail.innerHTML = html;
  }
  upBtn.addEventListener('click', function(){ if(stack.length){ stack.pop(); render(); } });
  render();
})();
'

docs.title: _TIMELINE_JS module-attribute
docs.text:
_TIMELINE_JS = '
(function(){
  var wrap = document.currentScript.closest('.tl-wrap');
  if(!wrap || wrap._tlInit) return; wrap._tlInit = true;
  var data = JSON.parse(wrap.querySelector('.tl-data').textContent || '{}');
  var svg = wrap.querySelector('svg.timeline');
  var tip = wrap.querySelector('.tl-tip');
  var rows = (data.rows||[]).map(function(r){
    return {view:r.view, s:Date.parse(r.start), e:Date.parse(r.end||r.start),
            status:r.status, filter:r.filter||''};
  }).filter(function(r){ return !isNaN(r.s); });
  if(!rows.length){ svg.outerHTML='<p class="muted">No run history.</p>'; return; }
  var views=[]; rows.forEach(function(r){ if(views.indexOf(r.view)<0) views.push(r.view); });
  var dmin=Math.min.apply(null, rows.map(function(r){return r.s;}));
  var dmax=Math.max.apply(null, rows.map(function(r){return Math.max(r.e,r.s);}));
  if(dmax<=dmin) dmax=dmin+86400000;
  var view0=dmin, view1=dmax; // current zoom window
  var LW=210, RW=20, RH=20, GAP=6, TOP=8, AXIS=22;
  var W=980, TRACK=W-LW-RW;
  var H=TOP+views.length*(RH+GAP)+AXIS;
  function esc(s){ return String(s==null?'':s).replace(/&/g,'&').replace(/</g,'<').replace(/>/g,'>'); }
  function clr(st){ st=String(st||'').toUpperCase();
    return st.indexOf('COMPLETED')===0?'#27ae60':(st.indexOf('FAIL')===0?'#e74c3c':'#95a5a6'); }
  function x(t){ return LW+((t-view0)/(view1-view0))*TRACK; }
  function fmtT(ms){ var d=new Date(ms); return d.toISOString().slice(0,16).replace('T',' '); }
  function ticks(){
    var span=view1-view0, n=6, out='';
    for(var i=0;i<=n;i++){ var t=view0+span*i/n; var px=LW+(TRACK*i/n);
      out+='<line x1="'+px+'" y1="'+TOP+'" x2="'+px+'" y2="'+(H-AXIS)+'" stroke="#eef1f5"/>'
         +'<text x="'+px+'" y="'+(H-6)+'" text-anchor="middle" class="tm-sub" fill="#5A6B7B">'+esc(fmtT(t))+'</text>';
    }
    return out;
  }
  function render(){
    svg.setAttribute('viewBox','0 0 '+W+' '+H);
    var out=ticks(); var y=TOP;
    views.forEach(function(v){
      out+='<text x="'+(LW-10)+'" y="'+(y+RH/2+4)+'" text-anchor="end" class="tl-label">'+esc(v.length>30?v.slice(0,29)+'…':v)+'</text>';
      out+='<rect x="'+LW+'" y="'+y+'" width="'+TRACK+'" height="'+RH+'" rx="4" fill="#f5f7fa"/>';
      y+=RH+GAP;
    });
    rows.forEach(function(r){
      if(r.e<view0 || r.s>view1) return;
      var yi=views.indexOf(r.view); var ry=TOP+yi*(RH+GAP);
      var xs=Math.max(LW, x(r.s)), xe=Math.min(LW+TRACK, x(Math.max(r.e,r.s)));
      var w=Math.max(2.5, xe-xs);
      out+='<rect class="tl-run" x="'+xs.toFixed(1)+'" y="'+ry+'" width="'+w.toFixed(1)+'" height="'+RH+'" rx="2" fill="'+clr(r.status)+'" '
         +'data-t="'+r.s+'" data-st="'+esc(r.status)+'" data-f="'+esc(r.filter)+'" data-v="'+esc(r.view)+'"/>';
    });
    svg.innerHTML=out;
    Array.prototype.forEach.call(svg.querySelectorAll('.tl-run'), function(el){
      el.addEventListener('mousemove', function(ev){
        tip.style.display='block';
        var b=wrap.getBoundingClientRect();
        tip.style.left=(ev.clientX-b.left+12)+'px'; tip.style.top=(ev.clientY-b.top+12)+'px';
        tip.innerHTML='<b>'+esc(el.getAttribute('data-v'))+'</b><br>'+esc(fmtT(+el.getAttribute('data-t')))
          +'<br>'+esc(el.getAttribute('data-st'))
          +(el.getAttribute('data-f')?('<br>filter: '+esc(el.getAttribute('data-f'))):'');
      });
      el.addEventListener('mouseleave', function(){ tip.style.display='none'; });
    });
  }
  // wheel zoom (centered on cursor)
  svg.addEventListener('wheel', function(ev){
    ev.preventDefault();
    var b=svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    var frac=Math.min(1,Math.max(0,((ev.clientX-b.left)/b.width*W - LW)/TRACK));
    var c=view0+(view1-view0)*frac;
    var k=ev.deltaY<0?0.8:1.25;
    var nv0=c-(c-view0)*k, nv1=c+(view1-c)*k;
    if(nv1-nv0>60000){ view0=Math.max(dmin,nv0); view1=Math.min(dmax,nv1); render(); }
  }, {passive:false});
  // drag to zoom
  var dragX=null, band=null;
  svg.addEventListener('mousedown', function(ev){ dragX=ev.clientX;
    band=document.createElementNS('http://www.w3.org/2000/svg','rect'); });
  svg.addEventListener('mouseup', function(ev){
    if(dragX!==null){
      var b=svg.getBoundingClientRect();
      var f0=Math.min(dragX,ev.clientX), f1=Math.max(dragX,ev.clientX);
      if(f1-f0>6){
        var t0=view0+(view1-view0)*Math.max(0,((f0-b.left)/b.width*W-LW)/TRACK);
        var t1=view0+(view1-view0)*Math.min(1,((f1-b.left)/b.width*W-LW)/TRACK);
        if(t1-t0>60000){ view0=t0; view1=t1; render(); }
      }
      dragX=null;
    }
  });
  svg.addEventListener('dblclick', function(){ view0=dmin; view1=dmax; render(); });
  wrap.querySelector('.tl-reset').addEventListener('click', function(){ view0=dmin; view1=dmax; render(); });
  render();
})();
'

docs.title: _GUIDE module-attribute
docs.text: _GUIDE = {'storage': "<p>Each <code>FS_T_*</code> table physically stores several features in one table. The bar/tile size is the table's <b>NONSEQUENCED VALIDTIME</b> row count — every temporal version, current and superseded.</p><ul><li><span class='o'>Orange</span> = <b>CURRENT VALIDTIME</b> rows (the live slice served today). <span class='n'>Navy</span> = <b>history</b> rows (superseded versions). A tall navy segment means deep temporal history — candidate for review if storage is a concern.</li><li>The treemap tile area is the stored-row share; deeper orange = a higher history ratio.</li><li>Counts come from the last <i>Build storage counts</i> pass and run fast when stats/PI/partitions are in place.</li></ul>", 'orphans': '<p>An <b>orphan</b> is a <code>FEATURE_ID</code> physically present in a storage table but no longer in <code>FS_FEATURE_CATALOG</code> — a feature de-registered without purging its stored rows. This is <b>report-only</b>; nothing is deleted.</p><p>Orphan rows still consume space and history depth. Investigate before purging.</p>', 'run_history': "<p>The execution record of each registered process from the follow-up table.</p><ul><li><b>Timeline</b>: each bar is one run, positioned on a shared time axis and coloured <span class='g'>green=completed</span> / <span class='r'>red=failed</span> / <span class='y'>grey=running</span>. Gaps reveal cadence; clustered red reveals instability.</li><li><b>Calendar</b>: day × process — a quick scan for stale processes (no recent cells) and failure days.</li></ul>", 'freshness': '<p>A process that runs <b>segmented</b> (a FilterManager or date-part loop) can be fresh for one segment and stale for another. These alerts flag the <b>stalest segment</b> per process, so a partially-stale process is never read as fully fresh.</p>', 'lineage': '<p>How connected each process/dataset is in the persisted lineage graph (<code>FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH</code>): <b>upstream</b> = objects it depends on, <b>downstream</b> = objects that depend on it. High downstream = high blast radius: a change here re-documents / can break many consumers.</p>'}

docs.title: CSS module-attribute
docs.text:
CSS = '
*{box-sizing:border-box}
.td-report{--navy:#00233C;--orange:#FF5F00;--ink:#2c3e50;--muted:#5A6B7B;
  --line:#E3E8EE;--bg:#f5f7fa;--panel:#ffffff;
  font:15px/1.55 system-ui,-apple-system,"Segoe UI",Roboto,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif;
  color:var(--ink);max-width:1080px;margin:0 auto;padding:8px 4px 60px}
.td-report h1{margin:.2em 0 .1em;font-size:28px;letter-spacing:-.02em;color:#fff}
.td-report h2{margin:0 0 6px;font-size:19px;color:var(--navy);letter-spacing:-.01em}
.td-report h4{margin:16px 0 8px;font-size:12px;color:var(--muted);
  text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.06em}
.td-report .muted{color:var(--muted);margin:.2em 0 12px;font-size:13.5px}
.td-report code{background:#eef1f5;border-radius:5px;padding:1px 6px;
  font-family:ui-monospace,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;font-size:12.5px;color:#c0392b}

.td-report header.hero{background:linear-gradient(135deg,#00233C,#004069);
  border-radius:16px;padding:26px 30px;margin-bottom:22px;position:relative;overflow:hidden}
.td-report header.hero:before{content:"";position:absolute;right:-50px;top:-50px;
  width:200px;height:200px;background:radial-gradient(circle,rgba(255,95,0,.30),transparent 65%)}
.td-report .kicker{letter-spacing:.16em;text-transform:uppercase;font-size:11.5px;
  color:var(--orange);font-weight:700}
.td-report .schema{font-size:17px;color:#fff;font-weight:600;margin-top:2px}
.td-report .meta-line{color:#bcd0e0;font-size:13px;margin-top:10px;display:flex;
  flex-wrap:wrap;gap:6px 18px}
.td-report .meta-line b{color:#fff;font-weight:600}

.td-report .kpi-grid{display:grid;grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fit,minmax(150px,1fr));
  gap:13px;margin:0 0 20px}
.td-report .kpi{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:13px;
  padding:15px 16px;position:relative;box-shadow:0 1px 4px rgba(0,35,60,.06)}
.td-report .kpi:before{content:"";position:absolute;left:0;top:13px;bottom:13px;width:3px;
  border-radius:3px;background:var(--orange)}
.td-report .kpi-label{font-size:11.5px;color:var(--muted);text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.05em}
.td-report .kpi-value{font-size:25px;font-weight:800;margin-top:5px;color:var(--navy)}
.td-report .kpi-sub{font-size:11.5px;color:var(--muted);margin-top:3px}

.td-report section.card{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);
  border-radius:15px;padding:20px 22px;margin:18px 0;box-shadow:0 1px 4px rgba(0,35,60,.05)}

.td-report .facts{display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:12px}
.td-report .fact{flex:1;min-width:210px;background:#f8fafc;border:1px solid var(--line);
  border-radius:11px;padding:13px 16px}
.td-report .fact-h{font-weight:700;color:var(--navy);font-size:.92rem}
.td-report .fact-d{color:#334155;font-size:.85rem;margin-top:4px}

.td-report table.data{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;margin-top:6px;font-size:13.5px}
.td-report table.data th{text-align:right;color:var(--muted);font-weight:600;font-size:11.5px;
  text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:.04em;padding:6px 10px;border-bottom:2px solid var(--orange)}
.td-report table.data th:first-child{text-align:left}
.td-report table.data td{text-align:right;padding:7px 10px;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
.td-report table.data td.name{text-align:left;font-weight:600;color:var(--navy)}
.td-report table.data tr:hover td{background:#f8fafc}
.td-report .mono{font-family:ui-monospace,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;font-size:12.5px;color:#334155}

.td-report .chart-wrap{margin-top:10px}
.td-report svg.barchart,.td-report svg.treemap,.td-report svg.timeline{width:100%;height:auto;display:block}
.td-report .bar-label{fill:var(--muted);font-size:12.5px}
.td-report .bar-track{fill:#eef1f5}
.td-report .bar-value{fill:var(--navy);font-size:12px;font-weight:600}
/* Treemap tiles: dark text on a legible orange for contrast */
.td-report .tm-label{fill:#00233C;font-size:12px;font-weight:700}
.td-report .tm-sub{fill:#00233C;font-size:11px;opacity:.8}
.td-report svg.timeline .tm-sub{font-size:10px}

/* Drillable treemap widget */
.td-report .tm-wrap{margin-top:10px}
.td-report .tm-bc{display:flex;align-items:center;gap:10px;margin-bottom:8px;font-size:13px;color:var(--navy)}
.td-report .tm-up{cursor:pointer;border:1px solid var(--line);background:#fff;border-radius:6px;
  padding:3px 10px;font-size:12px;color:var(--navy)}
.td-report .tm-up:disabled{opacity:.4;cursor:default}
.td-report .tm-crumb{font-weight:600}
.td-report svg.treemap .tm-can{cursor:pointer}
.td-report svg.treemap .tm-tile rect{transition:opacity .12s}
.td-report svg.treemap .tm-can:hover rect{opacity:.82;stroke:#00233C;stroke-width:2}
.td-report .tm-detail{margin-top:12px}

/* Calendar — small dedicated labels so the dense grid stays readable */
.td-report .cal-label{fill:var(--muted);font-size:10.5px}
.td-report .cal-head{fill:var(--muted);font-size:9.5px}

/* Interactive timeline */
.td-report .tl-wrap{position:relative;margin-top:10px}
.td-report .tl-ctl{display:flex;align-items:center;gap:12px;margin-bottom:6px}
.td-report .tl-reset{cursor:pointer;border:1px solid var(--line);background:#fff;border-radius:6px;
  padding:3px 10px;font-size:12px;color:var(--navy)}
.td-report .tl-hint{font-size:11.5px;color:var(--muted)}
.td-report .tl-label{fill:var(--muted);font-size:11.5px}
.td-report svg.timeline .tl-run{cursor:pointer}
.td-report svg.timeline .tl-run:hover{stroke:#00233C;stroke-width:1.5}
.td-report .tl-tip{position:absolute;z-index:5;pointer-events:none;background:#00233C;color:#fff;
  font-size:11.5px;line-height:1.4;padding:6px 9px;border-radius:7px;max-width:320px;
  box-shadow:0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,.25)}

.td-report .notice{padding:11px 14px;border-radius:10px;background:#f8fafc;
  border:1px solid var(--line);font-size:13.5px;color:var(--muted)}
.td-report .notice-warn{border-color:rgba(231,76,60,.4);background:rgba(231,76,60,.08);color:#a93226}
.td-report .notice-ok{border-color:rgba(39,174,96,.4);background:rgba(39,174,96,.08);color:#1e8449}

.td-report .legend{display:flex;gap:16px;flex-wrap:wrap;margin-top:8px;font-size:12.5px;color:var(--muted)}
.td-report .legend span{display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:6px}
.td-report .dot{width:11px;height:11px;border-radius:3px;display:inline-block}

.td-report details.plan{margin-top:12px}
.td-report details.plan>summary{cursor:pointer;color:var(--orange);font-size:13px;font-weight:600}
.td-report details.explain{margin-top:14px;border-top:1px dashed var(--line);padding-top:10px}
.td-report details.explain>summary{cursor:pointer;color:var(--orange);font-size:13px;
  font-weight:600;list-style:none;display:inline-flex;align-items:center;gap:7px;user-select:none}
.td-report details.explain>summary::-webkit-details-marker{display:none}
.td-report details.explain>summary:before{content:"?";display:inline-flex;align-items:center;
  justify-content:center;width:18px;height:18px;border-radius:50%;background:rgba(255,95,0,.15);

docs.title: tdfs4ds.serve.openai_router
docs.text:
OpenAI-compatible HTTP surface for the two tdfs4ds agents.

Exposes the minimal slice of the OpenAI API that chat clients (the openai SDK, Open WebUI, LibreChat, …) need:

GET  /v1/models               → list the two model ids
POST /v1/chat/completions      → run the selected agent

The model field of a chat-completions request selects the agent:

"tdfs4ds-consumer"          → consumer_agent()        (feature-store RAG)
"tdfs4ds-query-optimizer"   → query_optimizer_agent() (SQL EXPLAIN/optimize)

The latest user message becomes the agent's question. For the query optimizer the user pastes the SQL inline in that message — the optimizer parses it out of the turn, so no schema extension is needed.

Conversation memory is owned by the agents (LangGraph MemorySaver keyed by thread_id). A request that wants continuity sets the OpenAI user field to a stable id; otherwise each request gets a fresh thread_id and is effectively stateless.

docs.title: tdfs4ds.serve.panels
docs.text:
Gradio-free panel logic for the tdfs4ds admin web shell.

Each function here returns plain JSON-serialisable data or HTML strings — never gradio objects — so they can back FastAPI routes (see :mod:tdfs4ds.serve.app). The heavy lifting (Plotly figures, business-dictionary queries, optimiser, evaluation) is delegated to the already-module-level builder functions in :mod:tdfs4ds.agent.chatbot; this module only orchestrates them and strips the Gradio presentation layer.

docs.title: FilterManager
docs.text:
A utility for managing dynamic, versioned filter sets as database-backed views.

The FilterManager enables lightweight scenario management by storing multiple filter definitions in a hidden Teradata table and exposing a public view that dynamically switches between them by filter_id. Each row in the hidden table represents a complete filter configuration. The active configuration is controlled by updating the view definition rather than rewriting table data.

Key Features
  • Store multiple filter states (scenarios) indexed by filter_id
  • Switch filter states instantly by updating a view
  • Optionally include time-based slicing using a BUSINESS_DATE column
  • Clone filters between managers (soft or hard clone modes)
  • Prune obsolete filters to control table size
  • Retrieve current and historical filter definitions
Workflow Overview
  1. Create a FilterManager pointing to a target view name.
  2. Load one or more filter definitions using load_filter().
  3. Switch active filters using update(filter_id).
  4. Inspect the active filter via display() or view DDL.
  5. Optionally prune or clone filters as needed.
How It Works Internally
  • A hidden table named <view_name>_HIDDEN stores filter definitions.
  • A Teradata view named <view_name> exposes only the active filter row.
  • Each filter automatically receives a sequential filter_id (ROW_NUMBER() ordering ensures deterministic assignment).
  • If time-based filtering is used via time_column, a BUSINESS_DATE column is added and projected in all operations.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default table_name str

Public view name to manage or create.

required schema_name str

Teradata schema where artifacts will be created.

required filter_id_name str

Name of the filter ID column. Defaults to 'filter_id'.

'filter_id' time_column str

Optional name of a timestamp column from input DataFrames that maps to a BUSINESS_DATE column for time-aware filters.

None

Attributes:

Name Type Description schema_name str

Target schema for view and hidden table.

table_name str

Name of hidden table storing filters (auto-suffixed with _HIDDEN).

view_name str

Name of public view pointing to current filter.

filter_id_name str

Column containing filter ID.

nb_filters int | None

Number of stored filters (None until initialized).

col_names list[str] | None

Columns projected by the view (data columns only).

time_filtering bool | None

True if time-based filtering enabled.

Notes
  • Database objects are only created when load_filter() is first called.
  • Safe for iterative pipeline runs—auto-detects existing artifacts.
  • Designed for large production tables and Teradata-native workflows.

docs.title: __init__
docs.text:
__init__(table_name, schema_name, filter_id_name='filter_id', time_column=None, storage=None, ofs_map='TD_MAP2')

Initialize the FilterManager.

If the hidden table/view already exist, metadata (column names, maximum filter id, and time filtering status) are detected and cached. If they do not exist yet, attributes are initialized but no objects are created until load_filter() is called.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default storage

Where to create the hidden backing table when this manager (re)creates it. None (default) = system default (block storage, unchanged DDL); 'OFS' = Object File System on Vantage Cloud Lake (MAP = <ofs_map>, STORAGE = TD_OFSSTORAGE). Per-manager, so block and OFS managers can coexist in one session. Only affects table creation; reading an existing table is unchanged. NOTE: for a tdstone2-Apply view, storage='OFS' is necessary but not sufficient on its own — the ACC also rejects scalar subqueries against this filter (Error 6881); reference it via a JOIN instead. OFS + JOIN scores; OFS + scalar subquery still 6881s (see tdfs4ds.utils.storage).

None ofs_map

Map paired with STORAGE = TD_OFSSTORAGE when storage='OFS'.

'TD_MAP2'

docs.title: load_filter
docs.text:
load_filter(df, primary_index=None, time_column=None)

Load a new filter set into the hidden table and (re)point the public view at filter_id=1.

Each row in df is assigned a deterministic filter_id based on ROW_NUMBER() over the ordered set of its columns (plus BUSINESS_DATE when time filtering is enabled). If time_column is provided, values from that column are copied into BUSINESS_DATE and the view will include that time dimension.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default df DataFrame

Incoming filter definitions (one row per filter).

required primary_index list[str]

Primary index columns for the hidden table. Defaults to ['filter_id'] when omitted.

None time_column str

Name of the time column in df to map into BUSINESS_DATE. If provided, time-based filtering is enabled.

None

Raises:

Type Description ValueError

If time_column is provided but not present in df.

docs.title: get_column_types
docs.text:
get_column_types(df, columns)

Retrieve the column types for specified columns from a TeradataML DataFrame.

This function retrieves the data types of specified columns in a TeradataML DataFrame. It is tailored to work with DataFrames that have specific attributes like _td_column_names_and_types and _td_column_names_and_sqlalchemy_types, which are not standard in typical pandas DataFrames.

Parameters: - df (DataFrame): The TeradataML DataFrame from which to get the column types. - columns (list or str): A list of column names or a single column name whose types are to be retrieved.

Returns: dict: A dictionary where keys are column names and values are their types, including length and character set for character columns (CHAR / VARCHAR).

Notes: - This function is designed to work with TeradataML DataFrames, which may have extended column type information. - For character columns (CHAR and VARCHAR), it retrieves the exact SQL type — including length and character set — by delegating to get_feature_types_sql_format. This is critical for entity / primary-index columns: a CHAR(14) key must not be truncated to CHAR(1) when the feature store table is created from this dict.

Dependencies: - TeradataML DataFrame containing attributes '_td_column_names_and_types' and '_td_column_names_and_sqlalchemy_types'.

docs.title: get_column_types_simple
docs.text:
get_column_types_simple(df, columns=None)

Retrieve simplified column types for specified columns from a DataFrame.

This function simplifies the data types of the specified columns in a DataFrame, translating database-specific data types (such as INTEGER, BYTEINT, etc.) into more generalized Python data types (e.g., int, float). It assumes the DataFrame has a specific attribute '_td_column_names_and_types' that stores column names and their types.

Parameters: - df (DataFrame): The DataFrame from which to get the column types. - columns (list or str): A list of column names or a single column name whose types are to be retrieved.

Returns: dict: A dictionary where keys are column names and values are simplified Python data types.

Notes: - This function is designed to work with DataFrames that have a specific attribute '_td_column_names_and_types'. - It uses a mapping from specific database column types to simplified Python data types for simplification.

Dependencies: - DataFrame containing the '_td_column_names_and_types' attribute.

docs.title: get_feature_types_sql_format
docs.text:
get_feature_types_sql_format(tddf, columns=None)

Retrieve the SQL data types of specified columns from a Teradata dataframe.

This function executes a query to fetch the SQL data types of the given columns and returns them in a dictionary format.

Parameters: tddf (tdml.DataFrame): A Teradata dataframe. columns (list): List of column names to retrieve their data types.

Returns: dict: A dictionary where keys are column names and values are their corresponding SQL data types.

Example:

tdml.load_example_data("GLM", ["admissions_train"]) df = tdml.DataFrame("admissions_train") get_feature_types_sql_format(df, columns=df.columns) {'id': 'INTEGER', 'masters': 'VARCHAR(5)', 'gpa': 'FLOAT', 'stats': 'VARCHAR(30)', 'programming': 'VARCHAR(30)', 'admitted': 'INTEGER'}

df.to_sql(table_name='test_admission_train', types={'stats': tdml.VARCHAR(length=40, charset='UNICODE')}, if_exists='replace') df = tdml.DataFrame("test_admission_train") get_feature_types_sql_format(df, columns=df.columns) {'id': 'INTEGER', 'masters': 'VARCHAR(5)', 'gpa': 'FLOAT', 'stats': 'VARCHAR(40) CHARACTER SET UNICODE', 'programming': 'VARCHAR(30)', 'admitted': 'INTEGER'}

docs.title: _parse_cast_query_types
docs.text:
_parse_cast_query_types(sql_types)

Parse the select CAST("col" AS <type>) AS col, ... from ... query text produced by get_feature_types_sql_format into a {column: sql_type} dict.

The exact SQL type — including length and character set — is preserved for every column (e.g. CHAR(14), VARCHAR(40) CHARACTER SET UNICODE, DECIMAL(38,19)). Extracted as a standalone, DB-free helper so the parsing can be unit-tested without a live Teradata connection.

docs.title: update_varchar_length
docs.text:
update_varchar_length(feature_types: dict, new_varchar_length: int) -> dict

Updates the length of all VARCHAR fields in the feature_types dictionary based on an increment. The new length is calculated as ceil(previous_length / new_varchar_length) * new_varchar_length, ensuring that when new_varchar_length is equal to the current length, no change occurs.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default feature_types dict

A dictionary where keys are feature names and values are dictionaries with 'type' and 'id'.

required new_varchar_length int

The increment value for adjusting VARCHAR lengths.

required

Returns:

Name Type Description dict dict

A dictionary with updated VARCHAR lengths.

Issues a warning if the new length is smaller than the original length.
