# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# tdfs4ds.process_store.process_query_administration.remove_process(process_id)

print('tdfs4ds version:', tdfs4ds.__version__)

tdml.remove_context()

docs.title: Follow-up status values
docs.text:
Status Meaning COMPLETED Process ran successfully RUNNING Currently executing, or crashed before writing FAILED FAILED,... Exception raised; suffix contains the error message

A stale RUNNING record after a crash means followup_close did not execute — the feature data for that run is incomplete.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• All catalog queries are scoped to DATA_DOMAIN — always set it before querying.
  • get_process_id(view_name=...) looks up by the crystallized view name (e.g. 'FEAT_ENG_CUST'), not the feature or entity name.
  • remove_process removes only catalog metadata; underlying temporal feature tables and data are preserved.

docs.title: fs-lineage-graph
docs.text:
Maintain the persisted lineage graph (FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH): full/scoped rebuild, downstream EXPLAIN impact check, re-document the impact slice, and render an interactive pyvis network.

name: Feature Store: Persisted Lineage Graph description: For a developer maintaining the persisted lineage graph (FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH). Build or fully refresh the graph, scoped-refresh just the objects you edited, check which downstream processes still compile after a change, re-document from the persisted graph, and render an interactive pyvis network. Invoke when the user edited a view/process and wants to refresh lineage/documentation without re-deriving everything, or asks "what depends on this?" / "did my change break anything downstream?". SKIP for a one-off on-the-fly Sankey from a single process — use fs-lineage; SKIP for natural-language business-user Q&A — use ca-lineage. argument-hint: [object name(s) you changed, or empty for a full rebuild] allowed-tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep

Generate Python to manage the persisted, temporal lineage graph FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH. Unlike fs-lineage (which re-derives lineage on the fly for one seed), this graph is stored once and queried in-memory with networkx — so you get the reverse / impact direction ("what depends on X?") cheaply, and can re-document only the affected slice after an edit.

User context: $ARGUMENTS

docs.title: Related skills
docs.text:
Step Skill Why One-off lineage fs-lineage On-the-fly dependency graph + Sankey for a single process/dataset (no persistence). Documentation fs-document Document a single process; this skill re-documents the whole store or an impact slice. Find objects fs-inspect List process/dataset catalogs to pick object names. Business Q&A ca-lineage Natural-language "where does this come from?" through the consumer agent.

Ask for any missing values before generating code: - Objects — the object name(s) you edited (for a scoped refresh), or none for a full rebuild. Names are case / "-quote / db.name insensitive and verified (a typo raises). - LLM config — only needed if rebuild_documentation will run; lineage + impact check are deterministic and need no model.

Emit the relevant pattern, substituting real values:

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import teradataml as tdml
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# Teradata credentials from .env (TDML_*). TDFS4DS_* settings are auto-loaded by `import tdfs4ds`.
load_dotenv()
Param = {
    'host'              : os.getenv('TDML_HOST'),
    'user'              : os.getenv('TDML_USERNAME'),
    'password'          : os.getenv('TDML_PASSWORD'),
    'database'          : os.getenv('TDML_DATABASE'),
    'temp_database_name': os.getenv('TDML_TEMP_DATABASE'),
}
tdml.create_context(**Param)

import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.connect(database=Param['database'], create_if_missing=True)
tdfs4ds.select_data_domain('<DATA_DOMAIN>')   # or set TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN in .env

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Full rebuild / init  — wipe and re-derive the whole graph from every
#    registered process view + dataset (all data domains).
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
n_edges = tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph()          # all_data_domains=True
print(f"persisted {n_edges} edges")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Scoped refresh  — you edited a (possibly NON-process) view. Refresh only
#    its edges (no global wipe) and check whether downstream processes still
#    compile.  Names are verified; a typo raises ValueError.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=['<view_you_changed>'], check=True)

# Standalone impact check (no graph write): run EXPLAIN SELECT * on every
# registered process downstream of the change — verifies the query still
# COMPILES (a changed view's result columns can silently break consumers).
res = tdfs4ds.check_downstream_processes(['<view_you_changed>'])
print("checked:", res['checked'], "| ok:", len(res['ok']), "| broken:", len(res['broken']))
for b in res['broken']:
    print("  BROKEN:", b['view'], "->", b['error'])   # fix these processes

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Query the persisted graph in-memory (networkx — one table read, no SHOW VIEW)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
from tdfs4ds.lineage import get_descendants, get_ancestors, get_persisted_graph

get_descendants('<DB>', '<OBJECT>')   # impact: everything that depends on it
get_ancestors('<DB>', '<OBJECT>')     # upstream sources it depends on

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Re-document from the persisted graph (needs an instruct model configured)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Whole store, each process/dataset documented exactly once:
tdfs4ds.genai.rebuild_documentation()                       # force_update=False
# Only the impact slice of an edited object (object + all downstream), force-regenerated:
tdfs4ds.genai.rebuild_documentation(objects=['<view_you_changed>'])

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. Interactive pyvis visualisation (writes a self-contained HTML file)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
from tdfs4ds.lineage import plot_lineage_pyvis

plot_lineage_pyvis(get_persisted_graph(), output_path='lineage.html')                 # whole graph
plot_lineage_pyvis(get_persisted_graph(seed='<DB>.<OBJECT>'), output_path='impact.html')  # neighbourhood

tdml.remove_context()

docs.title: What each function does
docs.text: Call Effect rebuild_lineage_graph() Wipe + re-derive the whole graph from every process view + dataset. rebuild_lineage_graph(objects=[...], check=True) Refresh only those objects' edges (no wipe) + run the downstream compile check. check_downstream_processes([...]) EXPLAIN SELECT * on every registered process downstream → {checked, ok, broken:[{view,process_id,error}]}. get_descendants(db, obj) / get_ancestors(db, obj) In-memory impact / upstream sets (one table read). rebuild_documentation() / rebuild_documentation(objects=[...]) Document the whole store, or only the impact slice of edited objects (force-regenerated). plot_lineage_pyvis(graph, output_path=...) Interactive HTML network (datasets chocolate, views steelblue, tables seagreen).

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Edges are parent → child = parent depends on child. get_descendants is the impact direction (what breaks if this changes); get_ancestors is the sources direction.
  • Object names are normalised (quotes stripped, upper-cased, db.name split) and verified against the persisted graph then DBC.TablesV. An unrecognised name raises ValueError — never a silent no-op.
  • The graph is created when missing by setup() / connect(create_if_missing=True); run rebuild_lineage_graph() once to populate it on a pre-existing store.
  • rebuild_documentation needs tdfs4ds.INSTRUCT_MODEL_PROVIDER + ..._MODEL set; lineage refresh and check_downstream_processes are deterministic and need no model.
  • tdstone2 TDS_* objects (scoring views, model/code repositories) are legitimate upstream nodes and appear in the graph — they are not feature-store internals.

docs.title: fs-lineage
docs.text:
Build a dependency graph and render an interactive Sankey diagram.

name: Feature Store: Lineage description: For a developer who needs generated Python code and a Sankey diagram. Build the dependency graph for a registered process or dataset view, display primary index and partition columns for every source table, and render a Sankey diagram. Invoke when the user wants to understand data lineage or which tables a process depends on. SKIP for consumer-agent Q&A in natural language — use ca-lineage instead. argument-hint: [process-id or dataset-view-name] allowed-tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep

Generate Python code to build and visualise the dependency graph for a registered tdfs4ds process or dataset view. The graph reveals source tables, their primary index columns, and their partition columns.

User context: $ARGUMENTS

docs.title: Related skills
docs.text:
Step Skill Why Bigger picture fs-analyze Same dependency graph plus EXPLAIN score and FilterManager rewrite proposal. LLM docs fs-document Document the process this graph belongs to. Find process fs-inspect List the process catalog to pick a process ID. Consumer fs-agent Lineage is also reachable through the conversational agent (LINEAGE intent).

Ask for any missing values before generating code: - Target — a process_id (UUID) or a dataset view name to analyse. - LLM config — only needed if document_process will also be called; skip if just doing lineage.

Emit the following pattern, substituting real values:

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import teradataml as tdml
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# Teradata credentials from .env (TDML_*). TDFS4DS_* settings (DATA_DOMAIN,
# SCHEMA, etc.) are auto-loaded from the same .env by `import tdfs4ds`.
load_dotenv()
Param = {
    'host'              : os.getenv('TDML_HOST'),
    'user'              : os.getenv('TDML_USERNAME'),
    'password'          : os.getenv('TDML_PASSWORD'),
    'database'          : os.getenv('TDML_DATABASE'),
    'temp_database_name': os.getenv('TDML_TEMP_DATABASE'),
}
tdml.create_context(**Param)

import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.connect(database=Param['database'], create_if_missing=True)
tdfs4ds.select_data_domain('<DATA_DOMAIN>')   # or set TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN in .env

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Retrieve the process SQL
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
from tdfs4ds.process_store.process_store_catalog_management import get_process_info

process_info = get_process_info('<PROCESS_ID>')
sql = process_info['PROCESS_SQL']

# Alternatively, retrieve the SQL for a dataset view directly:
# sql = tdml.execute_sql("SHOW VIEW <DATASET_VIEW_NAME>").fetchall()[0][0].replace('\r', '\n')

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Build the dependency graph
#    Each node in graph['nodes'] contains:
#      - object_type: 'table' or 'view'
#      - table_info:
#          primary_index_columns: list of PI column names
#          partition_columns:     list of partition column names
#          partitioning_levels:   detailed per-level partition info
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
from tdfs4ds.lineage import build_teradata_dependency_graph

graph = build_teradata_dependency_graph(sql_query=sql)

# Inspect nodes and their table info
for name, node in graph['nodes'].items():
    print(f"\nTable/View: {name}")
    print(f"  Type            : {node['object_type']}")
    if node.get('table_info'):
        ti = node['table_info']
        print(f"  Primary Index   : {ti['primary_index_columns']}")
        print(f"  Partition cols  : {ti['partition_columns']}")
        if ti['partitioning_levels']:
            for lvl in ti['partitioning_levels']:
                print(f"    Level {lvl['level']}: {lvl['kind']} on {lvl['columns']}")

print("\nEdges:", graph['edges'])

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Sankey visualisation
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
from tdfs4ds.lineage import plot_lineage_sankey, show_plotly_robust

fig = plot_lineage_sankey(graph, title='<TITLE>')

# Display inline (Jupyter):
fig.show()

# Or save to HTML:
# show_plotly_robust(fig, html_path='lineage.html')

tdml.remove_context()

docs.title: What the graph output tells you
docs.text: Field Meaning primary_index_columns Columns Teradata uses to distribute rows across AMPs. Filtering on these avoids full-table scans. partition_columns Columns used for partition elimination. Filtering on these skips irrelevant partitions. partitioning_levels Multi-level partition details — RANGE_N, CASE_N, or COLUMNAR per level. edges Directed dependency edges (target, source). roots Top-level targets with no dependents.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Use tdfs4ds.select_data_domain(...) as an alternative to tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN = ...; both are equivalent.
  • get_process_info requires create_if_missing=True on connect() or the documentation tables must already exist.
  • If table_info is None for a node, the table DDL could not be retrieved (view, temp table, or permission issue).
  • For dataset lineage, use SHOW VIEW <view_name> to get the SQL, then pass it to build_teradata_dependency_graph.

docs.title: fs-rollout
docs.text:
Backfill features across a date range with TimeManager + roll_out.

name: Feature Store: Rollout description: Generate code to backfill features across a date range using TimeManager and roll_out. Invoke when the user wants to simulate historical ingestion or re-compute features over past periods. argument-hint: [process-view-name][date-column] [start-id][end-id] allowed-tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep

Generate Python code to step through time with TimeManager and execute registered feature engineering processes over a date range with roll_out.

User context: $ARGUMENTS

docs.title: Related skills
docs.text:
Step Skill Why Before fs-upload Processes must be registered before roll_out can iterate over them. Sibling fs-filter Combine TimeManager with FilterManager for date × partition iteration. Monitor fs-inspect Watch follow_up_report() while the roll-out advances. Diagnose fs-analyze If a roll-out step is slow, run the scalability analysis on its process.

Ask for any missing values before generating code: - Process view name(s) — the crystallized view name(s) used to look up process IDs (e.g. 'FEAT_ENG_CUST'). - Time table name — Teradata table holding the business date sequence (e.g. 'BUSINESS_DATE'). - Date column — column in the source table that provides time steps (e.g. 'Date_transaction'). - Start / end time IDs — integer positions in the time sequence defining the roll-out range.

Emit the following pattern, substituting real values for every <PLACEHOLDER>:

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import teradataml as tdml
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# Teradata credentials from .env (TDML_*). TDFS4DS_* settings (DATA_DOMAIN,
# SCHEMA, etc.) are auto-loaded from the same .env by `import tdfs4ds`.
load_dotenv()
Param = {
    'host'              : os.getenv('TDML_HOST'),
    'user'              : os.getenv('TDML_USERNAME'),
    'password'          : os.getenv('TDML_PASSWORD'),
    'database'          : os.getenv('TDML_DATABASE'),
    'temp_database_name': os.getenv('TDML_TEMP_DATABASE'),
}
tdml.create_context(**Param)

import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.connect(database=Param['database'])
tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN = '<DATA_DOMAIN>'   # or set TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN in .env

source_database = '<SOURCE_DATABASE>'
source_data = tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema(source_database, '<SOURCE_TABLE>'))

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Set up TimeManager
#    Creates (or reconnects to) the Teradata table + view holding the ordered
#    list of business dates. The view always exposes the current date position.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
from tdfs4ds.utils.time_management import TimeManager

business_time = TimeManager(
    table_name='<TIME_TABLE_NAME>',
    schema_name=Param['database'],
)

# First time only: populate from a date column in the source data.
business_time.load_time_steps(
    source_data.groupby('<DATE_COLUMN>').count(),
    '<DATE_COLUMN>',
)

print(business_time.get_current_step())

# Synchronise the feature store with the current time position
tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = business_time.get_date_in_the_past()

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Retrieve process IDs by view name
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
from tdfs4ds.process_store.process_query_administration import get_process_id

list_process_views = (
    tdfs4ds.process_catalog()[['VIEW_NAME']].to_pandas().VIEW_NAME.values
)

view_1 = [v for v in list_process_views if '<VIEW_SUBSTRING_1>' in v][0]
view_2 = [v for v in list_process_views if '<VIEW_SUBSTRING_2>' in v][0]

process_id_1 = get_process_id(view_name=view_1)
process_id_2 = get_process_id(view_name=view_2)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Roll out over a date range
#    Advances TimeManager step by step, updates FEATURE_STORE_TIME, and calls
#    run(process_id) for every step and every process in process_list.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
from tdfs4ds import roll_out

roll_out(
    process_list=[process_id_1, process_id_2],
    time_manager=business_time,
    time_id_start=<START_ID>,
    time_id_end=<END_ID>,
    force_display_logs=False,
)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Manual single-step alternative
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# business_time.update(time_id=10)
# tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = business_time.get_date_in_the_past()
# tdfs4ds.run(process_id=process_id_1)

tdml.remove_context()

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Call load_time_steps() at least once before using roll_out; after that TimeManager reconnects to the existing table.
  • time_id_start and time_id_end are 1-based integer positions in the ordered time sequence, not timestamps.
  • Processes must already be registered via upload_features; roll_out does not register new processes.
  • Keep tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME in sync with the time manager when manually stepping: tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = business_time.get_date_in_the_past().
  • Monitor execution with tdfs4ds.process_store.process_followup.follow_up_report().sort('START_DATETIME', ascending=False).
  • Vantage Cloud Lake + tdstone2 Apply — OFS manager table + JOIN the date in (not a scalar subquery). An Apply-scored view runs on the Analytical Compute Cluster (ACC), which reads from OFS. Two conditions must both hold when the scoring view references the TimeManager: (1) create it with TimeManager(..., storage='OFS') so the ACC can read the manager table (a block-storage manager table → Error 6881); (2) reference the current date via a JOIN to the manager view, not a correlated scalar subquery — the ACC rejects ... WHERE date_col <= (SELECT BUSINESS_DATE FROM <tm_view>) with 6881 but accepts a JOIN. Confirmed live on VCL: OFS + JOIN scores; OFS + scalar subquery still 6881s. Writing the filter as a JOIN is the user's responsibility (it's a property of the scoring view's SQL). A JOIN keeps the reference dynamic, so time-travel via update() still works (a hard-coded literal also passes but freezes the date). roll_out/run() still can't drive an Apply model — their ingestion CTAS also 6881s; call score() first, then upload_features.
  • roll_out/run() cannot drive a tdstone2-Apply model. Ingestion does CREATE VOLATILE TABLE AS SELECT * FROM <live scoring view>, and that CTAS also hits Error 6881 (ACC read + block-storage write). For Apply-backed features the working pattern is: call the model's score() first (predictions land in the OFS scores repo), then upload_features/run() over the resulting rows. roll_out remains the tool for ordinary SQL/denormalized-view processes.

docs.title: fs-setup
docs.text:
Generate the connection boilerplate and run tdfs4ds.setup() / connect(). Invoked at the very start of every session.

Source file

The full skill specification (frontmatter + body) lives in tdfs4ds/skills/fs-setup/SKILL.md. The version below is included verbatim.

name: Feature Store: Setup description: Generate code to connect to Teradata and initialise the tdfs4ds temporal feature store. Invoke when the user needs connection boilerplate, first-time setup, or wants to configure global feature store variables. argument-hint: [schema][data-domain] allowed-tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep

Generate Python code to connect to a Teradata Vantage database and initialise the tdfs4ds temporal feature store.

User context: $ARGUMENTS

docs.title: Related skills (typical next steps)
docs.text:
Next Skill When → fs-upload Engineer the first feature engineering view and register it. → fs-inspect Verify catalogs were provisioned and the data domain is active. → fs-document Once a process is registered, document it and score its EXPLAIN plan.

Ask for any missing values before generating code: - Schema / database name — Teradata database hosting the feature store (e.g. 'demo_db'). - Data domain — project namespace isolating features across teams (e.g. 'Customer Transaction Analytics').

Emit the following pattern, substituting real values for every <PLACEHOLDER>:

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import teradataml as tdml
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# Teradata credentials from .env (TDML_HOST, TDML_USERNAME, TDML_PASSWORD,
#   TDML_DATABASE, TDML_TEMP_DATABASE).
# tdfs4ds settings are also auto-loaded from the same .env (TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN,
#   TDFS4DS_SCHEMA, …) and from tdfs4ds.json — no extra load_dotenv call needed.
load_dotenv()
Param = {
    'host'              : os.getenv('TDML_HOST'),
    'user'              : os.getenv('TDML_USERNAME'),
    'password'          : os.getenv('TDML_PASSWORD'),
    'database'          : os.getenv('TDML_DATABASE'),
    'temp_database_name': os.getenv('TDML_TEMP_DATABASE'),
}
tdml.create_context(**Param)

# Import tdfs4ds AFTER create_context — it auto-detects SCHEMA from the session
# and loads any TDFS4DS_* settings from .env / tdfs4ds.json automatically.
import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.connect(database=Param['database'])

# Optional: keep VIEWS in a separate database from TABLES (governance policy).
# Omit database_view (or leave TDFS4DS_SCHEMA_VIEW unset) to co-locate them — the
# default, identical to before. When set, the view database must be granted
# SELECT (WITH GRANT OPTION) on the table database.
# tdfs4ds.connect(database=Param['database'], database_view='<VIEW_DB>')

# Optional overrides — can also be set via TDFS4DS_* in .env or in tdfs4ds.json
# tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = None     # None = current; '2024-01-01 00:00:00' = time travel
# tdfs4ds.DISPLAY_LOGS       = True
# tdfs4ds.DEBUG_MODE         = False
# tdfs4ds.STORE_FEATURE      = 'MERGE'  # or 'UPDATE_INSERT'

# Vector store backend for the consumer agent (optional — default is Chroma)
# tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND         = 'chroma'     # 'chroma' (default) | 'teradata' (keeps index in Vantage)
# tdfs4ds.TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX = 'tdfs4ds'    # prefix for 3 named stores in Vantage

# TeradataAI in-database embedding (only used when VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND='teradata')
# When None, reuses the standard get_embeddings() (vLLM/TEI/OpenAI-compatible).
# tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE   = None   # 'aws'|'azure'|'gcp'|'nim'|'hugging_face'|'onnx'
# tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_MODEL_NAME = None
# tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_REGION     = None   # aws only
# tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_BASE   = None   # nim only
# tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_ACCESS_KEY = None   # or TDFS4DS_TERADATA_AI_ACCESS_KEY env var

# First-time / administrator only (idempotent): deploy catalog tables and views.
# Add database_view='<VIEW_DB>' to place every view in a separate database from the tables.
tdfs4ds.setup()

# Create the data domain (idempotent — warns if already present).
# Analytics notebooks activate it with select_data_domain instead.
tdfs4ds.create_data_domain('<DATA_DOMAIN>')

Add tdml.remove_context() at the end of every script or notebook.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• tdml.create_context(...) must come before any tdfs4ds import or call.
  • Use tdfs4ds.create_data_domain(name) in the setup notebook (creates the domain, idempotent). Use tdfs4ds.select_data_domain(name) in all analytics/feature notebooks (activates an existing domain; raises ValueError if not found). Never set tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN directly.
  • Never hard-code credentials; always use a .env file or OS environment variables.
  • tdfs4ds auto-loads TDFS4DS_* variables from .env and tdfs4ds.json at import time. Call tdfs4ds.load_config(path=..., dotenv_path=...) to reload or point at a custom file.
  • tdfs4ds.setup() is idempotent but is an administrator action — note this in a comment.
  • To separate views from tables, pass database_view='<VIEW_DB>' to setup()/connect() (or set TDFS4DS_SCHEMA_VIEW). Default (None) co-locates views with tables in database — no behaviour change. The view database needs SELECT ... WITH GRANT OPTION on the table database, and an unqualified process/dataset name then defaults to the view database.

docs.title: fs-tdstone2
docs.text:
Register a trained tdstone2 model's per-partition predictions or computed features as governed tdfs4ds features — via the one-call bridge tdfs4ds.upload_tdstone2_scores(model).

Source file

Lives at tdfs4ds/skills/fs-tdstone2/SKILL.md.

name: Feature Store: tdstone2 Model Outputs description: Generate Python code to register a trained tdstone2 model's per-partition predictions or computed features as GOVERNED tdfs4ds features — gaining lineage, freshness, time-travel and reuse. Invoke when the user has a trained tdstone2 HyperModel / DeepLearningModel / FeatureEngineering and wants its outputs to live in the feature store. Covers the one-call bridge tdfs4ds.upload_tdstone2_scores(model), how the entity is derived from the model's mapper, and when to prefer a denormalized recomputable process view instead. SKIP for registering an ordinary SQL/DataFrame source — use fs-upload. argument-hint: [trained tdstone2 model object][data-domain] allowed-tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep

Generate Python code to persist a trained tdstone2 model's per-partition outputs as governed tdfs4ds features.

This is a tdfs4ds skill that consumes tdstone2 output — it belongs to the feature store, not to tdstone2. A tdstone2 model produces per-partition predictions (get_model_predictions()) or computed features (get_computed_features()) that otherwise live only in the tdstone2 scores repository. Registering them in tdfs4ds gives them lineage, freshness tracking, temporal time-travel, and discoverability through the consumer agent.

docs.title: Related skills
docs.text: Before / After Skill Why Before fs-setup Open the feature store; set SCHEMA + DATA_DOMAIN. Before (tdstone2 skills) Train the model that produces the scores — tdstone2-hypermodel / tdstone2-deeplearning / tdstone2-feature-engineering. Sibling fs-upload Register features from an ordinary SQL/DataFrame source (the non-tdstone2 path). Architecture td-analytics-workflow Why tdfs4ds + tdstone2 is the canonical pairing (no data movement). Read side ca-algorithm Business-user Q&A about the ML model behind a registered feature (walks the chain backwards).

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Feature store initialised (tdfs4ds.setup(...) / connect(...)), with tdfs4ds.SCHEMA and tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN set — see fs-setup.
  • A trained AND scored tdstone2 model in the same session, exposing get_model_predictions() (HyperModel / DeepLearningModel) or get_computed_features() (FeatureEngineering).

docs.title: The one-call bridge
docs.text:
import tdfs4ds
from tdfs4ds import upload_tdstone2_scores

tdfs4ds.SCHEMA      = DB
tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN = 'RISK_SCORING'      # isolates these features by project

# `model` is a trained + scored tdstone2 HyperModel / DeepLearningModel / FeatureEngineering
dataset = upload_tdstone2_scores(model)

What upload_tdstone2_scores(model) does in one pass (tdfs4ds/execution.py):

  1. Derives the entity from the model's mapper — tdstone2_entity_id(model) reads model.mapper_scoring (or model.mapper) id_row + id_partition and their SQL types. The feature-store entity keys are exactly the model's partition/row keys, so the features line up with how the model was segmented (one model per partition ⇒ features keyed by that partition).
  2. Registers the entity + features in the feature catalog (register_entity, register_features).
  3. Ingests the values — prepare_feature_ingestion_tdstone2(...) unpivots the melted FEATURE_NAME / FEATURE_VALUE scores into the feature-store storage tables (store_feature), then drops the volatile staging table.
  4. Tags each FEATURE_NAME with the model's ID_PROCESS (the tdstone2 run id) as its feature version — preserving lineage back to the exact model run.
  5. Builds and returns a dataset view over the registered features.

Auto-detection: if the model has a score method (HyperModel / DeepLearningModel) it reads get_model_predictions(); otherwise (FeatureEngineering) get_computed_features(). Both are the melted FEATURE_NAME / FEATURE_VALUE / ID_PROCESS shape, so the same path handles all three model types.

docs.title: Snapshot vs recomputable — choose the right path
docs.text:
upload_tdstone2_scores ingests a snapshot of the current scores. When you want the feature to be run()-recomputable on a schedule, register a denormalized process view over the tdstone2 score output instead (via fs-upload's upload_features(df='SCHEMA.v_scores', ...)), because a run() re-reads the view each ingestion.

Goal Path One-off snapshot of the model's current scores upload_tdstone2_scores(model) Governed, scheduled, re-runnable feature over evolving scores denormalized view over the score table + upload_features (fs-upload) — re-runs via tdfs4ds.run(process_id) / roll_out(...)

Both give lineage/freshness/time-travel; only the view path recomputes on run().

docs.title: The ML-model lineage you get for free
docs.text:
Because each feature carries the model's ID_PROCESS as its version, the store can later walk the full chain — exactly what ca-algorithm reads backwards:

feature → process view → SHOW VIEW DDL → TDS_STO_SCORING_<uuid>
        → TDS_MAPPER_REPOSITORY → trained model → code + ARGUMENTS (hyper-params, column_names_X)

So "which algorithm / hyper-parameters / code produced this feature?" is answerable with no extra documentation step.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Set DATA_DOMAIN before registering — it namespaces the features by project. Two models registered under the same domain with colliding FEATURE_NAMEs conflate; scope by domain (or alias the model's output columns / target).
  • A feature's identity is (FEATURE_NAME, ENTITY_NAME). The entity comes from the model's partition/row keys — a same-named score under a different partitioning is a different feature. Keep partition keys stable across re-registration.
  • Train AND score before registering — get_model_predictions() reads the scores repository, empty until score() has run.
  • Safe to re-run — the call drops its volatile staging table and re-registers/re-ingests the current snapshot; feature-store storage is preserved.
  • Deep-learning forecasts emit melted <target>_prediction rows — those become the features. If the model emits many auxiliary columns, alias/filter to the ones you actually want to govern before registering.
  • Do not hand-roll the melt/unpivot — prepare_feature_ingestion_tdstone2 already expects the tdstone2 FEATURE_NAME / FEATURE_VALUE / ID_PROCESS melted shape.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: Requires a live Vantage + feature store. After the call: the returned dataset has one row per entity-key with the model's features as columns; tdfs4ds.feature_catalog() lists the new FEATURE_NAMEs under your DATA_DOMAIN; and (read-side) get_process_info / the ca-algorithm skill resolves each feature back to the tdstone2 model run.

docs.title: fs-upload
docs.text:
Engineer features, persist them as a permanent view, and register them in the feature store with upload_features.

Source file

Lives at tdfs4ds/skills/fs-upload/SKILL.md.

name: Feature Store: Upload description: Generate Python code to engineer features from a Teradata source, persist as a permanent view, and register in the tdfs4ds feature store with upload_features. Invoke when the user wants to create or update a feature engineering process. argument-hint: [source-table][entity-column] [feature-columns] allowed-tools: Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep

Generate Python code to engineer features from a Teradata source, persist the logic as a permanent view, and register + upload the features to the tdfs4ds feature store.

User context: $ARGUMENTS

docs.title: Related skills (chain in this order)
docs.text:
Before / After Skill Why Before fs-setup Open the Teradata context and activate a data domain. Optional fs-filter Segment a large source into iterative passes with FilterManager. After fs-document LLM-generated business description + EXPLAIN score 1–5 for the registered process. After fs-analyze If USER_SCORE is 1–3, run the full scalability analysis and propose a rewrite. After fs-rollout Backfill features across a date range with TimeManager.

Ask for any missing values before generating code: - Source database and table — where the raw data lives. - Entity column(s) — the primary key. Plain string for single-column auto-detected types, or a dict with SQL types for multiple columns: {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT', 'EVENT_DATE': 'DATE'}. - Feature columns — list of derived columns to register as features. - View name — name for the permanent Teradata view. - Metadata — optional dict with descriptive tags. - Dataset view name — optional; if provided, also creates a ready-to-use dataset view.

docs.title: Pattern A — Raw SQL view (preferred)
docs.text:
Use when feature logic requires Teradata-specific SQL (e.g. PERCENTILE_CONT, complex aggregations, window functions). Persist directly with REPLACE VIEW — no crystallize_view needed.

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import teradataml as tdml
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# Teradata credentials from .env (TDML_*). TDFS4DS_* settings (DATA_DOMAIN,
# SCHEMA, etc.) are auto-loaded from the same .env by `import tdfs4ds`.
load_dotenv()
Param = {
    'host'              : os.getenv('TDML_HOST'),
    'user'              : os.getenv('TDML_USERNAME'),
    'password'          : os.getenv('TDML_PASSWORD'),
    'database'          : os.getenv('TDML_DATABASE'),
    'temp_database_name': os.getenv('TDML_TEMP_DATABASE'),
}
tdml.create_context(**Param)

import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.connect(database=Param['database'])
tdfs4ds.select_data_domain('<DATA_DOMAIN>')   # or set TDFS4DS_DATA_DOMAIN in .env

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Write feature SQL and persist as a permanent Teradata view
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
feature_sql = f"""
LOCKING ROW FOR ACCESS
SELECT
    <ENTITY_COLUMN>,
    <AGGREGATION_EXPRESSION>  AS <FEATURE_NAME>
FROM <SOURCE_DATABASE>.<SOURCE_TABLE>
GROUP BY <ENTITY_COLUMN>
"""

tdml.execute_sql(f"REPLACE VIEW {Param['database']}.<VIEW_NAME> AS {feature_sql}")
df_features_proc = tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema(Param['database'], '<VIEW_NAME>'))

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Register and upload features
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
from tdfs4ds import upload_features

feature_names = ['<FEATURE_NAME>']

upload_features(
    df_features_proc,
    entity_id=<ENTITY_ID>,           # str (single col) or list (composite key) — types auto-detected
    feature_names=feature_names,
    metadata={'project': '<PROJECT_NAME>'},
    dataset_view_name='<DATASET_VIEW>',  # name derived from VIEW_NAME, e.g. ds_<base_name>
    # partitioning='PARTITION BY COLUMN',  # SQL DDL clause appended to the feature table's CREATE TABLE
    #   — controls physical storage layout only; does not affect which columns are available
    # grouped=True,  # recommended for wide datasets (many features, same source table):
    #   MAX(CASE WHEN) pivot — fewer JOINs than the classic per-feature default (grouped=False)
)

tdml.remove_context()

docs.title: Pattern B — teradataml DataFrame methods + crystallize_view (fallback)
docs.text:
Use only when the feature transformation is expressed through the teradataml DataFrame API. Those build a temporary view that disappears when the context is removed; crystallize_view persists it as a permanent database object.

# ... connection setup same as Pattern A ...

from tdfs4ds.utils.lineage import crystallize_view
from tdfs4ds import upload_features

# Chained teradataml DataFrame methods produce a *temporary* Teradata view.
# crystallize_view persists it as a permanent database object before upload.
source_data = tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema('<SOURCE_DATABASE>', '<SOURCE_TABLE>'))
df_features = source_data.groupby('<ENTITY_COLUMN>').agg({
    '<VALUE_COLUMN>': ['sum', 'mean'],
})
feature_names = ['sum_<VALUE_COLUMN>', 'mean_<VALUE_COLUMN>']

df_features_proc = crystallize_view(
    df_features,
    view_name='<VIEW_NAME>',
    schema_name=Param['database'],
)

upload_features(
    df_features_proc,
    entity_id=<ENTITY_ID>,
    feature_names=feature_names,
    metadata={'project': '<PROJECT_NAME>'},
    dataset_view_name='<DATASET_VIEW>',  # ds_<base_name>
)

tdml.remove_context()

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Prefer Pattern A — write feature logic as raw SQL with REPLACE VIEW. Use DataFrame.from_query(sql) for any teradataml data access.
  • Only use crystallize_view (Pattern B) when the teradataml DataFrame API is unavoidable and produces a temporary view.
  • entity_id should normally be a plain string (single column) or list (composite key), e.g. ['CUSTOMER_ID', 'EVENT_DATE'] — SQL types are auto-detected from the source DataFrame, so there's no need to specify them explicitly. Only pass a dict ({'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT', 'EVENT_DATE': 'DATE'}) to override an inferred type.
  • feature_names must be a subset of the view's columns, excluding the entity column(s).
  • Never register a 1:1 copy of a raw source column (e.g. uploading claims_raw.amount unchanged). That is pure data duplication with no governance value. Join the source table in the training query instead and append the column to column_names_X.
  • upload_features internally calls run(process_id) — intentional; it exercises the same refresh path used by roll_out.
  • Always pass dataset_view_name to build a denormalised dataset view alongside feature registration. Name it by replacing the vw_feat_ prefix with ds_ (e.g. vw_feat_savings_dist → ds_savings_dist).
  • upload_features always uses the classic multi-join (grouped=False) by default when it builds a dataset view — pass grouped=True at upload time for wide datasets (many features sharing the same source table) to reduce JOIN count via MAX(CASE WHEN) pivoting instead of building the dataset separately afterward with build_dataset(..., grouped=True).
  • Always include LOCKING ROW FOR ACCESS as the first line of every REPLACE VIEW … AS body. This tells Teradata to use access locks, preventing read queries from blocking concurrent writers. Omitting it causes read-lock contention on busy feature views.
  • When features will feed hyper-segmented ML (one model per partition, e.g. per region): include the partition column (e.g. region) in the view SQL and in feature_names. This registers it as a feature so build_dataset() includes it naturally — no manual JOIN to the source table needed at training time. Use partitioning= separately if you want to physically co-locate rows by that column for efficient partition elimination.

docs.title: Agent skills
docs.text: tdfs4ds ships with 22 discoverable SKILL.md files organized in four families that teach agents — Claude Code, custom LangGraph agents, or any LLM with file access — how to drive the feature store end-to-end.

docs.title: What is a skill?
docs.text:
A skill is a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter declaring:

  • name — human-readable skill name (e.g., "Feature Store: Upload").
  • description — when an agent should pick this skill.
  • argument-hint — the inputs the skill expects (e.g., [source-table] [entity-column]).
  • allowed-tools — restricted tool list (Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep).

The body is a copy-paste-ready code pattern with <PLACEHOLDER> markers and a ## Rules block listing invariants. For query optimizer skills (qo-skill-*), additional nested prompt files live under prompts/ subfolders.

docs.title: Feature Store Operations (fs-* — 10 skills)fs-setupfs-uploadfs-filterfs-rolloutfs-datasetfs-inspectfs-documentfs-lineagefs-analyzefs-agent
docs.text:
Core feature store lifecycle from setup through analysis. All fs-* skills reference each other in their "Related skills" tables to help agents chain operations.

Connect to Teradata, run setup(), activate a data domain. First call of every session.

Engineer features in SQL or teradataml, persist as a view, register with upload_features.

Segmented ingestion with FilterManager — standard, hybrid (time × partition), clone.

Backfill across a date range with TimeManager + roll_out.

Resolve feature versions and build a denormalised dataset view.

Browse process / feature / dataset catalogs and the follow-up table.

LLM process documentation + EXPLAIN score 1–5.

Build a dependency graph and render a Sankey diagram.

Full scalability analysis — lineage + EXPLAIN + partition opportunities + FilterManager proposal.

Launch the consumer agent chatbot (LangGraph + Chroma + Gradio).

docs.title: Query Optimization (qo-skill-* — 3 skills)
docs.text:
LLM-powered query rewriting pipeline. Each qo-skill-* contains multiple nested prompt files under prompts/ that implement specific optimization steps (e.g., EXPLAIN analysis, strategy generation, candidate refinement).

Skill Purpose Nested Prompts qo-skill-explain Analyze EXPLAIN plans and score query quality (1–5) qo-explain, qo-classify-items, qo-document-sql qo-skill-simplify Flatten nesting and remove redundant wrappers qo-simplify, qo-syntax-fix qo-skill-optimize Full optimization pipeline with strategy-based rewrites qo-strategies, qo-generate, qo-refine, qo-candidates, qo-compare, qo-summary

Note: Nested prompts are discovered automatically via skill_prompt("qo-strategies") without needing to know which qo-skill-* they belong to. install_skills() copies the entire prompts/ folder structure.

docs.title: Teradata Utilities (td-* — 1 skill)td-explain
docs.text: Interactive HTML flow diagram from any raw Teradata EXPLAIN plan.

docs.title: Installing the bundle
docs.text:
import tdfs4ds

# Default — copy every SKILL.md to ~/.claude/skills/ (user-level, all projects)
tdfs4ds.install_skills()

# Or pick any target directory (works for non-Claude agents too)
tdfs4ds.export_skills('/path/to/agent/skills')

# Install a subset
tdfs4ds.install_skills(skills=['fs-setup', 'fs-upload'])

# Skip skills that already exist (export_skills default behaviour)
tdfs4ds.export_skills('.claude/skills')     # overwrite=False by default

# Discover what is bundled
tdfs4ds.list_skills()                       # ['fs-agent', 'fs-analyze', ...]
tdfs4ds.skill_catalog()                     # full frontmatter per skill
tdfs4ds.skill_path('fs-upload')             # absolute path to the SKILL.md

Or from the command line with the venv active:

tdfs4ds-install-skills                                # user-level ~/.claude/skills/
tdfs4ds-install-skills --target .claude/skills        # project-level
tdfs4ds-install-skills --skill fs-setup --skill fs-upload  # subset
tdfs4ds-install-skills --no-overwrite                 # skip existing
tdfs4ds-install-skills --list                         # list bundled names

docs.title: install_skills vs export_skills
docs.text: Function Default overwrite Typical use tdfs4ds.install_skills() True Upgrade skills when you update the package tdfs4ds.export_skills(dir) False Add only missing skills without touching existing customisations

docs.title: Option A — Check .claude/skills/ into git (recommended for teams)
docs.text: The cleanest team setup is to commit .claude/skills/ to the shared repository. Teammates get every skill automatically on git pull — no install step needed.

docs.title: Option B — Install from the package (colleague has tdfs4ds in their venv)
docs.text:
If each person installs tdfs4ds independently, they run one command after activating their virtual environment:

import tdfs4ds

# Add skills to the user-level directory (picked up in every project)
tdfs4ds.install_skills()                        # default: ~/.claude/skills/

# Or into the current project only, skipping skills already present
tdfs4ds.export_skills('.claude/skills')         # overwrite=False by default

Or from the terminal with the venv active:

# User-level (applies to all projects on this machine)
tdfs4ds-install-skills

# Project-level, skip existing
tdfs4ds-install-skills --target .claude/skills --no-overwrite

docs.title: Where skills land
docs.text: Target Scope When to use ~/.claude/skills/ (default) All projects for this user Personal workstation setup .claude/skills/ in the project repo This project only Shared via git — no extra install step for teammates

docs.title: Typical workflow chains
docs.text: The skills are designed to chain. Each SKILL.md includes a "Related skills" table at the top so the dispatching agent knows where to go next.

docs.title: First feature
docs.text: fs-setup  →  fs-upload  →  fs-document

docs.title: Slow process
docs.text: fs-document  →  fs-analyze  →  fs-filter  →  fs-rollout

docs.title: Conversational consumer
docs.text: fs-document  →  fs-agent

docs.title: Bulk migration audit
docs.text: fs-inspect  →  fs-lineage  →  graph_to_migration_manifest

docs.title: Authoring a new skill
docs.text:
1. Create tdfs4ds/skills/<skill-id>/SKILL.md (e.g., tdfs4ds/skills/fs-upload/SKILL.md).
  2. Add YAML frontmatter with:
  3. name — human-readable display name (e.g., "Feature Store: Upload")
  4. description — when an agent should pick this skill
  5. argument-hint — input hints (e.g., [source-table] [entity-column])
  6. allowed-tools — restricted tool list (Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Glob, Grep)
  7. Begin the body with a ## Related skills table linking neighbours.
  8. If your skill uses LLM prompts, store them as separate files under prompts/:
  9. Create tdfs4ds/skills/<skill-id>/prompts/<prompt-name>.md
  10. Reference them in the body with [prompt-name](prompts/prompt-name.md)
  11. skill_prompt("prompt-name") auto-discovers nested prompts
  12. Emit a single fenced Python block containing the pattern an agent should produce. Mark every user-supplied value as <PLACEHOLDER>.
  13. Close with a ## Rules block listing the invariants (e.g., never construct selected_features manually).
  14. Reinstall: pip install -e . — setup.py already globs skills/*/SKILL.md and skills/*/prompts/*.md.

tdfs4ds.list_skills() will pick up the new skill automatically. Nested prompts are accessible via tdfs4ds.skill_prompt("prompt-name") without naming which skill owns them.

docs.title: td-explain
docs.text:
Interactive HTML flow diagram from any raw Teradata EXPLAIN output — including queries that have nothing to do with tdfs4ds.

name: teradata-explain-analyzer description: Analyze Teradata SQL EXPLAIN output to visualize query execution plans, identify performance bottlenecks, and provide actionable optimization recommendations. Use proactively when users ask about query performance, slow queries, EXPLAIN analysis, query plan interpretation, or SQL optimization on Teradata. SKIP when the user needs a 1–5 quality score for a registered tdfs4ds process — use qo-skill-explain instead. argument-hint: "[sql-query-or-explain-text][process-id]"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Transform raw Teradata EXPLAIN output into interactive visual query plans with prioritized, actionable optimization recommendations. Serve both beginners needing educational context and experts expecting deep optimizer reasoning.

The optimization score used throughout this skill is on a 1–5 scale (1 = critical problems, 5 = excellent plan), matching the score returned by tdfs4ds.document_process() in the EXPLAIN_ANALYSIS field.

docs.title: Related skills
docs.text: Step Skill Why Source fs-document Score and persist the EXPLAIN for a registered tdfs4ds process. Bigger picture fs-analyze Combines EXPLAIN scoring with partition analysis and a rewrite proposal. Apply rewrite fs-filter Implement the partition-pruning recommendation with FilterManager.

docs.title: Using with tdfs4ds (registered processes)
docs.text:
When the user has a registered tdfs4ds process (they have a PROCESS_ID), use the Python API directly — it retrieves the SQL, runs EXPLAIN, scores the plan, and stores results in one call:

import tdfs4ds

# High-level: document + score in one call
doc = tdfs4ds.document_process(
    process_id='<PROCESS_ID>',
    show_explain_plan=True,   # fetches and stores the raw EXPLAIN text
)

user_score   = doc.get('USER_SCORE')         # int 1–5 — SQL quality (what you control)
global_score = doc.get('OPTIMIZATION_SCORE') # int 1–5 — overall execution (infra included)
analysis = doc['EXPLAIN_ANALYSIS']           # plain-text explanation
warnings = doc['EXPLAIN_WARNINGS']           # list[str]
recs     = doc['EXPLAIN_RECOMMENDATIONS']    # list[str]
raw_plan = doc.get('RAW_EXPLAIN_PLAN', '')   # raw EXPLAIN output for visual analysis

# Score interpretation (matches severity levels in this skill):
# user_score  = SQL author quality (joins, filters, predicates, stats hints)
# global_score = full execution quality including infra (PI design, partitioning, index gaps)
#
# user_score:
# 4–5 → GOOD (green)   — SQL well-written; any remaining issues are infra/DBA territory
# 3   → WARNING (orange) — some SQL-side improvements possible
# 1–2 → CRITICAL (red)   — SQL-level problems; rewrite recommended
#
# global_score:
# Use to assess the full picture; a low global / high user means DBA/data-engineer action needed

If a raw EXPLAIN text is already available (from raw_plan above, or pasted by the user), proceed directly to Step 2 of the workflow below.

docs.title: Core Workflow
docs.text: Execute this systematic 8-step workflow for every EXPLAIN analysis:

docs.title: Step 1: Input Processing
docs.text:
If user provides SQL: - Assess query complexity to choose EXPLAIN type: - STATIC EXPLAIN: Simple queries, few joins, straightforward predicates - DYNAMIC EXPLAIN: Correlated subqueries, multiple nesting levels, partition elimination potential, complex predicates - Execute via teradata:base_readQuery: 

EXPLAIN SELECT ...;
-- or
DYNAMIC EXPLAIN SELECT ...;
- On failure: explain the error clearly, offer to accept user-pasted EXPLAIN text

If user provides EXPLAIN output text: - Validate it contains step patterns (look for "We do", "We lock", estimated times) - Proceed directly to parsing

docs.title: Step 2: Parse EXPLAIN Output
docs.text:
Extract every component systematically:

Step structure to extract: - Step number and operation type (RETRIEVE, JOIN, SORT, AGG, LOCK) - Lock types (READ/WRITE/EXCLUSIVE) and scope - Table or spool names referenced - Estimated row counts and time (relative cost units, not wall-clock) - Confidence level: no confidence / low confidence / high confidence / index join confidence - Access method: all-rows scan / by way of the unique primary index / by way of index #n - AMP geography: single-AMP / all-AMPs / few AMPs / (all_amps) / (group_amps) - Data movement: redistributed by hash code / duplicated on all AMPs / locally built - Spool creation, numbering, and (Last Use) markers - Parallel execution markers: execute the following steps in parallel - Join methods: merge join / hash join / product join / nested join / exclusion merge join / inclusion merge join - Partition phrases: single partition of / n partitions of / all partitions of / m1 column partitions of - Conditional DML branches: If no update in step X then INSERT

Key phrases glossary (map these to optimizer intent):

Phrase Optimizer Intent single-AMP RETRIEVE by way of the unique primary index Best-case tactical access, no spool needed by way of an all-rows scan Full table scan — examine predicates, stats, partitioning redistributed by hash code Row movement to co-locate join keys — check skew risk duplicated on all AMPs Broadcast small input — verify it's truly small (group_amps) Spool built on subset of AMPs — potential skew signal (all_amps) Spool built on every AMP — expected for large tables SORT to order Spool n by row hash Preparing for merge/hash join — sorted by rowhash estimated with no confidence Missing statistics — unreliable cardinality estimate estimated with low confidence Partial/stale statistics — conservative planning estimated with high confidence Good statistics — optimizer has reliable estimates execute the following steps in parallel Independent sub-steps dispatched concurrently RowHash match scan Join/read driven by rowhash ordering eliminating duplicate rows DISTINCT or duplicate removal in spool

docs.title: Step 3: Collect Query Metadata
docs.text:
For each table referenced in the query, gather supporting context:

teradata:base_tableDDL       → Table structure, indexes, partitioning
teradata:base_columnDescription → Column types, constraints, nullable
teradata:dba_tableSpace      → Current table size (rows, bytes)
teradata:dba_tableUsageImpact → Usage patterns, which users/apps touch this table
teradata:base_tableAffinity  → Commonly joined tables (for index suggestions)
teradata:base_tablePreview   → Sample rows for data distribution context

Statistics Assessment: - Identify which predicates, join keys, GROUP BY columns, and partition columns lack statistics - Check for stale statistics where timestamps are available - Correlate missing stats with Low/No confidence steps in the plan

Index Inventory: - List UPI, NUPI, USI, NUSI, join indexes on referenced tables - Note which indexes were used vs. available but ignored - Identify index candidates for full-scan steps

docs.title: Step 4: Classify Issues by Severity
docs.text:
🔴 CRITICAL — Must fix: - Steps with No Confidence on large tables - Product joins on large tables (potential Cartesian explosion) - All-rows scans on very large tables (>1M rows) - Steps consuming >15% of total estimated time - Hot AMP signals: (group_amps) materializing on very few AMPs - Missing join conditions causing unintended Cartesian products - Massive intermediate spool files (>1 GB estimated)

🟡 WARNING — Should fix: - Steps with Low Confidence - Steps consuming 5–15% of total estimated time - Large redistributions (>500 MB spool) - Secondary index scans with large result sets - Multiple sequential redistributions - Missing partition elimination on a partitioned table - Stale statistics identified from collection timestamps

🟢 GOOD — Document and confirm: - High Confidence or Index Join Confidence estimates - Unique primary index access (single-AMP, no spool) - Single partition access on partitioned tables - Efficient broadcast duplication (small table to all AMPs) - Parallel execution of independent steps - Spool reuse patterns (Last Use properly placed) - Local aggregation (no redistribution needed)

Severity classification logic: 

IF product join OR no confidence → CRITICAL (red)
IF step time > 15% of total    → CRITICAL (red)
IF low confidence               → WARNING (orange)
IF step time > 5% of total     → WARNING (orange)
IF spool > 500MB               → WARNING (orange)
IF high confidence AND fast     → GOOD (green)
ELSE                           → INFO (blue)

docs.title: Step 5: Generate Interactive Flow Diagram (PRIMARY DELIVERABLE)
docs.text: ALWAYS generate an HTML flow diagram as the primary output. This is not optional.

docs.title: Layout: Vertical Flow (Top → Bottom)
docs.text:
Lock Acquisition
      ↓
[Parallel Branch Start — if applicable]
  Step A  |  Step B  |  Step C   (side-by-side)
[Parallel Branch End]
      ↓
Sequential Operations (joins, sorts, aggregations)
      ↓
Final Output

docs.title: Node Structure (each EXPLAIN step = one node)
docs.text:
<div class="flow-node [critical|warning|good|info]" data-step="N">
    <div class="node-header">
        <div class="node-step">Step N</div>
        <div class="node-type">[ALL-AMP]</div>
        <div class="confidence-badge [high|low|none]">HIGH</div>
    </div>
    <div class="node-title">Human-readable description of operation</div>
    <div class="node-metrics">
        <span class="metric-label">Rows</span><span class="metric-value">1,250,000</span>
        <span class="metric-label">Size</span><span class="metric-value">573 MB</span>
        <span class="metric-label">Time</span><span class="metric-value">2.5s (18%)</span>
        <span class="metric-label">Method</span><span class="metric-value">Hash join</span>
    </div>
    <div class="tooltip-content">
        <strong>Purpose:</strong> ...<br>
        <strong>Technical:</strong> Join condition: A.id = B.id<br>
        <strong>Performance:</strong> Why fast or slow<br>
        <strong>Issue:</strong> Specific problem if any<br>
        <strong>Fix:</strong> Immediate optimization action
    </div>
</div>

docs.title: Operation Type Labels (standardized icons)
docs.text: EXPLAIN Text Label Notes all-AMPs RETRIEVE [ALL-AMP] single-AMP RETRIEVE [1-AMP] Best access hash join [HASH-J] merge join [MERGE-J] product join [PROD-J] ⚠️ Always flag critical nested join [NEST-J] SORT [SORT] aggregate/SUM [AGG] redistribute [REDIST] duplicate on all AMPs [DUP-ALL] lock [LOCK] output to user [OUTPUT]

docs.title: Arrows Between Steps
docs.text:
<div class="flow-arrow">
    <div class="arrow-line"></div>
    <div class="arrow-label">Spool 3: 573 MB, 46,005 rows</div>
</div>

docs.title: Parallel Execution Pattern
docs.text:
<div class="parallel-start">◆ PARALLEL EXECUTION START (Steps 3.1–3.3)</div>
<div class="flow-row parallel">
    <!-- Nodes side-by-side using flex-direction: row -->
</div>
<div class="parallel-end">◆ PARALLEL EXECUTION COMPLETE</div>

docs.title: Confidence Indicators
docs.text:
• ●●●●● High Confidence
  • ●●●○○ Low Confidence
  • ○○○○○ No Confidence ⚠️
  • ●●●●◆ Index Join Confidence

docs.title: Required Interactive Features
docs.text:
1. Hover tooltips — Explain each step: purpose, technical details, performance implications, fix suggestions
  2. "Highlight Critical Path" button — Illuminate top 5 slowest steps
  3. "Highlight Product Joins" button — Flash all [PROD-J] nodes
  4. "Highlight No Confidence" button — Flash all No Confidence steps
  5. "Reset" button — Return all nodes to normal state
function toggleCriticalPath() {
    const criticalSteps = findTopStepsByTime(5); // top 5 by time %
    document.querySelectorAll('.flow-node').forEach(node => {
        if (criticalSteps.includes(node.dataset.step)) {
            node.style.boxShadow = '0 0 20px 5px rgba(231,76,60,0.6)';
            node.style.transform = 'scale(1.05)';
        } else {
            node.style.opacity = '0.4';
        }
    });
}

docs.title: Required Sections in HTML File
docs.text:
1. Teradata-branded page header (navy background, orange accent, base64 logo — see CSS section)
  2. Summary metrics bar — 4–6 cards: total time, step count, critical issues, confidence distribution, result rows
  3. Legend — Color meanings, badge types, operation icons
  4. Interactive control buttons
  5. Flow diagram container — All steps as nodes with arrows
  6. Key Insights panel — Critical path breakdown, data growth table, top 3 optimization actions

docs.title: Teradata Brand CSS (mandatory — include verbatim)
docs.text:
@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@300;400;500;600;700&display=swap');

:root {
    --td-orange: #FF5F02;
    --td-navy: #00233C;
    --td-white: #FFFFFF;
    --color-critical: #C0392B;
    --color-warning: #FF5F02;
    --color-good: #27AE60;
    --color-info: #4A90E2;
    --color-bg: #F5F7FA;
    --color-bg-card: #FFFFFF;
    --color-text: #00233C;
    --font-family: 'Inter', system-ui, -apple-system, sans-serif;
}
body { font-family: var(--font-family); color: var(--color-text); }

.td-page-header {
    background: var(--td-navy);
    border-bottom: 3px solid var(--td-orange);
    padding: 16px 30px;
    display: flex;
    align-items: center;
    gap: 16px;
}
.td-page-header .page-title { color: var(--td-white); font-weight: 600; font-size: 18px; }
h1, h2, h3 { color: var(--td-navy); font-weight: 600; }

/* Flow nodes */
.flow-node {
    background: white;
    border-radius: 10px;
    padding: 15px 20px;
    min-width: 280px;
    border: 3px solid;
    box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);
    transition: all 0.3s ease;
    cursor: pointer;
    position: relative;
}
.flow-node:hover { transform: translateY(-5px) scale(1.02); box-shadow: 0 8px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.2); }
.flow-node.good     { border-color: #27AE60; }
.flow-node.warning  { border-color: #FF5F02; }
.flow-node.critical { border-color: #C0392B; }
.flow-node.info     { border-color: #4A90E2; }

/* Confidence badges */
.confidence-badge { padding: 2px 8px; border-radius: 10px; font-size: 10px; font-weight: bold; text-transform: uppercase; }
.confidence-badge.high { background: #27AE60; color: white; }
.confidence-badge.low  { background: #FF5F02; color: white; }
.confidence-badge.none { background: #C0392B; color: white; }

/* Tooltips */
.tooltip-content {
    display: none;
    position: absolute;
    background: #2c3e50;
    color: white;
    padding: 15px;
    border-radius: 8px;
    max-width: 400px;
    font-size: 12px;
    top: 100%;
    left: 50%;
    transform: translateX(-50%);
    margin-top: 10px;
    z-index: 1000;
}
.flow-node:hover .tooltip-content { display: block; }

/* Flow container */
.flow-container { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 20px; min-width: 1200px; }
.flow-row { display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; gap: 20px; }
.flow-row.parallel { justify-content: space-around; }
.flow-diagram { background: #f8f9fa; padding: 30px; border-radius: 8px; overflow-x: auto; }

Teradata logo (base64 — use this exact value, never recreate the logo mark): 

<img src="data:image/png;base64,"
     alt="Teradata" height="32" style="filter: brightness(0) invert(1);" />

docs.title: File Naming & Delivery
docs.text:
1. Create: [query_name]_flow_diagram.html  (in the current working directory)
2. Present via present_files tool as PRIMARY deliverable
On Windows use a relative path or the user's working directory. On Linux/Mac use /tmp/[query_name]_flow_diagram.html if no working directory is available.

docs.title: Step 6: Build Optimization Playbook
docs.text:
Generate prioritized, specific, ready-to-execute recommendations:

Format per recommendation: 

Priority: 🔴 HIGH / 🟡 MEDIUM / 🟢 LOW
Issue: [Exact EXPLAIN phrase that triggered this]
Root Cause: [Why the optimizer chose this path]
Action:
  COLLECT STATISTICS ON db.table COLUMN col;
  -- or --
  CREATE INDEX idx_name (col) ON db.table;
  -- or --
  [Rewritten SQL]
Expected Impact: [Quantified or qualitative improvement]
Effort: [Low/Medium/High]
Risk: [None/Low/Moderate/High with explanation]

Standard recommendation types:

  1. Statistics Collection (most common, lowest risk) 

    COLLECT STATISTICS ON {db}.{table} COLUMN {predicate_col};
COLLECT STATISTICS ON {db}.{table} COLUMN ({col1}, {col2}); -- multicolumn for correlated
COLLECT STATISTICS ON {db}.{table} INDEX {index_name};

  2. Index Creation 

    CREATE INDEX ({filter_col}) ON {db}.{table};  -- NUSI for frequent filter
    Include maintenance cost warning for DML-heavy tables.

  3. Query Rewrite — Make predicates sargable:

  4. Remove functions on indexed columns: CAST(col AS DATE) → store as DATE
  5. De-OR predicates: col=1 OR col=2 → col IN (1,2)
  6. Push filters before joins

  7. Replace NOT IN with NOT EXISTS for nullable columns

  8. Join Geography Optimization

  9. If both large tables redistributed: consider one as staging pre-hashed on join key
  10. If small table redistributed: consider duplicated on all AMPs approach

  11. PI redesign when join pattern dominates workload

  12. Partition Strategy

  13. Add RPPI on date/range columns for frequent range predicates
  14. Adjust CP design for column-selective access patterns

docs.title: Step 7: Organize Output by Severity
docs.text:
If critical issues found → lead with crisis: 

🚨 CRITICAL ISSUES SUMMARY
    ↓
Quick Metrics Bar
    ↓
Flow Diagram (present_files)
    ↓
Step-by-step table
    ↓
Optimization Playbook (HIGH priority first)
    ↓
Metadata Summary

If warnings only → lead with overview: 

Performance Overview
    ↓
Flow Diagram (present_files)
    ↓
Optimization Opportunities
    ↓
Detailed Step Analysis
    ↓
Statistics & Index Status

If query is optimal → validate and educate: 

✅ PERFORMANCE VALIDATION
    ↓
Flow Diagram (educational)
    ↓
Step Analysis (with explanations)
    ↓
Best Practices Confirmation

If comparative analysis → lead with winner: 

Comparison Summary (A vs B, winner, % delta)
    ↓
Side-by-side Flow Diagrams
    ↓
Key Differences Explained
    ↓
Recommendation with reasoning

docs.title: Step 8: Adapt Explanation Depth
docs.text:
Auto-detect expertise from: - Terminology used in the request - Query complexity and structure - Questions asked ("what is a merge join?" vs. "check PRPD eligibility")

Beginner — Define all terms, use analogies, explain optimizer reasoning, educational tone Intermediate — Practical focus, assume basic concepts known, quick wins emphasis Expert — Technical depth, edge cases, PRPD/IPE/dynamic partition elimination, performance math

docs.title: Skew & PRPD Detection
docs.text:
Watch for: - (group_amps) with far fewer AMPs than total system AMPs - Large spools produced by small redistributed inputs - Join keys with known hot values (e.g., NULL, default codes)

PRPD (Partial Redistribution/Partial Duplication) opportunity: - Applicable when join key has severe skew on a subset of values - Requires statistics to reveal skewed values to the optimizer - Recommend: Collect statistics on join column + verify with EXPLAIN comparing skewed vs. balanced values

docs.title: Static vs. Dynamic EXPLAIN
docs.text:
• STATIC (default): Plan generated at parse time from statistics
  • DYNAMIC: IPE-based plan that may execute small fragments to feed better cardinality back to planning
  • Use DYNAMIC EXPLAIN for: correlated subqueries, multi-level nesting, queries where intermediate sizes dramatically change the plan
  • Note :* in Dynamic EXPLAIN output = security-masked intermediate values
  • If Dynamic returns a static plan with explanation, note why IPE threshold wasn't met

docs.title: Column-Partitioned Table Nuance
docs.text:
• Step count of N+1 column partitions means N user columns + the delete column partition (always present)
  • Phrases using CP merge Spool vs. using rowID spool indicate different CP access strategies
  • CP merge spool incurs additional overhead when contexts are insufficient

docs.title: Conditional DML (MERGE/UPSERT)
docs.text:
• EXPLAIN shows: If no update in step X, then INSERT ...
  • Parallel sub-steps for join index maintenance
  • (Last Use) markers indicate spool freed — verify correct placement

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
SQL Syntax Error: 

❌ SQL Syntax Error at line N, position P
Issue: [specific problem]
Suggested fix: [corrected SQL snippet]
Offer to retry with correction.

Permission Denied: 

⚠️ Permission Denied — insufficient SELECT on [table]
Contact DBA to grant access.
Offer: paste EXPLAIN output text instead.

Table/Object Not Found: 

❌ Object not found: [name]
Did you mean: [similar objects from base_tableList]

EXPLAIN Execution Failure: 

⚠️ Could not execute EXPLAIN: [reason]
Options: 1) Paste EXPLAIN text directly  2) Verify SQL and permissions

docs.title: Teradata MCP Tools Reference
docs.text: Tool Purpose in EXPLAIN Analysis teradata:base_readQuery Execute EXPLAIN statement teradata:base_tableDDL Get table structure, indexes, partitioning teradata:base_columnDescription Column types, constraints, nullable teradata:dba_tableSpace Table size for scan severity assessment teradata:dba_tableUsageImpact Usage patterns to contextualize recommendations teradata:base_tableAffinity Commonly joined tables for index design teradata:base_tablePreview Sample data for distribution context teradata:dba_resusageSummary System resource usage for broader context

docs.title: Validation Checklist (Before Presenting)
docs.text:
Run through this before every delivery:

  • All EXPLAIN steps represented in flow diagram
  • Color coding consistent (critical=red, warning=orange, good=green, info=blue)
  • Confidence levels correctly classified per step
  • Parallel execution blocks shown side-by-side
  • Arrows include spool names and sizes
  • Interactive buttons tested (highlight critical path, product joins, reset)
  • Tooltips contain purpose + technical detail + fix suggestion
  • Legend explains all visual elements
  • Summary metrics accurate (total time, step count, critical count)
  • Key Insights panel references actual step numbers from the plan
  • HTML file saved to /mnt/user-data/outputs/
  • File presented via present_files as PRIMARY deliverable
  • Response text is brief (3–4 paragraphs max — the diagram IS the analysis)
  • Each recommendation is specific with ready-to-run SQL

docs.title: Response Text Format (After Presenting Diagram)
docs.text:
Keep text to minimum — the visual does the heavy lifting:

## 🚨 [or ✅] [One-sentence critical finding or validation]

[Flow diagram presented via present_files]

## Top Bottlenecks
1. Step X: [Issue] — Y.YYs (ZZ% of total)
2. Step X: [Issue] — Y.YYs (ZZ% of total)
3. Step X: [Issue] — Y.YYs (ZZ% of total)

## Quick Win
```sql
COLLECT STATISTICS ON db.table COLUMN col_name;
Expected impact: [Quantified or qualitative improvement estimate]

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. [Immediate — do today]
  2. [Short-term — this week]
  3. [Consider — ongoing] ```

docs.title: Special Cases
docs.text: Scenario Handling Very complex query (20+ steps) Add collapse/expand sections; navigation buttons Query already optimal Lead with ✅ validation; use diagram educationally Comparative analysis Side-by-side diagrams; winner table; delta metrics Pre-generated EXPLAIN text Validate format, skip execution step, proceed to parse Dynamic EXPLAIN with masking Note :* values; document IPE fragments used MERGE/UPSERT Show conditional branches; verify parallel sub-steps Column-partitioned table Note N+1 partition count; flag CP merge spool if present Skew indicators Highlight group_amps with few AMPs; recommend PRPD stats

docs.title: Training
docs.text:
A guided tour through tdfs4ds. Follow the thirteen modules in order to go from a fresh install to running the full stack in production. Each module is short — most are a single screen of prose plus links into the reference docs.

Audience

These modules assume you have completed Getting started › Installation and have an active Teradata Vantage connection. If not, start there first.

docs.title: The journey
docs.text: # Module What you'll learn 1 First feature Ingest your first feature end-to-end 2 Documentation Let an LLM write the business dictionary 3 Query optimization Auto-rewrite slow process views 4 Consumer agent Chat with your feature store 5 Vector store backends Chroma vs Teradata — pick where the chatbot's memory lives 6 FilterManager Segmented ingestion for large or partitioned sources 7 TimeManager Replay history at business time 8 Partitioning Why and how to partition feature tables 9 NULLs in entities The entity_null_substitute story 10 Catalogs and datasets The three catalogs and why datasets matter 11 Logs The follow-up table and how to recover from failures 12 Lineage Dependency graphs and Sankey visualisations 13 Migration manifest Replay a feature store in another environment

docs.title: How to read it
docs.text:
Modules 1–4 are the day-one path: ingest, document, optimize, chat. Module 5 is the architectural decision that unlocks production deployments. Modules 6–9 are the operational toolbox you reach for when ingestion has to scale or recover. Modules 10–13 cover governance and lifecycle — catalogs, audit logs, lineage, and cross-environment replay.

Every module ends with a Learn more → block linking to the authoritative reference pages, which is where the deep parameter tables live. The training section's job is to give you the shape of the picture and the order of the moves; the reference docs answer "what does this flag do?".

docs.title: After training
docs.text: Once you have walked through the thirteen modules, the Concepts section is the right next stop for the deeper architectural model (temporal data, hidden-tables pattern, data domains).

docs.title: 01 basics
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 1. First feature
docs.text: Goal: take a Teradata SQL query and turn it into a registered feature you can re-run, version, and share. Five steps.

docs.title: The shape of the workflow
docs.text:
1. Connect to Vantage with tdfs4ds.connect(...). The first call also creates the catalog tables if they don't exist yet.
  2. Pick a DATA_DOMAIN with create_data_domain('MY_PROJECT') (or select_data_domain if it already exists). Every feature, process, and dataset is tagged with this domain, which keeps unrelated projects from colliding in the same physical schema.
  3. Write a Teradata DataFrame that selects your entity keys plus one or more feature columns from a source table.
  4. Call upload_features with the entity ID definition and the list of feature column names. tdfs4ds registers the engineering view as a process, stores its metadata in the catalogs, runs the process for the first time, and merges the results into the feature tables.
  5. Re-run later with run(process_id) — no need to keep the original Python around. The view is now first-class.
import tdfs4ds, teradataml as tdml

tdfs4ds.connect(host='vantage.example.com', user='alice', password='...')
tdfs4ds.create_data_domain('CUSTOMER_360')

df = tdml.DataFrame.from_query("""
    SELECT customer_id,
           DATE '2024-01-01' AS feature_date,
           SUM(amount) AS lifetime_amount
    FROM SALES.ORDERS
    GROUP BY 1, 2
""")

process_id = tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df=df,
    entity_id={'customer_id': 'BIGINT'},
    feature_names=['lifetime_amount'],
)

docs.title: Why DATA_DOMAIN matters
docs.text: A single Teradata database can host the feature stores of several teams or several modelling exercises. DATA_DOMAIN is the namespace that keeps their catalogs cleanly separated. Always set it before any registration call — otherwise features land in the previous domain and become hard to find later.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Installation — pip install, optional extras, and which dependencies are lazy.
  • Connect and configure — connect() vs setup(), .env and JSON config files.
  • First feature — the same workflow with more parameter detail.
  • Recipes › One-line bootstrap — a minimal .env-driven launch.
  • Data domains — isolation semantics.

docs.title: 02 documentation
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 2. Documentation
docs.text: A registered feature is useful to its author for about a week. After that — or for anyone else on the team — the SQL needs a business description. tdfs4ds generates those descriptions with an LLM and stores them in a three-level business dictionary that the consumer agent later searches.

docs.title: Two ways to document
docs.text:
Inline at upload time. Pass document=True to upload_features (the default) and the registered process is documented immediately after it runs. If you also pass a dataset_view_name, the dataset is documented bottom-up from its constituent processes in the same call.

Out-of-band, on demand. Call document_process_incremental(process_id) later — useful when the instruct model wasn't configured yet at upload time, or after editing the process view.

from tdfs4ds.genai import (
    document_process_incremental,
    document_dataset_incremental,
)

document_process_incremental(process_id)
document_dataset_incremental(dataset_id)

docs.title: What gets written
docs.text:
Level Table Content 0 FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS One business description per registered object 1 FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS Five structured sections per dataset (OVERVIEW, ENTITY, FEATURE_THEMES, BUSINESS_QUESTIONS, INTENDED_AUDIENCE) 2 FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS One description per feature column on the process view

The hierarchy is designed for chunking-free hierarchical RAG: the consumer agent's SEARCH and DEFINITION skills query these tables directly.

docs.title: When to choose which path
docs.text:
• Always documenting at upload? Keep document=True.
  • Iterating on the SQL before you trust the description? Use the out-of-band call — it's idempotent and won't re-document unchanged objects.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Process documentation
  • Dataset documentation
  • Business dictionary
  • Recipes › Upload & document
  • LLM & embedding models — required config before the LLM path runs.

docs.title: 03 query optimizer
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 3. Query optimization
docs.text: Process views are SQL — and SQL can be slow. The query optimizer generates several rewrite candidates for a registered process, scores each one against the original via the Teradata EXPLAIN plan, and (optionally) replaces the view with the best candidate. It is also exposed as a one-flag inline option on upload_features.

docs.title: Inline on upload
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df=df,
    entity_id={'customer_id': 'BIGINT'},
    feature_names=['lifetime_amount'],
    optimize_query=True,      # generate + score rewrite candidates
    update_view=True,         # replace the view DDL with the best candidate
    max_candidates=5,         # override QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES
)

Without an instruct model configured the flag is silently ignored, so it is safe to keep on in code that runs in environments that may or may not have an LLM available.

docs.title: Out-of-band
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.ai import skill_optimize_query

report = skill_optimize_query(process_id=process_id, update_view=False)
print(report['score_delta'], report['best_sql'])

The full Markdown report — original vs each candidate, EXPLAIN signals, spool counts — is saved under {QUERY_OPTIMIZER_REPORT_DIR}/{DATA_DOMAIN}/tdfs4ds_optim_{view}_{process_id}.md.

docs.title: How it scores
docs.text:
Four signals are persisted per process:

  • user_score (1–5) — LLM-derived SQL quality (the part the author controls).
