  • global_score (1–5) — LLM-derived plan quality, including infra factors.
  • n_steps — deterministic count of execution steps.
  • n_spool_objects — deterministic count of intermediate spool objects.

Improvement is judged on these four together, not on the LLM's opinion alone.

docs.title: When to tune the knobs
docs.text:
• QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATES (default 10) — lower it for short feedback loops, raise it when you want the optimizer to explore more aggressively.
  • QUERY_OPTIMIZER_MAX_CANDIDATE_TOKENS — cap the output length for small-context models (≤ 16 k) so they don't exhaust their budget on prose.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Query optimizer — the full nine-step pipeline.
  • EXPLAIN scoring — what each signal means.
  • Recipes › Upload & document — combine optimize + document in a single call.

docs.title: 04 consumer agent
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 4. Consumer agent
docs.text: Once features are registered and documented, business users want to ask questions like "do we have a feature for customer lifetime value?" or "is provider_z_score still up to date?". The consumer agent is the LangGraph-based chatbot that answers those questions from the catalogs and the business dictionary — in French or English.

docs.title: What it can answer
docs.text:
Intent Example question SEARCH "What features analyse customer spending?" DEFINITION "What is lifetime_amount?" USAGE "How do I use customer_age in a dashboard?" FRESHNESS "Are the pharmacy features still up to date?" SUMMARY "Give me an overview of the claims domain." LINEAGE "Where does provider_amount_zscore come from?" EXPLAIN "How is risk_index calculated?"

The agent is a LangGraph StateGraph with a MemorySaver singleton, so a follow-up question reuses the previous turn's resolved entity, feature group, and data domain — no need to repeat context.

docs.title: Launch the chatbot
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.agent import launch_chatbot_with_index

launch_chatbot_with_index(port=7860, force_rebuild=False)

That single call (1) builds or refreshes the vector index from the business dictionary, then (2) opens a Gradio UI with a chat pane, a collapsible agent trace panel, and a visualizations panel for charts returned by MCP tools.

docs.title: Programmatic use
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.agent import consumer_agent

answer = consumer_agent(
    "What features do we have for the customer entity?",
    thread_id="alice-session-1",
)

Pass the same thread_id on the next call and the agent picks up where it left off — same domain, same entity, same feature group in scope.

docs.title: Where the answers come from
docs.text: The agent uses structured SQL first (catalog lookups, follow-up reports, SHOW VIEW DDL) and falls back to vector search when the SQL exact-match path returns nothing. The vector index has its own training module — see Module 5 › Vector store backends for where the index actually lives and how to choose between Chroma and the Teradata Vector Store.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Consumer agent — graph flow, skill catalog, MCP integration, multi-turn memory model.
  • Recipes › Launch the chatbot — minimal end-to-end notebook.
  • LLM & embedding models — instruct and embedding configuration.

docs.title: 05 vector store
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 5. Vector store backends
docs.text: The consumer agent's SEARCH, DEFINITION, and USAGE skills fall back to semantic search when an exact catalog lookup returns nothing. That semantic search needs somewhere to keep the embedded business descriptions — a vector store. Since version 0.3.1.37 you can choose between two backends: ChromaDB (default, local) and the Teradata Enterprise Vector Store (in-database).

docs.title: Why a vector store at all
docs.text:
build_vector_index() reads the three business-dictionary tables and creates three collections:

Collection Source Used by objects FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_OBJECTS SEARCH (object-level matches) sections FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_SECTIONS SEARCH (FEATURE_THEMES, BUSINESS_QUESTIONS) columns FS_BUSINESS_DICTIONARY_COLUMNS DEFINITION (per-feature descriptions)

Embeddings are computed once and re-queried on every chatbot turn that needs them. Without a vector store, the agent can only answer questions that match catalog names verbatim.

docs.title: Backend choice at a glance
docs.text: Chroma (default) Teradata Vector Store Where vectors live Local file system or a separate Chroma HTTP server Inside Vantage, alongside your features Governance Outside Teradata RBAC Uses Teradata privileges (DB role-based) Setup cost pip install only Requires Enterprise Vector Store entitlement Embedding source LangChain Embeddings (your vLLM / TEI / OpenAI) LangChain or in-DB TeradataAI Incremental update Per-id upsert Tries add_documents, falls back to rebuild Best for Local dev, notebooks, single-user demos Shared deployments, governed environments, "no data leaves Vantage" mandates

docs.title: ChromaDB — and its limitations
docs.text:
Chroma is the default because it works out of the box and needs no infrastructure. Two operating modes:

tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND = 'chroma'   # default
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_MODE = 'local'             # persist to disk
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_PATH = './tdfs4ds_chroma'  # persist directory

# or
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_MODE = 'server'            # connect to a Chroma server
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_HOST = 'localhost'
tdfs4ds.CHROMA_PORT = 8000

Be aware of these limitations before promoting Chroma beyond development:

  • Single-machine, single-process. The local persist directory uses file-system locks; two concurrent Python processes writing to the same directory can corrupt the index.
  • Separate process in server mode. CHROMA_MODE='server' requires you to run and operate a Chroma HTTP daemon — that's another binary to manage, monitor, and back up.
  • No Teradata governance. Chroma sits outside Teradata RBAC; anyone with file-system or HTTP access to the index can read the embedded business descriptions, regardless of their Teradata privileges.
  • Client-side embedding. Vectors are computed by the Python client through your LangChain Embeddings object, which can hit your LLM proxy's rate-limits. tdfs4ds throttles to batches of 8 to soften this, but it is still a client-side bottleneck.

docs.title: Teradata Vector Store
docs.text:
The Teradata backend stores the same three collections as named vector stores inside Vantage:

tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND         = 'teradata'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_VECTOR_STORE_PREFIX = 'tdfs4ds'
# becomes: tdfs4ds_objects, tdfs4ds_sections, tdfs4ds_columns

Two embedding paths are supported. By default the Teradata backend reuses the same LangChain Embeddings object as Chroma — so if you have a working vLLM / TEI / OpenAI / Bedrock setup, switching VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND is the only change you need. TeradataVectorStore accepts a LangChain Embeddings at its embedding= argument.

For environments where no client-side embedding is allowed, the in-database TeradataAI path produces embeddings inside Vantage:

tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_API_TYPE   = 'aws'                         # also: azure, gcp, nim, hugging_face, onnx
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_MODEL_NAME = 'amazon.titan-embed-text-v2:0'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_REGION     = 'us-west-2'
tdfs4ds.TERADATA_AI_ACCESS_KEY = os.getenv('AWS_ACCESS_KEY')

Known limitation: the Enterprise Vector Store API does not yet guarantee per-id upsert, so build_vector_index(force_rebuild=False) first tries add_documents and falls back to a destroy + rebuild if the call is not supported.

docs.title: Switching backends
docs.text:
The two functions the chatbot already uses — build_vector_index and search_vector_index — dispatch on VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND at call time, so flipping the switch and re-indexing is the whole operation:

tdfs4ds.VECTOR_STORE_BACKEND = 'teradata'
from tdfs4ds.agent import build_vector_index
build_vector_index(force_rebuild=True)

No other code changes are needed; the SEARCH skill keeps using search_vector_index(...) and the chatbot launcher is the same.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Vector store backend — full configuration reference for both backends.
  • Consumer agent — how skills consume the index.
  • Changelog 0.3.1.37 — the release that added the Teradata backend.

docs.title: 06 filter manager
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 6. FilterManager
docs.text: When the source table is too large to ingest in a single pass — or is itself partitioned and you want to exploit that — FilterManager lets you step through it in segments. The feature view stays unchanged; the filter values are injected as scalar subqueries that the optimizer can eliminate.

docs.title: Why segmented ingestion
docs.text:
• Memory. A single MERGE over a 10-billion-row source spools forever and can fail before the catalog records the run. Smaller chunks succeed independently.
  • Parallelism. Each filter run is independent. You can fan them out to separate sessions or schedule them across the day.
  • Partition pruning. If the source is PARTITION BY RANGE_N(DATE_COL), filtering on DATE_COL skips entire partitions on every iteration — see Module 8 › Partitioning.
  • Failure isolation. A failing iteration logs FAILED,… in the follow-up table and the loop continues with the next filter — you re-run the bad one in isolation later. See Module 11 › Logs.

docs.title: Three modes
docs.text: Mode Use it when Standard One categorical column (e.g. REGION) Hybrid Date + categorical column (e.g. TX_DATE × REGION) Clone You already have a filter table you want to attach

docs.title: How to use it
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.utils.filter_management import FilterManager

fm = FilterManager(
    schema_name='MY_DB',
    view_name='V_TX_FACT',
    col_names=['region'],
    time_column=None,        # set to a date column for Hybrid mode
)
fm.load_filter()             # populate the filter table
print(fm.nb_filters)         # how many iterations

tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df=df,
    entity_id={'customer_id': 'BIGINT'},
    feature_names=['regional_amount'],
    filtermanager=fm,        # tdfs4ds iterates fm.update(filter_id=...) internally
)

Two operational rules from production experience: seed small (load a few filters first to validate the view), and order by date DESC in Hybrid mode so the most recent data ingests first.

docs.title: Cloning filters
docs.text:
When several processes share the same partition sequence, clone the filter instead of calling load_filter again:

fm_p2 = FilterManager(table_name='FS_FILTER_REGIONS_P2', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
fm_p2.clone_filter(fm_source)          # soft clone — no data copied

A soft clone shares the hidden table. A hard clone (clone_mode='hard') copies the data into a new table that this manager owns independently. See the FilterManager user guide for ownership semantics and if_exists options.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• FilterManager — full mode reference, inspection methods, cloning (soft/hard), ownership, and pruning.
  • Recipes › Hybrid date × partition filter — end-to-end example combining date and a categorical column.
  • Module 8 › Partitioning — why FilterManager pairs naturally with partitioned source tables.

docs.title: 07 time manager
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 7. TimeManager
docs.text: A feature engineered today on yesterday's data is fine — but training a model needs the feature as it was a year ago, not a recomputation of today's logic on yesterday's snapshot. TimeManager replays your registered processes at historical business times so the feature store accumulates a real time series.

docs.title: Why replay history
docs.text:
• Point-in-time correctness. The model trains on what the entity actually looked like at the prediction date, not today's value of an attribute that has since drifted.
  • Reproducibility. A future audit can read the feature value as of any past date by setting FEATURE_STORE_TIME to that date.
  • No data leakage. The features registered for date t only see source data available at or before t.

tdfs4ds separates two clocks: business time (the date you are materialising) and system time (Teradata VALIDTIME, when the row was written). See Concepts › Temporal model for the full mental model.

docs.title: How to use it
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.utils.time_management import TimeManager

bt = TimeManager(table_name='FS_BUSINESS_TIME', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
bt.load_time_steps(df_calendar, date_column='AS_OF_DATE')  # one-time setup

tdfs4ds.roll_out(
    process_list=[process_id_1, process_id_2],
    time_manager=bt,
    time_id_start=1,
    time_id_end=bt.nb_time_steps,
)

roll_out iterates each registered process across every business time in the manager. Manual stepping works too — set tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME to the desired past date and call run(process_id).

docs.title: Cloning a TimeManager
docs.text:
When multiple processes share the same date calendar, clone the timer rather than loading it again:

bt_p2 = TimeManager(table_name='FS_BT_PROCESS2', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
bt_p2.clone_timer(bt_source)          # soft clone — view points to source hidden table

A soft clone creates only a new view (no data copied). A hard clone (clone_mode='hard') copies the hidden table into this schema; the clone is fully independent and can evolve separately. take_ownership() promotes ownership of the shared table when a soft-clone manager should be responsible for cleanup.

See the TimeManager user guide for if_exists options and the create_copy / take_ownership details.

docs.title: Composing with FilterManager
docs.text: Both managers are independent and stack. A common pattern: TimeManager for the when, FilterManager for the what — replay each historical date segment-by-segment so a single chunk of work fits in memory. When multiple processes share both the same date calendar and different partition filters, soft-clone the timer for each process and attach the relevant FilterManager to each roll_out call.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• TimeManager & roll_out — full parameter reference, cloning (soft/hard), ownership, pruning, and composed patterns.
  • Temporal model — business time vs system time, write semantics.
  • Module 6 › FilterManager — for stacking time and segment loops.

docs.title: 08 partitioning
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 8. Partitioning
docs.text: A feature table that grows by tens of millions of rows per day quickly becomes the slowest part of every query that touches it. Teradata's solution is table partitioning: physically split the rows on a column so that a predicate on that column scans only a fraction of the table. tdfs4ds exposes partitioning as first-class parameters on upload_features.

docs.title: Why partition
docs.text:
Two different concepts share the name "index" in Teradata; they do different jobs and you usually want both:

  • PRIMARY INDEX governs row distribution across AMPs. A good primary index spreads rows evenly so the work parallelises. A WHERE clause on the primary index does not skip data — it just routes the query to the right AMP.
  • PARTITION BY governs scan footprint within an AMP. A WHERE clause on a partition column lets Teradata skip whole partitions on disk — partition elimination. This is where the wins on large fact tables come from.

A typical pattern for a temporal feature table:

  • Primary index: the entity key (e.g. customer_id) — even distribution and fast lookup of a single entity's history.
  • Partitioning: the event date — partition elimination when a downstream query filters by date range.

docs.title: How to declare it
docs.text:
Both controls are arguments to upload_features:

tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df=df,
    entity_id={'customer_id': 'BIGINT', 'event_date': 'DATE'},
    feature_names=['daily_amount'],
    primary_index='customer_id',
    partitioning=(
        "PARTITION BY RANGE_N("
        "event_date BETWEEN DATE '2020-01-01' AND DATE '2030-12-31' "
        "EACH INTERVAL '1' MONTH)"
    ),
)

primary_index accepts a column name (or comma-separated list); partitioning accepts a raw Teradata clause — anything you would normally append after the column list in a CREATE TABLE — which lets you use RANGE_N, CASE_N, or multi-level partitioning without tdfs4ds having to know about every variant.

docs.title: Partitioning enables FilterManager
docs.text: Module 6 › FilterManager becomes much more powerful when the source it iterates over is partitioned. Each filter iteration adds a WHERE event_date = ... (or WHERE region = ...) predicate; if those columns are also partition columns, Teradata skips every partition that doesn't match — so the per-iteration cost shrinks linearly with the number of partitions you have. The query optimizer in Module 3 flags partitioned-but-unfiltered tables and offers a FilterManager rewrite suggestion.

docs.title: Diagnosing existing tables
docs.text:
The lineage graph exposes the primary-index and partition-column information for every table it walks:

from tdfs4ds.lineage import build_teradata_dependency_graph

graph = build_teradata_dependency_graph(sql_query="SELECT * FROM MY_DB.V_TX_FACT")
for name, info in graph['table_info'].items():
    print(name,
          'PI:', info.get('primary_index_columns'),
          'partitions:', info.get('partition_columns'))

Use this before refactoring an ingestion to confirm which columns Teradata will eliminate on — then design your FilterManager and feature view to exercise those columns.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Dependency graph — primary_index_columns, partition_columns, partitioning_levels on every node.
  • upload_features — full parameter list.
  • FilterManager — the natural partner.
  • Query optimizer — flags partitioned-but-unfiltered access paths and proposes rewrites.

docs.title: 09 entity nulls
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 9. NULLs in entities
docs.text: A subtle production trap: if any entity-ID column in your source DataFrame can be NULL, the rows with NULL keys are silently dropped during ingestion unless you tell tdfs4ds what sentinel value to use instead. This module explains why, and how to declare the substitution.

docs.title: The problem
docs.text:
In SQL, NULL = NULL is unknown (not true), so an equi-join on an entity-ID column never matches when either side is NULL. The feature-store ingestion MERGE joins the incoming DataFrame to the existing feature table on the entity-ID columns to decide insert-vs-update; rows whose entity ID is NULL therefore neither update an existing row nor insert a new one — they just disappear, without an error.

The same issue surfaces on the SELECT that unpivots the source into the volatile staging table, because the entity columns also serve as a primary index there.

docs.title: The solution
docs.text:
upload_features accepts an entity_null_substitute dict that maps each entity-ID column to a sentinel:

tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df=df,
    entity_id={'customer_id': 'BIGINT', 'merchant_code': 'VARCHAR(20)'},
    feature_names=['amount'],
    entity_null_substitute={
        'customer_id':   -1,         # numeric sentinel
        'merchant_code': 'UNKNOWN',  # string sentinel
    },
)

Under the hood, tdfs4ds rewrites every comparison on those columns as COALESCE(col, sentinel) on both sides — the staging SELECT and the join predicate of the MERGE. NULL rows are now joined and stored under the sentinel value, and downstream queries can find them.

docs.title: Choosing a sentinel
docs.text:
The sentinel must be a value that cannot collide with a legitimate entity ID. Common safe choices:

Entity-ID type Sentinel BIGINT / INTEGER -1 (or another large negative reserved value) VARCHAR 'UNKNOWN', '__NULL__', or a magic UUID DATE DATE '1900-01-01' TIMESTAMP TIMESTAMP '1900-01-01 00:00:00'

If a sentinel can collide with real data, you get a worse bug: legitimate rows merge with the NULL bucket.

docs.title: What if you forget
docs.text:
There is no error and no warning. Inspect the row counts after a load:

import teradataml as tdml
tdml.execute_sql(
    "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM SRC_TABLE WHERE customer_id IS NULL"
).fetchall()

If the count is non-zero and you didn't pass entity_null_substitute, those rows are not in the feature store.

docs.title: Composability
docs.text: entity_null_substitute works alongside FilterManager and TimeManager: the substitution is applied at SELECT and MERGE time for every iteration, so segmented and replayed ingestion behave consistently.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• upload_features — the parameter is part of the public signature.
  • Data model — entity-ID semantics and the hidden-tables pattern.

docs.title: 10 catalogs and datasets
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 10. Catalogs and datasets
docs.text: Every artefact you registered in the previous modules — features, processes, datasets — lives in one of three catalogs. The catalogs are what makes tdfs4ds a feature store rather than a pile of views: they record what was registered, when, by whom, and with which lineage.

docs.title: The three catalogs
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.feature_catalog()    # registered features (one row per feature × entity × domain)
tdfs4ds.process_catalog()    # registered processes (one row per process / view)
tdfs4ds.dataset_catalog()    # registered datasets (one row per dataset view)
Catalog Stores Use it to Feature FEATURE_NAME, ENTITY_NAME, FEATURE_TYPE, DATA_DOMAIN, source table/view Discover which features exist, where the values land Process PROCESS_ID, VIEW_NAME, original SQL, registered metadata Re-run a feature engineering step, inspect its DDL Dataset DATASET_ID, DATASET_NAME, DATASET_DATABASE Find a downstream dataset, look up its features

All three are temporal: FEATURE_STORE_TIME = '2024-01-01 00:00:00' returns the catalog as it was on that date.

docs.title: What a dataset is — and why it matters
docs.text:
A tdfs4ds dataset is a registered view that joins multiple features across one or more entities into a single wide table. It is never materialised; the view is built on top of the per-feature storage tables and re-evaluated every time it is queried. That design is deliberate, and it gives you four advantages:

  1. Composition. Pick any subset of features and produce a dataset in one build_dataset(...) call — no ad-hoc joins littered across notebooks.
  2. Versioning. Each feature reference in the dataset is pinned to a process_id (the version of the engineering logic that produced it). Re-running the upstream process under a new process_id doesn't change the dataset's definition until you rebuild it.
  3. Time travel. Because the storage tables are temporal, every dataset inherits time travel for free — set FEATURE_STORE_TIME and the dataset returns the values that were valid at that timestamp.
  4. Governance. The dataset_catalog gives the consumer agent (and any downstream BI tool) a single source of truth for "which datasets exist and what features do they contain".
from tdfs4ds.feature_store.feature_query_retrieval import get_feature_versions

versions = get_feature_versions('customer', ['lifetime_amount', 'churn_score'])
dataset_id, view_name = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id={'customer_id': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features=versions,
    view_name='V_CUSTOMER_360',
)

docs.title: Inspecting catalogs at runtime
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.dataset.dataset_catalog import DatasetCatalog

DatasetCatalog(
    schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA,
    name=tdfs4ds.DATASET_CATALOG_NAME,
).get_dataset_features(dataset_id).to_pandas()

The inspect & monitor user-guide page covers the rest of the read-only introspection surface.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Data model — catalog schemas, hidden-table pattern, how the public views are built on top.
  • Datasets — build_dataset, grouped strategies, time-travel snapshots.
  • Inspect & monitor — read-only catalog helpers and follow-up reports.
  • First dataset — the end-to-end walkthrough.

docs.title: 11 logs
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 11. Logs
docs.text: Every ingestion or replay leaves a row in the follow-up table (FS_FOLLOW_UP). It is the canonical record of what ran, when, with which filter, and how it ended. Read it whenever you need to answer "did that upload actually finish?" or "which runs failed last week?".

docs.title: Schema
docs.text:
CREATE MULTISET TABLE {SCHEMA}.FS_FOLLOW_UP, FALLBACK, ... (
    RUN_ID         VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    PROCESS_TYPE   VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    PROCESS_ID     VARCHAR(255),
    START_DATETIME TIMESTAMP(6) WITH TIME ZONE NOT NULL,
    END_DATETIME   TIMESTAMP(6) WITH TIME ZONE,
    VALIDTIME_DATE TIMESTAMP(6) WITH TIME ZONE,
    APPLIED_FILTER JSON(2000),
    STATUS         VARCHAR(2048)
)
PRIMARY INDEX (RUN_ID, PROCESS_ID);
Column Meaning RUN_ID UUID generated per upload_features / run call PROCESS_TYPE 'RUN', 'UPLOAD', etc. — what triggered the row PROCESS_ID The registered process (NULL for some bookkeeping rows) START_DATETIME When the run opened (always set) END_DATETIME When the run closed (NULL while still running) VALIDTIME_DATE FEATURE_STORE_TIME at the time of the run (NULL = current) APPLIED_FILTER JSON snapshot of the FilterManager row in use STATUS 'RUNNING', 'COMPLETED', or 'FAILED,…' (with the error tail)

A view FS_V_FOLLOW_UP over the table provides LOCK ROW FOR ACCESS reads — use it for dashboards or BI tools.

docs.title: Reading the report
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.follow_up_report()  # most recent runs first

The output is a teradataml DataFrame; filter with normal SQL or pandas:

recent_failures = (
    tdfs4ds.follow_up_report()
    .to_pandas()
    .query("STATUS.str.startswith('FAILED')", engine='python')
)

docs.title: Status lifecycle
docs.text:
followup_open()           →  STATUS = 'RUNNING'
   │
   ├──  success           →  STATUS = 'COMPLETED'
   └──  exception         →  STATUS = 'FAILED,<message>'

A row that has been 'RUNNING' for hours is a stale row — the Python process crashed (or the session was killed) before followup_close could fire. Two recovery patterns:

  • Re-run. tdfs4ds.run(process_id) opens a new RUN_ID and ingests again; if the underlying MERGE is idempotent the old 'RUNNING' row is cosmetic.
  • Manual cleanup. Update the stale row to 'FAILED,manual cleanup' so reports stay clean. Stale rows do not block anything; they are just misleading on dashboards.

Known quirk

followup_close re-raises before the UPDATE executes when called with a FAIL* status. This means a failing ingestion path can leave the follow-up row in 'RUNNING' rather than 'FAILED,...'. Treat any old 'RUNNING' row as evidence of an unfinished or crashed run and apply one of the recovery patterns above. See the "followup_close" note in CLAUDE.md for the full background.

docs.title: When to widen the audit trail
docs.text: If STATUS truncates your error message (the column is VARCHAR(2048)), log the full traceback to a separate sink in your application — the follow-up table is intentionally narrow so it stays cheap to scan.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Inspect & monitor — full follow_up_report parameter reference, plus process_catalog, feature_catalog, dataset_catalog read helpers.
  • Temporal model — the write lifecycle and why a 'RUNNING' row left over after a crash is the canonical signal of an incomplete ingestion.

docs.title: 12 lineage
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 12. Lineage
docs.text: A registered process is a SQL view that depends on other views and tables. A registered dataset is a view that depends on registered processes, which in turn depend on raw sources. tdfs4ds walks those dependencies for you, returns a graph object, and renders it as an interactive Sankey diagram.

docs.title: Why look at lineage
docs.text:
• Audit before refactor. Before you change a source table, see every feature, process, and dataset that depends on it.
  • Impact assessment. A request to deprecate RAW.LEGACY_CUSTOMERS? Walk forward and list everything you'd break.
  • Onboarding. "Where does risk_index come from?" → one diagram.
  • Migration planning. The graph feeds the migration manifest in Module 13.

docs.title: Build the graph
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import build_teradata_dependency_graph

graph = build_teradata_dependency_graph(
    sql_query="SELECT * FROM MY_DB.V_CUSTOMER_360",
    expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=True,
)

graph is a dict with three useful keys:

Key Content nodes One entry per object: name, object_type ('table', 'view', 'process', 'dataset'), and table_info with PI / partition columns edges Directed dependencies (source → consumer) roots Nodes with no upstream dependencies — the raw sources

Two flags worth knowing:

  • stop_at_feature_store_datasets=True — recognise registered datasets as leaves rather than walking through them.
  • expand_datasets_via_process_catalog=True — when a dataset is hit, resolve its FEATURE_VERSION UUIDs against FS_V_PROCESS_CATALOG and link to the process views (not the raw storage tables). Defaults to True.

docs.title: Visualise it
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import plot_lineage_sankey

fig = plot_lineage_sankey(graph, title='Customer 360 lineage')
fig.show()                # interactive in a notebook
fig.write_html('lineage.html')   # share offline

The consumer agent's LINEAGE skill uses the same graph under the hood — chatting "Where does lifetime_amount come from?" returns the depth-ordered dependency list and a plain-language summary.

docs.title: Diagnose partitioning at the same time
docs.text:
Every node carries primary_index_columns and partition_columns when known. Combine that with Module 8 › Partitioning to audit which source tables exploit partition elimination and which don't:

unpartitioned = [
    n for n, info in graph['table_info'].items()
    if info.get('object_type') == 'table' and not info.get('partition_columns')
]

docs.title: Persistent lineage (the second approach)
docs.text:
The on-the-fly graph above re-derives lineage every call. For repeated queries and impact analysis ("what breaks if I change this?"), tdfs4ds also maintains a persisted, temporal lineage graph in FS_LINEAGE_GRAPH. It is:

  • Created automatically when missing.
  • Updated every time you call upload_features().
  • Queryable in both directions — ancestors (upstream) and descendants (impact).
  • Reusable for documentation, impact checking, and visualization.
from tdfs4ds.lineage import get_descendants, get_ancestors

# Impact: everything downstream of this view
get_descendants('MY_DB', 'V_CUSTOMER_FEATURES')

# Sources: everything upstream of this dataset
get_ancestors('MY_DB', 'DS_CHURN_RISK')

For the first time, or after adding many processes, rebuild the whole graph:

import tdfs4ds
tdfs4ds.rebuild_lineage_graph()        # re-derive all edges, store once

Then document your entire store from that persisted graph:

tdfs4ds.rebuild_documentation()        # document every process/dataset once, topo-sorted

See Persisted lineage graph for full details, and Rebuild documentation for how to auto-document your store in a single pass.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Persisted lineage graph — query in both directions, rebuild, rebuild documentation, scoped refresh.
  • Dependency graph — full parameter reference, graph shape, expansion controls (for on-the-fly analysis).
  • Sankey visualisation — Plotly customisation and the show_plotly_robust helper for offline-safe sharing.
  • Module 13 › Migration manifest — export the graph as JSON to replay it in another environment.

docs.title: 13 migration manifest
docs.text: ← Training overview

docs.title: 13. Migration manifest
docs.text: A feature store built in DEV eventually needs to land in STAGING and PROD. The migration manifest turns the lineage graph from Module 12 into a JSON document you can replay in a target environment — a portable, declarative bill of materials.

docs.title: What the manifest contains
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.lineage import (
    build_teradata_dependency_graph,
    graph_to_migration_manifest,
)

graph = build_teradata_dependency_graph(
    sql_query="SELECT * FROM DEV_DB.V_CUSTOMER_360",
)
manifest = graph_to_migration_manifest(graph, filter_database='DEV_DB')

The manifest is a dict with three lists:

Section Content views Every view's name, schema, DDL, and dependency list tables Source tables with PI and partition columns edges The directed dependency edges, filtered to the target database

filter_database='DEV_DB' restricts the export to objects in that schema — edges that cross the boundary are excluded so the manifest is a self-contained slice you can apply elsewhere.

docs.title: Replay it
docs.text:
A typical end-to-end migration:

  1. Source side. 
    import json
with open('manifest.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(manifest, f, indent=2, default=str)
  2. Target side. 
    tdfs4ds.connect(host='prod.example.com', user='prod_user', password='...')
tdfs4ds.create_data_domain('CUSTOMER_360')
tdfs4ds.setup()
  3. Re-create the views in dependency order (the manifest already lists them sorted) — for each entry, run its DDL against the target.
  4. Re-run each process — tdfs4ds.run(process_id) re-ingests features under the target schema using the same SQL.
  5. Rebuild datasets — build_dataset(...) from the manifest entries.

docs.title: Compare two environments
docs.text:
Manifests are JSON, so a regular diff highlights drift between environments:

diff dev-manifest.json prod-manifest.json

A missing view, a stale partition clause, an extra dependency — all visible in plain text.

docs.title: Composing with the rest of the training
docs.text:
• Module 8 › Partitioning — the manifest preserves primary_index and partition_columns so PROD ends up partitioned the same way as DEV.
  • Module 10 › Catalogs and datasets — after replay, the target catalogs hold the same PROCESS_ID and DATASET_ID rows, which keeps lineage and documentation consistent across environments.
  • Module 11 › Logs — the follow-up table is per-environment, so each replay creates its own RUN_ID rows in PROD.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Migration manifest — full graph_to_migration_manifest reference and the exact JSON schema.
  • Dependency graph — the upstream function that built the graph.

That's the end of the training journey. From here, the Concepts section explains the underlying architecture, Configuration covers every knob in detail, and the API reference documents every public function.

docs.title: Datasets — build_dataset
docs.text: A dataset is a denormalised Teradata view that joins selected feature versions for a given entity. It is always a view, never a materialised table.

docs.title: Resolve feature versions first
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.feature_store.feature_query_retrieval import get_feature_versions

selected_features = get_feature_versions(
    entity_name = 'CUSTOMER_ID',
    features    = ['nb_transactions', 'total_amount', 'avg_amount'],
)

selected_features is {feature_name: process_uuid}. Always go through get_feature_versions — manual construction skips type validation and is brittle to renames.

docs.title: Build
docs.text:
dataset = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
    view_name         = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND',
    comment           = 'Customer spending features',
)

dataset.shape

docs.title: Query strategy — grouped parameter
docs.text:
By default build_dataset emits one sub-query and one JOIN per feature:

SELECT A1.CUSTOMER_ID, A1.FEATURE_VALUE AS nb_transactions,
       A2.FEATURE_VALUE AS total_amount, ...
FROM (SEQUENCED VALIDTIME SELECT ... WHERE FEATURE_ID=1) A1
JOIN (SEQUENCED VALIDTIME SELECT ... WHERE FEATURE_ID=2) A2
  ON A1.CUSTOMER_ID = A2.CUSTOMER_ID
...

Pass grouped=True to activate the pivot strategy instead — features that share the same source table and process ID are collapsed into a single MAX(CASE WHEN FEATURE_ID=… THEN FEATURE_VALUE END) … GROUP BY sub-query:

dataset = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
    view_name         = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND',
    comment           = 'Customer spending features — grouped strategy',
    grouped           = True,
)

The generated SQL looks like:

SELECT A1.CUSTOMER_ID
  ,MAX(CASE WHEN FEATURE_ID=1 THEN FEATURE_VALUE END) AS nb_transactions
  ,MAX(CASE WHEN FEATURE_ID=2 THEN FEATURE_VALUE END) AS total_amount
  ...
FROM (SEQUENCED VALIDTIME SELECT ...
      WHERE FEATURE_VERSION='...' AND FEATURE_ID IN (1, 2, ...)) A1
GROUP BY A1.CUSTOMER_ID
HAVING MAX(CASE WHEN FEATURE_ID=1 THEN FEATURE_VALUE END) IS NOT NULL
   AND MAX(CASE WHEN FEATURE_ID=2 THEN FEATURE_VALUE END) IS NOT NULL

Single-feature groups are unaffected

When a group contains only one feature, build_dataset emits a plain FEATURE_ID = N scan with no GROUP BY — no performance penalty.

When to use grouped=True

The Teradata optimizer normalises both patterns to equivalent execution plans for typical queries. grouped=True is most beneficial when you have 20+ features from the same table/process and want to reduce the structural JOIN count — useful if the optimizer's rewrite budget is exhausted by very wide feature sets.

docs.title: Inspect the generated SQL
docs.text:
Use return_query=True to retrieve the DDL without executing it or touching the dataset catalog:

sql = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
    view_name         = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND',
    return_query      = True,
)
print(sql)

docs.title: Point-in-time snapshots
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = '2024-01-01 00:00:00'

dataset_jan = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
    view_name         = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND_2024_01_01',
)

tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = None

The dataset view embeds a VALIDTIME AS OF TIMESTAMP '2024-01-01 00:00:00' qualifier inside each sub-query, so it always returns values as of that point even when queried later.

Both grouped=False (default) and grouped=True work with time travel. The temporal qualifier applied inside each sub-query changes automatically:

Mode Sub-query temporal qualifier Current (FEATURE_STORE_TIME = None) SEQUENCED VALIDTIME Time travel (FEATURE_STORE_TIME = '…') VALIDTIME AS OF TIMESTAMP '…' 
tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = '2024-01-01 00:00:00'

dataset_grouped_past = tdfs4ds.build_dataset(
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
    view_name         = 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND_2024_01_01_GROUPED',
    grouped           = True,
    feature_store_time = True,
)

tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = None

docs.title: Augment a source DataFrame in place
docs.text:
augment_source_with_features is useful for ad-hoc model exploration when you do not want to register a permanent dataset view:

from tdfs4ds.dataset.builder import augment_source_with_features

augmented = augment_source_with_features(
    source_df         = my_source_df,
    entity_id         = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    selected_features = selected_features,
)

docs.title: Inspect and re-attach
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.dataset_catalog()
tdfs4ds.get_dataset_entity()       # entity per dataset
tdfs4ds.get_dataset_features()     # feature versions per dataset

# Re-attach in any later session — no rebuild
ds = tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema(tdfs4ds.SCHEMA, 'DS_CUSTOMER_SPEND'))

docs.title: FilterManager — segmented ingestion
docs.text: FilterManager lets upload_features iterate through a source one partition at a time: one date, one category, or one (date × category) combination per pass. It is the right tool whenever a single full-source ingestion would exhaust spool, exceed a maintenance window, or scan way more data than necessary.

docs.title: When to use it
docs.text:
• A source table is partitioned but the feature engineering view does not filter on the partition columns. The query optimizer / fs-analyze will flag this and propose a FilterManager rewrite.
  • The source is too large for a single MERGE pass.
  • The business logic should ingest one region / one segment / one date at a time for traceability.

docs.title: Three modes
docs.text: Mode Filter columns Typical use Standard One categorical column Iterate by region / segment / category Hybrid Date + categorical Iterate by (date × category) — BUSINESS_DATE always present Clone Copy an existing filter Re-use the same filter sequence across multiple processes

docs.title: How FilterManager works internally
docs.text:
FilterManager maintains two database objects:

  1. Hidden table (<view_name>_HIDDEN) — stores every filter row, each assigned a deterministic filter_id via ROW_NUMBER(). This table is written once by load_filter() and never mutated during ingestion.
  2. Public view (<view_name>) — always exposes exactly one row: WHERE filter_id = N. Switching to a different partition rewrites only this view definition — no data is touched.

Your feature engineering view references the public view with scalar subqueries. Teradata resolves those at plan time and pushes the constant into the source scan, enabling partition elimination.

load_filter()          → writes hidden table + creates view at filter_id=1
update(filter_id=N)    → REPLACE VIEW … WHERE filter_id = N
display()              → SELECT * FROM public view  (shows current partition)

When upload_features(filtermanager=fm) is called, the framework calls fm.update(filter_id) for each iteration internally.

docs.title: Backing-table storage on Vantage Cloud Lake (storage='OFS')
docs.text:
By default the hidden table is created on block storage — unchanged. On Vantage Cloud Lake you can create it on the Object File System (OFS) instead:

fm = FilterManager(
    table_name  = 'FS_FILTER_REGIONS',
    schema_name = tdfs4ds.SCHEMA,
    storage     = 'OFS',        # None (default) = block storage, unchanged
)

This emits MAP = TD_MAP2, STORAGE = TD_OFSSTORAGE. The choice is per manager (block and OFS managers coexist in one session) and only affects table creation; ofs_map= overrides the map (default TD_MAP2).

tdstone2 Apply on the ACC: OFS manager table and a JOIN (not a scalar subquery)

A tdstone2 model scored via the Apply backend runs on the Analytical Compute Cluster (ACC), which reads from OFS. For a scoring view that references the FilterManager, two conditions must both hold: (1) storage='OFS' so the ACC can read the manager table (block storage → Error 6881); (2) reference the filter/date values via a JOIN to the FilterManager view, not correlated scalar subqueries — the ACC rejects scalar subqueries with 6881 but accepts a JOIN. Confirmed live: OFS + JOIN scores; OFS + scalar subquery still 6881s. Writing the filter as a JOIN is the user's responsibility. Note this is the opposite of the block-storage default guidance elsewhere in this page (scalar subqueries for partition elimination) — it applies only to the tdstone2-Apply/ACC path. See the TimeManager guide for a worked JOIN example.

docs.title: Standard FilterManager
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.utils.filter_management import FilterManager

fm = FilterManager(
    table_name  = 'FS_FILTER_REGIONS',
    schema_name = tdfs4ds.SCHEMA,
)

# Populate with the distinct REGION values from the source
fm.load_filter(tdml.DataFrame.from_query("""
    SELECT DISTINCT REGION
    FROM source_db.TRANSACTIONS
    ORDER BY REGION
"""))

print('Iterations:', fm.nb_filters)
fm.display()   # shows the current (first) partition

docs.title: Wire the filter into the feature view
docs.text:
The source query must reference the FilterManager view with scalar subqueries — the optimizer resolves the value before scanning the source table, enabling partition elimination:

df_features_raw = tdml.DataFrame.from_query(f"""
    SELECT A.*
    FROM source_db.TRANSACTIONS A
    WHERE A.REGION = (SELECT REGION FROM {fm.schema_name}.{fm.view_name})
""")

# ... your feature engineering ...
df_features_proc = crystallize_view(
    df_features_raw.groupby('CUSTOMER_ID').agg(...),
    view_name   = 'FEAT_ENG_TX',
    schema_name = tdfs4ds.SCHEMA,
)

tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df_features_proc,
    entity_id     = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    feature_names = ['nb_tx', 'total_amount'],
    filtermanager = fm,            # ← framework iterates over all partitions
)

docs.title: Hybrid FilterManager (time × partition)
docs.text:
The hybrid mode adds a BUSINESS_DATE column so one iteration covers both a calendar date and a categorical dimension. The public view always exposes BUSINESS_DATE as the time column — your feature view reads it with a scalar subquery just like any other filter column.

fm = FilterManager(table_name='FS_FILTER_REGION_DATE', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)

fm.load_filter(
    tdml.DataFrame.from_query("""
        SELECT DISTINCT REGION, TX_DATE
        FROM source_db.TRANSACTIONS
        ORDER BY TX_DATE DESC, REGION
    """),
    time_column='TX_DATE',      # → stored as BUSINESS_DATE
)

print(fm.nb_filters)            # total iterations = |dates| × |regions|

df_features_raw = tdml.DataFrame.from_query(f"""
    SELECT A.*
    FROM source_db.TRANSACTIONS A
    WHERE A.TX_DATE = (SELECT BUSINESS_DATE FROM {fm.schema_name}.{fm.view_name})
      AND A.REGION  = (SELECT REGION        FROM {fm.schema_name}.{fm.view_name})
""")

The BUSINESS_DATE column is also consumed by roll_out to set tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME automatically on each step.

Cloning a hybrid filter works identically to a standard filter — clone_filter() preserves the BUSINESS_DATE column in the shared hidden table. See Cloning.

docs.title: Inspection methods
docs.text:
Method Returns Description display() teradataml.DataFrame Current partition row from the public view get_all_filters() teradataml.DataFrame All rows in the hidden table get_current_filterid() int Active filter_id parsed from view DDL get_date_in_the_past() str BUSINESS_DATE of the current partition (hybrid only) get_filter_condition() dict or str Active filter as a Python dict or JSON string print_view_ddl() — Logs the view DDL for troubleshooting 
# Inspect active filter as a dictionary
print(fm.get_filter_condition(output_type="dict"))
# → {'REGION': 'WEST'}

# Jump manually to partition 5 for debugging
fm.update(filter_id=5)
fm.display()

# Roll back to the beginning
fm.update(filter_id=1)

docs.title: Pruning
docs.text:
prune_filter() removes all partitions with filter_id < N and renumbers the remaining ones so the smallest id becomes 1. Use it to discard already-ingested partitions and keep the table lean.

# Remove all partitions before the current one (compact to where we are)
fm.prune_filter()                  # uses get_current_filterid() automatically

# Or remove everything before partition 10
fm.prune_filter(filter_id=10)

After pruning the public view is repointed to filter_id=1.

docs.title: Cloning
docs.text: Cloning lets one process re-use the filter definitions owned by another without duplicating the hidden table data. There are two clone modes:

docs.title: Soft clone (default)
docs.text:
The new manager's view points directly to the source hidden table. No data is copied. Both managers remain in sync: if the source is repopulated with load_filter, both managers automatically reflect the new data (though their active filter_id is independent).

# Source manager already exists and has been populated
fm_source = FilterManager(table_name='FS_FILTER_REGIONS', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)

# New manager for a second feature process — shares the same hidden table
fm_new = FilterManager(table_name='FS_FILTER_REGIONS_PROCESS2', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
fm_new.clone_filter(
    source_filtermanager = fm_source,
    filter_id_to_apply   = 1,         # start at the first partition
    clone_mode           = 'soft',    # default
)

The new FS_FILTER_REGIONS_PROCESS2 view is created (or replaced) in the same schema. The underlying hidden table remains FS_FILTER_REGIONS_HIDDEN in the source schema.

Ownership note: by default take_ownership=False so _drop() will not touch the hidden table when called on the new manager. Pass take_ownership=True if this manager should be responsible for cleanup (rare for soft clones).

docs.title: Hard clone
docs.text:
The new manager gets its own physical copy of the hidden table. Changes to the source afterwards do not affect the clone.

fm_copy = FilterManager(table_name='FS_FILTER_REGIONS_COPY', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
fm_copy.clone_filter(
    source_filtermanager = fm_source,
    filter_id_to_apply   = 1,
    clone_mode           = 'hard',
)
# fm_copy._owns_hidden is now True — it may drop its table if needed

A CREATE TABLE … AS (SELECT * FROM …) WITH DATA is issued into fm_copy.schema_name. The copy is fully independent.

docs.title: Handling existing tables with if_exists
docs.text:
if_exists controls what happens when the destination hidden table already exists. It only has effect in hard clone mode — soft clones create only a view, never a table:

Value Behaviour "error" (default) Raises RuntimeError if the destination already exists "replace" Drops and recreates the destination table "skip" Reuses the existing table without re-copying 
fm_copy.clone_filter(
    source_filtermanager = fm_source,
    clone_mode           = 'hard',
    if_exists            = 'replace',   # safe for repeated pipeline runs
)

docs.title: Taking ownership after a soft clone
docs.text:
If you soft-cloned and later decide this manager should own cleanup:

fm_new.take_ownership()   # sets _owns_hidden = True

This does not copy any data; it only marks the instance. Use with care — two managers that both believe they own the same hidden table will both try to drop it.

docs.title: Choosing between soft and hard
docs.text: Soft clone Hard clone Data footprint No extra storage Doubles the hidden table Sync with source Automatic Independent snapshot Can drop hidden table Only with take_ownership=True Always (owns the copy) Typical use Multiple processes, same filter sequence, same shard window Archived copy for audit / replay

docs.title: Operational notes
docs.text:
• Seed small, expand later. Start with 3–5 partitions to validate the pipeline. Remove any QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() cap before a full roll-out.
  • Order by date descending in hybrid mode so the first ingestion run reflects the most recent business state.
  • Move the manager manually with fm.update(filter_id=N) when debugging a single partition. filter_id is 1-based.
  • Errors do not abort the loop. Each partition is wrapped in try/except — upload_features records FAILED,… in the follow-up table and continues with the next filter.

See the fs-filter skill for a copy-paste agent template.

docs.title: Inspect & monitor
docs.text: Five built-in catalogs — all scoped to the active DATA_DOMAIN — give you full visibility into what is registered, what ran, and where it lives.

docs.title: Catalogs
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.process_catalog()     # PROCESS_ID, VIEW_NAME, ENTITY_NAME, FEATURES, METADATA, ...
tdfs4ds.feature_catalog()     # FEATURE_NAME, ENTITY_NAME, FEATURE_TABLE, PROCESS_ID, ...
tdfs4ds.dataset_catalog()     # VIEW_NAME, ENTITY_NAME, COMMENT, ...
tdfs4ds.get_dataset_entity()
tdfs4ds.get_dataset_features()

Each returns a teradataml DataFrame — chain .to_pandas() for local manipulation.

docs.title: Find a process by view name
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.process_store.process_query_administration import get_process_id

# Fully qualified name
process_id = get_process_id('"my_db"."FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER"')

# Or just the view name (defaults to tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
process_id = get_process_id(view_name='FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER')

docs.title: Follow-up — every run, every status
docs.text:
follow_up = (
    tdfs4ds.process_store.process_followup.follow_up_report()
    .sort('START_DATETIME', ascending=False)
)

docs.title: Status values
docs.text:
Status Meaning COMPLETED Process ran successfully RUNNING Currently executing — or crashed before writing FAILED FAILED,… Exception raised; the suffix carries the error message

A stale RUNNING row after a crash means followup_close never executed (tdfs4ds.process_store.process_followup.followup_close re-raises on failure before its @execute_query_wrapper decorator runs the UPDATE). The feature data for that run is incomplete — re-run the process before relying on those values.

docs.title: Re-execute and remove
docs.text:
# Re-run at the current FEATURE_STORE_TIME
tdfs4ds.run(process_id=process_id)

# Remove a process from the catalog. Feature DATA is preserved.
tdfs4ds.process_store.process_query_administration.remove_process(process_id)

remove_process is metadata-only — the underlying temporal feature tables keep every row they ever had. Use it freely to clean up the catalog without worrying about data loss.

docs.title: Discover available features
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.feature_store.feature_query_retrieval import (
    get_list_entity,
    get_list_features,
    get_available_features,
    get_feature_versions,
)

get_list_entity()                          # entities in the active DATA_DOMAIN
get_list_features(entity_name='CUSTOMER_ID')
get_available_features(entity_id='CUSTOMER_ID')

docs.title: Version + diagnostics
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.__version__       # package version
tdfs4ds.list_skills()     # bundled agent skills
tdfs4ds.skill_catalog()   # full skill metadata

docs.title: TimeManager & roll_out
docs.text: TimeManager advances a "current business date" view, and roll_out iterates registered processes along that sequence. Together they backfill features across an arbitrary date range.

docs.title: How TimeManager works internally
docs.text:
TimeManager maintains two database objects:

  1. Hidden table (<view_name>_HIDDEN) — stores every time step as a row with TIME_ID (1..N, deterministic ROW_NUMBER()) and BUSINESS_DATE. Populated once by load_time_steps() and never mutated during replay.
  2. Public view (<view_name>) — always exposes exactly one row: WHERE TIME_ID = N. Advancing to the next step only rewrites this view definition — no data is touched.

Your feature engineering view reads the current date with a scalar subquery: 

WHERE TX_DATE <= (SELECT BUSINESS_DATE FROM MY_DB.FS_BUSINESS_TIME)
Teradata resolves the subquery at plan time and uses it as a filter constant.

load_time_steps()      → writes hidden table + creates view at TIME_ID=1
update(time_id=N)      → REPLACE VIEW … WHERE TIME_ID = N
display()              → pandas DataFrame with current BUSINESS_DATE
get_date_in_the_past() → formatted string e.g. "2024-03-15 00:00:00"

docs.title: Set up TimeManager
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.utils.time_management import TimeManager

bt = TimeManager(table_name='FS_BUSINESS_TIME', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)

# First time only: populate from a date column in the source
bt.load_time_steps(
    df       = source_data.groupby('TX_DATE').count().to_pandas(),
    time_column = 'TX_DATE',
)

print('Steps loaded:', bt.nb_time_steps)
print('Current step:', bt.get_current_step())

# Sync FEATURE_STORE_TIME with the current step
tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = bt.get_date_in_the_past()

After the first call, TimeManager reconnects to the existing hidden table on every subsequent instantiation — load_time_steps is a one-time setup.

docs.title: TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE upcasting
docs.text: When the time column carries timezone information, load_time_steps automatically upcasts the BUSINESS_DATE column from TIMESTAMP(N) → TIMESTAMP(N) WITH TIME ZONE to preserve offsets. The data_type attribute on the instance reflects the stored SQL type.

docs.title: Backing-table storage on Vantage Cloud Lake (storage='OFS')
docs.text:
By default the hidden table is created on block storage — unchanged, and the right choice for full primary-cluster processes. On Vantage Cloud Lake you can create it on the Object File System (OFS) instead:

bt = TimeManager(
    table_name  = 'FS_BUSINESS_TIME',
    schema_name = tdfs4ds.SCHEMA,
    storage     = 'OFS',        # None (default) = block storage, unchanged
)
bt.load_time_steps(df=..., time_column='TX_DATE')

This emits MAP = TD_MAP2, STORAGE = TD_OFSSTORAGE. The choice is per manager, so block-storage and OFS managers coexist in one session; it only affects table creation (ofs_map= overrides the map, default TD_MAP2). Confirm with SHOW TABLE <view>_HIDDEN.

tdstone2 Apply on the ACC: OFS manager table and a JOIN (not a scalar subquery)

A tdstone2 model scored via the Apply backend runs on the Analytical Compute Cluster (ACC), which reads from OFS. For a scoring view that references the TimeManager, two conditions must both hold:

  1. The manager table on OFS — storage='OFS' (above), so the ACC can read it. A block-storage manager table read by the ACC raises Error 6881.
  2. Reference the date via a JOIN, not a correlated scalar subquery. The ACC rejects ... WHERE tx_date <= (SELECT BUSINESS_DATE FROM <tm_view>) with 6881, but accepts a JOIN to the manager view. Confirmed live on VCL: OFS + JOIN scores; OFS + scalar subquery still 6881s.

Authoring the filter as a JOIN is the user's responsibility — it is a property of the scoring view's SQL, e.g.: 

bt.update(time_id=i)                       # advance the manager view
tdml.execute_sql(f"""
    REPLACE VIEW my_db.vw_scoring_input AS
    SELECT s.*
    FROM source s
    JOIN {tdfs4ds.SCHEMA}.{bt.view_name} tm   -- JOIN, not a scalar subquery
      ON s.tx_date <= tm.BUSINESS_DATE
""")
model.score()                              # lands predictions in the OFS scores repo
tdfs4ds.upload_features(...)               # register the scored rows
The JOIN keeps the date dynamic, so time-travel via update() still works (a hard-coded literal also passes on the ACC, but freezes the date). roll_out/run() cannot drive an Apply model themselves — their ingestion CTAS (CREATE VOLATILE TABLE AS SELECT * FROM <live scoring view>) also hits 6881; call score() first, then upload_features.

docs.title: roll_out across a range
docs.text:
from tdfs4ds.process_store.process_query_administration import get_process_id

views = tdfs4ds.process_catalog()[['VIEW_NAME']].to_pandas().VIEW_NAME.values
v1 = [v for v in views if 'FEAT_ENG_TX' in v][0]
v2 = [v for v in views if 'FEAT_ENG_CUST' in v][0]

pid_1 = get_process_id(view_name=v1)
pid_2 = get_process_id(view_name=v2)

tdfs4ds.roll_out(
    process_list      = [pid_1, pid_2],
    time_manager      = bt,
    time_id_start     = 1,
    time_id_end       = 30,           # roll over 30 steps
    force_display_logs= False,
)

time_id_start and time_id_end are 1-based positions in the ordered time sequence, not timestamps. Step 1 is the earliest date in the loaded range.

roll_out does three things per step:

  1. bt.update(time_id=i) — advance the TimeManager view.
  2. Sync tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME to bt.get_date_in_the_past().
  3. Call tdfs4ds.run(process_id=pid) for every process in process_list.

docs.title: Manual single-step
docs.text:
bt.update(time_id=10)
tdfs4ds.FEATURE_STORE_TIME = bt.get_date_in_the_past()
tdfs4ds.run(process_id=pid_1)

Use manual stepping when you need to inspect intermediate output between steps — roll_out runs to completion (or until a process raises).

docs.title: Inspection methods
docs.text:
Method Returns Description display() pd.DataFrame Current BUSINESS_DATE from the public view get_date_in_the_past() str Formatted date string (with tz offset when applicable) get_list_date() teradataml.DataFrame All rows in the hidden table get_current_step() int TIME_ID that matches the current business date get_current_timeid() int Active TIME_ID parsed from view DDL generate_timeline() teradataml.DataFrame Timeline view up to (or strictly before) current date print_view_ddl() — Logs the view DDL for troubleshooting 
# Inspect all available dates
bt.get_list_date().to_pandas()

# Create a cumulative timeline view for a dataset join
bt.generate_timeline(
    schema_name      = tdfs4ds.SCHEMA,
    view_name        = 'FS_TIMELINE',
    current_included = True,    # include today's date in the window
)

docs.title: Pruning
docs.text:
prune_time() removes all steps with TIME_ID < N and renumbers the remaining ones so the smallest id becomes 1. Use it to free space in the hidden table after a successful backfill.

# Compact to the current position (discard everything before today)
bt.prune_time()                   # uses get_current_timeid() automatically

# Or keep only from step 20 onwards
bt.prune_time(time_id=20)

After pruning, the public view is repointed to TIME_ID=1.

docs.title: Cloning
docs.text: Cloning lets one TimeManager re-use the step sequence owned by another. The API mirrors FilterManager's clone_filter and supports the same two modes.

docs.title: Soft clone (default)
docs.text:
The new manager's view points directly to the source hidden table. No data is copied. Both managers see the same steps; their active TIME_ID is independent.

# Source manager already loaded and in use for process 1
bt_source = TimeManager(table_name='FS_BUSINESS_TIME', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)

# New manager for process 2 — shares the same hidden table
bt_new = TimeManager(table_name='FS_BUSINESS_TIME_P2', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
bt_new.clone_timer(
    source_timemanager = bt_source,
    time_id_to_apply   = 1,       # start at the first step
    clone_mode         = 'soft',  # default
)

The FS_BUSINESS_TIME_P2 view is created (or replaced) in the same schema. The underlying hidden table remains FS_BUSINESS_TIME_HIDDEN in the source schema. roll_out can now advance bt_new independently of bt_source.

Ownership note: by default take_ownership=False so this manager will not drop the shared hidden table. Pass take_ownership=True only when this manager should be responsible for the shared table's lifecycle.

docs.title: Hard clone
docs.text:
The new manager gets its own physical copy of the hidden table. Changes to the source afterwards do not affect the clone.

bt_copy = TimeManager(table_name='FS_BUSINESS_TIME_ARCHIVE', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
bt_copy.clone_timer(
    source_timemanager = bt_source,
    time_id_to_apply   = 1,
    clone_mode         = 'hard',
)
# bt_copy._owns_hidden is now True

A CREATE TABLE … AS (SELECT * FROM …) WITH DATA is issued into bt_copy.schema_name. The copy is fully independent.

docs.title: Handling existing tables with if_exists
docs.text:
Value Behaviour "error" (default) Raises RuntimeError if destination already exists "replace" Drops and recreates the destination table "skip" Reuses the existing table without re-copying 
bt_copy.clone_timer(
    source_timemanager = bt_source,
    clone_mode         = 'hard',
    if_exists          = 'replace',    # safe for repeated pipeline runs
)

docs.title: Taking ownership after a soft clone
docs.text:
take_ownership() on a TimeManager instance can either just flag ownership or physically copy the data into this schema, depending on the create_copy parameter:

# Option A — copy data into this schema and repoint the view
bt_new.take_ownership(create_copy=True, if_exists='replace')

# Option B — just mark as owned (only works when the hidden table is already
#             in this schema with the expected name)
bt_new.take_ownership(create_copy=False)

After create_copy=True, the manager's hidden table is the new local copy; the view is repointed to the active TIME_ID before the call.

docs.title: Choosing between soft and hard
docs.text: Soft clone Hard clone Data footprint No extra storage Doubles the hidden table Sync with source Automatic Independent snapshot Can drop hidden table Only with take_ownership=True Always (owns the copy) Typical use Multiple processes sharing one calendar Archived snapshot for audit / replay

docs.title: Combining with FilterManager
docs.text:
roll_out and FilterManager compose naturally:

Setup Effect roll_out only One ingestion call per business date roll_out + standard FilterManager (on a different column) One ingestion call per (business date × filter value) Hybrid FilterManager only Date × partition pre-computed; no TimeManager needed

Pick the hybrid FilterManager when the date × partition combinations are sparse and known in advance; pick roll_out when you need a dense daily roll over a continuous range.

Pattern: cloned timer + filtered roll-out

When multiple processes share the same date calendar but each has its own partition filter, clone the timer and attach the relevant FilterManager to each process:

# Both processes use the same calendar
bt_p1 = TimeManager(table_name='FS_BT_PROCESS1', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
bt_p1.clone_timer(bt_source, clone_mode='soft')

bt_p2 = TimeManager(table_name='FS_BT_PROCESS2', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)
bt_p2.clone_timer(bt_source, clone_mode='soft')

# Process 1 is also segmented by region
fm = FilterManager(table_name='FS_FILTER_REGIONS', schema_name=tdfs4ds.SCHEMA)

# roll_out automatically calls fm.update() on each time step when filtermanager is set
tdfs4ds.roll_out(process_list=[pid_1], time_manager=bt_p1, filtermanager=fm,
                  time_id_start=1, time_id_end=bt_source.nb_time_steps)

# Process 2 rolls without a segment filter
tdfs4ds.roll_out(process_list=[pid_2], time_manager=bt_p2,
                  time_id_start=1, time_id_end=bt_source.nb_time_steps)

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.process_store.process_followup.follow_up_report().sort('START_DATETIME', ascending=False)

The follow-up report lists every run with STATUS, START_DATETIME, END_DATETIME, and the failure message when applicable. See Inspect & monitor.

docs.title: UAF ART-layer features ('uaf art view')
docs.text: Register a UAF (Unbounded Array Framework) feature whose value lives in a secondary ART (Analytical Result Table) layer as a governed, run()-recomputable tdfs4ds feature — ARIMA fit-metrics (R_SQUARE/MAPE/AIC in ARTFITMETADATA), residual-adequacy tests over ARTFITRESIDUALS (Ljung-Box / Durbin-Watson / Breusch-Godfrey), TD_PACF, and similar.

docs.title: Why a dedicated process type
docs.text:
A UAF operator whose entire result is in its inline output — TD_ACF, TD_DICKEY_FULLER — can be served by wrapping the inline EXECUTE FUNCTION inside a REPLACE VIEW and registering it as an ordinary 'denormalized view'. But a feature whose value lives in a secondary layer needs two statements:

  1. materialise the operator's ART, then
  2. TD_EXTRACT_RESULTS over a named LAYER of that ART.

tdfs4ds ingestion is a single CREATE VOLATILE TABLE AS (SELECT * FROM <view>), with nowhere to run the first statement — and every attempt to fold both into one object is rejected by Vantage (nested EXECUTE FUNCTION → 3706, INTO ART in a view → 3707, multi-statement view/macro → 3576).

The 'uaf art view' process type (v0.3.1.143) closes this gap. It stores the ART-materialising statements as art_sql and runs them per FilterManager partition, in the same session, immediately before the ingestion CTAS — so the session-scoped volatile ART is visible to the CTAS reading the feature view.

docs.title: The model — one feature view + art_sql + art_layers
docs.text:
A 'uaf art view' process stores three things:

Piece What art_sql A list of EXECUTE FUNCTION ... INTO VOLATILE ART(<name>) statements. Session-scoped: no PERM, auto-cleaned. Stored in the process METADATA JSON column — no DDL migration. art_layers The declared LAYER(...) name(s) the feature view reads (e.g. ['ARTFITMETADATA']). Used for documentation and lineage. the feature view The ordinary feature view, reading each layer via TD_EXTRACT_RESULTS(ART_SPEC(TABLE_NAME(<art>), LAYER(<layer>))).

Order matters in art_sql

Statements execute in list order, and a later statement may read an ART an earlier one created (chained operators — e.g. a residual test over the ARTFITRESIDUALS layer of an ARIMA fit). A JSON array preserves that order; never sort or reorder art_sql.

docs.title: Full example — ARIMA fit-metrics (R_SQUARE + MAPE)
docs.text:
import teradataml as tdml
import tdfs4ds
from tdfs4ds import upload_features
from tdfs4ds.utils.filter_management import FilterManager

DB = 'MY_FS'; SRC = 'MY_SRC'
tdfs4ds.SCHEMA = DB
tdfs4ds.DATA_DOMAIN = 'FORECASTABILITY'

# 1. FilterManager over the series id (one model per series/partition)
fm = FilterManager(table_name='fm_series', schema_name=DB)
fm.load_filter(tdml.DataFrame.from_query(f"SELECT DISTINCT item_id FROM {SRC}.sales"))

# 2. series-input view — the store filter lives here as a scalar subquery
#    (partition-aware, no templating)
tdml.execute_sql(f"""
REPLACE VIEW {DB}.vw_series_in AS
LOCKING ROW FOR ACCESS
SELECT item_id,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY item_id ORDER BY cal_date) AS seq_no,
       sales_qty
FROM {SRC}.sales
WHERE item_id = (SELECT item_id FROM {fm.schema_name}.{fm.view_name})
""")

# 3. art_sql — materialise the ARIMA ART with FIT_METRICS(1) (generates ARTFITMETADATA)
art_sql = [f"""
EXECUTE FUNCTION INTO VOLATILE ART(arima_fit)
  TD_ARIMAESTIMATE(
    SERIES_SPEC(TABLE_NAME({DB}.vw_series_in), ROW_AXIS(SEQUENCE(seq_no)),
      SERIES_ID(item_id), PAYLOAD(FIELDS(sales_qty), CONTENT(REAL))),
    FUNC_PARAMS(NONSEASONAL(MODEL_ORDER(1,0,0)), CONSTANT(1), ALGORITHM(MLE),
      FIT_PERCENTAGE(100), FIT_METRICS(1)))
"""]

# 4. materialise the ART ONCE, then create the feature view (see lifecycle note below)
fm.update(1)
tdml.execute_sql(art_sql[0])
tdml.execute_sql(f"""
REPLACE VIEW {DB}.vw_feat_arima_fit AS
LOCKING ROW FOR ACCESS
SELECT item_id,
       CAST(R_SQUARE AS FLOAT) AS r_square,
       CAST(MAPE     AS FLOAT) AS mape
FROM ( EXECUTE FUNCTION TD_EXTRACT_RESULTS(
         ART_SPEC(TABLE_NAME(arima_fit), LAYER(ARTFITMETADATA))) ) x
""")

# 5. register + ingest — art_sql runs per partition, before the CTAS
upload_features(
    tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema(DB, 'vw_feat_arima_fit')),
    entity_id     = {'item_id': 'VARCHAR(30)'},
    feature_names = ['r_square', 'mape'],
    art_sql       = art_sql,
    art_layers    = ['ARTFITMETADATA'],
    filtermanager = fm,          # tdfs4ds drives its own per-partition loop
    metadata      = {'project': 'forecastability'},
    # optimize_query is force-disabled for a uaf art view; document=True is fine.
)

docs.title: Verified grammar (Vantage 20)
docs.text:
• TD_EXTRACT_RESULTS is invoked via EXECUTE FUNCTION inside a derived table — FROM ( EXECUTE FUNCTION TD_EXTRACT_RESULTS(ART_SPEC(TABLE_NAME(<art>), LAYER(<layer>))) ) x. A plain FROM TD_EXTRACT_RESULTS(...) is a syntax error (3707).
  • TD_ARIMAESTIMATE uses FUNC_PARAMS(NONSEASONAL(MODEL_ORDER(p,d,q)), CONSTANT(1), ALGORITHM(MLE), FIT_PERCENTAGE(100), FIT_METRICS(1)). FIT_METRICS(1) generates the ARTFITMETADATA layer; RESIDUALS(1) generates ARTFITRESIDUALS.
  • The ARTFITMETADATA layer projects named columns (item_id, ROW_I, NUM_SAMPLES, R_SQUARE, R_ADJ_SQUARE, MAE, MSE, MAPE, …), one row per series — not a METRIC/VALUE key-value shape. Confirm the exact columns against your Vantage build.

Creation-time lifecycle constraint

A view referencing a volatile ART cannot be REPLACE VIEW-created unless that ART already exists in the session (Error 3807). So materialise the ART once (EXECUTE FUNCTION ... INTO VOLATILE ART) before creating the feature view (step 4 above). The permanent view then survives across sessions, but reading it always needs the ART present — which run() guarantees by re-materialising the ART per partition before each ingestion.

docs.title: What run() does for this type
docs.text: Per FilterManager partition: filtermanager.update() → DROP TABLE <art> then execute each art_sql statement (materialise the volatile ART) → ingest from the feature view (which reads the ART layer) → close follow-up. run(process_id, force_compute=True) re-materialises and re-ingests idempotently.

docs.title: AI features, lineage, gates
docs.text:
• optimize_query is force-disabled — art_sql is an opaque UAF invocation and the view EXPLAIN needs the ART materialised first.
  • Documentation skips the view-EXPLAIN and feeds the art_sql operator + art_layers into the column-documentation context (grounding the layer columns in the real computation). get_process_info surfaces ART_SQL/ART_LAYERS.
  • Lineage traces through the ephemeral ART — the dangling feature_view → <volatile ART> edge is rewritten to feature_view → <operator/layer node> → series_input_source (parsed from art_sql).

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• upload_features — the art_sql / art_layers kwargs.
  • FilterManager — per-partition segmentation.
  • Persisted lineage graph — where the traced edges land.

docs.title: upload_features — register and ingest
docs.text: tdfs4ds.upload_features is the single entry point for going from a Teradata view to a registered, persisted, time-travel ready feature set.

docs.title: Signature
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df,
    entity_id,
    feature_names,
    *,
    metadata        = None,
    primary_index   = None,
    filtermanager   = None,
    dataset_view_name = None,
    document        = True,
    optimize_query  = False,
    update_view     = False,
    max_candidates  = None,
    sql_query       = None,   # SQL-first: a SELECT defining the source
    target_view_name = None,  # where to create the view for sql_query
    art_sql         = None,   # UAF ART view: INTO VOLATILE ART statements
    art_layers      = None,   # UAF ART view: the LAYER(...) names the view reads
)
Argument Type Notes df teradataml.DataFrame or 'schema.view' str A DataFrame wrapping a permanent view, or a fully-qualified view name (schema mandatory). Use crystallize_view first if a DataFrame wraps a temporary view. sql_query str SQL-first path: a SELECT defining the source. Requires target_view_name. Takes precedence over df. target_view_name str 'schema.view' where the view for sql_query is created (schema mandatory; must not already exist). entity_id dict or str {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'}. A plain string is allowed for single-column entities (types are auto-detected); lists are rejected. feature_names list[str] Subset of df.columns excluding the entity columns. metadata dict Free-form metadata stored on the process catalog row. filtermanager FilterManager Drives segmented ingestion. See FilterManager. dataset_view_name str If set, builds a denormalised dataset view in the same call. Convention: vw_feat_<x> → ds_<x>. document bool (default True) When an instruct model is configured, document the process automatically. Silently skipped otherwise. optimize_query bool (default False) Run the query optimizer on the registered process view. update_view bool When True and an optimization improves the score, REPLACE VIEW is issued so subsequent runs use the rewritten SQL. art_sql list[str] UAF ART view (opt-in). A list of EXECUTE FUNCTION ... INTO VOLATILE ART(<name>) statements materialised per partition before ingestion so the feature view can read a secondary ART layer. Registers the process as PROCESS_TYPE='uaf art view'. Requires art_layers. See UAF ART-layer features. art_layers list[str] Required with art_sql: the declared LAYER(...) names the feature view reads (e.g. ['ARTFITMETADATA']). Used for documentation and lineage.

docs.title: Three ways to supply the source
docs.text: upload_features accepts the feature source in three forms. The classic DataFrame path is unchanged; the other two are for pure-SQL users who don't build teradataml DataFrames.

docs.title: 1. A teradataml DataFrame (classic)
docs.text:
df = tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema('my_db', 'FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER'))
tdfs4ds.upload_features(df, entity_id=['CUSTOMER_ID'],
                        feature_names=['nb_transactions', 'total_amount'])

docs.title: 2. A view name ('schema.view')
docs.text:
Already have a view? Pass its fully-qualified name. The schema is mandatory — a bare view name is rejected.

tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    'my_db.FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER',                       # schema.view — schema required
    entity_id     = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    feature_names = ['nb_transactions', 'total_amount'],
)

docs.title: 3. A SQL query + a target view (SQL-first)
docs.text:
Give a SELECT and the schema.view to create. tdfs4ds validates the SQL with EXPLAIN, creates the view (it must not already exist), confirms it is executable, and uses it as the source. The created view is permanent — it becomes the process view re-run by run(process_id), so pick a stable name.

tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    sql_query = """
        SELECT CUSTOMER_ID,
               COUNT(*)    AS nb_transactions,
               SUM(AMOUNT) AS total_amount
        FROM source_db.TRANSACTIONS
        GROUP BY CUSTOMER_ID
    """,
    target_view_name = 'my_db.FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER',     # schema.view to CREATE (schema required)
    entity_id        = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    feature_names    = ['nb_transactions', 'total_amount'],
)

Errors are logged, not raised

Recoverable input problems — a view name without a schema, a target_view_name that already exists, or SQL/​a view that fails to compile or isn't executable — do not raise a Python traceback. Instead a clear message with a fix is written to the log and upload_features returns None. If a call returns None, read the log. For example, if the target view already exists you'll be told to DROP VIEW my_db.FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER; first (or choose a different target_view_name) — the existing view is never silently overwritten.

docs.title: UAF ART-layer features (art_sql / art_layers)
docs.text: For a UAF (Unbounded Array Framework) feature whose value lives in a secondary ART layer — ARIMA fit-metrics (R_SQUARE/MAPE/AIC in ARTFITMETADATA), residual tests over ARTFITRESIDUALS, TD_PACF — pass art_sql (the INTO VOLATILE ART statements that materialise the ART) and art_layers (the layer names your view reads). tdfs4ds materialises the ART per partition, in-session, before ingestion, so the view can read the layer. See the dedicated guide: UAF ART-layer features.

docs.title: Full example
docs.text:
tdml.execute_sql("""
    REPLACE VIEW my_db.FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER AS
    SELECT
        CUSTOMER_ID,
        COUNT(*)        AS nb_transactions,
        SUM(AMOUNT)     AS total_amount,
        AVG(AMOUNT)     AS avg_amount
    FROM source_db.TRANSACTIONS
    GROUP BY CUSTOMER_ID
""")

df = tdml.DataFrame(tdml.in_schema('my_db', 'FEAT_ENG_CUSTOMER'))

tdfs4ds.upload_features(
    df,
    entity_id     = {'CUSTOMER_ID': 'BIGINT'},
    feature_names = ['nb_transactions', 'total_amount', 'avg_amount'],
    metadata      = {'project': 'customer_analytics'},
    dataset_view_name = 'ds_customer_spend',
    document      = True,
)

docs.title: What happens internally
docs.text:
Step Module Type detection on entity columns utils.info.Gettdtypes Entity & feature registration feature_store.entity_management, feature_store.feature_store_management Process registration process_store.process_registration_management Optional simplify_query + optimize_query agent.query_optimizer Optional document_process_incremental genai.documentation Volatile staging table + MERGE (or UPDATE+INSERT) feature_store.feature_data_processing Optional build_dataset + document_dataset_incremental dataset.builder, genai.documentation COLLECT STATS on every target feature table feature_store.feature_data_processing.apply_collect_stats

A failing batch always re-raises after recording FAILED in the follow-up table. With a FilterManager, each filter is wrapped in try/except so a single failed partition does not abort the whole loop.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
• Always pass a permanent view. Temporary views vanish with the teradataml context — your registered process will be unable to re-run. Use tdfs4ds.utils.lineage.crystallize_view if your DataFrame chain produced a temporary view.
  • Never construct selected_features manually for the dataset. When dataset_view_name is set, tdfs4ds resolves feature versions automatically.
  • upload_features calls run() internally — this is intentional, not a bug. It exercises the exact same code path used by roll_out and re-execution, catching corner cases at registration time.
  • Entity-key types are preserved exactly. When entity_id is a list or string, the SQL type of each key is inferred from the source view with its full length and character set — a CHAR(14) key is created as CHAR(14) in the feature table, never truncated to CHAR(1). Pass a dict only when you want to override the inferred type (it is then used verbatim — include the length).
  • NULL entity keys are forbidden. A NULL in any entity / primary-index key column raises a ValueError during ingestion, because a NULL key produces a cartesian-product MERGE (severe skew and storage blow-up). Either filter the NULL rows out of the source view, or pass entity_null_substitute={'COL': <value>} to replace them via COALESCE.

docs.title: Re-execution
docs.text:
tdfs4ds.run(process_id=process_id)

run re-applies the registered view at the current FEATURE_STORE_TIME. For historical replay, advance TimeManager and re-run — see TimeManager & roll_out.
